<!-- page 222 -->

Michael Dunn
204
4.4 More complex cases
It is also possible to use phonological and morphological innovations as characters in the 
analysis, rather than constraints. This is the approach taken by Ringe, Warnow, and Taylor 
(2002) and Nakhleh (2011), in an analysis which seeks to reconcile both cognate histories 
and shared phonological and morphological innovations. Within the Bayesian Phylogenetic 
Inference approach it would probably be most appropriate to do this using partitions – 
carrying out the analysis with a different model used for cognate evolution than for 
morphological/phonological innovations. A similar partitioned analysis can be used to carry 
out phylogenetic inference on other kinds of disparate data where different evolutionary 
processes apply to different parts of the data.
Dunn et al. (2008) and Dunn (2009) present Bayesian Phylogenetic inferences based on 
typological features of language rather than lexicon. The rationale in this case was to 
investigate the history of a set of languages showing typological similarities which were 
suggestive of a historical relationship, but which did not show lexical similarities suf cient 
to demonstrate their relatedness (see also Dunn and Terrill 2012). This approach has been 
adopted for languages where suf ciently complete word lists are unavailable (e.g. Daniëlsen, 
Dunn, and Muysken 2011). The reliability of historical inference based on typological 
features is the subject of some controversy (Donohue and Musgrave 2007; Dunn et al. 2007); 
a non-controversial use of typological features is for comparison of the linguistic similarity 
between languages which are not (or not known to be) related for the purposes of hypothesis 
generation or testing patterns of genetic versus linguistic diversity (Hunley et al. 2007; 
Hunley et al. 2008; Dunn 2009; Reesink, Singer, and Dunn 2009).
A number of Bayesian phylogenetic methods deal with reticulation. The reticulated tree 
model of Shijulal et al. (2011) adapts some of the insights of evolutionary microbiology to 
linguistic questions of family-internal borrowing. Bacterial evolution is highly complex, as 
bacteria have a number of mechanisms which allow them to combine and exchange DNA, 
including swapping of the DNA coding for complete functional subsystems (e.g. antibiotic 
resistance). A naïve reconstruction of evolutionary history of bacteria would infer an ancestral 
mega-bacterium, already embodying the precursors to all the DNA diversity of its descendants. 
The essential insight of this method is to apply the uniformitarian hypothesis, that bacteria in 
the past were not substantially different to bacteria in the present; more precisely, the size of 
the genome of ancestral bacteria should  t within the distribution of observed genome sizes 
in present-day bacteria. A Bayesian phylogenetic tree search is carried out with an additional 
‘horizontal gene transfer’ parameter. This parameter is optimised to maintain the inferred 
genome size at ancestral nodes to within the statistical distribution of known bacteria. The 
linguistic adaptation of this uses the same logic: rather than inferring an ancestral language 
containing all the lexical diversity of the contemporary languages, the algorithm infers lexical 
transfer where it can ef ciently account for shared cognates in distant branches. By keeping 
the inferred size of the ancestral lexicon within the bounds of the observed distribution, this 
avoids pushing the date of proto-forms further back than they need to be.
Ringe, Warnow, and Taylor (2002) take a different kind of perspective to the problem of 
phylogenetic inference from the Bayesian inference approach. They develop an algorithm to 
test the perfect phylogeny problem, that is, whether a tree can be found which perfectly 
reconciles a set of phonological, morphological and lexical characters. In a series of tests on 
data from the Indo-European language family, they show that a perfect phylogeny is not 
possible. While most characters were compatible with a single evolutionary tree, there were 
inconsistencies localised particularly in the Germanic languages, presumably re ecting

---

<!-- page 223 -->

Language phylogenies
205
borrowing. Extensions of this method, reported in Nakhleh, Ringe, and Warnow (2005; 
Warnow et al. 2006), adapt the perfect phylogenies method to search instead for perfect 
phylogenetic networks. The perfect phylogenetic network infers a tree with a certain number 
of contact/reticulate branches which allow horizontal transfer events. The method seeks to 
maximise the amount of tree-like character transmission (as per the previous perfect 
phylogenetic tree approach), and minimise the contact edges and transfer events.
In a complete departure from tree and network models, the STRUCTURE method 
(Pritchard, Stephens, and Donnelly 2000; Rosenberg et al. 2002) infers ancestral population 
structure by modelling evolutionary admixture. This method has been used by Reesink, 
Singer, and Dunn (2009) to investigate language dispersal and contact in the Sahul region, 
and explore the possible traces of ancient Australian–Papuan connections.
5 Testing hypotheses about language change
Quantitative phylogenetic approaches to language change should not be treated solely as an 
alternative to traditional methods in historical linguistics. As a tool for inferring language 
phylogeny, quantitative methods are inseparable from the Comparative Method. They are 
methodologically inseparable, because they rely on the same fundamental notions such as 
‘cognacy’, but also conceptually inseparable, because the Comparative Method provides a 
kind of gold standard for evaluating new phylogenetic results. But from another perspective, 
quantitative phylogenetic methods have much broader ambitions: that they are the  rst step 
for incorporating statistically framed models of historical relatedness into tests of hypotheses 
about evolved phenomena. In a rather unfortunate collision of terminology, these methods are 
called ‘comparative methods’ in evolutionary biology (less often ‘The Comparative Method’, 
but see Harvey and Pagel [1991]). Phylogenetic comparative methods use phylogenetic trees 
to model other evolved aspects of language. This can involve inference of ancestral states, 
tests of dispersal order of languages, rates of change and evolutionary regime, coevolution of 
aspects of language, or of language and culture (Mace and Pagel 1994; Greenhill, this volume).
5.1 Dating and Phylogeography
The techniques for dating phylogenetic trees are closely integrated into Bayesian phylogenetic 
inference, as implemented by the BEAST package, although it is possible to log trees 
calibrated by substitution rates only. It is only a small conceptual step from substitution rates 
to chronological rates: if some tree-internal chronological calibration points are known (such 
as ancient inscriptions recording the arrival of a new ethnolinguistic group, or archaeological 
evidence dating the  rst arrival on an island), normalising factors can be calculated for each 
branch according to what is required to morph the uncalibrated tree so as to match the 
calibration points. The dates of internal nodes are speci ed as a probability distribution, and 
different types of distribution can express different kinds of prior knowledge. A lognormal 
prior can express a situation where we know that node N1 must be dated before time T1, but 
unlikely to be a long time before; a normal prior expresses knowledge that node N2 must be 
around time T2 with a certain standard deviation; a uniform prior expresses that node N3 must 
be some time between T3 and T4. During the MCMC parameter search it is possible to log the 
inferred dates for the root and any other unobserved internal nodes of interest.
Other kinds of historical prior are also possible. In the phylogeographic approach a model 
of spatial diffusion is incorporated into the model, along with spatial priors (Walker and 
Ribeiro 2011; Bouckaert et al. 2012). This allows probabilistic inference of the spatial

---

<!-- page 224 -->

Michael Dunn
206
location of particular nodes in the past. As the spatial inference is incorporated into the 
likelihood calculation, this represents a principled violation of the principle of ‘Only 
Linguistic Evidence’ (Campbell 1998: 323).
5.2 Character evolution
One of the principal uses of phylogenetic comparative methods is to model the evolution of 
characters along trees. The tree is generated independently to this analysis; in effect, the Bayesian 
tree sample is a phylogenetic prior in the analysis of the evolutionary behaviour of another 
character. Phylogenetic comparative methods can be used to infer ancestral states of a feature 
which is known to evolve along with the family. There are three requirements: (i) a genealogical 
hypothesis; (ii) a model of transitions between states of a feature (e.g. probabilities for transition 
from ‘presence’ to ‘absence’, and from ‘absence’ to ‘presence’); and (iii) a set of observations 
of feature states from the tips of the tree. From these the method allows us to infer the most 
likely parameters of the model, and thus to make probabilistic judgements about the state of the 
feature at unobserved nodes of the tree. This is a standard technique used in computational 
phylogenetics, and has been applied in cultural evolutionary studies of kinship and residence 
systems in Austronesian (Jordan et al. 2009; Jordan 2011) and Indo-European societies 
(Fortunato, Holden, and Mace 2006; Fortunato 2011a, 2011b, 2011c), as well as to infer 
ancestral subsistence mode in Bantu-speaking societies (Mace and Holden 2005). Ancestral 
state reconstruction methods have barely begun to be exploited for purely linguistic questions.
Phylogenetic comparative methods can also be used to test for evolutionary dependencies, 
i.e. whether changes in one trait on a tree regularly correspond to changes in another trait. 
This technique (currently available as the DISCRETE test in the BayesTraits package) has 
been used to test evolutionary hypotheses in anthropology and linguistics. Holden and Mace 
(1997, 2003) use the method to show how the cultural innovation of pastoralism led to the 
evolution of lactose tolerance and patrilineal descent in Bantu societies (see also Mace and 
Holden 2005). Dunn et al. (2011) use the same methods to test some of Dryer’s generalisations 
about the Greenbergian word order universals.
5.3 Evolutionary ordering and evolutionary mode
I date the start of the modern phylogenetic era in linguistics to Gray and Jordan (2000), 
whose clever treatment of dispersal order as an ordered set of multistate features allowed 
them to use a parsimony analysis to test the Taiwanese Origin hypothesis for Austronesian. 
Later, Bayesian analyses addressing the same question have improved upon these beginnings 
statistically, but have not produced a substantially different result (Gray, Drummond, and 
Greenhill 2009). Holden (2002) addresses a similar question for Bantu-speaking cultures, but 
in terms of subsistence rather than spatial location.
The best known study is Atkinson et al. (2008), which tested whether the Austronesian 
languages showed punctuated evolution, or jumps in the rate of change correlating with 
nodes on the tree. Punctuated evolution can be measured with Pagel’s }, one of Pagel’s three 
comparative method statistics (Pagel 1997; Pagel 1999). Pagel’s , along with Blomberg’s K, 
tests for phylogenetic signal: the extent to which a trait (e.g. a feature of language) follows a 
pattern which is determined by phylogeny (Pagel 1997, 1999; Blomberg, Garland, and Ives 
2003). Pagel’s other phylogenetic comparative method test, Pagel’s , which tests whether 
rates of change increase or decrease over time, does not have any obvious application to any 
linguistic questions.

---

<!-- page 225 -->

Language phylogenies
207
6 Conclusion
Recent advances in quantitative approaches to language history have put diachronic 
approaches at the forefront of modern linguistics. The power of computational phylogenetics 
and network analyses to perform explicit testing of evolutionary scenarios is only beginning 
to be explored.
7 Technical advice
MrBayes (Huelsenbeck and Ronquist 2001; Ronquist and Huelsenbeck 2003) is a good, 
general-purpose Bayesian Phylogenetic Inference package, and is well-documented and 
reasonably easy to use. BEAST (Drummond et al. 2012) is more complex, but is also closer 
to the forefront of methodological development in phylogenetics, and the developers are 
active participants in language evolution projects. The Stochastic Dollo model in BEAST 
was implemented speci cally for modelling the evolution of language. One of the dif culties 
in using BEAST is that it doesn’t offer any default setting: the developers do not want to 
encourage researchers to use software without making informed decisions about priors. This 
uncompromising attitude is offset, however, by the friendly and helpful user community.
The phylogenetic packages provided for the R statistical programming language are 
highly  exible and the number of different packages is growing rapidly. The Phylogenetics 
Task View on CRAN (http://cran.r-project.org/web/views/Phylogenetics.html) is a 
continuously updated list of the different methods available.
8 Outstanding problems
Phylogenetic linguistics is an area undergoing rapid development, and we can look forward 
to improvements and further developments to many of the ideas described above. With 
respect to phylogenetic inference proper, we can expect to see more work on computational 
replication of the Comparative Method (detecting sound changes, inferring cognates and 
sound changes simultaneously). At the time of writing Bouchard-Côté et al. (2013) doesn’t 
yet offer a usable methodology for non-developers to apply their techniques, but we can 
expect that this approach will gain in power and credibility. Borrowing and reticulation in 
trees has been addressed (Nakhleh, Ringe, and Warnow 2005; Shijulal et al. 2011), but these 
methods are not currently mature enough to be used more widely: no standard software 
incorporates these methods, and they are not yet being used outside the teams that developed 
them.
Once phylogenetic network methods become more widely usable, then we can hope that 
it will become possible to begin using networks in phylogenetic comparative method 
hypothesis testing. Phylogenetic comparative methods are also developing rapidly at the 
moment, and the possibilities for using them to examine new linguistic questions in tree-like 
phylogenies are certainly not exhausted either.
Finally, we can always use better databases. This should include more, higher quality 
language data, as well as linguistic databases linked to databases from the cultural, 
geographical, ecological, historical, and biological domains. The Austronesian Basic 
Vocabulary Database (http://language.psy.auckland.ac.nz/austronesian/) and the Indo-
European Lexical Cognate database (http://ielex.mpi.nl/) stand out here as large, accessible 
databases undergoing active development.

---

<!-- page 230 -->

212
8
Diachronic stability 
and typology
Søren Wichmann
1 Introduction1
Traditionally, historical linguists have been concerned about tracking how items consisting 
of form–function pairings change between the different stages of a linguistic lineage. The 
form of an item can change, as when a sound change applies to a segment in a word, or the 
function can change, as when a word undergoes semantic change. Sound change and semantic 
change can be studied independently of one another. But for the purpose of the traditional 
Comparative Method form and function are inseparable; form–function pairings constituting 
the essential comparanda. Since at least some aspects of form–function pairings must be 
ignored for unrelated languages to be at all comparable, language typologists have looked 
more abstractly at languages. This is probably the reason why the idea of studying the 
historical behaviour of generalised (abstract) structural features came from typologically 
oriented linguists such as Joseph Greenberg and Johanna Nichols. Historical typologists are 
not ‘particularistic’ (Greenberg 1978: 64). That is, they do not investigate the histories of 
individual items in a particular language, but rather the diachronic behaviour of linguistic 
features in a more abstract sense. Introducing a level of abstraction entails a movement from 
 nding facts to observing tendencies, and this, in turn, introduces a need for seeking statistical 
validation for different claims.
This chapter focuses on differences among structural features of phonology, morphology, 
and syntax with regard to their temporal stabilities. I will provide a critical review of work in 
this area and discuss possible explanations for major  ndings.
2 Review of literature on the stability of typological features
2.1 Greenberg (1978)
The  rst explicit formulation of a research programme of historical typology is that of 
Greenberg (1978). Here the question of whether the typologist’s notion of a synchronic 
type can somehow be meaningfully combined with the historical linguist’s notion of 
language change is answered in the af rmative. The combination of the two notions is 
schematically presented as a ‘state-process’ model where states are boxes and arrows are 
processes, as in (1).

---

<!-- page 231 -->

Diachronic stability and typology 
213
(1) 
A
B
According to the model in (1), there can be two states, A and B, and processes exist by 
which A changes into B or B into A. Greenberg excludes from the model cases with no 
arrows or just one arrow on the following grounds. Assuming that the states refer to aspects 
of the same phenomenon, such as having or not having tones, there should be processes 
linking the two states, i.e., at least one arrow. Otherwise we would have something 
corresponding to two independent lineages of human languages where, for instance, one 
always had tones and the other never did. But having just one arrow is also not a desirable 
possibility to include, because if changes can only go in one direction, one of the two states 
would eventually not be attested in any language. For instance, if languages can acquire 
tones but not lose them, then all languages would eventually become tonal. Although he is 
not explicit about this, what Greenberg does by restricting his state-process model to the 
bidirectional situation is to exclude the possibility that some feature of languages that 
previously existed has become irrevocably lost or lingers on in just a few languages, 
awaiting complete loss with no possibility of a reversion. This limits the space of 
possibilities that are in the purview of the historical typologist to just the ones that are 
attested in known human languages.
The basic model having two states can be expanded with more states connected in a 
network to account for languages passing through any number of transitory stages B1, B2, 
B3… as they change from A to C or the other way around. A further, and very important, 
extension is to combinations of states, where the tetrachoric tables in Greenberg’s earlier 
work on (statistical) implicational universals are dynamised. To illustrate this we can 
elaborate on Greenberg’s own example (1978: 77), but using data from Dryer (2005a, b) 
on the relationship between the order of adjective (Adj) and noun (N) and of numeral 
(Num) and noun. First, in (2), I show a tetrachoric table with numbers indicating how 
many families exhibit the four possible combinations of  xed word orders for Adj, N, 
and Num.
(2) 
Num-N
N-Num
Adj-N
52
8
N-Adj
32
49
The table in (2) shows the kind of frequency distribution typical of an implicational universal, 
with one cell showing a much lower frequency than the other three. In a static interpretation 
the skewed frequency distribution would be interpreted as a statistical implicational universal 
according to which N-Num order implies the N-Adj order. The synchronic typological 
description using a tetrachoric table can, however, be ‘dynamised’, combining it with the 
state-process model, as shown in (3).

---

<!-- page 232 -->

Søren Wichmann
214
(3)
Adj-N
Num-N
Adj-N
N-Num
N-Adj
Num-N
N-Adj
N-Num
The thin, dotted arrows indicate the relative improbability of transitions to and from the 
statistically dispreferred state where Adj-N and N-Num orders co-occur. Remember also that 
N-Adj and Num-N in the bottom left of (3) is somewhat less frequent than the remaining two 
word order co-occurrences, so that state can be interpreted as mostly transitional. The 
tetrachoric table of synchronic preferences has now been translated into a diachronic state-
process system embodying hypotheses about how languages may be expected to change: 
numeral or adjectival modi ers preferably sit on the same side of nouns, but the order can 
shift going through transitional states, where the N-Adj and Num-N combination is a more 
probable transitional state than Adj-N and N-Num.
Going somewhat beyond Greenberg’s own interpretations, we can stipulate that the state-
process model relates to the issue of stability in the following way. It predicts that, all else 
being equal, the more preferred states – Adj-N and Num-N and N-Adj and N-Num – are more 
stable than the transitory state of N-Adj and Num-N, which, in turn, is more stable than the 
less probable transitory state of Adj-N and N-Num. Moreover, there may be intermediate 
states where the orders of noun and adjective or noun and numeral are variable (Dryer 2005a, 
b also counts frequencies for variable orders). These would be more unstable than any of the 
states where the orders are  xed.2 It would seem, then, that as far as co-occurring phenomena 
are concerned, differential frequencies of co-occurrences can be translated into hypotheses 
about differential stabilities. But this is not a settled issue; rather, it is an open question 
emerging from Greenberg’s dynamised version of tetrachoric tables. The relationship 
between transition probabilities and the distribution of paired typological features is discussed 
in Dunn et al. (2011), Cysouw (2011), and Croft et al. (2011). Although some of the 
groundwork towards estimating the stability of certain combinations of typological features 
has been made, actual results are not yet available.
The preceding discussion has been concerned with co-occurring typological features 
whereas most work on the frequencies of typological features has been concerned with 
isolated features, for instance the order of nouns and adjectives on the one hand and nouns 
and numerals on the other, rather than the relationship between the two. When Greenberg 
opened up the research of diachronic stability of typological features he, in a sense, 
simultaneously took two steps into this realm of unknowns by considering combinations of 
changes before working out a way of measuring the stabilities of features individually. But 
he also presented some thoughts on the latter issue, considering whether stability and 
typological frequency are related and coming to the conclusion, based on general experience, 
that frequency and stability are independent phenomena, or, to soften the interpretation of his 
suggestion, that they do not always behave in tandem:

---

<!-- page 233 -->

Diachronic stability and typology 
215
The two factors of probability of origin from other states and stability can be considered 
separately. If a particular phenomenon can arise very frequently and is highly stable 
once it occurs, it should be universal or near universal. This could be true of front 
unrounded vowels. If it tends to come into existence often and in various ways, but its 
stability is low, it should be found fairly often but distributed relatively evenly among 
genetic linguistic stocks. A possible example is vowel nasalization. If a particular 
property rarely arises but is highly stable when it occurs, it should be fairly frequent on 
a global basis but be largely con ned to a few linguistic stocks, e.g. vowel harmony. If 
it occurs only rarely and is unstable when it occurs, it should be highly infrequent or 
non-existent and sporadic in its geographical and genetic distribution, e.g. velar 
implosives.
(Greenberg 1978: 76)
The interesting hypotheses here are that a feature may be found ‘fairly often’ although it is 
unstable and also that it may be ‘fairly infrequent’ but stable. Thus, a degree of relation 
between stability and frequency is assumed, but one does not always follow from the other.
2.2 Nichols (1992)
Once the notion of typological stability had been introduced, some scholars began to venture 
opinions on some features being more or less stable than others. For instance, Hawkins 
(1983: 93) claimed that the position of demonstratives, numerals, and adjectives with respect 
to the noun is less stable than genitive-noun and relative clause-noun order; and Croft (1996: 
206–207) claimed, among other things, that verb-initial word order is stable whereas de nite 
articles, possessive classi ers and O-initial word order are unstable. While these various 
claims happen to be supported by later, more methodologically explicit work,3 they were 
stated in the absence of an explicit method for measuring stability, and without supporting 
evidence.
With Nichols (1992, 1995, 2003) more explicit methods for measuring stability of 
typological features were introduced. In her 1992 book Nichols distinguishes genealogical 
(‘genetic’) and areal stability, where the latter is synonymous with diffusibility, since it 
describes the propensity for features to show uniform distributions in different world areas. 
The features looked at are broad characteristics: head vs. dependent marking, complexity, 
alignment, word order, voice, inclusive vs. exclusive distinction in pronominals, plurality 
neutralisation, inalienable possession, and noun classes. Each is encoded in a coarse-grained 
way with few values. These are investigated for eight stocks (in Nichols’ terminology, the 
deepest level of proven relatedness) and nine areas containing a total of 94 languages – a 
small but carefully selected sample. Both genealogical and areal stability are measured by 
de ning a ‘modal type’, i.e., the value which is numerically best represented in the stock or 
area, then tabulating the number of deviations, expressed as percentages of the languages in 
the given stock or area, and  nally taking the average. Some results of this exercise are easier 
to interpret than others. Head vs. dependent marking, complexity, alignment, and word order 
are treated comparatively, and alignment comes out as the most genealogically stable of the 
four, but the differences between the other three are too marginal for establishing a hierarchy 
as Nichols does. Similarly, word order is the most diffusible, whereas the other three features 
are roughly equally diffusible, although Nichols nevertheless proposes a hierarchy for all 
four features.4 For other features the results are as follows. For voice it is found that the 
directionality of valence-changing processes is somewhat genealogically stable. The

---

<!-- page 234 -->

Søren Wichmann
216
inclusive/exclusive opposition and noun classes are genealogically highly stable but also 
diffusible. Plurality neutralisation and inalienable possession are low or moderate for both 
kinds of stabilities.
A problematical assumption which is not made explicit is that genealogical and areal 
stabilities are consistent enough across areas that it is meaningful to interpret average 
measures as indicators of universal stabilities. Differently said, it cannot a priori be assumed 
that stabilities are universal. Is there support in Nichols’ data for this assumption? In general, 
the magnitudes of the standard deviations for the percentages of departures from the modal 
type should be a cause for concern. In precisely the two cases where Nichols seemed to have 
signi cant results, i.e., the genealogical stability of alignment and the areal stability of word 
orders, standard deviations are greater than the means, calling into question the meaningfulness 
of these averages. In short, the results of Nichols (1992) raise questions both about the 
signi cance of differences in stabilities and also the very notion of the universality of 
stabilities. Nevertheless, her work set new standards for the investigation of diachronic 
stability, and later work has established that this enterprise is a fruitful one.
2.3 Nichols (1995)
In her 1995 paper Nichols studies some of the same features as in her 1992 book, but adds 
others to the database. She again sets out to determine whether a feature is more genealogically 
or more areally consistent. This is done through  ve different types of metrics (1–5). These 
are suitable for different kinds of features, and sometimes more than one metric is suitable for 
a given feature. Brie y, (1) registers the range of variation for a feature whose values are 
expressible as numbers on a scale; (2) is an average of the number of different categorical 
values; (3) is applied to related languages only and counts how many among all possible pairs 
share the same feature value; (4) registers the percentage of languages that have the feature 
value which is best represented; (5) compares frequencies of the +present values of a binary 
feature across genealogical and areal groupings. Unlike in her 1992 book Nichols does not 
attempt to put numbers on different stabilities to establish hierarchies, but instead aims at 
deciding categorically whether features are (mainly) areal, (mainly) genealogical, or both. 
She  nds that word order and A-increasing derivation (causatives) are mainly areal, that 
tones and numeral classi ers are both genealogical and areal, while the remaining features 
surveyed are all mainly genealogical. Methodologically the paper is valuable for introducing 
different metrics, the adequacy of which are testable, and it emphasises a point which is very 
important although it is forgotten in some later approaches to the measurement of stabilities, 
namely that both areal perspective and universal preferences must be considered when 
approaching the problem of measuring genealogical stability. Her remarks on this issue 
deserve to be quoted at length. The importance of the areal perspective is illustrated through 
the inclusive/exclusive opposition:
For instance, inclusive/exclusive oppositions are nearly universal in Australia. The 
sample languages from the Gunwingguan family of Australia unanimously show an 
inclusive/exclusive opposition; is this then a stable genetic feature or simply a general 
continental feature? Inclusive/exclusive oppositions are almost entirely lacking in 
western Eurasia. The Indo-European languages in the sample all lack inclusive/exclusive 
oppositions; is this a stable genetic feature of Indo-European or a general western 
Eurasian feature?
(Nichols 1995: 349)5

---

<!-- page 235 -->

Diachronic stability and typology 
217
The importance of taking universal preferences into account is illustrated through valency-
changing mechanisms and word order:
Most of the world’s languages have causatives, and verb- nal word order is universally 
the most frequent, so high consistency in showing either of these two features is not a 
particularly strong demonstration of either genetic or areal stability.
(Nichols 1995: 349)
2.4 Parkvall (2008)
In this paper Parkvall tries to take into account both the universal and areal perspectives in 
his computations of stability indices. The units of analyses are the families of Ethnologue 
families (Gordon 2005)6 and a set of culture areas as de ned in the anthropological literature, 
and the linguistic data are from WALS (Haspelmath et al. 2005).
The computation proceeds in a series of steps. First a measure of diversity is applied, i.e., 
the Her ndahl-Hirschman index. This index comes from economics, where it is used to 
measure the degree to which a market deviates from a situation of monopoly. The equivalent 
of monopoly when it comes to the distribution of values of a typological variable over a 
group of related languages is maximal stability, and when it comes to the distribution of 
values over an areally de ned group, a monopoly would be equivalent to maximal diffusibility. 
In both cases a single feature value dominates. The idea behind using this index is that a 
business can be seen as equivalent to a typological feature value and a market share can be 
seen as equivalent to the number of languages that have the given value. Expressed 
mathematically, the diversity, DG, of a given group, G, is 1 – ¬(Pn²), where Pn is the fraction 
of the total group G that has the n-th feature-value. As an example of how to apply this 
formula, Parkvall offers the distribution of the values of WALS feature #1, ‘Consonant 
inventories’ (Maddieson 2005a) within Uralic. Forty-four per cent of the languages have 
feature value 2, another 44 per cent have feature value 4, and the remaining 11 per cent have 
feature value 5. So DURALIC = 1 – (0.11² + 0.44² + 0.44²) = 0.60. This diversity measure can 
take values between 0 and 1, where low values correspond to high stability.
In the following steps of the computation DG is  rst converted from a measure of diversity 
into one of homogeneity, HG, by taking the inverse, i.e., by de ning homogeneity as HG = 
1/DG.7 Homogeneity is computed for each family and subsequently averaged across all 
families, to arrive at HFAM. Parkvall then calculates a measure of ‘genealogical cohesiveness’, 
CFAM, by dividing HFAM by HWORLD, where the latter is the homogeneity across all languages 
in the sample, regardless of genealogical af liation. For instance, the number of languages 
having each of the  ve different values of WALS feature #1 are: 91 (value 1), 121 (v. 2), 181 
(v. 3), 116 (v. 4), 53 (v. 5). So HWORLD = 1.295 in this case.8 The normalisation (division) by 
HWORLD is intended to make features with different numbers of values directly comparable. 
All else being equal a greater number of values will tend to lower HFAM. The division by 
HWORLD provides a balance against this effect.
In the  nal steps of the computation Parkvall seeks to take into account the areal 
perspective. This is done by  rst  nding the areal cohesiveness, CARE. This is computed in the 
exact same way as genealogical cohesiveness, CFAM, except that the units now used are areas 
rather than families. Stability is de ned as the proportion CFAM/CARE.
Up to the point where the division CFAM/CARE is introduced, the procedure is unproblematic. 
But the results lose transparency during the last step, which makes the problematical 
assumption that both genealogical and areal cohesiveness are inherent in typological features,

---

<!-- page 236 -->

Søren Wichmann
218
i.e., that a given feature more-or-less will have one and the same behaviour across all 
languages with regard to both areal and genealogical cohesiveness. However, as shown in 
Wichmann and Holman (2009) this is generally true for genealogical cohesiveness but not 
areal cohesiveness. So dividing CFAM by CARE is bound to produce a noisy measure, and CFAM 
alone would have been more adequate.
2.5 Wichmann and Holman (2009)
Although the computation of feature stabilities of Wichmann and Holman (2009) is similar 
in some respects to those of Parkvall (2008), they differ in two important methodological 
respects from his work as well as from other work in this area. First, they test the performance 
of several methods for measuring feature stabilities (henceforth stability metrics) through 
simulations. By introducing preset stabilities into a simulation model, it can be tested to what 
extent a metric correctly picks up these preset differences in stability by running simulations. 
Second, they measure both stability and diffusibility (without confounding the two) and 
check whether these are meaningful notions in the  rst place, i.e., whether stability and 
diffusibility are, indeed, inherent in typological features. This is done by correlating results 
across the relatively independent units of the old vs. the new world (excluding the 
geographically intermediate families Eskimo-Aleut, Altaic, Ainu, Chukotko-Kamchatkan, 
Japanese, Korean, Nivkh, Yeniseian, and Yukaghir). The unambiguous results of this exercise 
are that stability ranks are highly correlated across the two areas whereas ranks for diffusibility 
are not. Thus the same features are stable or unstable to roughly the same degree in different 
areas, but diffusibility is area-speci c and cannot be predicted.
Among the different metrics tested one (A) is that of Wichmann and Kamholz (2008) (not 
described in the present chapter); another (B) is the method of counting the fraction of 
languages having the modal (best represented) value which was applied in Nichols (1992, 
1995); and the third metric (C) is introduced for the  rst time in Wichmann and Holman 
(2009). It is found that Metric C performed better than Metric A, which performed better than 
Metric B. In the following I brie y describe Metric C.
Metric C works on the assumption that if one given feature more often tends to have the 
same value for languages that are related than does another given feature, then the  rst of the 
two may be considered to be more stable; additionally it takes into account the tendency for 
traits to be similar among languages that are not related. This is implemented in the following 
way. Within each group of languages within a genus (closely related languages, as de ned by 
Dryer 2005c)  nd, for all pairs of languages for which the feature is attested in both 
languages,9 the proportion of pairs for which the feature has the same value. This proportion 
is averaged across all genera, with each genus weighted by the square root of the number of 
language pairs for which the feature is attested, to obtain R. Now subtract the average 
proportion of pairs for which the feature has the same value across unrelated languages 
(languages belong to different families according to Dryer 2005c); this proportion is called 
U. Finally the stability index S is obtained by dividing R – U by 1 – U (for more detail and 
justi cation see Wichmann and Holman 2009: 12–13).
Results of this procedure and their interpretation are discussed in section 3 below.
2.6 Dediu (2011)
Largely neglecting previous work in the area, Dediu devises his own method for measuring 
stability, which is to estimate numbers and rates of change in different typological features

---

<!-- page 237 -->

Diachronic stability and typology 
219
using existing language classi cations and applying Bayesian methods to infer ancestral 
states of the different features. Results are presented for combinations of different 
classi cations (Dryer 2005c; Lewis 2009), different encodings (binary vs. non-binary), and 
different software, but across the various tests the same thinking applies, namely that an 
exclusively phylogenetic approach will give meaningful results.
The fundamental problem arising from the neglect of the areal and/or universal perspectives 
– perspectives which all previous literature had tried to consider – is illustrated in the results 
obtained for WALS features #18, ‘Absence of common consonants’ (Maddieson 2005b) and 
#11, ‘Front rounded vowels’ (Maddieson 2005c), which come out as respectively the most 
and the next-most stable features. In the WALS sample, only 11.3 per cent of languages lack 
one or more of the common consonants, and only 15.2 per cent of genera, and 19.3 per cent 
of families include at least one such language lacking one or more of the common consonants. 
On the other hand, in one part of the world, namely Sahul (especially Australia), the feature 
value ‘no fricatives’ is widespread. So this feature is highly contingent upon universal and 
areal factors. If it comes out as the most stable of all features in Dediu’s results it would be 
due primarily to all those families where the inferred ancestral state of having all common 
consonants is retained throughout the phylogenetic evolution. The explanation for why front 
rounded vowels is next on the list ranked for stabilities is very similar. Only a small minority 
of languages (6.6 per cent), genera (8.2 per cent), and families (14.2 per cent) in the WALS 
sample have front rounded vowels, so there is a universal preference for their absence. 
Moreover, the presence of the feature is highly concentrated in Eurasia. Thus, again we 
encounter a feature whose distribution is highly contingent upon universal and areal factors, 
and again its apparent stability is mainly due to all those families where the universally 
preferred value (here: absence of front rounded vowels) is retained. Dediu’s results, then, cast 
serious doubts on the adequacy of a purely phylogenetically-based measure of stability.
2.7 Correlating results across different methods
In this  nal section I present evidence to the effect that the methods discussed are not only 
different, but actually lead to different results. The methods that are directly comparable are 
those of Parkvall (2008), Wichmann and Holman (2009), and Dediu (2011). The last of these 
does not present results for all WALS features, but otherwise the three studies refer to the 
same dataset, so they can be directly compared. Since the numerical values of the three 
different sets of stability indices lie in different ranges I have converted them to ranks, where 
the most stable feature on a given measure receives rank #1, the next-most rank #2, and so 
on. These rank numbers can then be correlated using Pearson’s r (which is actually equivalent 
to using Spearman’s  on the original numbers). Figure 8.1 shows scatterplots of the three 
paired comparisons, and values of Pearson’s r are given in the caption.10 It is not straightforward 
to provide p-values for the signi cance of the correlations because of non-independence of 
the features, but they would also not be highly relevant since we are interested in differences 
between methods and not so much in whether or not they come up with signi cantly similar 
results. What matters is that there are differences in results, and the simple reason must be 
that the methods are not equally adequate.11
Since the metric of Wichmann and Holman has been found to be superior to some other 
metrics through simulations and has been cross-validated through a correlation of results for 
different world areas, there is reason to have most con dence in this metric. When discussing 
the interpretation of results of measuring the different stabilities of typological features I will 
therefore make exclusive reference to their results.

---

<!-- page 238 -->

Left: Dediu vs. Parkvall (r = 0.48); mid: Wichmann and Holman vs. Parkvall (r = 0.35); right: Wichmann and 
Holman vs. Dediu (r = 0.40).
3 Summary of the results of Wichmann and Holman (2009)
As summarised in Wichmann and Holman (2009: 51–52), a striking overall result is that 
features that have a pragmatic orientation, relating to discourse organisation, the illocutionary 
level of clausal interpretation, and extra-linguistic context, tend to be unstable, whereas basic 
morphosyntactic features tend to be stable. The former (unstable) features include de nite 
and inde nite articles, politeness distinctions, imperative-hortative systems, epistemic and 
evidentiality distinction, negation, and distance contrasts in demonstratives. The latter 
(stable) features include gender, case, and af x or constituent ordering.
Another contribution to the general explanation for differential stabilities is that there is a 
weak tendency for some features that are more inter-correlated (implicationally related) than 
others to also be more stable. Following Holman (2008), Wichmann and Holman (2009: 56) 
used R´, the adjusted Rand index (Hubert and Arabie 1985), to calculate interrelatedness 
among features, observing that the correlation between the average R´ for a feature with all 
other features and stability indices is between 0.27 and 0.44, depending on whether the full 
WALS sample of languages or more restricted sample of only the better attested ones is used. 
This means that between 0.27² and 0.44² or 7–19 per cent of the variance in stability is 
accounted for by interrelatedness among features. This explanation, then, only tells a small 
part about why some features are more stable than others.
Wichmann and Holman (2009: 25–51) provided a table with stabilities for individual 
feature values in addition to the table with stabilities for whole features. The vast majority of 
features have one or more values whose stability is one standard deviation from the mean 
stability of values and a few have one value whose stability is two standard deviations from 
the mean, but the distributions tend to be relatively even. Nevertheless, it is recommended to 
refer to the indices for individual features in addition to those for whole features. For instance, 
the feature ‘Locus of marking: whole-language typology’ (Nichols and Bickel 2005) is stable 
(i.e., within the second-highest quartile for whole feature indices), but the value ‘Consistently 
dependent-marking’ is very unstable (i.e., within the lowest quartile for individual feature 
value indices); Number of Cases (Iggesen 2005) is stable but the value ‘2 case categories’ is 
very unstable; ‘Distributive numerals’ (Gil 2005) is stable but the value ‘Marked by pre x’ 
is very unstable; the various word order features, e.g., ‘Order of subject, object, and verb’ 
(Dryer 2005d) tend to be very stable (top quartile), but values to do with lack of a dominant

Diachronic stability and typology 
221
order tend to be unstable (second-lowest quartile); ‘Verbal person marking’ (Siewierska 
2005) is very unstable, but ‘Person marking of only the A argument’ is stable; ‘Comparative 
constructions’ (Stassen 2005) is stable, but ‘Conjoined comparative’ is very unstable; 
‘Relativisation on obliques’ (Comrie and Kuteva 2005) is stable but values take a wide range 
of indices from very stable (‘Relative pronoun strategy’) to very unstable (‘Not possible’).
Summarising the results of Wichmann and Holman (2009), it is shown there for the  rst 
time that differential stability is indeed inherent in abstract typological features, whereas 
differential diffusibility is not (i.e., the latter is not a meaningful concept). Basic 
morphosyntactic features tend to be more stable than pragmatically oriented features, and a 
feature can be stable as a whole even though some values are not stable (or vice versa), as 
exempli ed in the previous paragraph. Finally (something not mentioned up to this point), 
these authors also show that absolute rates of change in structural features are roughly similar 
to those of basic vocabulary. Based on the assumption that WALS genera are on average 
4,000 years old they infer an average retention rate of 81 per cent per millennium, which is 
equal to the rate estimated by Lees (1953) for the 215 lexical concepts in the list of Swadesh 
(1950). This observation is of particular relevance for the following and  nal section where I 
comment on why it is useful to know something about differential feature stabilities.
4 What’s important about the differential stability of structural 
features?
One motivation which has driven research on the differential stability of structural features 
of languages is the hope that the use of such features for historical linguistic research might 
increase the depth of time within which we can discern genealogical relations among 
languages (Nichols 1992; Dunn et al. 2005, 2008; Wichmann and Saunders 2007), but it is 
now becoming clear that structural features do not preserve more ancient phylogenetic signals 
than does the basic vocabulary; and if this was not clear earlier it is certainly clear now, that 
they are more prone to diffusion (Donohue et al. 2008; Wichmann and Holman 2009; 
Greenhill et al. 2010; Donohue et al. 2011). Nevertheless, having more data at hand with 
rates of change similar to basic vocabulary is good news for historical linguistics, and the fact 
that such data are sensitive to lateral diffusion is not necessarily bad news either, because it 
supplies us with new ways of detecting ancient contact events. In any and all such exercises 
involving the study of linguistic prehistory through the use of typological features it is of 
course a great advantage to know that some are more stable than others. Whether for the 
purpose of detecting contact or genealogical relatedness, more stable features will take us 
further back in time than less stable ones.
More importantly, perhaps, when explaining language change it matters to know which 
features are more prone to change than others. Presumably language change is only to some 
extent random, and to a larger extent is adaptive. There would, however, seem to be several 
ways in which languages can adapt: they can adapt their pragmatic apparatus to needs for 
controlling discourse production, preferred communicative strategies, and the social or even 
the geographical environment. The results discussed above indicate that such adaptation can 
be relatively rapid. Or languages can adapt to alternative universal preferences for right- or 
left-branching constituent ordering strategies or other overall ways of streamlining their 
grammatical apparatus yet to be understood. Such adaptation appears to be slow, and one 
reason is probably that it involves coupled changes of interrelated features. The dynamics of 
coupled changes is still not well understood, however (but see Dunn et al. 2011; Cysouw 
2011; Croft et al. 2011 for recent research in this area). Finally, languages may adapt their

---

<!-- page 240 -->

Søren Wichmann
222
structures to those of other languages – something which is known to take place all the time. 
It does not seem possible to make predictions about the type of abstract structural features 
which is more prone to undergo such adaptation. For all we know any feature can change as 
the result of language contact at any speed.
There are certainly more explanations to seek for the differential stabilities of structural 
features than the ones provided above. Future work should be directed at processing and 
language acquisition as some of the possible arenas for the further investigation of differential 
feature stabilities.
Notes
 1 This chapter has bene ted from comments by Bernard Comrie, Claire Bowern, and Eric W. 
Holman. All are gratefully acknowledged.
 2 In fact, Wichmann and Holman (2009), discussed below, show that the stability of orders of nouns 
and adjectives as well as orders of nouns and numerals are higher when orders are  xed than when 
they are variable.
 3 According to the results of Wichmann and Holman (2009: Table 3) Hawkins’ claims are supported 
inasmuch as the average of the stability indices for the supposedly less stable features is lower than 
the average for the supposedly more stable features, but the individual differences are marginal. 
Croft’s claims are more strongly supported.
 4 When a stock or area does not have a modal type it is ignored in the statistics even though it is 
recognised (Nichols 1992: 172) that not having a modal type is evidence of instability. If the 
absence of a modal type is included in the statistics by counting n/2 departures for a stock or area 
with n representatives, alignment still remains the genealogically most stable feature and word 
order the most diffusible, but further down the hierarchy features trade places.
 5 For Nichols the focus is exclusively on the mere presence of a functional category of clusivity, not 
on whether it is stable or not with regard to the way in which it is expressed. The latter point of 
view might lead to other conclusions since, as I am informed by Claire Bowern (p.c., 2013), in 
Pama-Nyungan alone clusivity is expressed in several different ways, which indicates that this 
category has undergone changes in its formal expression.
 6 Analyses are carried out both with and without families judged to be highly controversial.
 7 It would have been more straightforward to use the de nition HG = 1 – DG = ¬(Pn²), whereby HG 
would lie within the range from 0 to 1, but using one or the other de nition has no consequence 
for the results.
 8 The  lled-in formula for HWORLD is 1/(1 – ((91/562)² + (121/562)² + (181/562)² + (116/562)² + 
(53/562)²).
 9 A feature can be attested for both languages without being present in both languages because 
WALS feature values allow for encoding absences. For instance, feature #7 ‘Glottalised 
Consonants’ has as one of its possible values the absence of glottalised consonants.
10 Parkvall gives results using both all WALS families as well as results using only the more 
commonly accepted families. Figure 8.1 reports the correlations involving the results for all 
families. For the commonly accepted families the correlations are: Parkvall vs. Dediu: r = 0.24; 
Parkvall vs. Wichmann and Holman: r = 0.46. Dediu gives results both for binarily recoded 
features, polymorphic features (features as originally encoded in WALS), and a combination of 
the two. The correlations in Figure 8.1 are for polymorphic features, which are also used by 
Parkvall and Wichmann and Holman.
11 Dediu and Cysouw (2013), a paper which appeared during the  nal stage of preparing the present 
chapter, also presents comparisons of results of the methods discussed here, as well as some 
additional method comparisons, and the authors put emphasis on similarities in results across 
methods. In contrast, I  nd similarities of minor interest since, after all, the different methods were 
designed to measure similar properties. Moreover, in most cases the methods are applied to the 
same data.

München: LINCOM Europa.
Wichmann, Søren and David Kamholz. 2008. A stability metric for typological features. STUF – 
Language Typology and Universals 61: 251–262.
Wichmann, Søren and Arpiar Saunders. 2007. How to use typological databases in historical linguistic 
research. Diachronica 24: 373–404.

---

<!-- page 243 -->

Part III
Language change

---

<!-- page 244 -->

This page intentionally left blank

---

<!-- page 245 -->

227
9
Sound change
Andrew Garrett
[T]he causes of sound change are unknown.
Leonard Bloom eld, Language (1933: 385)
1 Introduction1
If new words and lexical usages are the most recognisable aspect of language change, the 
emergence of novel pronunciation patterns and the existence of distinct accents of the same 
language are also familiar to casual observers. Despite its ubiquity, there is no generally 
accepted de nition of sound change. It certainly includes phonologisation, whereby an 
automatic phonetic property evolves into a language-speci c phonological one (Jakobson 
1931; Hyman 1977; Hyman 2012). For some analysts, the phenomenon of sound change also 
encompasses phonological change with other origins, such as dialect contact, paradigm 
regularisation, or structural simpli cation. De ning the object of study is important when 
different de nitions lead to different views of its mechanisms and properties. Nonetheless, in 
what follows I take a pragmatic approach, highlighting some phenomena, questions, and 
further literature that seem especially interesting at present, and setting others aside.
Students entering the  eld in 2013 will  nd a different landscape from that of a generation 
ago. Inquiry into the causes and pathways of sound change has been transformed by the work 
of  ve scholars in particular. Three of them, Paul Kiparsky (1988, 1995), William Labov 
(e.g. 1981, 1994, 2001, 2010), and Björn Lindblom (Lindblom 1990; Lindblom et al. 1995), 
have proposed in uential structural analyses of changes within their sound systems. Two 
others, Juliette Blevins (e.g. Blevins and Garrett 1998, 2004; Blevins 2004, 2006a) and John 
Ohala (e.g. 1974, 1981, 1983, 1993), have also emphasised the role of articulatory detail and 
perceptual processes in determining the course of sound changes. The last decade has seen 
valuable synoptic research, including books (Blevins 2004; Solé and Recasens 2012; Yu 
2012b), review articles (Bermúdez-Otero 2007; Hansson 2008; Hualde 2011; Garrett and 
Johnson 2012), and dossiers of sound changes (Blevins 2004, 2008a; Kümmel 2007; still 
essential are Grammont 1939; Hock 1991). All this work offers a  ne array of starting points 
for new research projects.
Despite such work and the new questions that come with it, the theories that de ned the 
 eld still guide many debates. The classical theory of sound change, due to the Neogrammarians 
(Osthoff and Brugman 1878; Paul 1880; Sievers 1901) and adopted in American structuralist 
thinking (Bloom eld 1933), had two main tenets. The  rst and more celebrated, the so-called 
‘Neogrammarian hypothesis’ was that sound changes are exceptionless, gradual, and

---

<!-- page 246 -->

Andrew Garrett
228
imperceptible while underway. The second was that changes that are not grounded in 
articulatory processes have an entirely different nature; dissimilation was the parade example. 
The Neogrammarians also contended that gradual articulatory change is caused by random 
variations in exemplar memory. Issues raised by these claims are still of interest. For example, 
Ohala’s distinction between perceptual ‘hypocorrection’ and ‘hypercorrection’ is one 
proposed solution to the dissimilation problem, while Labov’s (1981, 1994) study of lexically 
irregular sound change is a well-known approach to the Neogrammarian hypothesis. And 
some current attempts to model sound change (e.g. Pierrehumbert 2001; Wedel 2006; Garrett 
and Johnson 2012; Kirby 2012) use exemplar models of phonetic knowledge that formalise 
ideas of Paul (1880) or Kruszewski (1887: 145–146), who wrote that “each articulation is 
governed by the unconscious memory of similar articulations already produced earlier.”
Equally in uential is the foundational work on language change from a socially 
contextualised perspective. Weinreich et al. (1968) frame the  eld through several key 
problems. The three problems listed in (1) also serve to organise this chapter.
(1) Three problems raised by Weinreich et al. (1968).
a 
Constraints: What changes are possible and what are their preconditions?
b 
Embedding: How is a change embedded in its linguistic and social system?
c 
Actuation: Why does a possible change take place in one language, but not in 
another language with similar conditions?
After a review in section 2 of prototypical sound changes and other changes that should 
perhaps not be treated as sound change, this chapter is organised as follows. The constraints 
problem and the actual mechanisms of sound change are discussed in section 3, the embedding 
problem in section 4, and the actuation problem in section 5. A brief conclusion follows in 
section 6.
2 What sound change is and isn’t
2.1 Phonologisation
Prototypical sound changes involve phonologisation, in which a direct link can be found 
between a change and some automatic phonetic process: a precursor. In the examples that 
follow, it should go without saying that calling changes phonologisation does not explain 
how they happen.
A typical case is Old English umlaut, illustrated for two vowel qualities in (2).
(2) Old English umlaut in consonant-stem nouns (Brunner 1965).
Singular
Plural
Umlaut
lus ‘louse’
*lusi
>
lys ‘lice’
mus ‘mouse’
*musi
>
mys ‘mice’
fot ‘foot’
*foti
>
*føt > fet ‘feet’
gos ‘goose’
*gosi
>
*gøs > ges ‘geese’

---

<!-- page 247 -->

Sound change
229
Certain plurals originally had a nominative plural ending *-i, which disappeared after 
causing vowel fronting in a preceding syllable. Fronting was not restricted to plural nouns (it 
caused the alternation in modern old, elder); it is also notable that the fronted mid vowel was 
unrounded (e.g. fet ‘feet’) earlier than the high vowel (e.g. lys ‘lice’). Umlaut as seen in this 
example is cross-linguistically common. It seems obvious that umlaut is related to the well-
established coarticulatory pattern whereby, in V1CV2 sequences, a back V1 is automatically 
somewhat fronted by a front V2 (Öhman 1966). Such a coarticulatory process was 
phonologised as umlaut in Old English.
Tonogenesis is a second well-documented sound change pattern with a clear phonetic 
precursor. An example is given in (3) from the Austroasiatic language Khmu (a.k.a. Kammu; 
cf. Svantesson 1983).
(3) Tonogenesis in a western dialect of Khmu (Suwilai 2003; Kingston 2011).
Eastern Khmu
Western Khmu

‘rice wine’
puc

‘to take off clothes’
gla

‘stone’
kla
klâ
‘eagle’
a
cà
‘to weigh’
ca
câ
‘astringent’
ra
rà
‘ ower’
hra
râ
‘tooth’
In the eastern dialect, an original voicing contrast is preserved and surface pitch differences 
are allophonic. In one of the western dialects, the voicing contrast has been replaced by a 
tonal contrast. Low tone is found on syllables with a former voiced onset, a higher tone on 
syllables with a former voiceless onset. It is well known that lowered pitch is an automatic 
phonetic side effect of obstruent voicing (Hombert et al. 1979; Kingston 2011); tonogenesis 
in cases like (3) is obviously a phonologisation of this effect.
A third and less widely-known sound change pattern is the emergence of obstruents after 
high vowels, illustrated in (4) from the Tangkhulic (Tibeto-Burman) language Huishu.
(4) Epenthetic k after high vowels in Huishu (Mortensen 2012).

---

<!-- page 248 -->

Andrew Garrett
230
Here an epenthetic k arose after high vowels, but not (as the last example shows) after 
non-high vowels. Similar changes are documented in several unrelated languages. Mortensen 
(2012: 443) suggests that their precursor is a realisation of “the  nal portions of high vowels 
… as devoiced or fricative off-glides,” as in French. This phonetic pattern, driven by 
aerodynamic constraints, is also responsible for phonological high vowel devoicing in many 
languages.
2.2 Analogical changes in phonological form
In typical cases of phonologisation like (2–4), a change can be described in purely phonological 
or phonetic terms, with no additional lexical or morphological conditions; within its environment 
the change is regular. But there are other pronunciation changes whose causes do not involve 
phonetic precursors. One class of examples includes contamination, as in (5). In such cases 
only one or a few lexical items are affected, rather than all words with a certain phonological 
context, and the change re ects the in uence of related words. For these reasons, analysts 
usually treat contamination not as ‘sound change’ but as ‘analogical’ or lexical change.
(5) Two types of contamination:
a 
Sequence contamination (Osthoff 1878)
 
Example: Latin Octber  dialectal Romance *Octember (Old French Octembre), 
under the in uence of September, November
b 
Lexical polarisation (Malkiel 1951)
 
Example: Romance *sinistru ‘left’  *sinestru (Old Spanish siniestro), under 
the in uence of *destru ‘right’ (diestro)
There are other cases with similar properties in which the classi cation is less clear. An 
example is what Moulton (1960, 1967) called ‘morphophonemic analogy’. In this type of 
change, a phonological alternation that expresses some morphological relationship is 
generalised to create a new segment. Yet the context in which the new segment arises is 
de ned morphologically, not (as in ordinary sound change) in purely phonological terms. 
This is shown by the Swiss German dialect data in (6).
(6) Morphophonemic analogy in Swiss German dialects (Enderlin 1911; Wanner 1941; 
Moulton 1960):
a  
Morphological vowel fronting: /o/  /ø/
Plurals: /bog	/ ‘bow’  /bøg	/, /of	/ ‘oven’  /øf	/
b  
Sound change: /o/ > // before coronal obstruents and /r/
i  
Examples: /hrn/ ‘horn’, /rss/ ‘steed’, /xrt/ ‘toad’
ii  No change: /gold/ ‘gold’, /ops/ ‘fruit’, /xoxx	/ ‘to cook’
c  
Morphological vowel fronting and raising in Schaffhausen
Plural: /bd	/ ‘ oor, ground’  /bød	/
Diminutive: /lxx	/ ‘hole’  /løxxli/
d  
Regularised morphological vowel fronting in Kesswil
Plural: /br	r/ ‘borer’  /bœr	r/
Diminutives: /xrt/ ‘toad’  /xrœtl®/, /lss/ ‘castle’  /lœssl®/
e  
Morphologically isolated forms in Kesswil: /frø/ ‘frog’, /gøtti/ ‘godfather’

---

<!-- page 249 -->

Sound change
231
The morphologically conditioned vowel fronting process in (6a) is dialectally widespread, and 
common in categories such as plurals and diminutives; it descends from a much earlier umlaut 
sound change. The vowel lowering sound change in (6b) is found in multiple dialects and may be 
assumed to have a phonetic basis. This sound change gave rise to a phonological asymmetry in 
the Schaffhausen dialect, as shown in (6c): plurals and diminutives of words with lowered // 
have a higher vowel /ø/. In the Kesswil dialect, this asymmetry was regularised as shown in (6d): 
nouns with // form derivatives with the corresponding front vowel /œ/, which was not previously 
part of the phonological system. But crucially, the replacement of earlier */xrøtl®/ by /xrœtl®/ did 
not involve a general lowering of /ø/ before coronal obstruents. This is shown by the 
morphologically isolated forms in (6e); only derived forms underwent the change (Enderlin 
1911: 34). If morphologically isolated forms as in (6e) happened to be lacking, there would be no 
way to tell that the morphophonemic analogy was not a general lowering sound change.
Morphophonemic analogy is relevant because its analysis affects our notions of sound 
change. If it is sound change, then sound changes can be morphologically conditioned. But 
this kind of change involves the generalisation of a morphological pattern, in this case vowel 
fronting, so it resembles contamination as in (5) and other strictly ‘analogical’ changes (e.g. 
the extension of a strong verb pattern to English dig, whose earlier past-tense form digged 
was replaced by dug in the fourteenth century). A key question is whether there is a purely 
phonetic precursor stage, for example in this case a stage where */xrøtl®/ had a partly lowered 
vowel, or whether morphophonemic analogy always involves a categorical replacement.
Another case involving generalisation of a morphological or lexical pattern is the 
regularisation of stress in English nouns. Disyllabic nouns more often have initial stress than 
 nal stress, for example, a pattern to which speakers are known to be sensitive. French 
disyllabic nouns are often borrowed into English with  nal stress, as in fatígue, grenáde, or 
reviéw, but sooner or later their stress tends to be regularised. Some results are shown in (7), 
with completed regularisation cases in (7a) and currently variable words in (7b).
(7) English stress regularisation in borrowed disyllabic nouns (Svensson 2004).
a 
Historical regularisation: áspect, cástle, mánner, nátion, pálace
b 
Ongoing regularisation (stress varying by dialect or within dialects): chauffeur, 
debris, garage, massage, paté
The phenomenon is relevant here because the change is lexically irregular, and would 
contradict the claim that sound changes are regular if it is counted as a sound change. But 
changes of this type appear to be grounded not in a phonetic pattern but in a morphologically 
de ned regularity about English nouns. If the key Neogrammarian insight was to distinguish 
between (phonetically driven) sound changes and (morphologically driven) analogical 
changes, then changes like those in (7) may not bear on the properties of the former. Yet the 
patterns seen in stress levelling (Phillips 2006), like those of morphophonemic analogy 
(Kiparsky 1968; Robinson 1976), play a notable role in modern discussions of sound change.
The changes in (6d) and (7) are sensitive to aspects of phonological form and may even 
approach regularity, but they also differ signi cantly from ordinary sound changes. Certain 
contact-induced changes comprise another category whose origins may differ from 
phonologisation. For example, postvocalic voiceless stops are preaspirated in some Hebridean 
English varieties in Scotland (Shuken 1984). This re ects transfer from Scots Gaelic, which 
has a phonemic contrast between unaspirated and aspirated stops. The latter are preaspirated 
in postvocalic positions; cf. boc /phk/ ‘male goat’ vs. bog /pok/ ‘soft’ (Nance and Stuart-

---

<!-- page 250 -->

Andrew Garrett
232
Smith 2013). The Scots Gaelic pattern in turn is attributed to Scandinavian contact (Hansson 
2001; Helgason 2002). Preaspiration does arise via phonologisation (Clayton 2010), but the 
mechanism of change in Hebridean English and Scots Gaelic may have been quite different 
if we assume, say, that Gaelic-dominant speakers transferred allophonic patterns from Gaelic 
into English (cf. Lucas, this volume). In such a scenario there is no need to suppose that the 
phonetic precursor of preaspiration existed in local English dialects prior to the transfer.
In retrospect, if we do not know much about the possible contact languages, it may be 
dif cult to decide whether a regular change resulted from phonologisation of a universal 
phonetic pattern or contact-induced transfer of a phonological pattern. For example, the 
Celtic, Germanic, and Italic branches of Indo-European innovated initial stress. This might 
have originated internally, or it might be due to transfer from indigenous languages of western 
and central Europe; we do not know.
In sum, words change phonological form in ways that are variously similar and dissimilar 
to prototypical cases of phonologisation. Some pathways of phonological change (e.g. 
morphophonemic analogy) mimic phonologisation, while others (e.g. English stress 
regularisation) are unlike prototypical sound changes. Different researchers may  nd it useful 
to take a narrower or broader approach to the subject matter. Here, for clarity of focus, 
phonologisation is taken as the prototype.
3 Constraints on sound change
If prototypical sound change is phonologisation, it would ideally follow that the typologies 
of sound change and possible phonetic precursors correspond perfectly. This hypothesis 
serves as a restatement of the Neogrammarian position. Problems are then posed by any 
mismatch between sound change and phonetic patterns. Studying such mismatches is a 
strategy for addressing the constraints problem: what are the preconditions for sound change? 
The following overview describes two basic mismatch patterns (section 3.1) and then 
examines enhancement (section 3.2) and ‘minor’ sound change (section 3.3).
3.1 Phonetic patterns and sound change: basic mismatches
One class of mismatches consists of regular phonetic patterns that never lead to sound change. 
For example, in Seoul Korean, a contrast between lenis and aspirated stops (e.g. /p/ vs. /ph/) 
was formerly primarily cued by voice onset time; more recently, f0 expresses the same 
contrast (Silva 2006; Kang and Guion 2008). This is a classic case of phonologisation akin to 
that in (3). Still, as Kirby (2012: 229) writes, “in addition to VOT and f0, spectral tilt and the 
amplitude of the release burst are relevant perceptual cues to the initial onset contrast,” but 
only f0 is being phonologised. Tonogenesis is a common change, but not ‘tilting’ or ‘burst 
amplitude enhancement’. From a broader point of view, this is the question of why only some 
phonetic patterns seem to play a role in phonology.
A second class of mismatches consists of sound changes that do not correspond to phonetic 
precursors. A notable subclass involves those that allegedly lack any phonetic motivation. 
For example, Blust (2005) presents a dossier of ‘bizarre sound changes’ like the development 
of a Proto-Manus prenasalised trill *dr to Drehet kh (e.g. *dra > kha ‘blood’); the change of 
initial *dw to Armenian erk is a similar Indo-European crux (Hock 1991: 583–584; De Lisi 
2013). One obvious dif culty with this line of reasoning is that a series of sound changes, 
each phonetically grounded, can yield an outcome or synchronic pattern that corresponds to 
no phonetic process; on Blust’s dossier see Goddard (2007) and Blevins (2007, 2008b). The

---

<!-- page 251 -->

Sound change
233
error in such cases is to assume that the telescoped result of multiple changes arose through 
a single sound change; admittedly, it may not be clear when telescoping is plausible rather 
than a hypothesis of convenience.
A convincing case of telescoping is described by Stausland Johnsen (2012) in Standard 
East Norwegian, which has a synchronic pattern whereby /t d n s/ become apical postalveolar 
[¯ ° ³ µ] when preceded by an apical alveolar /¶/, which deletes. Examples are shown in (8a), 
together with examples in (8b) of a second process, triggered by /·/; the examples in (8c) are 
for comparison.
(8) Retro exion in Standard East Norwegian (Stausland Johnsen 2012).
a 
/t¸:¶/ ‘take’ (PRES)

[stæ:°in]
