<!-- page 304 -->

286
12
Morphological reconstruction
Harold Koch
1 Introduction and overview
Morphological reconstruction cannot be described in terms of an exact set of procedures 
comparable to the Comparative Method for phonological reconstruction; this follows from 
the fact that morphological change is not ‘regular’ in the sense that phonological change is. 
This does not mean, however, that morphological reconstruction is not possible. To practise 
such reconstruction successfully, however, requires not only (as is to be expected) a 
knowledge of the phonological changes that have applied in the given languages but also a 
general understanding of the kinds of morphological changes that typically occur. That is 
why the following discussion will be in terms of the types of change that are involved (cf. 
Koch 1996, 2011 for typologies of morphological change).
Morphological reconstruction can be viewed as an analytical process that ‘undoes’ the 
processes of change and posits earlier morphological structures. Changes that affect the 
morphological structure of words include not only the much discussed cases of ‘analogical’ 
change within and between in ectional paradigms (discussed in section 8 and section 9), but 
also alterations to the order of bound morphemes (section 5) and to their functions (section 
6). Functional change of morphemes can be viewed as a kind of semantic change. The 
morphological complexity of words can be eliminated, requiring reconstruction for its 
recovery from simple word forms (section 3); this kind of investigation is somewhat akin to 
the etymological study of vocabulary (cf. Mailhammer, this volume). On the other hand 
morphological elements may be created from lexical and/or grammatical elements by 
processes of grammaticalisation and/or morphologisation;1 here reconstruction from 
morphology yields aspects of earlier syntax (section 4). Similarly, where morphological 
material results from phonological changes, reconstruction yields aspects of earlier phonology 
(section 7). Bound morphology can result from language contact, so methods used in 
analysing borrowing are required for reconstructing borrowed morphology as well (section 
10). Finally, where complex words are transmitted without change to their morphological 
analysis, morphological reconstruction requires simply synchronic morphological analysis 
applied to sets of words in a proto-language (section 2).
Morphological reconstruction is here understood to include any reconstruction that posits 
morphologically complex words in earlier states of language or historical processes that 
modify the internal morphological structure of words. Reconstructions discussed here are 
based on the comparison of forms between related languages (as in the Comparative Method), 
within the same language (as in internal reconstruction), or between languages that are not 
necessarily related (in reconstructing borrowing). Morphological reconstruction, like

---

<!-- page 305 -->

Morphological reconstruction
287
reconstruction in general, involves both hypotheses about earlier situations and hypotheses 
about changes that have taken place during the course of history; hence every reconstruction 
needs to be justi ed in terms of both the evidence for earlier states and the plausibility of the 
changes that are implied. If a procedure for morphological reconstruction needs to be 
formulated, it will be quite general and could read like what is presented in (1).
(1) Match forms that are potentially both complex morphologically (i.e. consist of more 
than one morpheme) and cognate according to the established sound correspondences of 
the language(s) supplemented by principles of morphological and semantic change. 
Posit earlier (complex) forms and show how the attested forms have developed from 
these earlier forms by plausible historical processes.
Case studies presented here are taken primarily from Australian languages, especially those 
of the Pama-Nyungan family (Miceli, this volume), and in particular from the Arandic 
subgroup (Koch 2004). This serves two purposes: it provides fresh alternatives to the usual 
textbook examples, and it emphasises the universal applicability of the methods. 
Morphological reconstruction as presented here is not tied to any particular formal theory of 
morphology, but uses the traditional concepts of morpheme, allomorph, and paradigm. For 
most kinds of change discussed here a caution is included about what not to expect.
2 Applying morphological analysis to reconstructed words
The  rst technique to be considered consists of simply applying synchronic morphological 
analysis to the results of comparative phonological reconstruction which yields sets of proto-
words that share a common element. This is the sense in which Anttila (1989: 351) claims 
that “comparative morphology is simply applied phonology.” Such a morphological analysis 
“yields morpheme boundaries and the sequences of morpheme slots” (p. 352). As an example, 
consider the Proto-Pama-Nyungan (pPN) paradigms reconstructable by comparative 
phonological methods, shown in Table 12.1.2
Morphological analysis applied to these forms yields the stems (*nga-, *nyun, *nhan, and 
*nhu) plus the following case suf xes: ergative *-tu after n, *-lu after a vowel in 3SgM but 
*-thu in 1Sg; nominative *-y in 1Sg otherwise -Ø; accusative *-nha after a vowel and *-a 
after a consonant; dative *-tyu in 1Sg, *-ngu after the other vowel- nal stem, and *-u after a 
consonant. These reconstructed case suf xes differ somewhat from those reconstructable for 
lexical nouns: ergative *-ngku (after disyllabic vowel- nal stems), *-lu (after other vowel-
 nal stems), *-Tu (with homorganic stop, after nasal- nal stems), nominative *-Ø, accusative 
*-Ø (but *-nha after personal stems), dative *-ku. These differences show the importance of 
inferring the proto-suf xes from actual in ected words rather than comparing disembodied 
suf xes. (Further synchronic analysis of pPN could posit a single underlying form for the 
allomorphs of each case, e.g. *-lu for ergative, *-nha for accusative, *-ngu for dative, plus 
morphophonemic rules to produce the correct surface forms. Comparative morphological 
reconstruction, however, is completed when allomorphs and their distribution are established; 
however, internal reconstruction could later be applied to the proto-forms to generate 
hypotheses about how their allomorphy came about as the result of phonological changes 
from a pre-proto-language stage.)
Morphological analysis can be performed on proto-forms regardless of whether their 
re exes have continued to be analysable in descendant languages. If there has been no change 
to the morphological analysis in descendant languages, this is an example of reconstruction

---

<!-- page 306 -->

Harold Koch
288
Table 12.1 Reconstructed Pama-Nyungan singular pronouns
1Sg
2Sg3
3SgF

*ngatyu
*nyunu
*nhanu
*nhungu
that has not involved morphological change. One cannot assume, however, that it is typical for 
morphologically complex words to descend unchanged except by phonological change 
processes, since they are liable to be affected by a number of processes of morphological change.
3 Reconstructing lost morphological complexity
Loss of morphological complexity is actually a common phenomenon of language change; 
which must therefore be considered in reconstructing earlier stages of language. Reconstruction 
may posit morphological complexity which is not attested in the attested languages, if they 
have altered their morphological analysis by processes such as the loss of a boundary and 
absorption of a formative into the stem. The complex structures to be reconstructed may be 
compounds, reduplicated words, words consisting of a lexical stem plus a derivational 
formative, or in ected words. Each of these will be illustrated in the following sections.
This kind of reconstruction may be carried out purely as an exercise in discovering 
evidence about earlier morphological patterns. Or it may be conducted as part of the 
etymological study of vocabulary, and valued for the information it provides on the prehistory 
of (parts of) lexemes. In either case, a practical issue is where to  nd the forms to compare, 
especially since they are likely to have diverged semantically during the course of linguistic 
evolution (think, for example, of how the senses of English quick, quicken, quicksand, and 
quicksilver have diverged from their shared earlier sense ‘alive’). What one looks for is forms 
whose parts have enough formal resemblance to qualify as (partial) cognates according to the 
established phonological changes as well as bearing some semantic relation that allows them 
to be related through plausible semantic and lexical changes. Where earlier complex structure 
is posited, it needs to be justi ed by evidence for the separate status of the erstwhile 
components, as well as by the plausibility of the changes undergone by the word.
3.1 Reconstructing compounds
One kind of morphologically complex word is a compound. Arrernte alkngenthe ‘ ame’ can 
be compared to alknge ‘eye’, to which it bears a partial formal (but not semantic) resemblance. 
If these words are historically related, the remainder of the  rst word,  nthe, needs to be 
accounted for. The plausibility of the second element -nthe being an originally separate 
morpheme comes from comparative evidence: ‘ ame’ in the related language Kaytetye, 
rlwenthe, contains the same element following a non-cognate term for ‘eye’, erlwe; this can 
hardly be accidental. Internal evidence within Arrernte for a morpheme nthe comes from the 
verb nthile- ‘light a  re, make  ames’, where -ile is a factitive suf x, which must here 
combine with an erstwhile noun nthe ‘ ame’.4 (This example is discussed in more detail in 
Koch 2003a: 274–275.)

---

<!-- page 307 -->

Morphological reconstruction
289
A more subtle example of such reconstruction involves a noun+verb compound in Arrernte 
(discussed in Koch and Hercus 2013). Language-internal comparison of atnarnpe- ‘step 
down’ with arnpe- ‘put foot down’ and of rake- ‘grab away from someone’ with ake- ‘pick 
(fruit from a plant)’ suggests that atn- and r- may have originally been separate elements. A 
further comparison of atniwe- ‘put someone on their feet’ with iwe- ‘throw’ suggests that 
atn- may be associated with feet. Forms like atnalthe ‘a lot of tracks in an area’, where -althe 
is an intensive suf x, suggests that atn- here means ‘footprints’, which in Australian languages 
is usually an extension of ‘foot’. The normal Arrernte term for ‘foot’, however, is ingke. All 
this suggests that atn- in the verbs is a former morpheme meaning ‘foot’, which survived 
only in complex forms. Sound changes established for the history of the Arandic languages 
(Koch 2004) allow it to be a re ex of a widespread Pama-Nyungan *thana.5 This presupposes 
a pattern of noun+verb compounds, which is supported by the existence of more obvious 
compounds involving bodyparts such as ingke-ntewe- ‘stamp one’s foot’, using the 
contemporary word for ‘foot’, and arr-angke- ‘cry, bawl’, consisting of ‘mouth’ plus ‘talk’. 
Returning to rake-, this can now be analysed as etymologically ‘hand-grab’; the  rst element 
r- can be the re ex of Proto-Pama-Nyungan *mara ‘hand’, which has otherwise been replaced 
in Arrernte by iltye (originally ‘ ngernail’).
3.2 Reconstructing reduplicated words
Consider the Kaurna (Thura-Yura language of South Australia) reduplicated kin term 
kawawa ‘mother’s brother’ (MB). An apparent cognate kawa occurs in many related 
languages. So what is the origin of the  nal wa? A similar pattern is found in a number of 
other ‘senior’ kin terms, when compared to simpler forms of typically reciprocal meanings. 
(In Australian languages it is common for some kin terms, e.g. grand-kin terms, to be 
reciprocal, the senior member [e.g. mother’s mother] being addressed by the same term as the 
junior member [female’s daughter’s child]; it is also relatively common for kin terms to be 
reduplicated.) The forms shown in the second column of Table 12.2 suggest a strange pattern 
of reduplication of the type C1 V1 C2 V2 r C1 V1 C2 V1 C2 V2, where C2 can be a consonant 
cluster. The suggested pattern would take thamu, make a copy of its V1 (a) and C2 (m), and 
insert the sequence am after C2, producing tham-am-u; likewise kam-am-i from a presumed 
earlier (and well attested) *kami. The historical evidence for their prehistory, however, points 
to a different origin. Other compounds in Kaurna and closely related languages show that 
when two disyllabic stems come together the last vowel of the  rst stem and the  rst consonant 
of the second stem can be dropped: compare Kaurna ngatyu-yarli and Narungga ngaty-arli 
‘father’ (lit. ‘my male’). If this process applied to reduplicated disyllabic stems such as 
*kami-kami (treated as compounds) the form would have been reduced via an optional 
*kam(ik)ami to kamami.6 We can therefore reconstruct the whole series of senior kin terms 
shown in the second column of Table 12.2 as former reduplicated stems. Meanwhile the 
simple forms have typically been augmented by a suf x, especially -tya.
A more complex example of reconstructing reduplicated forms comes from the Arandic 
languages. Koch (2013b) shows that a number of Arrernte kin terms must descend from 
earlier reduplicated structures. The evidence is largely language-internal. For example, kake 
‘elder brother’ has in ected forms with a shorter stem k(e)-: k-atye ‘my elder brother (eB)’, 
k-angkwe ‘your eB’, k-ikwe ‘his/her eB’, ke-nhenge ‘pair consisting of an eB and younger 
sibling’. One could posit an earlier free form *ake, from which this ke- is truncated. The form 
ake is attested in Lower Arrernte as the second member of compounds akely-ake and werr-
ake, both ‘brother’, the  rst elements occurring also on their own as words for ‘brother’ and

‘boy’ respectively. The free form ake (as well as ke) is actually attested in the earliest 
dictionary of Western Arrernte (Strehlow n.d.). So kake can easily be interpreted as continuing 
a reduplicated stem *ak(e)-ake – it is normal for initial vowels to drop in this dialect. Other 
Arandic kin terms of similar form can therefore also be interpreted as former reduplicated 
stems: yaye (< *ay(e)-aye ‘elder sister’, Kaytetye tyatye ‘mother’s father’, beside which there 
are derivatives of a shorter stem atye- (Arrernte atyemeye, Kaytetye atyewaleye), and nyanye 
‘mother’s mother’. This reconstructed pattern is made more plausible by the fact that 
reduplication of kin terms is synchronically used for affectionate purposes and constitutes 
one of several means by which the Arandic languages have replaced short lexical stems with 
longer ones.
3.3 Reconstructing derived words
It is common for some derived words to lose their synchronic morphological complexity, as 
they become separated from related words through the effects of sound change, semantic 
change, and loss of vocabulary. For example, the English words health and sloth are no 
longer perceived as derivatives from hale and slow respectively, as wide is derived from 
width. In Kaytetye (and some other Arandic varieties) kwerrralye ‘star’ bears a partial 
phonological resemblance to kwerre ‘girl’; is there a historical derivational relation? In 
Western Arrernte kwerrralye more speci cally designates the Pleiades and this constellation 
is interpreted in mythology as a group of girls (Wilkins 1997). Although a certain etymology 
cannot be provided for the suf x -alye, its recurrence in ngkerralye ‘ribs’ (in several Arandic 
varieties) makes an earlier collective function plausible. This example illustrates how 
morphological reconstruction overlaps with etymology (cf. Koch 2003a; Mailhammer, this 
volume), and the holistic study of the lexicon of a language in its cultural as well as linguistic 
setting.
3.4 Reconstructing inﬂ ected words from stems
Part of a lexeme may be revealed by morphological reconstruction to have been an earlier 
in ectional formative. An earlier word would have undergone a reanalysis whereby the 
in ectional af x was interpreted as part of the stem. To be successful, reconstruction should 
demonstrate from cognates the prior existence of such an in ectional formative and present 
plausible grounds for a reanalysis that absorbs this into the stem.

---

<!-- page 309 -->

Morphological reconstruction
291
3.4.1 Absolutive case
In Arrernte and the closely related Alyawarr language interrogative and demonstrative stems 
contain a  nal syllable nhe (cf. Koch 1995: 39). The absolutive (= nominative/accusative) 
case lacks an in ection. Table 12.3 shows a partial case paradigm for Arrernte iwenhe ‘what’ 
and Alyawarr nhenhe ‘this’. It also shows rarer variants based on a shorter stem iwe- and 
nhe-; in the short paradigms -nhe is to be analysed as the absolutive in ectional suf x. The 
absolutive form, e.g. iwenhe, is identical in the two paradigms; the only difference is that an 
internal morpheme boundary occurs in the short paradigm. The historical relation between 
the two paradigms can be explained as follows: the short paradigm is older; its absolutive 
form iwe-nhe has been reanalysed as iwenhe-Ø, and a new paradigm has been built on the 
longer stem iwenhe- that results from this reanalysis. This scenario is justi ed by the following 
facts. First, cognates of the suf x -nhe (-nha and -nya in neighbouring languages) mark the 
absolutive case of de nite nouns and pronouns. Second, most Australian languages have a 
pattern whereby many nominal paradigms lack an overt case marker for the absolutive. This 
general pattern allows a reanalysis in which the absolutive forms iwe-nhe and nhe-nhe are 
interpreted as unsuf xed forms, which entails the treatment of nhe as part of the stem and 
allows for the creation of new forms of the paradigm by adding suf xes to this extended 
stem. Third, this diachronic process of reanalysing overtly marked forms in basic in ectional 
slots such as absolutive case is a widespread kind of morphological change (see Koch 1995).
3.4.2 Third person singular
In the Nyulnyulan language family Bardi (Bowern 2012a) and Nyulnyul (McGregor 2011) 
have possessive pre xes which mark the person and number of possessors of certain bodypart 
nouns. In the related languages Nyikina and Yawuru possession is rather expressed by an 
independent pronoun. The form of bodypart nouns, however, includes an initial syllable 
which is missing in the Bardi and Nyulnyul cognates but which resembles the 3Sg possessor 
pre x of Bardi – see Table 12.4.9 It must be concluded that Bardi represents the earlier 
system, that 3Sg forms have been reanalysed as if they lacked a pre x and the erstwhile

---

<!-- page 310 -->

Harold Koch
292
pre x has been treated as part of the lexical stem of the bodypart noun, and this new extended 
stem is modi ed by appropriate pronouns to specify the possessor. The 3Sg, like the absolutive 
(or nominative) case, is considered the basic member of its in ectional paradigm, and such 
forms are most liable to be interpreted as zero-marked and to have a large in uence in 
reformulating the paradigm – as explained in Koch (1995). Table 12.4 shows that possession 
in Yawuru is expressed by a free pronoun accompanying what was formerly the 3Sg form of 
the noun; in Nyikina the possessive pronouns have further evolved to enclitics, with some 
reduction of the initial consonant.
3.4.3 First person singular in kin terms
Although it is the 3Sg which is the basic or unmarked member of a person paradigm in a 
general sense, in kin terms we often  nd it is a 1Sg marker that gets absorbed into the 
lexical stem. Many kin terms in Arrernte include an element ye that seems to have no 
function but disappears before some in ections: meye ‘mother’ has in ected forms  rst 
person m-atye, m-angkwe second person, m-ikwe third person, me-nhenge dyadic (‘mother 
and her child’). Its status as a morpheme is dubious: there is synchronic evidence for its 
separability (since it disappears before in ectional suf xes) but none for its meaning. 
Comparison with Kaytetye suggests an explanation: there -ye is the marker of a 1Sg 
possessor. We can thus reconstruct for an earlier stage of Arrernte a situation where -ye 
was an in ection marking 1Sg, posit that such in ected forms were interpreted as bare 
stems (‘my mother’ becoming just ‘mother’), and explicit possessors were signalled by 
attaching a pronoun, including atye ‘my’, to (re-)mark 1Sg. (In section 4.1 it is shown how 
these enclitic dative pronouns were eventually reanalysed as suf xes.) This reconstruction 
explains why Kaytetye lacks a 1Sg af x cognate with -atye (see Table 12.6): it has 
preserved the original marker -ye. A plausible reason for treating a 1Sg in ected form as 
the stem in kin terms is that it is 1Sg forms which are used in address, and address forms 
of kin terms are often used as the basic form.10
3.4.4 Locative case
Other members of in ectional paradigms may also be reanalysed as stems. Locative forms of 
place names and demonstratives and ‘where’ words often undergo this change. This is 
demonstrated in Koch (1995: 47–51): for example in Dyirbal a whole paradigm of the article-
like ‘noun marker’ bala was formed from the locative case form ba-la of a demonstrative 
stem *ba-. A more complex set of developments from the cognate *pa- is reconstructed for 
some western Pama-Nyungan languages in Dench (2007). Table 12.5 illustrates from the 
Yankunytjatjara dialect of Western Desert the paradigms of stems that appear in various 
languages as mid-distal demonstratives, ‘de nite nominals’, or third person pronouns. All the 
paradigms involve a stem that consists of pa- plus another syllable: lu, nya, or la. In many 
other languages cognates of these syllables are each case suf xes: -lu ergative, -nya (< *nha) 
nominative/accusative, and -la locative. Reconstruction in the light of cognates in other 
languages thus suggests that these three stems continue separate case-forms of an original 
single paradigm of *pa-. Dench (2007) gives reasons – in terms of their discourse functions 
– why it is plausible that each of these case-forms should have been reanalysed as the stem 
of a separate paradigm, with the result that the original paradigm has split into three 
paradigms.11

---

<!-- page 311 -->

Morphological reconstruction
293

3.4.5 Tense markers
In languages of the Aranda branch of the Arandic subgroup (i.e. all except Kaytetye) about 
30 verb stems end with a synchronically meaningless syllable rne, e.g. angerne- ‘dig/
scoop’. This cannot be identi ed as a current in ection or a former derivational suf x, nor 
as an original part of the verbal root, since: (a) it is absent from nominal derivatives (e.g. 
angentye ‘soakage‘ [place where one can scoop up water], where -ntye is in origin a 
nominalising suf x); and (b) it is missing from the sister-language Kaytetye, where the 
cognate verb has the shorter form ange-. Verbs with this rne are all transitive, and some 
contrast with an intransitive stem that lacks it; e.g. amperne- ‘burn’ (tr) vs. ampe- ‘burn’ 
(itr); for such verbs rne can be considered synchronically a transitivising morpheme. 
Related languages to the west of Arandic have in ectional classes for verbs, and one class 
has suf xes whose characteristic consonant is either l or rn (a retro ex nasal). In Warlpiri 
-rni marks present and -rnu past. If pre-Proto-Arandic had such a system the vowel of both 
would have become e (shwa) as the result of regular sound changes and ambiguity would 
have arisen. It is proposed that this did happen and that a form like *amperne (< *kampa-
rni) was reanalysed as having a zero present tense marker, rne was treated as part of the 
stem, and an overt suf x was added to indicate past. The coexisting intransitive stems, as 
in other languages, would have belonged to a different in ectional class and taken a 
different set of suf xes, which did not include rne – as is the case for kampa- ‘burn’ in the 
Western Desert language. In other languages the l/rn class included predominantly 
transitive verb stems, and some of the Arrernte verbs are cognate with these. It thus appears 
that the origin of rne in Arrernte verbs was with a number of transitive verbs of an earlier 
rn/l class. Further support for such a class in the ancestor of the Arandic languages comes 
from the fact that Kaytetye uses l as the combining form of transitive verbs in some 
complex formations, in contrast to rr for intransitive verbs; e.g. are-l-alpe- ‘see and go 
back’ vs. angke-rr-ayte- ‘speak and go back’ (cf. section 4.5). This example shows not 
only that a part of a lexical stem can be the re ex of an in ection but that the earlier 
existence of in ectional classes can be reconstructed from facts in a language which 
synchronically lacks such classes.
4 Reconstructing morphologisation from syntax
The situations described in section 3 could be described as involving demorphologisation: 
reconstruction posits morphological complexity where there is none in later stages of a 
language. The following section describes the opposite situation, morphologisation. Here

---

<!-- page 312 -->

Harold Koch
294
reconstruction begins from morphologically complex words and yields earlier constructions 
which are syntactic rather than morphological; the morphological structures are the result of 
components having lost their status as separate words. The boundaries that have been lost in 
these cases are word boundaries. Plentiful examples of such change can be found in the 
copious literature on grammaticalisation, beginning with Givón (1971), who coined the well-
known aphorism that “today’s morphology is yesterday’s syntax” (cf. Joseph 2003; 
Frajzyngier, this volume). The focus of the examples presented here is not so much on 
reconstructing earlier syntactic constructions as on discovering the etymology of bound 
morphemes which originated outside of morphology. These examples show that one cannot 
expect that all morphological elements of a language continue elements of the same status in 
earlier stages of the language.
4.1 Person afﬁ xes from pronouns
The Arandic languages use af xes to indicate the person of the possessor12 of a kin relation. 
Cross-language comparison between Arrernte and Kaytetye suggests that the person af xes 
(except for the 1Sg) are cognate. A problem for morphological reconstruction involves the 
order of the cognate af xes: they are suf xes in Arrernte but pre xes in Kaytetye. It is 
implausible that historically suf xes re-ordered to become pre xes or vice versa. Language-
internal comparison reveals that these person-markers resemble the dative forms of 
independent pronouns in both languages, except that they lack the  nal syllable (nge in 
1/2Sg, re in 3Sg) of the free pronouns. The free pronouns in the two languages are cognate 
as are the af xes: their pre-Arandic forms (reconstructed on the basis of cognates in other 
Pama-Nyungan languages) are given in Table 12.6. It is possible to reconstruct a long and a 
short version of each set of forms: the long forms are re ected as free pronouns and the short 
forms as person af xes. This presupposes that the short forms were separate words, probably 
clitics, that could occur either before or after the kin noun that they quali ed, and that they 
became  xed in opposite sides of the noun in the separate languages before being reanalysed 
as af xes. The prior existence of two sets of pronouns is supported by the general tendencies 
both to reinforce pronouns (by adding a particle) to mark pragmatic prominence and to reduce 
pronouns (by de-stressing and omitting segments) to mark continuity of reference (Mushin 
and Simpson 2006).
4.2 Case sufﬁ x from postposition
A comitative case suf x marks accompaniment in Arandic languages. It has two forms, 
-larlenge and -ngarlenge. In Kaytetye these allomorphs are conditioned by the length of 
the stem; in Arrernte -ngarlenge is a rare alternative to the normal -larlenge. In Kaytetye 
the locative case suf x has allomorphs -le and -nge, which are conditioned by the same 
factors as the comitative variants, and this allomorphy is reconstructable to Proto-
Arandic (on the evidence of cognate suf xes in related Pama-Nyungan languages that 
have a similar conditioning). Hence the comitative forms can easily be analysed as the 
locative case followed by an element *arlenge (the  rst vowel normally elides in such a 
combination). A plausible explanation is that *arlenge was a postposition that occurred 
after a noun in ected in the locative, and that this form has been reanalysed as part of the 
word, combining with the erstwhile locative suf x into a longer suf x marking a new 
case value.13

---

<!-- page 313 -->

Morphological reconstruction
295

4.3 Inclusive pronoun from inclusory noun phrase
Many Australian languages make an inclusive vs. exclusive distinction for  rst person 
dual and plural pronouns. Comparative study shows recurrent diachronic processes of 
loss and later reintroduction of a morphological contrast between inclusive and exclusive 
forms (see Dench 1994 for languages of the Pilbara, and section 6.1 below for an example 
of the loss of the contrast). Where there is no morphological contrast, the distinction may 
be indicated by syntactic means, including the inclusory construction, whereby the 
inclusive for example is expressed as ‘we you’, and the exclusive by ‘we he/she’, i.e. by 
mentioning the whole set and a member of the set. The Karnic language Pitta-Pitta is one 
language that uses the syntactic strategy. Some other Karnic languages do have a 
morphological contrast, but the inclusive forms can be reconstructed as re exes of a 
syntactic ‘we you’ construction. In the Arabana-Wangkangurru language the  rst dual 
exclusive is ari (which on the basis of cognates clearly descends from earlier *ngali, r 
here denoting a tap). The inclusive category, however, is expressed (differently in the 
several dialects) by a pronominal stem which appears to combine ari with a form of the 
2Sg pronoun: Wangkatyaka aruntu (cf. ergative untu), Arabana arimpa (cf. nominative 
inpa in Pitta-Pitta), Wangkangurru aruna (cf. accusative una(nha)). The historical 
explanation for the inclusive stems is that they originated as inclusory phrases, which 
were reanalysed as a single word meaning 1DuIncl, which was then in ected with the 
case suf xes appropriate to a non-singular pronoun.15
4.4 Case sufﬁ x from case-inﬂ ected pronoun
In Diyari (one of the Karnic languages) there is a special set of case suf xes used for female 
personal names. Table 12.7 shows how these differ from the suf xes for common nouns and 
for male personal names. Several of the feminine suf xes – the ergative, nominative, and 
accusative – resemble the forms of the third singular feminine pronoun, minus the  rst 
consonant and vowel of the latter: these are in bold type in Table 12.7. This formal 
resemblance, as well as the typological oddity of female case suf xes, can be explained if we 
hypothesise that these suf xes originated as enclitic pronouns that marked the noun as 
de nite and feminine, and that the pronouns occurred at the end of the noun phrase. This is a 
typical syntactic structure for Australian languages. It is also common for enclitic forms to be 
reduced phonologically by the loss of a syllable. The reanalysis here would have involved not 
only a change of status from enclitic word to suf x but also a bleaching out of the third 
person meaning.16

---

4.5 Verb inﬂ ection from auxiliary phrase
In Arrernte a number of complex verb forms transparently include another verb stem preceded 
by a linking consonant: see Table 12.8. Here a full stop is used to separate off this ligative, 
which could possibly be given a meaning if it were treated as a morpheme, although it is 
really the whole construction that provides the meaning.

Similar complexes in Kaytetye are less transparent: see Table 12.9. It is doubtful whether

intransitive verb stems (e.g. entwe-yane and angke-rrane ‘lie’ and ‘talk’) can be 
synchronically analysed in terms of the verb ‘sit’. The imperfective allomorph  -rranytye, 
which occurs after transitive stems, cannot be identi ed with any independent verb; the 
same applies to -yene, which etymologically presumably re ects a participial -ye 
(< *tye) followed by a verb. In all these examples the likely source of the complex 
morphology is a participial suf x followed by an auxiliary verb. In other words, the 
earlier constructions were syntactic constructions which expressed an aspectual or 
associated motion function.

5 Reconstructing morpheme re-ordering
Although the order of morphemes within a word is generally more stable than that of words 
within a clause, there are instances of change in the order of af xes. Hence one cannot always 
assume that the same order prevailed in earlier stages of a language. In Arrernte the order of 
person-marking and case suf xes in kin terms is variable: thus when ‘mother’ me- is in ected 
for both  rst person (marked by -atye) and dative (marked with -ke) the result is either me-k-
atye or m-atye-ke. What is the original order and what accounts for the change? Since the 
person marker originated as an enclitic pronoun outside of the word (cf. section 4.1), it must 
have followed the case suf x, e.g. *me -ke=atye. When it was reanalysed as a suf x, a 
situation arose where the order of morphemes within the word is not syntagmatically iconic, 
i.e. does not re ect the meaning relations.18 In the expression ‘for my mother’, ‘for’ (marked 
by dative) has scope over the whole complex ‘my mother’. These semantic relations would 
be better re ected if the order were ‘dative- rst person-mother’ or its mirror image ‘mother-
 rst person-dative’ (in a suf xing language like Arrernte). A switch in order is made possible 
by the ambiguity of the absolutive case form m-atye, with zero marking of the case; the 
earlier analysis m-Ø-atye could easily be reinterpreted as m-atye-Ø, and this analysis extended 
to create a new dative m-atye-ke (‘mother-my-to’) to replace the counter-iconic me-k-atye 
(‘mother-to-my’).
6 Reconstructing functional change of morphemes
All of the morphologisations reconstructed in section 4 involve a change in the status of 
grammatical elements, from syntactic to morphological elements. Just as one cannot expect 
the status of grammatical elements to remain constant, so it cannot be expected that the 
earlier grammatical function of morphemes was the same as in the language from which one 
is reconstructing, since functions can be altered over time. Grammatical morphemes, 
especially those that belong to the in ectional system of a language, have speci c functions 
as exponents of morphosyntactic properties within grammatical categories. These functions 
are subject to changes both within and across in ectional categories. This section will discuss 
some examples of reconstructed changes in the grammatical meaning or function of 
morphological elements.19

---

<!-- page 316 -->

Harold Koch
298
6.1 Change in number
In Western Arrernte, unlike other dialects of Arrernte and related languages, the 1Sg dative 
pronoun is not atyenge (cf. section 4.1) but a completely unrelated form nweke. Its place in 
the paradigm requires an explanation. The 1Pl stem is nwerne, with suf xes -nhe for 
accusative and -ke for dative – see Table 12.10. Some other related varieties, such as 
Northeastern Arrernte, Anmatyerr and Alyawarr, make a distinction in the plural between 
inclusive and exclusive. The cognate form anwerne, which is an undifferentiated 1Pl in 
Central and Eastern Arrernte, like nwerne in Western Arrernte, is in these other varieties 
the exclusive form, and the inclusive is just anwe;20 here -rne has to be treated as the marker 
of exclusive. We reconstruct for Proto-Arrernte the system with contrasting inclusive and 
exclusive forms, and posit that Western, Central and Eastern Arrernte have lost the distinction 
and generalised the former exclusive form, making the former inclusive forms redundant. It 
can be inferred that Western Arrernte has re-used the redundant former 1PlIncl form nweke 
as its 1Sg form, changing its function in the process.
Table 12.10 Arrernte  rst person pronouns
WArrernte

6.2 Change in tense
A purposive suf x *-ku can be reconstructed for Proto-Pama-Nyungan on the basis of its 
widespread distribution and common function as a marker of subordinate clauses. In many 
languages its re exes mark the future tense, which is explicable on the basis that purpose 
clauses are often used as independent clauses with a modal sense that is not very different 
from a predictive future tense sense. Dench (2003) describes how in three languages of the 
Pilbara region, Panyjima, Ngarluma and Yinjibarndi/Kurrama, the re exives of a suf x 
reconstructable as *-(l)ku (-lku is the form taken after a particular in ectional stem class) 
mark present tense rather than future tense or purposive mood. The direction of change from 
purposive/future to present rather than the reverse is claimed on the basis of the wider 
distribution and solid reconstruction of the purposive/future sense and the fact that the change 
to a present tense sense can be supported. Dench justi es a plausible pathway from 
independent uses of the purposive via a prospective aspect to an imperfective present, with 
each of the different stages represented in at least one of the Pilbara languages.
6.3 Change in case
In most of the Karnic languages the dative case suf x has the form -nga, but in Arabana-
Wangkangurru this form rather marks locative case. This form can easily be seen to be a 
re ex of the *-ngka allomorph of locative that occurs in many other Pama-Nyungan 
languages. Hence it is concluded that the locative sense of Arabana-Wangkangurru -nga is

---

<!-- page 317 -->

Morphological reconstruction
299
inherited but that the other Karnic languages have innovated by shifting the function from 
locative to dative.
Another instance of a reconstructable shift of case function involves the causal suf x -la 
of Pitta-Pitta and its cognate -ra in Arabana-Wangkangurru (r is a tap that regularly developed 
from l). This case indicates a concomitant cause or event to be avoided; the latter function, 
called ‘aversive’ in Australian linguistics, has separate case marking in some Australian 
languages but in others is a secondary function of another case, which may be locative (Dixon 
2002: 171). The causal suf x of Pitta-Pitta and Arabana-Wangkangurru is plausibly derived 
from the securely reconstructed Proto-Pama-Nyungan *-la, an allomorph of the *-ngka 
mentioned in the previous paragraph. This requires the plausible positing of a slight functional 
shift of *-la.21
6.4 More complex refunctionalisations
Bowern (2012b) reconstructs some complex changes in Nyikina verb in ection. Table 12.11 
shows the realis forms of Nyikina, in which non-future (nFut) indicates a tense that does not 
distinguish past from present. Comparison with other Nyulnyulan languages shows that the 
Intr(ansitive) forms of ‘laugh’ re ect an earlier past tense, while the Tr(ansitive) forms 
continue a present tense: the Proto-Nyulnyulan (pNN) forms are shown in column three and 
their meanings in column four. A functional merger of tenses has to be reconstructed. The 
contrast between ngangkama and ngankama yields a synchronic morphemic analysis in 
which -ng- marks intransitive and -n- signals transitive. This analysis is con rmed by the 
(new) future forms. An analysis of the contrasting Proto-Nyulnyulan (pNN) forms *ngakama 
and *ngangkama, however, identi es -ng- rather as a marker of past tense. Hence, although 
the morpheme boundaries have not changed the function of -ng- has been radically altered as 
a consequence of the forms in which it occurs entering into a different set of contrastive 
relations.

7 Undoing the morphological effects of phonological change
It is well known that phonological change can alter the formal relationship between 
morphologically related words. This may involve adjustment to the borders of morphemes 
(boundary shift) or insertion of morpheme boundaries where there were none before. 
Another consequence of phonological change is that phonological differences may be 
morphologised, i.e. acquire morphological functions. Reconstruction from morphology

---

<!-- page 318 -->

Harold Koch
300
must therefore reckon with the possibility that not all current bound morphemes continue 
earlier forms with the same boundaries and that phonological differences that currently 
signal morphological functions may descend from earlier forms that lacked a 
morphological function.
7.1 Reconstructing boundary shift
In most Pama-Nyungan languages the majority of nouns end in a vowel and case suf xes 
begin with consonants, e.g. ergative *-ngku or *-lu, locative *-ngka or *-la, dative *-ku, and 
ablative *-mu. But in some languages of Cape York Peninsula most nouns end in a consonant 
and case suf xes may begin with a vowel, e.g. Wik-Mungkan -ang ergative/locative, -ak 
dative, -am ablative. It is easily seen that these are the result a sound change that dropped 
 nal vowels (and simpli ed the ngk cluster), and that as a consequence the position of the 
morpheme boundary has been reanalysed and the  nal vowel of the stem has been treated as 
part of the suf x. A similar situation can be seen in Yir-Yoront: compare in Table 12.12 the 
current partial paradigm of ‘person’ with the one that is reconstructable on the basis of 
cognates. Now consider the paradigm of ‘some’. Here the suf xes begin with a different 
vowel; the choice of vowel in the allomorph of the ergative suf x re ects merely a difference 
in the  nal vowel of the stem at an earlier stage. The reanalysis has involved the creation of 
allomorphs of the case suf x: where previously stems ended in a, u, or i, and the ergative had 
only a single form -lu, after the loss of  nal vowels and reanalysis of the position of the 
morpheme boundary stems ended in a consonant but the ergative suf x had allomorphs -al, 
-ul, and -il.
7.2 Reconstructing the morphologisation of phonemes
The Yir-Yoront paradigm of ‘some’ also shows allomorphy in the vowel of the stem (u vs. 
a). This can be posited to be the re ex of an earlier phonetic difference conditioned by the 
vowel of the following syllable – at a stage (after the loss of  nal vowels) when the paradigm 
was *wap, *wapul, *wapun – which involved the stem vowel assimilating to the roundness 
of the following vowel. Here the difference of vowel has become a co-signal of case 
distinctions. Likewise the paradigm of ‘egg’ shows an alternation of the  rst vowel, which 
can be reconstructed to re ect a similar harmony of a with the vowel of the second syllable 
in the dative form. Here another, more radical reanalysis can be seen. The vowel of the 
second syllable appears synchronically to be an in x that signals the dative case. A 
consequence of phonological change – the regular loss of the vowel in the citation form (the 
absolutive) while it was preserved in the dative – has involved the creation of a morpheme 
boundary where there was none before.

---

<!-- page 319 -->

Morphological reconstruction
301
The morphologisation of phonemes is hard to illustrate from Australian languages. 
Plentiful examples are available, however, from Germanic and other Indo-European 
languages. It is well-known that the vowel difference which in English signals plural (feet vs. 
foot) or the difference in voicing of fricatives which marks verb derivation (e.g. to halve vs. 
half) is the result of the reinterpretation of former phonetic differences which after 
phonological changes took place happened to be left as the best signals of a morphological 
difference. Such morphophonemic alternations provide most of the input for the internal 
reconstruction of earlier phonology, and the literature on internal reconstruction provides 
many examples (e.g. Fox 1995: chapter 7). This reconstruction method is not really 
morphological reconstruction, however, except insofar as its input is morphophonemic 
alternations.23
8 Reconstructing intra-paradigmatic change
Members of in ectional paradigms exert an in uence over one another. The resulting 
changes have often been described as being due to ‘analogy’ (e.g. Anttila 1989: chapter 5; 
Campbell 2004: chapter 4; Hock 2003). Changes may affect the (allomorphic) forms of the 
stem or the form of the af xes. The form of one member of the paradigm may be extended 
to other members. If one allomorphic variant has been extended to the whole paradigm, the 
forms are said to have been levelled. There are no fool-proof explanations for the direction 
of extensions, but the in uence of basic (‘unmarked’) members of the paradigm has often 
been noticed. Because of the prevalence of analogical paradigmatic change one cannot 
expect that each form in an in ectional paradigm has descended into the language from 
earlier stages by sound change alone. In reconstructing earlier forms of paradigms one 
should put particular reliance on cognate forms that are least likely to result from analogical 
changes, while positing instances of reanalysis, extension, etc. that are likely to have 
produced the innovating forms.
An example of reconstructing the extension in paradigms can be seen in the distal 
demonstratives of languages of the Pilbara in Western Australia (see Table 12.13, taken 
from Dench 2007). By comparing Payungu with Jiwarli and Panyjima we can see that 
Payungu has built a new accusative by adding an explicit suf x to the nominative form. The 
Payungu and Panyjima pattern is more likely to represent the original situation because it is 
attested in more languages and conforms to a very widespread (in Pama-Nyungan languages) 
of having identical forms for the nominative and accusative case of nominals (except 
personal pronouns). In Panyjima the stem of the nominative/accusative has been extended 
to the whole paradigm, and new case suf xes have been added to form the ergative, dative 
and locative. In Nyangumarta the whole paradigm is built on a stem ngurnungu, which 
appears to consist of the original dative form *ngurnu (preserved in Jiwarli and Payungu) 
extended with an increment -ngu (of indeterminate origin). All these innovated paradigms 
can be analysed in terms of extensions from particular cells of an original paradigm which 
was identical to that of Jiwarli. The result has been complete levelling of the stems in 
Panyjima and Nyangumarta.

---

<!-- page 320 -->

Harold Koch
302

A somewhat similar phenomenon can also be reconstructed from more distantly related 
Pama-Nyungan languages. Many languages have an in ectional class of verbs – some of 
which have monosyllabic roots and are synchronically anomalous – which includes 
in ections containing both n and rr. This pattern is widespread enough to be reconstructed 
to Proto-Pama-Nyungan for a number of verb roots, including ya- ‘go’ and ma(a)- ‘get’ 
(Alpher 1990). In Table 12.14 this pattern can be seen in Guugu Yimidhirr and 
Nyangumarta. In some languages the root is disyllabic but includes either rr or n in its 
second syllable. For example, Gumbaynggirr has two disyllabic stems (beside a 
monosyllabic stem seen in maa-nku purposive): maani-, which supplies a past maani-ng, 
purposive maani-ku, and a zero-marked imperative maani, and maana-, which supplies 
a purposive maana-yku and a zero-marked imperative maana. It appears that these new 
imperative formations have replaced an inherited *maarra. But the closely related 
Yaygirr has maani, maana, maarra as in ected forms. An opposite development to that 
of Gumbaynggirr is seen in Ritharrngu (and other languages of the Yolngu subgroup): 
here a stem including rra has been extended to the whole paradigm, except that the past 
retains an n-suf x.24
Table 12.14 Paradigms of *maa- ‘get’
Guugu Yimidhirr
Nyangumarta
Gumbaynggirr
Ritharrngu
maa-rraa Imperative
ma-rra Imperative
maarra- stem
maa-naa nPast
ma-na nFuture
maana- stem
maana Past
maa-ni Past
ma-ninya Present
maani- stem
ma-nku Potential
maa-nku Purposive
Both of these examples illustrate morphological changes consisting of the extension of 
allomorphs within a paradigm and the reanalysis of in ected forms as stems. They also 
provide examples of reconstruction that proceeds by comparing anomalous forms across 
languages – i.e. forms that could not result from analogical changes within their own 
paradigms – and positing a prehistoric pattern that includes these irregular forms within a 
single paradigm (cf. Meillet 1967: chapter 3).

---

<!-- page 321 -->

Morphological reconstruction
303
9 Reconstructing cross-paradigmatic change
Where languages have in ectional classes one can expect these classes to in uence one 
another. The direction of in uence is more often from the dominant classes to others. The 
membership of the classes may be affected: it is common for lexemes to be attracted into the 
dominant classes (e.g. English past holp has been replaced by the regular helped) and for 
newly created lexemes to be assigned to these classes. Hence one should not expect every 
member of an in ectional class to have always been a member of the same class. Reconstruction 
may include positing a change in class membership.
In Nyangumarta ma- ‘get’ belongs to a very small n-class, whereas most transitive verbs 
belong to a large rn-class – classes being named after a consonant characteristic of some of 
their in ectional suf xes. Causative verbs that are derived by means of a suf x -ma, which is 
apparently the stem of the verb ‘get’, however, in ect according to the rn-class (rn denotes a 
retro ex nasal), as is appropriate for transitive verbs. Table 12.15 illustrates the paradigms of 
ma- ‘get’, a derivative ‘to name’ which involves the -ma suf x added to the noun yini ‘name’, 
and a transitive verb ‘break’ of the rn-class. In reconstructing the causative suf x it can be 
identi ed etymologically with the verb ma-, provided we assume that derivatives have 
changed their in ectional class from the original n-class of ma- to the rn-class, motivated by 
the fact that this is the dominant in ection for transitive verbs. This case provides a general 
caution: if in a language most transitive verbs are in a particular class, say the rn-class, the 
chances are great that any given transitive verb will be there regardless of its class membership 
in an earlier stage of the language.
Table 12.15 Nyangumarta verb paradigms
get
name
break
Imperative
ma-rra
yini-ma-la
ngarta-la
nFuture
ma-na
yini-ma-rna
ngarta-rna
Present
ma-ninyi
yini-ma-rninyi
ngarta-rninyi
Potential
ma-nku
yini-ma-lku
ngarta-lku
10 Identifying borrowed morphology
Some morphological material in a language is the result of borrowing from another language 
(cf. Lucas, this volume). (We are not concerned here with tracing simplex words which 
happen to have a complex structure in the language from which they were borrowed – e.g. 
the fact that the Russian source for sputnik includes an agent suf x  nik – since this doesn’t 
involve morphological change in the borrowing language.) Reconstruction of this borrowing 
requires identifying a source external to the language, justifying the plausibility of borrowing 
(because of contact between the speakers of both languages, for example), and showing why 
the material is unlikely to have been inherited within the language. The two examples 
discussed here involve suf xes that have been borrowed from Arandic languages by their 
western neighbours. The interaction of speakers of the Arandic languages with those of 
Warlpiri and the Western Desert language over a long period of time is well established. The 
western languages also came into contact with English later than the Arandic languages did, 
and borrowing from English came through the intermediary of Arandic, which helps to 
explain the second example.

---

<!-- page 322 -->

Harold Koch
304
Warlpiri has an ‘associated motion’ verb formation (discussed by Simpson 2001: 182) 
that consists of suf xing an element -yalpi- to a verb stem already extended by the 
participial suf x -nja, which adds the meaning ‘go back and VERB’; e.g. nguna-nja-yalpi- 
‘lie-PPL-GO.BACK&’. The in ectional category of associated motion is much more highly 
elaborated in the Arandic languages than in Warlpiri, and exactly this suf x occurs in 
Kaytetye (and a cognate form -tyalpe in other Arandic varieties) with the same meaning; 
the synonymous form in Kaytetye is enwe-yalpe-. The form is moreover analysable within 
Arandic in terms of a linking consonant y or ty (probably originally a participial suf x 
*-tye) and a verb root alpe- ‘return’. The Warlpiri verb ‘return’, on the other hand, is not a 
good match: it has the form kulpa-; if Warlpiri had used its internal resources the form 
would be *VERB-nja-kulpa-.
The eastern dialects of the Western Desert dialect, e.g. Yankunytjatjara and Pintupi-
Luritja, have a derivational suf x -mila- that is used only for in ecting transitive verbs 
borrowed from English. This form bears no resemblance to other formatives used to derive 
other transitive verbs, e.g. kintil-ma- ‘make level’. But in their eastern neighbour Arrernte a 
similar form is used, e.g. help-em-ile-. Here em derives from the form that English transitive 
verbs took in earlier Pidgin English, e.g. help’em, and -ile- is the factitive suf x which derives 
transitive verbs from nominals, e.g. mwerr-ile- ‘make better’ from mwerre ‘good’. It appears 
that Western Desert has borrowed from Western Arrernte its strategy for adapting English 
verbs and in the process has reanalysed the em+ile- complex as a single form. This and the 
previous example show that not all synchronic morphology is inherited from earlier stages of 
the same language; for complete reconstruction historical linguists need to know about the 
contact history of their target languages as well as details of the languages with which they 
were in contact.
11 Conclusion
There are no precise procedures that can be prescribed for reconstructing morphology (and 
textbooks are not very helpful in this respect): only some general principles can be offered. 
Morphological reconstruction involves comparing words that are potentially related 
historically and positing earlier structures which may be different from the starting point. 
Reconstructions need to be justi ed by showing evidence for the posited earlier structures 
and demonstrating how it is possible to get from these earlier forms by plausible changes. 
This in turn presupposes knowledge of the historical phonology of the relevant languages as 
well as an understanding of the general processes of morphological change. (For this reason 
this survey has been organised around the kind of changes that are known to affect 
morphological structure.) The best way to learn how to do morphological reconstruction is: 
(a) to learn as much as possible about the typology of morphological change; and (b) to study 
accounts of reconstruction in various languages of the world.
Different kinds of morphological changes can be expected in different languages. For 
example, languages that have undergone little phonological change are unlikely to provide 
instances of the morphologisation of phonology (section 7.2). Languages without in ectional 
classes will not display cross-paradigmatic in uences (section 9). Languages without much 
morphophonemic alternation will manifest fewer intra-paradigmatic extensions and levellings 
(section 8). The richer the morphological system, the more scope there is for all kinds of 
morphological change.
