<!-- page 326 -->

308
13
Functional syntax and 
language change
Zygmunt Frajzyngier
1 The general notions of functional syntax and the present approach1
1.1 General assumptions of functional syntax approaches  
The term ‘functional syntax’ is a cover term for a large number of approaches that differ 
signi cantly from each other in their theoretical assumptions and methodologies. The 
common thread shared by these approaches is the belief that the form (‘syntax’) and the 
function/meaning, rather than being autonomous entities, are linked together. The differences 
among various functional approaches have to do with what kind of linkage exists between the 
forms and functions, what factors determine the forms, and how to explain similarities and 
differences among languages. Many functional approaches use cross-linguistic data, 
discourse and conversational properties, language acquisition and language use to explain 
facts of languages, and many invoke language-external factors to explain language structure. 
Because the listing of functional approaches and their characteristics would take up too much 
space in this short chapter, the reader is referred to the discussion in DeLancey (2001).
Within the various functional approaches, the prevalent factors affecting language change 
are the same factors that have been invoked in the study of language change throughout 
linguistic tradition and are by no means con ned to functional approaches: phonetic and 
phonological changes that trigger morphological changes, which in turn are contributing 
factors to changes in phrase and clause structures; reanalysis of the components of clause 
structure; frequency of use; word order changes; borrowing; and other phenomena involved 
in language contact. Most functional approaches pay a great deal of attention to 
grammaticalisation (cf. early ideas proposed by von Humboldt; modern studies, starting with 
Kuryowicz 1965; Samuels 1972, followed by Lehmann 1982; Heine, Claudi, and 
Hünnemeyer 1991; Heine and Kuteva 2002; papers in Traugott and Heine 1991; Hopper and 
Traugott 1993; Narrog and Heine 2012). As described below, grammaticalisation is the main 
cause of an increase in the frequency of use of a linguistic form. This approach to frequency 
and grammaticalisation is opposite to the one most often proposed, where the increase in 
frequency leads to grammaticalisation. 
In what follows, I present a functionalist model that makes speci c claims about the 
connection between semantics and syntax. Some components of this model have been 
presented in Frajzyngier and Shay (2003), Frajzyngier (2004, 2010a,b). The crucial 
component of the model, viz. the notion of the grammaticalised meaning, is introduced in

---

<!-- page 327 -->

Functional syntax and language change 
309
Frajzyngier (2013). The historical application of the model is used here for the  rst time. 
The advantage of the approach is that it is capable of explaining a large number of 
similarities and differences across languages. It is also useful for explaining syntactic 
changes. In describing syntactic change, the model also takes into consideration two types 
of factors: language-internal factors, i.e. changes that are not provoked by language contact 
or by political or cultural interferences in language structure, and language-external factors, 
that is changes that are initiated through language contact or through political or cultural 
interference.
1.2 Basic elements of the proposed model
Within the proposed version of functional syntax, the term ‘syntax’ is understood as the 
totality of coding means in a given language and the interactions among those means. The 
term ‘function’ refers to the role of the coding means in the coding of a semantic or pragmatic 
function encoded in the grammatical system of the language. At any given time, each 
language codes a  nite number of functions. The number and types of functions encoded 
differs from one language to another. Any overlaps in the types of functions encoded may be 
due to the commonality of human perceptions, reactions to these perceptions, and functions 
required by interactions within the community of speakers, such as asking questions, 
expressing wishes and commands, exchanging information etc., and perhaps, depending on 
future research  ndings, the human neurological make-up. Such commonalities, however, do 
not in any way imply commonalities among the speci c functions or the forms by which 
these functions are realised. In the current state of knowledge, they also do not imply the use 
of a speci c methodology in the synchronic or diachronic study of language.
The role of the set of formal means in a given language is to code the functions 
grammaticalised in the language and to ensure the principle of functional transparency. The 
principle of functional transparency states that the role of every element in the utterance must 
be transparent with respect to the functions encoded in the given language. The principle of 
functional transparency is unrelated to the listener’s need to understand the reference of the 
utterance in a non-linguistic reality. Thus, when reading a text about a  eld that is new to us, 
we ask ourselves about the reference of particular nouns or verbs, not about the roles of 
components in the utterance. Thus, in the following sentence, the understanding of the terms 
‘standard theory’, ‘quasar’, ‘black hole’, ‘black hole engine’, and ‘supermassive black hole 
engine’ all require some familiarity with astronomy and physics. Understanding the roles of 
these elements within the utterance does not require familiarity with physics, astronomy, or 
linguistics. The roles of the elements within the utterance are marked by linear orders, 
prepositions, subordinators, lexical categories, and the copula:
(1) The standard theory is that quasars turn on when there is matter to feed their supermassive 
black hole engines at the center and turn off when there is no longer fuel for the black 
hole.
(http://csep10.phys.utk.edu/astr162/lect/active/quasars.html)
Grammaticalised functions, the principle of functional transparency, and the formal means to 
encode them constitute the necessary components for explaining the form and function of an 
utterance and so are the necessary basis for the explanation of language change. Speakers 
start with the grammaticalised function that they want to express. They then choose the 
lexical items with which to express them. This is followed by the choice of other formal

---

<!-- page 328 -->

Zygmunt Frajzyngier
310
means that code the grammaticalised function in conjunction with the lexical items chosen. 
If the principle of functional transparency may be violated, the speaker needs to introduce 
other formal means to assure that the principle of functional transparency is not violated. 
What follows is a brief outline of the principles of the approach, followed by sections 
describing various domains of language change from the perspective of the proposed 
approach.
2 The coding means
The following sections brie y describe the coding means that have been discovered in various 
languages.
2.1 Lexical categories and associated derivational morphology 
Languages differ in the types of lexical categories they have. The presence or absence of a 
given lexical category has important consequences for the form of the utterance. For example, 
in languages that do not have the category adjective, the function of attributive modi cation 
may be encoded by a form similar to or identical with the relative clause. This is the case in 
Mupun (Chadic: Frajzyngier 1993), where the relative clause requires the presence of a 
subject marker in addition to the relative clause marker, while the modifying construction, 
which also has the relative clause marker, does not have the subject marker.
2.2 Free grammatical morphemes 
This class includes determiners, articles, complementisers, subordinators, conjunctions, 
adpositions, and perhaps other markers. Adpositions have been known to emerge when a 
language loses other coding means and/or to ensure the principle of functional transparency. 
Thus in English, the addition of a noun phrase that is neither the subject nor the object to any 
construction other than the ditransitive construction requires the use of a preposition. In Indo-
European languages the emergence of adpositions as a coding means for grammatical 
relations is linked with the loss of the in ectional coding of case (Bourciez 1967: 87; 
Frajzyngier 2004). Adpositions may be a product of the evolution of serial verb constructions 
and/or of the use of nouns as grammatical markers. Complementisers are a product of the 
further grammaticalisation of verbs, nouns, determiners, and adpositions. The emergence of 
articles from determiners is associated with the loss of in ectional coding of gender and with 
the further grammaticalisation of topicalisation markers. For the lexical sources of free and 
bound grammatical markers, see Lehmann (1982) and Heine and Kuteva (2002). 
2.3 Linear orders
The fundamental difference between the proposed model and the existing formal or functional 
models is that linear orders are treated here as a coding means. The term ‘linear orders’ 
subsumes coding by the position of one element with respect to some reference point; by the 
relative linear order of two constituents; by changes to the default order; and by linear 
precedence, i.e. coding the precedence of one constituent over others in a sequence of 
numerous constituents. A given language may also have a default linear order, i.e. an order 
that is not in itself a coding means. Any synchronic variation in the default linear order is, 
however, a coding means. This is the case with some verb-initial languages, such as Classical

---

<!-- page 329 -->

Functional syntax and language change 
311
Arabic and Hdi (Central Chadic: Frajzyngier with Shay 2002), and some verb- nal languages, 
such as Japanese and Korean. In these languages, where the verb occupies clause-initial or 
clause- nal position, the position of the verb is not a coding means for grammatical or 
semantic relations between the verb and noun phrases. Whether it is a coding means for 
information structure largely depends on how one analyses the unmarked value within the 
system of marking information structure. Placing a noun before the verb in Hdi or after the 
verb in Japanese is a means of coding focus. A language may have several types of linear 
orders as a coding means at any given time. For the functions of linear order, see Frajzyngier 
(2011).
Within the proposed approach, a question of the type ‘What was the linear order in proto-
language X?’, i.e. a question about linear order that does not include a question about its 
function, has no heuristic value. A question of the type ‘What were the linear orders in proto-
language X, and what functions did they perform?’ can reveal the forms, the functions, and 
their relationships.
2.4 Inﬂ ectional morphology
In ectional morphology may apply to all lexical categories, e.g. nouns, verbs, adjectives, and 
numerals, and also to free grammatical morphemes such as complementisers, subordinators, 
conjunctions, and prepositions. In ectional morphology with respect to nouns, pronouns, 
adjectives, numerals, and determiners includes case distinctions; construct or absolute state 
distinctions in Semitic languages, where the construct state codes the head in the modifying 
construction; and a quite distinct type, exhibited in Berber, where one state is the default state 
and the other narrows the scope of an antecedent in the same clause (for details, see Mettouchi 
and Frajzyngier 2013). In ectional morphology on verbs can code grammatical and semantic 
relations within the proposition, number, directionality, point of view, voice, aspect, tense, 
modality, and a host of other functions. In ectional morphology on all lexical and grammatical 
categories also includes the coding of the constituent structure of the utterance and the 
internal structure of constituents (Frajzyngier 2012a).
2.5 Serial verb constructions
Serial verb constructions, the coding means which is known in hundreds of languages of 
West and Central Africa, Southeast Asia, and parts of Australia and South America2 code a 
variety of functions, including directionality, spatial orientation, semantic relations between 
the predicate and the noun phrases, manner, and the resultative function. It is often the case 
that one of the components of the serial verb construction evolves into an adposition. The 
functions of serial verb constructions have been well known for almost a hundred years now. 
For a collection of recent papers, see Aikhenvald and Dixon (2006).
2.6 Prosodic and phonological means 
The phonological means of coding include tone, intonation, stress, and vowel reduction. The 
prosodic means may include the preceding means and other means used to set prosodic 
boundaries. Intonation, tone, and stress are often used to code interrogative and deontic 
modalities and contrastive focus. Vowel reduction can code phrase-internal position 
(Frajzyngier 2012b).

---

<!-- page 330 -->

Zygmunt Frajzyngier
312
Prosodic means serve a variety of functions in the setting of syntactic units, which do not 
necessarily correspond to the categories of sentence or clause (Mithun in press). Prosodic 
means and the in ectional marking of the internal structure of the utterance only partially 
overlap. Prosodic means by themselves may carry a semantic function, while the in ectional 
marking of the internal structure of the utterance may not carry such a function.
3 Functions of the coding means
Each language codes its own set of functions. It is therefore quite reasonable to expect that 
languages differ signi cantly in the functions they have grammaticalised, i.e. encoded in the 
grammatical system of the language. Nevertheless, given common human cognitive abilities, 
needs, and social interactions, one can expect some overlap at the level of functional domains. 
Thus many languages have the functional domains of tense, aspect, and modality; information 
structure, including topicalisation, focus, and backgrounding; reference systems, including 
deixis and anaphora; and the broadly understood domain of semantic relations between 
predicates and noun phrases (if languages code semantic relations between predicates and 
NPs). The internal structures of each domain differ signi cantly across languages. Thus some 
languages may have two or three aspects, while others may have  ve or six. The role of the 
coding means is: (1) to ensure the realisation of the functions grammaticalised in the language; 
and (2) to satisfy the principle of functional transparency, which states that the role of every 
element in the utterance must be transparent with respect to the functions grammaticalised in 
the given language. The Principle of Functional Transparency explains the need for many 
grammatical markers that do not have a referent in either the cognitive system of the speaker 
or the external world; for example, the preverbal preposition to, which marks the in nitive in 
English, and the complementiser e in Polish, whose only function is to separate arguments 
in the complex sentence (Frajzyngier 1996). For an extended discussion of the principle of 
functional transparency, see Frajzyngier and Shay (2003) and Frajzyngier (2004). 
4 Causes of syntactic change
There are two types of syntactic change: one is a change in the coding means, and the other 
is a change in the functions coded. The two changes may be linked by a cause–effect 
relationship going in either direction, viz. from form to function or from function to form. 
The two changes may also be independent, in the sense that a change in form does not have 
to trigger a change in function, and a change in function does not have to trigger a change in 
form. Changes in either form or function may have language-internal or language-external 
causes.
4.1 Language-internal causes of syntactic change
Language-internal changes affecting coding means may have their source in low-level 
phonetic changes that in themselves are neither changes in coding means nor changes in 
function. Thus, the emergence of tone from the loss of  nal voiced and voiceless consonants, 
as occurred in Vietnamese (Haudricourt 1954, con rmed by numerous subsequent studies), 
does not yet constitute a change in the coding means. When tone becomes a grammatical 
marker, that constitutes a grammatical change, in this case an addition, to the set of coding 
means in the language.

---

<!-- page 331 -->

Functional syntax and language change 
313
A change in the stress pattern of a language may leave some syllables unstressed, which 
may lead to the loss of unstressed vowels and consonants. The loss of vowels is unrelated to 
the speaker/listener interaction. This is what happened in the transition from Latin to modern 
French, when the vowels and consonants in unstressed  nal syllables were deleted. The 
language in this way lost indicators of gender and case-marking on nouns (Bourciez 1967: 
672ff) and indicators of person on verbs with singular subjects. The markers of plural subject 
on the verb were not lost because they consisted of more complex phonological structures.
4.2 Change in the coding means
Three types of changes in the coding means affect the form of the utterance: the loss of a 
coding means, the addition of a coding means, and a change in the functions of coding means, 
including the functions of linear orders. An adposition that becomes a case marker, an 
independent pronoun that becomes a person marker on the verb, an adposition that becomes 
a verbal extension are examples of changes in coding means. The loss of in ectional marking 
of person on verbs in English had as its outcome the obligatory presence of subject pronouns 
or nominal subjects in the non-imperative mood. The addition, through borrowing and 
calquing, of counter-expectation markers and coordinated conjunctions corresponding to 
‘but’ and ‘and’ in many Chadic languages has resulted in the emergence of two functions that 
previously were not differentiated from other functions, namely the coordinated clause and 
the counter-expectation clause (Frajzyngier 1996). 
The causal connection between the loss of case marking and changes in linear order 
remains a controversial issue. One approach postulates that the loss of case marking triggers 
changes in the functions of linear order, whereby linear order, usually SVO, assumes the 
function of the coding of grammatical relations (for a slightly different formulation, see 
Vennemann 1974). This presumably is what happened in Bulgarian, English, and French, 
and many other languages. The other approach postulates that in French and English the 
linear order was increasingly becoming SVO making the case marking on nouns redundant. 
This is what Bourciez (1967: 688) postulates as the cause of the loss of in ectional case 
marking in French. Similar claims have been made for English. The controversy can be 
resolved as follows. If the role of case marking were only to distinguish subject and object, 
one could take the increased frequency of the SVO word order to be the cause of the loss of 
case marking. But that would account only for the loss of two cases, viz. nominative and 
accusative. However, case marking in Indo-European languages marked a large number of 
semantic relations. Loss of all case markers would not have been caused by the shift to SVO 
order as a coding means. And yet, the other case marking disappeared as well. An additional 
argument for the shift to SVO as a coding means for grammatical relations is provided by the 
fact that the loss of in ectional markers on nouns in English is accompanied by the loss of 
in ectional marking of person and number on verbs, both of these changes were triggered by 
the weakening of  nal syllables. Hence, the change to SVO order as a coding means for 
grammatical relations might have had two independent causes: the loss of nominal in ection 
for case and the loss of verbal in ection for person and number. These changes, and 
accompanying changes in the functions of linear order, were gradual and simultaneous rather 
than sequential. Although such a scenario in its totality is not explicitly stated in the literature, 
there are suf cient facts to support it. For example, Old English had case in ection on nouns 
and person and number in ection on verbs (see Campbell 1959; Quirk and Wrenn 1955; 
Curme 1931: 3 for explicit statements about case). Linear order was available to code a 
variety of information structure functions.

---

<!-- page 332 -->

Zygmunt Frajzyngier
314
4.3 Compensatory change
The loss of a coding means may result in the loss of a function. Such a loss may trigger 
compensatory changes, whereby another coding means emerges that ensures continuation of 
some elements of the function coded by the forms that were lost, or an already existing coding 
means assumes the coding of the endangered function. Examples of compensatory changes 
include: the obligatory use of pronouns in the absence of nominal subjects to code the subject 
in English (compensation for the loss of person in ection on the verb); the use of the position 
after the verb to code the object in English (compensation for the loss of the accusative case 
marker); and the use of the structure V NP NP and prepositions to code the benefactive 
relationship and indirect object relationships (compensation for the loss of the dative case 
marker). If one assumes that the case system of Classical Arabic represents the earlier stage 
(the issue is debatable; see Owens 1998), then the linear order SVO in contemporary spoken 
Arabic varieties would constitute compensatory change. 
Compensatory change seldom involves just the deployment of another coding means to 
code the same function. Compensatory change is usually accompanied by a broadening or a 
narrowing of the function. Here is an example: the loss of dative case marking in English 
resulted in replacing the indirect object predication, which is still found in a number of Indo-
European languages, with several other types of predication. One of these is the benefactive 
predication of the type V NP NP, usually referred to as the ‘ditransitive construction’. Its 
function is narrower than that of the indirect object predication, as coded by dative case 
marking. The other predications that replaced indirect object predications are marked by the 
prepositions ‘for’ and ‘to’, each of which codes a considerably broader function than the 
function coded by the erstwhile dative case:
(2) Do you feel that, Joel, as a photographer, there’s a certain type of image that’s kind of 
been ruined for you by that type of perfect image or perfect scenery?
(COCA)
4.4 Non-reductive change in the coding means
Non-reductive change in the formal means is a change that does not involve the loss of a 
coding means but rather a change in the form or the properties of the formal means. This type 
of change is illustrated by a change in linear order whereby the default linear order is changed 
and becomes a coding means. This is what most likely happened in many Chadic languages. 
Some Central Chadic languages are verb-initial. These languages have a rich in ectional 
system on the verb and a few prepositions that mark the grammatical and semantic relationship 
of the noun that follows the verb. Some Chadic languages are verb-initial in the completive 
aspect but subject-initial in the imperfective aspect. These languages use in ection on the 
verb to code the grammatical roles of arguments in both aspects. Many other Chadic languages 
use linear order, viz. SVO, to code the grammatical relations of subject and object. While 
there are several synchronic means that code a variety of pragmatic functions, and a few 
tenses that create noun-initial structures, there is not a single synchronic process that creates 
a verb-initial structure. Therefore, the most likely scenario is that many languages have 
undergone a change from the non-coding verb-initial position to the subject- and object-
coding SVO structure. One of the functions that involves fronting of the noun phrase is 
topicalisation. It is likely that, over time, the topicalisation structure in some languages lost 
its erstwhile function and was reinterpreted as a pragmatically neutral clause. For a description 
of the processes involved, see Frajzyngier (1983).

---

<!-- page 333 -->

Functional syntax and language change 
315
4.5 Loss of function
The loss of a coding means does not necessarily result in compensatory change, but may 
result in the loss of a function. The narrowing of the function of the genitive case in English 
to express only the relationships between nouns has resulted in the loss of a grammatical 
means to express the partitive or unaffected object relationship, which is still coded by the 
genitive case in a number of Indo-European languages. The use of linear order to code the 
grammatical relations subject and object in English resulted in the loss of a grammatical 
means to mark functions that used to be marked by different linear orders. We do not know 
for sure what these functions were, but judging by the varieties of functions coded by linear 
orders in Latin (Marouzeau 1922–1953) and other languages that have rich in ection on all 
lexical categories, there must have been a rather large number of them, including the coding 
of information structure, such as new/old information; topicalisation; focus; and possibly 
other functions.
5 Functional reanalysis
An existing formal means may be deployed to code a new function. Functional reanalysis 
may be a language-internal cause for the emergence of a new function. Thus, Givón (1976) 
and Shibatani (1991) propose grammaticalisation of topics into subjects as being a product of 
reanalysis. Frajzyngier (2012b) proposes grammaticalisation of the focus markers in Hebrew 
and Hdi (Chadic) into object markers.
6 Frequency of use 
Frequency of use has been often taken as a cause of language change and even one of the 
determining factors of language structure (Bybee 2007). The attribution of change to 
frequency appears to be taking the secondary rather than the primary cause into consideration. 
The reason for the given form to be used more frequently than others is that it is used as a 
coding means for some grammatical function. The use of verbs ‘come’ and ‘go’ to code a 
variety of functions, including past and future tenses (Bybee et al. 1994) and sequential 
clauses (Frajzyngier 2005), leads to the increased use of these verbs in comparison to other 
verbs. Frequency of use of lexical items to code grammatical functions leads to the emergence 
of free grammatical morphemes. The frequency of use also leads to predictability. 
Predictability leads to the reduction of phonological material, which leads to af xation. This 
is what happened in many Chadic languages, where equivalents of the verbs ‘to go’ and ‘to 
come’ started as markers of movement away from the place of speech and toward the place 
of speech and ended up as andative and ventive extensions to the verb (Frajzyngier 1987a). 
The use of lexical items to code a grammatical function is the  rst step in grammaticalisation 
from lexical items to grammatical morphemes.
7 Principle of indirect means as a language-internal cause of change
One of the causes of language change is the principle of indirect means. This principle, 
whose nature is functional, has an impact on both the forms and the functions of utterance. 
The principle states that indirect, rather than direct, means of coding are used in certain 
functional domains. Direct means are means that have been grammaticalised to code a given 
function. Direct means are those that are used for the function for which they have been

---

<!-- page 334 -->

Zygmunt Frajzyngier
316
grammaticalised, while indirect means are those that are used for functions other than those 
for which they have been grammaticalised. The principle of indirect means, whose scope is 
the language structure, is motivated by social factors. The domains in which the principle of 
indirect means is operational include personal interactions between the speaker and the 
listener, and discourse about procreation and sex, death, deities, and other social issues, 
which may be different in different societies. These are not functional domains of the 
grammar of the language but rather domains in the social life of a given society. The evidence 
for the existence and the role of the principle of indirect means is provided by the fact that 
there are many more grammaticalised means used in the grammatical and social domains 
where the principle operates than in the domains where it does not. Thus, in the domain of 
deontic modality, a domain that always involves relations between the speaker and the 
listener or some other participant, languages use many more means of coding than they do in 
domains that do not involve interpersonal relations. To express a wish, English uses not only 
the imperative mood but also a number of modal auxiliaries such as must, should, ought to, 
need to, may, will, shall, as well as the interrogative modality. The reason for the abundance 
of coding means in a given domain is that once a coding means is perceived as a direct means, 
another means is deployed to code the same function. Compare the deontic mood in English 
to the negative indicative modality, which does not involve interpersonal relationships and 
where the number of coding means is limited to just a few formal means. Another domain 
where the principle of indirect means operates is in the reference system, especially among 
 rst-, second- and third-person pronouns, where the change of forms is rather frequent. For 
the operation of the principle of indirect means, see Frajzyngier and Jirsa (2006).
8 Language-external causes of syntactic change
8.1 Political and social factors
Language-external causes of syntactic change include: language contact, social and political 
interferences in language structure (Hagège 1993), and the least-studied area, the re ection 
of social and sometimes historical factors in language structure (see also Michael, this 
volume). In contemporary English, the in uence of social and political movements on syntax 
can be seen in the replacement of the pronouns ‘he’ and ‘him’ by ‘gender-neutral’ expressions 
such as ‘they’ and ‘them’, ‘he/she’, etc. In addition to Hagège (1993), the reader interested in 
political factors may also consult works dealing with attempts to modify languages in 
twentieth-century ideologies (see de Saussure and Schulz 2005). Most discussion of the 
issues of language and ideology has the lexicon in its scope.
8.2 Language contact
Language contact has been recognised as a powerful source of language change throughout 
the history of linguistics, regardless of the linguist’s theoretical orientation (see Lucas, this 
volume). With respect to functional approaches to language change, language contact affects 
change in three ways: direct change in the formal means; direct change in the functions coded 
by language; and indirect change resulting from changes in either the formal means or 
functions, each of which can in turn cause a change in the other component. With respect to 
formal means, one of the most frequent effects of language contact is the borrowing of 
grammatical markers, where the borrowing may involve both the form and the function or 
just the function (see papers in Matras and Sakel 2007). The frequent targets of borrowing are

---

<!-- page 335 -->

Functional syntax and language change 
317
markers that do not exist in the language that borrows them, on the principle that you borrow 
what you don’t have. Among the grammatical markers that are frequently borrowed are 
clausal coordinated conjunctions and subordinators. An example of the borrowing of form 
and function at the same time is the borrowing, in many Chadic languages, of the counter-
expectation marker a(m)ma (with different tones), corresponding to ‘but’, either directly 
from Arabic or via Fula or Hausa. An example of calquing is provided by the use in a few 
Chadic languages of the associative preposition or nominal coordinating conjunction as a 
clausal coordinating conjunction, thereby copying the pattern from French or English, where 
the nominal and clausal coordinating conjunctions are identical.
Adoption of a new linear order through language contact has been postulated for Amharic, 
Tigrinya and some other Ethio-Semitic languages, in early comparative Semitic studies. 
Many Semitic languages are verb-initial. The classical Ethio-Semitic language Geez is verb-
initial as well. All Cushitic and Omotic languages with which Ethio-Semitic languages are in 
contact are verb- nal. It is reasonable to postulate that Amharic and Tigrinya underwent a 
change from verb-initial to verb- nal order as a result of language contact. It is important to 
note that this change did not involve a change in the function of the linear order, as both verb-
initial and verb- nal are default positions of the verb rather than a coding means in Semitic 
languages. It is also likely that language contact was a contributing factor in the shift from 
verb-initial to subject-initial order in many Chadic languages.
One of the most striking effects of language contact is the emergence of pidgins and 
creoles (Thomason and Kaufman 1988). These languages emerge when adult speakers start 
speaking a language other than their native language. McWhorter, in his many writings (see 
McWhorter 2011), proposes that pidgins and creoles have simpler grammars than their 
lexi er languages, i.e. the languages that provided the bulk of the lexicon and some elements 
of grammar. It appears that most creolists have a different view on this issue (see papers in 
Plag 2003 and multiple rebuttals in McWhorter 2011). Whether indeed the creole grammars 
are simpler than grammars of non-creole languages could potentially be demonstrated by 
comparing all the coding means and all the functions encoded in the grammatical systems of 
both types of languages.
Extensive language contact may have yet a different, so far undescribed, effect on language 
change. In a situation of language contact, a function in one language can fall into disuse if 
another language in contact doesn’t have such a function in its grammatical system. Here is 
an illustration. Mina (Central Chadic) has a locative predication, whose distinct characteristic 
is that if the predicate is not inherently locative, the locative predication requires the use of 
the predicator á (see Frajzyngier et al. 2005). However, I have heard multilingual speakers of 
Mina omit the locative predicator. Their locative predications have thus begun to rely only on 
the use of the locative preposition, as is done in French.
Language contact has also been considered to be one way for new functions to emerge in 
a language (Heine and Kuteva 2005). The idea behind this hypothesis is that a language can 
acquire a grammatical function from another language and code it by its own means.
9 Generalisations re: syntactic change
9.1 Linguistic cycle
An early generalisation with respect to change concerned the existence of cyclical changes 
(Hodge 1970, and more recently van Gelderen 2011). A frequent example of the cycle in 
language change is when a lexical item becomes grammaticalised, then loses its independence,

---

<!-- page 336 -->

Zygmunt Frajzyngier
318
then becomes an af x, then undergoes further phonological reduction, and eventually 
disappears, thus creating a need for a new means to code the function whose markers have 
disappeared. The cyclic nature of change has been con rmed by subsequent research.
9.2 Grammaticalisation within a domain
Linear order and tone sometimes emerge as coding means for different functions within the 
same domain. Thus, if SV order marks the af rmative modality, VS order may emerge as a 
means to mark interrogative modality, as is the case in English and a number of other Indo-
European languages. Similarly, if the default tone, whether low or high, is associated with the 
present tense, use of the opposite tone is likely to emerge as a coding means for another tense 
(Frajzyngier 2010a).
There is evidence that the default linear order, i.e. the linear order that carries no coding 
function, can change into another default linear order. This is what happened in some Ethio-
Semitic languages, most likely through contact with Cushitic and Omotic languages, all of 
which have verbs in clause- nal position. The default linear order can also change into a 
linear order as a coding means. This is what most probably happened in some Chadic 
languages, where the default verb-initial order changed to the order SVO as a coding means 
for grammatical relations.
One cannot exclude, on theoretical grounds, a change from linear order as a coding means 
to default linear order, i.e. to a linear order that has no coding function. A possible scenario 
for such a change, a change that has not happened yet, is illustrated here on Songhay (Nilo-
Saharan). In the pragmatically neutral clause, grammatical relations in Songhay are coded by 
the position before the verb (subject) and the position after the verb (object). Topicalisation 
of the object requires the object to be placed in the position preceding the verb but following 
the subject. This would result in the NP NP V structure, a structure in which the roles of the 
noun phrases would be marked solely by the relative order of the two NPs. Because of the 
potential ambiguity with respect to the roles of the two NPs, placement of the object before 
the verb also requires the use of the preposition n, resulting in the structure NP n-NP V. 
Although at present both NP V NP and NP n-NP V structures occur in the language, it is 
possible that future generations of Songhay speakers may take the structure NP n-NP V to 
represent the pragmatically neutral clause.3
10 Tools for the study of syntactic change
In the present section I discuss general theoretical tools for the study of language change and 
one very speci c technical tool.
10.1 Language-internal tool
As in all historical studies, the existence of old texts provides the most viable starting point 
for the description of syntactic change. Old texts constitute incomplete data, as most often 
they do not contain information on prosodic characteristics, intonation, and pauses. In any 
case, the texts are merely data that must be interpreted. The way they are interpreted depends 
very much on what kind of assumptions one brings to the analysis and what kind of questions 
one asks. The texts do provide an opportunity to establish a list of the coding means, minus 
prosodic means, that existed in the language at the time the texts were produced. Armed with 
such a list, the linguist can then establish what functions were coded by what means and

---

<!-- page 337 -->

Functional syntax and language change 
319
establish a list of the functions coded in the language at the time the texts were produced. The 
list of forms and functions in contemporary language compared with the list of forms and 
functions at the time the texts were produced allows one to establish a reasonable list of 
differences between the two periods and thereby make conjectures about the syntactic 
changes that must have taken place. 
A useful tool for the study of language change is the creation of a list of constructions that 
are no longer used. To the best of my knowledge, there is only one such list so far, the one 
created by Klemensiewicz’s team in Cracow in the early 1960s. They have collected, 
described, and analysed constructions used in Polish from the very beginning of written 
records, constructions that are no longer used in contemporary Polish (see Klemensiewicz et 
al. 1966). Such a list, with examples, constitutes an invaluable tool for deciding which 
functions used to be coded, which functions are coded by new means, and which functions 
have been lost. If we had such tools for other languages, one could base generalisations about 
syntactic change on solid empirical grounds.
10.2 Comparative Method
When old texts are not available, the Comparative Method should be applied. Although 
application of the Comparative Method to syntactic reconstruction is considered by some to 
be problematic (see chapters by Weiss, Hale and Barðdal, this volume), there are no theoretical 
or methodological reasons why the Comparative Method should not be used (Frajzyngier 
1987b). The proposed model of the relationship between functions and the forms coding 
them lends itself rather well to the application of the Comparative Method. In applying the 
Comparative Method to syntax, one has to take into consideration both forms and functions 
across related languages. In this way one can reconstruct what forms existed in the proto-
language, what functions existed in the proto-language, and which forms coded which 
functions. Having reconstructed such a system for the proto-language, one can use it to 
describe the syntactic changes that contemporary languages have undergone. More 
importantly, one can explain why certain syntactic changes have taken place and, conversely, 
why certain constructions did not emerge.
Here are two examples of the application of the Comparative Method to the study of 
syntactic change. In contemporary English, in the absence of nominal subject, a pronominal 
subject must be used. The function of pronominal subjects in contemporary English is quite 
different from the functions of pronominal subjects in Latin or Polish. Comparative study 
indicates that in Proto-Indo-European, the verb carried information about the person of the 
subject  (Kuryowicz 1964: 148ff). Verbs in contemporary English are not marked for person 
and number, other than in the third-person singular present. But English has subject pronouns. 
One can thus draw a causal connection between the absence of person and number coding on 
the verb and the presence of subject pronouns. The causality stems from two facts:  rst, the 
coding of the person of the subject is one of the grammaticalised functions in the language. 
Second, once the markers of such coding start to disappear, the principle of the functional 
transparency is violated. Therefore, compensatory changes emerge. The results of the 
Comparative Method converge with the results obtained through the analysis of old texts. In 
Beowulf, when the verb is marked for person and number, the subject pronoun doesn’t have 
to appear even if there is no coreferential subject in the preceding clause.
Here is another example. In many Chadic languages, verbs display a rich in ectional 
system that includes verbal extensions coding the manner, the directionality, the spatial 
orientation, the point of view, and a host of other functions. Some West Chadic languages,

---

<!-- page 338 -->

Zygmunt Frajzyngier
320
e.g. Mupun (Frajzyngier 1993), do not have verbal extensions. It has been demonstrated that 
many verbal extensions come from erstwhile verbs that followed the main verb to form a 
serial verb construction. In Mupun, the verbs that code the semantic functions of manner and 
directionality precede rather than follow the main verb, hence the verbal extension could not 
emerge (Frajzyngier 1987a). There are no pre xed extensions in Chadic languages.
10.3 Grammaticalisation 
Grammaticalisation, as a study of the emergence of new coding means and new functions 
regardless of the sources from which they emerge (Frajzyngier 2008), is useful for detecting 
syntactic change. If the coding means are transparent enough with respect to the sources from 
which they emerged, they provide a window into a previous state of the language. For 
example, if adpositions are still phonologically similar to body-part terms, and if there are 
morphological markers of relations between two nouns, as is the case in many African and 
other languages, one can deduce that there existed a construction with the body-part term in 
a previous stage of the language. If the de dicto complementiser is phonologically similar to 
a verb of saying, this indicates that at some earlier stage the complements of the verb of 
saying were preceded by the verb ‘say’ (Frajzyngier 1996). If the preposition marking the 
benefactive argument is similar to the verb ‘to give’, this indicates that the erstwhile 
construction for coding the benefactive argument had the form of a serial verb construction 
with the verb ‘to give’ being the second verb in the series (Frajzyngier 2001). 
Grammaticalisation thus provides a useful tool for internal reconstruction.
10.4 Linguistic typology
Typological studies, where both forms and functions are compared across unrelated languages, 
can be useful for distinguishing between language-universal correlations and family-particular 
products of language change. In this way, linguistic typology may give an indication as to 
which areas of syntax need to be explored with respect to their history within the language or 
the family. Unfortunately, there are not enough typological studies of this type.
11 Unsolved questions in historical functional syntax
The present section addresses only the major unsolved questions pertaining to the theory of 
language change rather than to particular language families or particular languages. 
11.1 How did default linear orders come about?
Most linguistic theories take the linear order of major categories for granted. Given, however, 
that linear orders can serve as a coding means, it is important to  nd out why so many 
languages have the verb in clause-initial or clause- nal position, depriving themselves of one 
coding position.
11.2 Why have so many languages grammaticalised the categories subject and 
object?
This is perhaps a non-question for adherents of theories that postulate the universality of the 
categories subject and object. Within the functional approach this is a non-trivial question,

---

<!-- page 339 -->

Functional syntax and language change 
321
given that not all languages have the identi able categories subject or object. There are 
languages that have grammaticalised agent and patient instead of subject or object. Mithun 
(1999: 213) states that such is the case in Iroquoian, Muskogean, Caddoan, Siouan, and 
Pomoan families, among others. There are languages that have grammaticalised subject but 
not object (see Frajzyngier and Munkaila 2004 with respect to Hausa). Interestingly, it 
appears that there are no languages that have grammaticalised object but not subject. One 
possibility is that the existence of the categories subject and object is the effect of speci c 
coding means in a given language that favour the binary distinction (Frajzyngier and Shay 
2003). The other possibility may lie in a language-speci c limitation on the number of noun 
phrases that may occur in the clause in natural discourse.
11.3 Are there any preferences with respect to which functions are preserved and 
which functions can be lost?
There is considerable evidence that in the process of language change some functions are 
more vulnerable than others. This is the case with the coding of dual number, which has 
disappeared in many Indo-European languages. This doesn’t mean that the category dual is 
vulnerable in other languages. The question that needs to be explored is which functions are 
more likely to be lost and which are more likely to persist over time. One would expect some 
hierarchy of functions with respect to their persistence. One possible factor affecting the 
persistence of the function is the scope of the function. The term ‘scope’ here refers to how 
many constituents of the utterance are affected by the function. The larger the scope of the 
function, the less likely it is to disappear. Thus the coding of the system of grammatical 
relations is less likely to disappear than the coding of a speci c semantic relation, e.g. the 
partitive/unaffected second argument in some Western Indo-European languages (see also 
Wichmann, this volume).
12 Contribution of historical functional syntax to the study of 
language change
The basic theoretical contribution of the proposed approach to language change is that it can 
explore the limits of language-internal cause/effect relationships in language change. 
Contributions in other areas are listed below.
12.1 Differences among related languages
The proposed model provides explanations for why related languages may differ in the 
functions they code and in the forms used to code those functions. The default assumption 
with respect to genetically related languages is that they continue to use the forms and 
functions of the proto-language. For all forms and functions that differ from those in related 
languages, one must seek an explanation. One type of explanation is that if the form coding 
a given function is lost, either the function is also lost or another form is grammaticalised to 
code the function, or at least some components of the function. The same explanation can be 
used to account for changes in the function. If a language has the function that was not 
present in the proto-language, one can attribute this new function to language-internal causes, 
such as grammaticalisation within a domain, or to language-external causes, such as adopting 
a function from another language.

---

<!-- page 340 -->

Zygmunt Frajzyngier
322
12.2 Explanation of why unrelated languages display non-universal similarities 
of form
Once one has built a hierarchy of functions indicating which functions are more often and 
which are less often encoded in grammatical systems, one of the desirable goals of linguistic 
typology is to explain how two unrelated languages are similar, and also the reasons for this 
similarity. If the languages have the same formal means, e.g. the same lexical categories, 
in ectional coding means on nouns and other lexical categories, it is expected that the same 
domains will be coded in a similar manner. If the two languages have different formal means, 
one can expect that similar or identical functions will have quite different realisations.
It is quite natural that similar forms across languages will code quite different functions. 
Thus the structure SV in English codes af rmative modality in the pragmatically neutral 
clause. The structure SV in Hdi (Central Chadic: Frajzyngier with Shay 2002) or Kabyle 
(Berber: Mettouchi 2008) codes topicalisation of the subject. This demonstrates that in 
comparing languages one must compare the totality of grammatical systems rather than 
individual sentences or aspects of grammar.
Grammatical morphemes can come from a variety of sources. Only when the source is 
another lexical item does grammaticalisation obey the principle of unidirectionality, but even 
then there are exceptions. With respect to all other sources, viz. grammaticalisation from one 
grammatical morpheme to another and grammaticalisation within the domain, bidirectionality 
is to be expected (Frajzyngier 2008; for a different view, see Heine’s work cited in this 
chapter).
Compensatory change only seldom results in the coding of the same function. Usually, the 
new function contains just one of the elements of the former function. The compensatory 
change may also result in substantial restructuring of the functional domains.
Notes

made many useful suggestions regarding the content and the organisation of the chapter, which I 
was happy to implement. Erin Shay read the whole chapter several times and made important 
substantial and editorial comments. I am most grateful to her for her insightful comments and I 
was happy to include a few of her formulations.
2 
I am grateful to Claire Bowern for the reminder.
3 
For information on Songhay see Heath 1998. The present description is also based on my own 
work on Songhay spoken in Niger.
