<!-- page 411 -->

393
17
Formal semantics/pragmatics 
and language change
Ashwini Deo
1 Introduction
Semantics and pragmatics are both concerned with how meaning is made in natural language. 
Semantics studies the invariant meanings of morphemic primitives in languages and rules of 
syntactic combination that determine the build-up and interpretation of complex expressions. 
Pragmatics studies the use of language for communicating information, and in particular, 
how formally encoded content systematically exploits, in uences, and is in uenced by the 
context in which it is uttered. Formal semantics and pragmatics both seek to precisely 
characterise the nature of linguistic content, the context, and the interaction between the two, 
using mathematical and logical tools.
Formal approaches to meaning typically take an atemporal perspective on their 
explanandum: the focus is on invariant universals – both conceptual and communicational – 
that characterise meaning subsystems. For instance, a theory of time expressions in language 
investigates the logic of how natural language forms such as tense/aspect markers or 
adverbials relate events to a universal time structure and how pragmatic universals serve to 
further guide inferences about these relations. A theory of the scopal properties of 
quanti cational elements such as every or no examines the nature and space of scopal 
interactions and how these may be constrained or facilitated by pragmatic factors. The 
centrality of nuanced native speaker judgements in determining inferential patterns, readings 
of ambiguous expressions, and contextual felicity serves to further constrain the temporal 
perspective of the discipline; if the study of linguistic meaning crucially depends on access 
to the minds of the encoders and interpreters of a language, then diachronic semantics 
becomes a logical impossibility. In this chapter, I will argue to the contrary, focusing on 
research that reconciles sub-disciplinary concerns and demonstrates that systematic patterns 
in the evolution of meanings can be fruitfully investigated and explained using methodological 
tools that have been developed within a synchronically oriented programme of formal 
semantics and pragmatics.
The other discipline of interest here is grammaticalisation. The typologically oriented 
scholarship on grammaticalisation patterns has generated cross-linguistic empirical 
generalisations about the patterning of form and meaning in functional categories. These 
patterns take the form of unidirectional diachronic trajectories – recurring cross-linguistic 
regularities in the ways that functional morphemes undergo semantic change (Heine et al. 
1991; Bybee et al. 1994; Haspelmath 1999; Dahl 2000; Traugott and Dasher 2002; Hopper

---

<!-- page 412 -->

Ashwini Deo
394
and Traugott 2003). As Bybee and Dahl (1989: 53) observe, these regularities suggest that 
“there may be some way of arriving at a cross-linguistic understanding of grammatical 
meaning, or more ambitiously, a universally valid theory of grammatical meaning.” Formal 
semantics, coming from a different perspective, operates under the hypothesis that functional 
meanings are invariant (or at least restricted to a small set of well-de ned options) across 
languages. The functional lexicon, i.e. the inventory of grammatical or functional elements in 
languages, is the main object of study here as well,1 with the question becoming increasingly 
central to semantic and pragmatic inquiry in recent years. A natural expansion of a cross-
linguistic semantics/pragmatics programme is the cross-temporal or diachronic semantics 
programme which would seek to explain systematic patterns of change in the functional 
lexicon of languages. If synchronic grammatical systems and changes to these systems arise 
from the same grammatical, cognitive, and communicative principles, then any theory about 
a synchronic state has implications for transitions into and out of that state.
In addition to being explananda for semantics/pragmatics, semantic change phenomena 
can offer both a new perspective on interpreting the properties of synchronic states and 
constraints on synchronic semantic theorising. This chapter articulates the directions towards 
such an amphichronic programme (Kiparsky 2006, 2012) in which linguistic explanations 
are not a priori either all diachronic or synchronic but can be empirically determined to be 
one or the other on the basis of systematic investigation. The task is to tease apart properties 
of the grammar from the uses it is put to at a given state and the ways in which usage at some 
state facilitates or constrains the transitions into later states. Grammaticalisation paths, which 
involve systematic shifts between functional meanings or from lexical to functional meanings, 
present an ideal empirical domain for such investigation.
1.1 A theory of semantic change
The emergence of new functional markers in a linguistic system and the changes in their 
distribution and interpretation over time has been observed to show systematicity in a variety 
of semantic domains, leading to such changes being construed in terms of clines or trajectories 
along which forms move in time. The observed regularities pertain to the lexical material 
harnessed to create functional resources as well as the changes undergone by grammaticalised 
material within functional domains. On the assumption that there is a common core of 
functional meanings that human languages may encode grammatically, we can conceive of 
three sorts of changes in a language-speci c functional domain: (a) Recruitment: in which 
there is an addition to the inventory of overtly expressed functional meanings (e.g. when a 
language without a distinct marker of alienable possession develops one); (b) Loss: in which 
there is a reduction in the inventory (e.g. when changes lead to the loss of a morphosyntactically 
expressed contrast between the perfect aspect and the past tense); and (c) Redistribution: in 
which functional material acquires new uses relative to a semantic domain (Generalisation) 
or becomes obligatory, rather than optional in certain contexts of use (Categoricalisation). 
The changes involved in the well-studied grammaticalisation paths described in (1) below 
can all be sorted into one or more of these categories.
(1) a 
Progressive markers are sourced from locative expressions and gradually become 
obligatory for describing events in progress. Over time, they generalise into markers 
of imperfective aspect (Comrie 1976; Bybee et al. 1994).
b 
Expressions encoding motion are frequently recruited in marking future tense or 
prospective aspect (Bybee et al. 1994; Eckardt 2006).

---

<!-- page 413 -->

Formal semantics/pragmatics and language change 
395
c 
Perfect marking diachronically evolves from resultative marking and generalises 
into perfective or past tense marking (Dahl 1985, 2000; Bybee et al. 1994).
d 
Expressions encoding location evolve into expressions encoding alienable/
inalienable possession (Clark 1978; Aristar 1996; Heine 1997; Stassen 2009).
e 
Expressions restricted to expressing deontic modality diachronically acquire 
epistemic uses, but not vice versa (Traugott 1989; Traugott and Dasher 2002).
Given the logical space of possible changes in the system of functional elements in languages, 
we can now characterise the basic questions for a theory of semantic change.
(2) a 
What is the semantic content corresponding to the functional expressions that 
constitute the input to or the output of a grammaticalisation path?
b 
What is the logical relation between the meanings of these expressions such that a 
‘path’ may exist between them?
c 
What are the necessary and suf cient conditions for the recruitment of lexical 
material to generate functional material?
d 
What factors of usage and grammar are involved in categoricalisation and 
generalisation of innovated functional material in a given linguistic system?
e 
Is reduction in inventory (i.e. loss) spontaneous, is it a concomitant of generalisation 
or can it be both?
These questions can be related to some of the theoretical questions that Weinreich et al. 
(1968) set up for an empirically based investigation of language change. (2a–b) have to do 
with the constraints problem for semantics: what is the precise content of grammatically 
relevant elements of meaning and what are the constraints on their interaction? (2c) is a 
special instance of the actuation problem: what are the cognitive contrasts and communicative 
goals that are implicated in the emergence of new semantic variants in a semantic subsystem 
– alternative forms for expressing existing meanings? (2d–e) imply a version of the embedding 
and evaluation problems – how are innovated variants embedded within the larger linguistic 
matrix in which they occur and how can the functional load they carry be evaluated relative 
to such a matrix? The propagation of innovated variants across a population cannot be 
attributed to mere imitation of newly emerged variants but must be systematically related to 
the pragmatic uses to which such forms are put and the contrasts that they enable speakers 
and hearers to linguistically access. Identifying these connections will lead to an understanding 
of the question in (2d), which is ultimately about the dynamics of the propagation process in 
a given semantic domain – or the transition problem. Finally, it makes sense to determine, 
perhaps on a case-by-case basis, the relation between the propagation of innovated variants 
and the previously existing system (2e). Is loss a causal factor in the propagation of innovated 
variants or is loss an effect of the competition between innovated and existing variants?
What the questions in (2) offer is a way for approaching grammaticalisation-based changes 
in the functional lexicon of languages from a semantic/pragmatic perspective. Any 
investigation of a particular change in this domain should consist of two components – a 
static, structural one and a dynamic one. Taking a parallel from phonology, an explanation 
of a synchronic pattern such as nasalisation of vowels in pre-nasal contexts or the palatalisation 
of consonants before high vowels is based in the mechanics of articulation and its acoustic 
correlates on the one hand (the structural component) and the mechanism(s) by which these 
acoustic effects come to be interpreted differently and propagated through a speech 
community (the dynamic component) on the other. Providing explicit, experimentally

---

<!-- page 414 -->

Ashwini Deo
396
testable structural and dynamic explanations for sound change is part of the general enterprise 
of phonology, which concerns itself with both synchronic and diachronic sound patterns.2
For a similarly well-articulated understanding of semantic change, we will need to draw 
on semantic theory to explicate the structural component – giving a precise characterisation 
of the logical relation (i.e. the similarities and differences) between the meanings of related 
functional categories (2a–b). The dynamic component, on the other hand, will draw from 
theories of language use and language evolution in order to provide a plausible account for 
the morphosyntactic emergence of a new functional category in a language and its subsequent 
generalisation to a broader meaning, under normal conditions of usage and transmission 
(2c–e). The rest of this chapter undertakes a preliminary development of this programme, 
contextualising prior research and introducing tools that will serve future research in this 
area.
2 Towards an integration
Answering the questions above involves integrating the empirical insights and generalisations 
coming from work on grammaticalisation with the results of formal semantics and pragmatics. 
Implementing such an integration requires formally precise interpretations of intuitive 
notions such as bleaching or generalisation of meaning (e.g. Heine et al. 1991; Bybee et al. 
1994), conventionalisation of invited inferences (e.g. Traugott and Dasher 2002), and 
in ationary use of linguistic material (e.g. Haspelmath 1999; Dahl 2001) – which have been 
centrally used in grammaticalisation research for describing semantic change phenomena. 
For instance, an explicit theory of the semantic contribution of progressive and imperfective 
markers can give explanatory bite to the characterisation of the change from progressive to 
imperfective meaning in terms of generalisation. Similarly, a formal modelling of the 
contextual conditions under which inferences may reliably arise, can help better understand 
the process of their conventionalisation. Attempts at marrying semantics with 
grammaticalisation phenomena to date have been undertaken relatively recently and are still 
quite sporadic. Nonetheless, they collectively present a picture that demonstrates that the 
questions in (2a–e) are explicitly answerable within speci c semantic domains. I turn now to 
discussing the results coming from this new, but quite promising, approach to semantic 
change.
2.1 Recruitiment via conventionalisation of pragmatic inferences
Eckardt (2006) represents the only explicit monograph to date investigating how the insights 
of truth-conditional semantics can be applied to understanding meaning change in reanalysis 
scenarios. The compositional view taken in truth-conditional approaches holds that the 
meaning of complex expressions, including sentences, is determined by the meaning of the 
simplex parts of these expressions and the way in which they are combined. Structural 
reanalysis involves changes in the relation between a lexical item and the material it combines 
with. In semantic change, reanalysis is driven by hearers who seek to assign meanings to 
individual linguistic expressions such that the whole meaning can be derived compositionally. 
When hearers encounter linguistic structure whose literal content reliably gives rise to certain 
pragmatic implications, they are likely to reinterpret parts of this structure in such a way so 
as to make the pragmatic implication part of the literal content. Eckardt’s work implements 
this intuitive idea in a formal compositionality-based framework. She analyses a number of 
phenomena involving recruitment such as the grammaticalisation of the be going to

---

<!-- page 415 -->

Formal semantics/pragmatics and language change 
397
construction in English, the rise of Negative Polarity Items (NPIs) such as pas ‘step’ and mie 
‘crumb’, personne ‘person’ in French, and the reanalysis of an intensi er selbst to a focus 
particle in German. We can understand the essential elements of her approach here through 
the case of the be going to construction in English, which realises the prospective aspect.
Most accounts of this construction take the view that its prospective meaning results from 
the systematic use and interpretation of be going to sentences to convey information about 
future action (Mossé 1938; Danchev and Kytö 1994; Hopper and Traugott 2003). Syntactically, 
the change involves the reanalysis of a lexical verb that combines with an in nitival 
complement to an aspectual operator that combines with a VP. Speci cally, the progressive 
form of a lexical verb is reinterpreted over time to express the future-oriented prospective 
aspect that combines with an event description.
(3) a 
I am going to visit my friend.
b 
[PRES[PROG [I go [to visit my friend VP]VP]]]
c 
[PRES [PROSP [I visit my friend VP]]]
This change has been understood to involve the conventionalisation of a pragmatic inference. 
Hearers are able to infer from a sentence that literally conveys ‘the movement of an agent in 
order to undertake an activity’ the proposition that the agent has an intention to undertake this 
activity in the imminent future. Such an inference is initially localised and arises only in 
speci c contexts where the shared information between a speaker and hearer can support it. 
Gradually, as speakers make use of this construction more, it turns into a ‘generalised invited 
inference’ that is reliably associated with the be going to construction (Traugott and Dasher 
2002). Eckardt undertakes a close study of the contexts in which future-oriented inferences 
arise and discovers that the construction is exploited systematically to express the intentions 
of an individual in those contexts where it is unlikely that the hearer will be able to recover 
such intentions directly. The empirical observation is that an overwhelming majority of early 
uses of be going to occur in dramatic prose where the structure of a  ctional narrative is being 
conveyed to an audience (the hearer) that has no access to how it will unfold. The hypothesis 
is that contexts involving the hearer’s ignorance about future intentions and the speaker’s 
intention to convey them are crucial to the conventional association of future-oriented 
meaning with the movement construction.
In later generations, hearers encountering the be going to construction and the inference 
of futurity that it obligatorily gives rise to are faced with the task of assigning a compositional 
meaning to the whole sentence. This requires that there is a systematic part-by-part relation 
between the lexical material comprising the sentence and the content that it conveys. Given 
that the meaning of ongoing movement (associated with the progressive form of go) is no 
longer part of the content conveyed by the be going to sentences, hearers assign to this part 
the meaning of an operator that applies to an event description and literally expresses the 
content that the event description will obtain in the near future. The reanalysis is thus 
understandable as a compositionality-driven problem-solving process: What meaning Ý can 
be assigned to be going to such that it can be used to compositionally derive the future-
oriented meaning of a sentence like John is going to visit a friend? Once hearers have assigned 
the prospective aspect operator to the string be going to, future-orientedness becomes part of 
the literal content (rather than inferentially derived content) of the sentence.
For expository ease, I have abstracted away from the formal semantic analysis and its 
precise representational language that is integral to Eckardt’s argumentation, but I hope to 
have conveyed the general nature of her compositionality-driven programme. Any instance

---

<!-- page 416 -->

Ashwini Deo
398
of a conventionalised inference associated with a particular construction poses an interpretive 
puzzle for a compositionality-driven hearer. Reanalysis is enabled only when it is possible to 
reassign pieces of meaning to pieces of lexical material so that the literal content of the 
sentence using such a construction matches the pragmatically conveyed content.
2.2 Generalisation as conventionalisation of entailments
Semantic reanalysis is not restricted only to the grammaticalisation of lexical material. In 
grammaticalisation paths, functional expressions tend to be diachronically reanalysed as 
instantiating broader, more general functional categories. In such cases, it is not clear that 
the reanalysis involves the conventionalisation of speaker-invited inferences that arise in 
context. On the contrary, it appears that each successive functional stage involves a 
systematic expansion in the domain of application of a given expression. Condoravdi and 
Deo (2008) examine the grammaticalisation path involving the resultative, the perfect, and 
the perfective operators as it is instantiated in the history of the Indo-Aryan languages from 
the earliest Vedic stage to Middle Indo-Aryan. The form of interest is a deverbal stative 
adjectival participial form with the af x -ta (allomorph -na) attached to the verb root.3 The 
observation is that the range of readings available to the -ta form at each successive stage 
is a superset of the readings available at prior stages. This expansion is schematically 
expressed in the table in (4).4
(4) Readings
Resultative
Stage I
Perfect
Stage II
Perfective
Stage III
Resultative perfect
Õ
Õ
Õ
Existential perfect
Ø
Õ
Õ
Universal perfect
Ø
Õ
Õ
Eventive/Past
Ø
Ø
Õ
Condoravdi and Deo give an explicit formulation of the logical semantics of the resultative, 
perfect, and perfective operators as functions that apply to event descriptions (sets of events) 
and return temporal predicates (sets of times). These operators are characterised by a relation 
of asymmetric entailment. This means that the meaning (set of times) describable by using the 
resultative marker is a subset of the meaning describable by using the perfect marker, which, 
in turn, is a subset of the meaning corresponding to the use of the perfective marker. Their 
proposal is that the cross-linguistically robustly attested resultative > perfect > perfective shift 
is underpinned by this logical relation between successively more general operators.
The question of the semantic content and logical relation between functional categories 
(2a–b) is also investigated in Deo (2009) for the progressive and the imperfective aspects. 
The proposal there is that progressive and imperfective aspects are both universal quanti ers 
over temporal intervals, but that they differ in the type of intervals that they quantify over. 
The progressive quanti es over parts of the reference interval, allowing for the assertion that 
some eventuality is ongoing throughout the reference interval. That is, a sentence like John 
was biking along the road asserts that every ‘small-enough’ part of the interval made 
reference to by the speaker overlaps with an event of John biking along the road. This is the 
event-in-progress reading saliently observed with progressive morphology cross-
linguistically. The imperfective aspect essentially involves the generalisation of the

---

<!-- page 417 -->

Formal semantics/pragmatics and language change 
399
quanti cational domain to superintervals of the reference interval. Thus, a sentence like John 
bikes along the road asserts that there is some superinterval of the reference time now such 
that every ‘small-enough’ part of the larger interval overlaps with an event of John biking 
along the road. Given that superintervals can be arbitrarily small or large, the imperfective 
gives rise to both the event-in-progress and the habitual characterising readings.
The analyses in both the case of the perfect/perfective and the progressive/imperfective 
offer precise formal interpretations of the notions of bleaching or generalisation, which have 
been centrally invoked as mechanisms of change in the grammaticalisation literature. The 
grammaticalisation path connecting related categories is, in these cases, at least, demonstrably 
underpinned by the logical relation of asymmetric entailment. In contrast to treating 
bleaching/generalisation as mechanisms of change, the focus is on the structural relationship 
between grammatical meanings, which serves as an enabling factor in the process of change. 
The precise dynamic processes and (possibly causal) factors which propel an expression with 
a more speci c meaning to transition to a more general meaning are left unaddressed here but 
receive a partial answer in the literature discussed in the next section.
2.3 Semantic weakening driven by inﬂ ationary use
Dahl (2001) describes a class of semantic changes in natural language that involve expressions 
that encode high values on evaluative or other scales. The observation is that such expressions 
are used much more frequently than warranted, motivated by pragmatic and social, rather 
than purely descriptive, considerations. For example, expressions such as excellent or 
fantastic convey the speaker’s highly positive evaluation of an object or a situation. A teacher, 
wanting to give positive feedback, may use the term excellent to describe her students’ work 
often enough and indiscriminately enough, for the term to lose its informative strength over 
time. Generalised over instances and populations of speakers, the loss of strength of such 
expressions is an unintended result that obtains over time from the local linguistic choices of 
agents that have socially oriented rather than purely information-transfer goals.5 The semantic 
phenomena that Dahl discusses include evaluative adjectives, politeness systems, and 
emphatic constructions (e.g. emphatic negation), which all share the underlying dynamic 
behaviour in which increase in frequency has pragmatic/social motivation and semantic 
effects. Dahl schematically sketches out three ways in which what he calls “rhetorical 
devaluation” in the meaning of expressions may take place: (a) a lexical item which expresses 
a strong value of some parameter may tend to be used even when a weaker value is called for; 
(b) a construction whose function is to draw attention to an element whose content is counter 
to expectation is used indiscriminately also for elements which are not counter to expectation; 
and (c) an expression may tend to be used even when the information it carries is irrelevant 
(does not belong to the intended message) or predictable (presupposed or inferrable).
One formalisation of these intuitive ideas is found in Kiparsky and Condoravdi (2006), 
which offers an analysis of Jespersen’s cycle as it occurs in Greek over three millennia.6 They 
observe that at each stage of the language, Greek dialects structurally distinguish between 
emphatic negation and plain negation, although the precise markers employed at different 
stages and in different dialects differ. Emphatic negation is compositionally built from a 
focused inde nite (which is either a minimiser or a generaliser) together with plain negation. 
Minimisers are expressions that denote small amounts or quantities (a bit, a drop, a twig) and 
serve to strengthen negation quantitatively, thus making it stricter. A speaker who asserts 
There wasn’t a drop of water in that tank strengthens their claim by eliminating even those 
possibilities in which an insigni cant amount of water is present in the tank. Generalisers, on

---

<!-- page 418 -->

Ashwini Deo
400
the other hand, are qualitative strengtheners; they extend the negation to wider domains. For 
instance, in response to a question such as Did you see a girl walk by?, an answer like I didn’t 
see a soul, rules out sightings of any living object – thereby strengthening the negative claim 
considerably. The observation for Greek is that such qualitative and quantitative strengtheners 
routinely get reanalysed as exponents of plain negation and are replaced in their emphatic 
function by newer strengtheners.
Kiparsky and Condoravdi take the distinction between emphatic and plain negation to be 
a crucial semantic distinction that must  nd expression through the morphosyntactic resources 
of a language. Indeed, Jespersen’s cycle has been attested in both Indo-European and non-
Indo-European families such as Bantu (Devos and van der Auwera 2013) and Austronesian 
(Vossen and van der Auwera 2013). In Dahl’s terms, one could say that emphatic negation 
expresses the ‘strong value’ of the negation parameter, while plain negation expresses the 
weaker value. Emphatic negation is felicitous in certain contexts, which help de ne its 
functions: (a) it may be used to mark contradiction of (a possibly implicit) assertion (5a); (b) 
it may be used to deny a presumption or an expectation (5b); (c) it may be used to lift 
restrictions on inde nites in the scope of negation and widen the domain to the entire domain 
of discourse (5c).
(5) a 
A: John ate all my cookies, didn’t he? B: He didn’t eat a thing of yours!
b 
A: How much did the therapy cost you? B: It didn’t cost me a thing.
c 
A: Did you hear a police car? B: I didn’t hear a thing.
The core contexts of use for emphatic negation are thus those in which it serves to eliminate 
a contradictory proposition that is either explicitly or implicitly present in the discourse 
context, either as a direct belief or as a hearer bias. Being informationally stronger, emphatic 
negation serves to restrict the common ground of discourse more than plain negation – the 
source of its overuse.7 As speakers extend the use of emphatic negation to make stronger 
claims in contexts that do not necessarily warrant its use, hearers may fail to associate 
emphatic negation with its contextual felicity conditions. Thus, overuse of the emphatic form 
leads to its de-emphasis or change to plain negation and the emphatic function is renewed by 
new morphosyntactic material.
Schaden (2012) presents yet another innovative formal implementation of in ation-based 
semantic weakening in the domain of the perfect and perfective aspects. Like Condoravdi 
and Deo (2008), he is interested in the diachronic path from the present perfect to the 
(perfective) past tense, but rather than focusing on the logical meanings of these categories 
that facilitates the change, his focus is on the causal user-based dynamics that drives the 
process of change. The proposal rests on a basic contrast between the present perfect and the 
past tense that is often assumed but not precisely explicated in the literature on aspectual 
semantics: perfect marking signals that a past situation being described is of ‘current 
relevance’ while past tense marking is neutral with respect to such a relation with the present 
time. For instance, a sentence such as John has eaten camel meat conveys that this past 
eventuality has some bearing on some current issue, perhaps our evaluation of John’s culinary 
adventurousness, or his skills as a survivor, or something else. The corresponding simple past 
sentence John ate camel meat need not be associated with such a requirement. Schaden 
makes the assumption that ‘current relevance’ is a gradable notion (measurable on the real 
interval from 0 to 1) and that it must be relativised with respect to a discourse participant. 
Speakers and hearers assign to the past and the present perfect some subinterval of this 
interval – that is, they use the present perfect if the eventuality being described is considered

---

<!-- page 419 -->

Formal semantics/pragmatics and language change 
401
to have current relevance above some threshold n. If the current relevance value of the 
eventuality does not meet this threshold, the simple past tense is used instead. The threshold 
parameter n is the crucial ingredient since changes in this parameter determine the distribution 
of the present perfect and the past at any given stage of the language. Further, the formal 
model assumes that hearers can calculate the degree of current relevance of a sentence based 
on information contained in the sentence and the utterance-context of that sentence.
The diachronic dynamics now works as follows: The core contexts for the use of the 
present perfect are those in which the past eventuality is related to some ‘perfect state’ that 
holds at the reference time. For instance, while cleaning the broken shards of the vase lying 
on the  oor, a speaker can legitimately utter John has broken the vase. However, speakers 
tend to use the present perfect as a rhetorical strategy, inviting the hearer to infer the situation 
at the reference time that makes the past eventuality relevant to the current issue in the 
discourse. That is, rather than using the present perfect only in contexts where its felicity 
conditions are met: i.e. where it is contextually clear that the past eventuality is relevant at 
least to the threshold level n, speakers use it also in contexts where it is relevant to levels 
below n. According to Schaden, this happens because speakers systematically tend towards 
overestimating the current relevance of their contribution in discourse.8 That is, they use the 
present perfect with the belief that the past eventuality being described is relevant to a degree 
above n in contexts where it is not. Hearers, attuned to the context of utterance, do not fully 
compensate for this in ationary behaviour, and infer from the speaker’s use of the perfect 
that the threshold n which licenses the use of the present perfect in a particular context is 
lower than it actually is. These assumptions about speaker biases and hearer failures to 
compensate for these biases inevitably result in the lowering of the threshold n across 
generations of learners (Schaden’s analysis is framed in terms of an Iterated Learning Model). 
The effect of this is that the simple past is gradually driven out of the language as the present 
perfect gets used even in contexts where the past eventuality has a negligible degree of 
current relevance.
2.4 Summary
This section presented a sampling of formal approaches to the questions raised in (2), showing 
how notions invoked in grammaticalisation like conventionalisation of pragmatic inferences, 
generalisation of semantic meaning, and in ationary weakening can be modelled explicitly 
using the tools of truth-conditional semantics, formal pragmatics, and learning models. The 
next section introduces a recently emerging sub eld within formal pragmatics that uses 
game-theoretic insights for understanding the emergence of conventional meaning. Although 
this research is not tied in any way yet to concrete empirical studies of particular diachronic 
changes, it develops explicit models of discourse contexts and the speaker–hearer dynamics 
that lead to a population arriving at conventional ways of signalling meaning.
3 Game-theoretic pragmatics and semantic change
Game-theoretic pragmatics concerns itself centrally with language use, in particular, with 
modelling how interlocutors establish conventional ways of signalling meaning given their 
information state and alternative options for conveying information. David Lewis (1969) 
undertook the  rst attempt to apply game theory to this problem. He modelled linguistic 
convention in terms of repeated plays of signalling games with possibly arbitrary signals in 
which signals come to acquire particular meanings because of the ways in which they are

---

<!-- page 420 -->

Ashwini Deo
402
used by rational agents. Later work (e.g. van Rooij 2004a, 2004b; Jäger 2007) investigates 
why particular strategies of conventionalisation or marking are re ected robustly in natural 
language systems. This research adopts an evolutionary approach to the problem of strategy 
selection and its propagation across a population, thus attributing a non-central role to 
rationalistic reasoning in how linguistic systems might come to exhibit certain tendencies.9 It 
is this property that makes the evolutionary approach a useful tool for modelling semantic 
change, in which new semantic variants or new uses for older variants might come about 
initially as a result of pragmatic reasoning, but their conventionalisation and propagation 
over time must rely on less rationalistic processes such as learning by imitation and adaptation 
over multiple generations.
3.1 The basic structure of game-theoretic explanations
The discourse context is modelled as a game between the speaker and the hearer in which the 
speaker aims to convey some private knowledge to the hearer through their utterance. The 
game model speci es possible choices of linguistic signals for the speaker and possible 
interpretations of these signals for the hearer. Solution concepts for a language game can be 
understood as formal rules that predict how the game will be played out based on speaker and 
hearer preferences (signal economy, successful communication). Let us illustrate the structure 
of a language game by walking through a simple game model of a discourse context and 
determining the possible and optimal courses of action for the speaker and the hearer in a 
speci c state of affairs. Consider a context in which the speaker has uttered (6).
(6) a 
John has got to see a psychiatrist.
b 
John has to see a psychiatrist.
How should the hearer interpret such a sentence and crucially, the intention of the speaker in 
uttering it? The speaker could have uttered the more economical variant in (6b) but she used 
a truth-conditionally (somewhat) equivalent but wordier variant with the complex modal 
have got to. So what could account for the speaker’s choice of a costlier signal? The reasoning 
for the speaker goes as follows: If I were to utter (6b) the hearer may possibly interpret my 
assertion weakly, as conveying that it is necessary but not absolutely urgent/inevitable that 
John see a psychiatrist.10 Since the state of affairs is such that John urgently needs psychiatric 
help, I should use the stronger modal form to communicate this state of affairs. The reasoning 
for the hearer is parallel: The speaker uttered (6a) when a simpler variant (6b) was available. 
Given that the speaker would not utter extra material without a reason, it must be that (6b) 
does not convey the appropriate information in this context. By choosing to utter (6a), the 
speaker has eliminated the possibility of a weaker interpretation and made an explicitly 
stronger claim. The phenomenon here illustrates an instance of Horn’s pragmatic division of 
labour. Horn (1984: 37–38) proposes that in several domains of the lexicon and grammar, the 
existence of a semantically stronger form, together with the speaker’s choice of the unmarked, 
semantically weaker form in a given context allows the addressee to construct a quantity-
based scalar implicature that the semantic content associated with the more informative form 
was not intended by the utterance of the unmarked form. That is, in this case, the hearer might 
infer from an utterance of (6b) that the speaker does not believe that there is an urgent need 
for John to visit the psychiatrist.
We can represent the speaker’s production choices and the hearer’s interpretational choices 
in the form of the possible strategies in (7) and (8) respectively. Here, a speaker’s strategy is

---

<!-- page 421 -->

Formal semantics/pragmatics and language change 
403
a function from states/meanings to forms (in this case, an element of [{necessity, 
necessity+}{HAS TO, HAS GOT TO}]) while a hearer’s strategy is a function from forms to 
meanings/states (in this case, an element of [{HAS TO, HAS GOT TO}{necessity+, necessity}]).11
(7)
necessity
necessity+
S1
HAS TO
HAS TO
S:
S2
HAS GOT TO
HAS GOT TO
S3
HAS GOT TO
HAS TO
S4
HAS TO
HAS GOT TO
(8)
HAS TO
HAS GOT TO
H1
necessity
necessity+
H:
H2
necessity+
necessity
H3
necessity+
necessity+
H4
necessity
necessity
Given these possible mappings from meanings to forms (the speaker strategies) and from 
forms to meanings (the hearer strategies), what strategy pairing will tend to conventionalise 
across a given population of speakers? Intuitively, one may expect that those strategy pairs 
offering the greatest communicative success with minimal cost for the speaker (the ones with 
the greatest overall utility) will tend to get selected by individuals and become stably 
instantiated in a population. In order to determine which of these strategy choices will lead to 
the biggest average bene t (utility) to the speaker and the hearer, let us make some further 
assumptions. Let us assume that the structure of the has got to modal is more complex than 
that of the has to modal leading to greater production cost for the speaker and that the speaker 
prefers economical strategies modulo equivalent expressivity. Let us also assume that the 
speaker desires to communicate the true state of affairs and that the hearer desires to know 
the true state of affairs. Finally, we assume that the necessity+ situation is strictly less frequent 
than the necessity situation – i.e. urgent necessity obtains less often than plain, non-urgent 
necessity. Then among the 16 possible AS, H S strategy pairs, only AS4, H 1S emerges as the 
strategy pair with maximal communicative success. In this strategy pair, successful 
communication results with has to, the simple form, being reserved for marking necessity, 
while has got to, the costlier form is reserved for marking necessity+.
Simplifying somewhat, the higher payoff or success rate of the strategy AS4, H 1S means 
that speakers interacting with one another in a population will tend towards adopting or 
imitating this strategy (thereby replacing less successful strategies) at greater rates than other 
strategies. This, in turn, will lead to the increase in the proportion of the most successful 
strategy within the population. The precise nature of the imitation dynamics – whether it is 
interpreted as learning in acquisition, or as the learning of adult speakers through experience, 
and whether it involves deterministic or probabilistic individual grammars – is left 
unexplicated here. What is crucial is that the spread and gradual dominance of a strategy in a 
population is closely tied to its communicative success and economy. The relative distribution

---

<!-- page 422 -->

Ashwini Deo
404
of have to and have got to in contemporary English can possibly be interpreted as an ongoing 
process in which the division of labour between the two forms (re ecting the AS4, H 1S 
strategy) is being gradually conventionalised.
3.2 Summary
A detailed discussion of the application of evolutionary approaches to semantic/pragmatic 
phenomena is far beyond the scope of this chapter. However, I want to suggest that the 
literature in game-theoretic pragmatics is extremely relevant to understanding the dynamics 
of regular semantic change. Speci cally, there is a class of semantic developments which 
consist of the innovation of new formal variants for disambiguating speci c meanings that 
were earlier expressed by existing variants (for instance, some of the grammaticalisation 
paths in (1). Grammaticalisation amounts to the conventionalisation of a ‘pragmatic division 
of labour’ pattern among such variants. Game-theoretic pragmatics offers the formal tool of 
choice for a precise modelling of the complex processes of reasoning and learning that must 
underlie such developments. The formidable task of applying this tool systematically to 
particular diachronic patterns is, however, yet to be actualised.
4 Concluding remarks
Semantic change phenomena, on closer examination, reveal themselves to be emergent 
effects arising from the interaction of the dynamic and structural properties of language – the 
complex ways in which language use shapes how universally similar functional meanings get 
expressed over time. Research on semantic grammaticalisation has adopted, for the most 
part, a functionalist, use-based approach to grammar, which takes the view that grammatical 
representations are fundamentally shaped by the use of language in discourse (e.g. Givón 
1979; Bybee 2003, 2006; Hopper and Traugott 2003). The rise and spread of new functional 
categories in particular has been tied to repeated instances of usage in which ‘structure 
emerges from the repetition of many local events’ (Bybee 2006: 714). The idea is that 
languages conventionalise frequently used structures, which then gradually undergo processes 
such as semantic bleaching or pragmatic enrichment, because of the way they are employed 
in speech contexts. Grammar is thus an emergent object shaped by the cognitive and 
communicative functions of language rather than a static object with abstract formal 
properties.
One consequence of this view has been that the facts of usage-based conventionalisation, 
frequency effects, and the gradualness of grammaticalising changes are assumed to be 
incompatible with a generative view of grammar and it is held that they cannot be modelled 
using formal tools. But there have been no attempts to demonstrate what aspects of these 
tools make them inherently inadequate in understanding and modelling the facts of semantic 
change. In contrast, in recent years, theories of meaning, and in particular, formal pragmatics, 
have increasingly evolved in the direction of precisely modelling the interaction between 
semantic content, utterance context, and pragmatic pressures in the generation of meaning. 
More relevantly, there has been work that attempts to offer precise characterisations of 
notions such as semantic generalisation, conventionalisation of pragmatic inferences, and 
in ationary weakening in terms of the structure of the meanings and the nature of the 
processes involved in such changes. Game-theoretic pragmatics explicitly brings functional 
pressures and cognitive processes as part of the explanandum to be modelled in building a 
theory of language use.

---

<!-- page 423 -->

Formal semantics/pragmatics and language change 
405
In light of these recent developments, the divide between functional and formal approaches 
to meaning becomes unnecessary, since the goal for both approaches converges to that of 
understanding the cognitive and communicative factors that structure language. The 
substantive question for both approaches boils down to describing the precise content of 
these cognitive and communicative factors and the dynamics of their interaction in context. 
The different approaches to change phenomena presented here are fully compatible with the 
view that change to grammar involves gradual change in lexical material and is ultimately 
rooted in usage and the goals of communication. What they add to the table are formally 
explicit (and therefore falsi able) accounts of the content that is grammaticalised and the 
nature of the processes that lead to its grammaticalisation. It is only through such explicit 
accounts that we can begin to reconcile research agendas that have until now existed as 
distinct domains of investigation.
Notes
1 
As von Fintel (1995: 177), in a programmatic paper on grammaticalisation remarks, “[t]he 
semantics of determiners, modals, tenses, aspects etc. is after all the bread and butter of working 
semanticists.”
2 
The seed of systematic sound change is located in the variation in the acoustic realisation of 
phonological segments introduced by coarticulation (Ohala 1981; Blevins 2004; Yu 2010; 
Bermúdez-Otero in press). The basic account is as follows: coarticulation introduces context-
dependent phonetic variants which vary systematically in a particular direction relative to the 
target. Although listeners typically tend to compensate for coarticulatory effects, they may 
sometimes attribute such effects to the grammatical component of speakers’ grammars, leading to 
changed perceptual and production patterns. Thus, what starts off originally as a non-grammatical 
phonetic effect arising from the mechanics of articulation, becomes ‘grammaticalised’ as part of 
the listeners’ grammar in the form of gradient phonetic implementation rules (phonologisation), 
and later as categorical phonological rules. Of course, not all sound change has coarticulatory 
origins (Bowern and Evans, this volume).
3 
The form is an inheritance from Indo-European but the precise changes in its meaning and 
distribution are speci c to the Indo-Aryan branch.
4 
The labels used to describe the distinct readings come from the literature on the perfect (Iatridou 
et al. 2001; Portner 2003). The resultative perfect reading obtains with predicates that have 
lexically associated result states and asserts that the relevant state holds at the reference time as a 
result of an event of the type denoted by the verb having occurred. For instance, John has put the 
cake in the oven implies that the cake is now in the oven as a result of John’s putting it there. The 
existential perfect reading is one in which the sentence asserts that the predicate has been 
instantiated at some time before the reference time. For instance, John has visited Korea many 
times implies the past occurrence of many separate visits to Korea by John, but crucially no result 
state. On the universal perfect reading, the predicate is understood to have continuously held 
throughout an interval stretching from some time in the past up to the reference time, as in John 
has lived in Korea for the last three years. The eventive/past obtains when the sentence is 
understood to describe completed events that are contained within the reference time (typically in 
the past). Sentences with perfective aspect are used in narrative discourse to advance reference 
time, contrasting with the perfect aspect in this respect. Compare John iced the cake. He (then) 
went shopping with #John has iced the cake. He (then) went/has gone shopping. The English 
Perfect morphology, unlike the German or French Perfect, does not express perfective aspect and 
cannot be used in narratives (Pancheva and von Stechow 2004; Schaden 2009).
 5 Haspelmath (1999) over-extends this mechanism to all grammaticalising changes, describing 
grammaticalisation as an ‘Invisible-hand Phenomenon’ (Keller 1989, 1994). His claim is that all 
innovation and generalisation emerges from extravagance – the speaker’s tendency to use

---

<!-- page 424 -->

Ashwini Deo
406
unusually explicit formulations in order to attract attention. While the dynamics described by 
Keller, Haspelmath, and Dahl is clearly at work in some domains of language change, it is not 
clear that all of semantic change can be described using the heuristic of the ‘Invisible-hand 
Phenomenon’. Moreover, how such a dynamic process plays itself out in any particular empirical 
domain crucially rests on the precise explication of underlying meanings and how they might be 
enriched through “unusually explicit formulations” (Haspelmath 1999: 1043).
 6 Jespersen (1917) discovered this cross-linguistically robust historical process through its 
instantiation in the diachrony of Germanic and Romance languages.
 7 Condoravdi (2009), an unpublished handout, offers a formal analysis of the comparative 
informative strength of emphatic and plain negation assuming Stalnaker’s view of discourse as a 
communal inquiry into the state of the world (Stalnaker 1974, 1978) and its structuring as a 
sequence of questions and answers (Groenendijk and Stokhof 1984; Ginzburg 1995; Roberts 
1996). The idea is that emphatic negation presupposes  ner-grained partitions of the common-
ground and an emphatic negative assertion serves to eliminate more possibilities than plain 
negation.
 8 Schaden brings in an interesting parallel from evolutionary psychology as a possible explanation 
for why speakers might systematically overestimate the current relevance of their contributions. It 
has been well-observed that agents operate with cognitive biases that favour positive self-images 
and eliminate or background negative aspects. Such biases lead to errors in self-ranking tasks, for 
instance, where agents have to compare their own merits within a larger group. Trivers (2011) 
reports that 94 per cent of academics involved in a study judged themselves as belonging to the 
upper half of their profession. In evolutionary terms, Trivers’ (and by analogy, Schaden’s) 
explanation is that self-deception is a species-general behaviour that allows individuals to deceive 
others by allowing the speaker to believe that the signal they convey corresponds to reality. 
Speakers overestimating the current relevance of their utterance is simply a species-speci c 
particular example of this more general evolutionarily useful behaviour.
 9 Evolutionary game theory, which was developed in evolutionary biology to model selection and 
propagation of genetic traits (Maynard Smith and Price 1973), has been used more generally to 
model situations in which behavioural choices do not rely on rational decision-making and explicit 
reasoning on the part of individuals.
10 I have little to say here about the formalisation of the difference between the semantic contribution 
of have got to in contrast to have to. Both modal forms express strong necessity, but there is an 
intuition that the former sounds stronger than the latter in that the prejacent seems more urgent and 
unavoidable. I am only appealing to this pre-theoretic intuition in this discussion.
11 necessity+ is the placeholder term for the stronger, more urgent necessity that is often associated 
with the use of have got to.

410
18
Discourse
Alexandra D’Arcy
1 Introduction
In historical linguistics, evolutionary linguistics, computational (phylogenetic) linguistics, 
variationist (socio)linguistics, and a number of related  elds, we pursue the facts of language 
change. The fact of language change itself is a given, “too obvious to be recorded or even 
listed among the assumptions of our research” (Labov 1994: 9), yet despite its banality, this 
observation is among “the most stubborn and dif cult to assimilate as we try to come to grips 
with the nature of language in general” (ibid.). In our attempts to ‘come to grips’, we must 
necessarily rely on linguistic snapshots – the state of a language at some point(s) – yet any 
stage of a language is a historical artefact, implicating diachrony and synchrony as 
complementary modes of enquiry (Labov 1989). As such, a theory of language change cannot 
be developed from a single perspective. Romaine (1980, 1982) has argued that to be viable, 
such a theory must link past with present, and Labov has argued consistently and forcibly 
against the Saussurian separation of diachronic and synchronic linguistics (Weinreich et al. 
1968; Labov 1975, 1989, 1994). At the core of the issue then is the way in which to resolve 
what is known about historical change with the mounting evidence from the study of change 
in progress. This latter  eld of enquiry has proved a valuable resource in the investigation of 
linguistic change, and one of the ways in which variationist sociolinguistics problematises 
the issue is through direct examination of embedding – the route a change follows through the 
language and through the speech community that uses that language. In this chapter I pay 
particular attention to the  rst part of the embedding problem and consider what happens 
when structure changes: what does change look like, and how does it proceed? To answer 
such questions we must  rst explore what is understood by ‘the grammar’. Change is 
longitudinal, and this has a critical rami cation: linguistic structure cannot be monolithic. It 
must be either composite (cf. Hale 1998) or  uid, capable of accommodating perpetual  ux. 
Operationalisation of this  uidity entails recognition of orderly heterogeneity. Natural 
language is not only in a constant state of change, but it is also characterised by variation that 
is highly constrained, both linguistically and socially. This is particularly true of spoken 
language, the prime locus of change: language in social context. Thus, the primary target of 
variationist sociolinguistics is not simply discourse, but the vernacular.

---

<!-- page 429 -->

Discourse
411
2 Variation and change
Speech style represents a long and diverse continuum of contextual, interactional, and topical 
in uences, but the vernacular is the baseline. It represents the model  rst acquired, before the 
standardising in uence of elitism and/or education becomes an overlay on usage and 
systematically eradicates the regularity of the constraints on language variation, and language 
change (Labov 1984: 29). The greatest challenge facing the linguist is to tap this style, but once 
accessed, it proffers a window on language as deployed in living speech communities, the 
crucible of language variation and change (Weinreich et al. 1968: 177). The variationist 
literature is thus replete with examples of speakers using two or more forms to express a single 
meaning or function. This variability, exempli ed in (1) (from Tagliamonte 2006: 10–11; York 
English Corpus), is regular and probabilistic, conditioned by language internal and language 
external factors. It simultaneously operates at all levels of linguistic structure, from phonetics 
and phonology to morphology, syntax, pragmatics, and the lexicon, as well as being visible at 
interfaces. Thus, one of the key contributions of variationist sociolinguistics has been to 
establish the empirical validity and – as a consequence – the theoretical import of heterogeneity.
(1) a 
I did a college course when I lef-Ø school, but I lef-[t] it because it was business 
studies.
b 
You go to Leeds and Castleford, they take it so much more serious-ly…they really 
are, they take it so serious-Ø.
c 
I think she’s gonna be pretty cheeky. I think she’ll be cheeky.
d 
He’s got bad breath; he has smelly feet.
e 
She were a good worker. She was a helluva good worker.
f 
I wish that forty or  fty years ago I’d as much con dence. I wish Ø I’d had it then.
Crucially, variation and change are not coterminous: variation does not ipso facto entail change. 
Variation is the norm; change is the exception. But change does involve a period of oscillation, 
as older and newer forms compete for the expression of some meaning or function in discourse. 
A does not become B; rather, A and B alternate for a period and the frequency of one (or more) 
competing variants increases, spreading in both linguistic and social space. For this reason, 
change is often formalised as (2), which elegantly captures the central role of variation.
(2) A > A/B > B
Considered in retrospect, looking back from one stage of a language to another, more remote 
stage, change may appear abrupt and replacive. In this sense the  nal product can appear 
resolutely Neogrammarian in character. But while change is regular in many respects, it is 
rarely so tidy and conclusive as (2) suggests. Change is not deterministic, and there is no 
requirement that, once initiated, a change must progress to completion (where ‘completion’ is 
the wholesale loss of an earlier form; see also Labov 2001; Mair 2004; Brinton and Traugott 
2005; Heine and Kuteva 2005). A change may begin and simply fail to advance, receding 
before it has the opportunity to accelerate and spread. It may progress to a certain point and then 
reverse, as was the case for analytic comparatives in English. It may reach an equilibrium in 
which the alternants remain in a state of stable variation, such as the case of [n] ~ [] variation 
in the pronunciation of the English -ing suf x. Another possibility is for an older form to 
become entrenched in a particular context or construction, such as has affected English shall. 
Once the primary marker of future temporal reference, its use is now highly restricted, virtually

---

<!-- page 430 -->

Alexandra D’Arcy
412
non-existent outside  rst-person interrogatives and formal speech. With respect to sound 
change, numerous examples of both lexical and geographic exceptions illustrate the great 
frequency with which innovations ‘fail’ to diffuse in full (e.g. Chambers and Trudgill 1980).
To suggest that this lack of a consistent endpoint entails chaotic, unordered trajectories in 
the progression of linguistic change would, however, be erroneous. Variationist research has 
uncovered a number of constants, a perspective that is enabled by the fundamentally 
longitudinal nature of change. This is because, although individual changes are instantiated 
through distinct pathways of change, the transition period from one language state to another 
can last centuries, enabling a nuanced view of the mechanisms driving and constraining 
change. We are thus once again confronted with linguistic structure: what happens to it 
during the course of change? The question becomes particularly complex when linguistic 
material is recruited to a particular sector from disparate linguistic sources, at different stages 
in the history of a language. This level of complexity is not exceptional – it is in fact quite 
common. Paradigmatic examples include adhortative lets in English ([you] let us go > let’s 
go > lets give you), pas as a generalised negator in French (ne va > ne va pas > va pas; Hock 
1991), the in ectional future of Romance languages (from Latin habere + in nitive 
constructions; e.g. French nous chanterons; Pinkster 1987; Roberts 1993), accusative cases 
in West African languages (from serialised verbs; e.g. k> in Gã, ‘take’ > ACC; Lord 1993), 
and complementisers in a number of African and Asian languages (from locutionary verbs; 
e.g. Ewe bé ‘say’; Lord 1976).
In English, ongoing change in the modal auxiliary system is giving rise to ‘a new 
taxonomical layer’ (Krug 2000: 3) as ‘quasi’ or ‘semi-modals’ such as have (got) to, need to, 
dare to, ought to, and be to are recruited from lexical verbs (see also Denison 1993; Warner 
1993). Indeed, the modal system is a paramount example of longitudinal instability: it has 
experienced constant  ux since at least the Old English period. The expression of obligation 
or necessity (henceforth deontic modality) provides a view to just one trajectory within this 
complex that is characterised by robust layering of forms across time. Illustrated in Figure 
18.1, these forms have developed deontic functions at distinct times throughout the history of 
the language, and competition between them is ongoing. A wide range of lexical verbs have 
been recruited for this single modal function over hundreds of years. As the examples in (3) 
demonstrate (from Tagliamonte and D’Arcy 2007b), the contemporary re ex of this 
diachronic scenario is that speakers use multiple forms to encode necessity/obligation, often 
in parallel linguistic contexts, within single utterances.
Old Eng.
Middle Eng.
Early Mod. Eng.
Mod. Eng.

1066–1500
1501–1750
ca. 1751–pres

(Tagliamonte and D’Arcy 2007b: 52; adapted in part from Jespersen 1961: 51–54, Visser 1963–1973: 
1478–1479)

---

<!-- page 431 -->

Discourse
413
(3) a 
I said ‘You have to come up’. I said ‘You must come up’. And to the person on the 
phone I said ‘I’ve gotta go’.
b 
Do what you gotta do in class. It’s not like you enjoy doing it but you have to do it 
if you want to go anywhere so.
c 
She would be like ‘No no no, we’ve got to keep going, we have to make it to the next 
level’.
In the English system of future temporal reference, cross-linguistically a notoriously 
multifarious area of the grammar, there is also ongoing, longitudinal development. The most 
recent strategy for futurity is periphrastic going to, which developed from purposive 
directional constructions with non- nite complements (e.g. Danchev and Kytö 1994; Hopper 
and Traugott 2003). This change began around the onset of the Early Modern period, but 
layering already characterised the sector. The traditional strategy for future reference utilised 
the simple present tense and an optional adverb. During Middle English, shall and will 
entered the system and began to mark futurity. These strategies compete with a number of 
other established ones, including the present progressive (optionally with an adverb), be to + 
in nitive constructions, and collocations such as be about to and be due to (e.g. Fries 1940; 
Visser 1963–1973; Traugott 1992; Nesselhauf 2007, 2010). Exempli ed in (4) (from 
Tagliamonte 2012), this wide range of morphological, lexical, and collocational options co-
exist in contemporary use, despite the different times at which each strategy entered the 
future temporal system.
(4)  a 
Who knows what the next century’ll bring? It’s gonna be quite interesting.
b 
Some’ll work, and some is not gonna work.
c 
I shall have to put the kettle on in a minute.
d 
Aye she’s at Workington but she goes to Carlisle in September.
e 
They’re spending Christmas and New Year with her.
3 Trajectories of historical language change
Sociolinguistic variables are the formal representations of heterogeneity, re ecting the set of 
delimitable options for the realisation of some linguistic meaning or function. To paraphrase 
Labov (2001: 84), such variables are in the grammar (the set of generalised conventions over 
a set of speakers, i.e. the speech community, who understand one another), constrained by the 
grammar, and cannot be described apart from the grammar. Perhaps nowhere is this more 
evident than in the longitudinal evolution of grammatical systems, which I take here, 
following Langacker (1987: 3), to represent all symbolic structures of language (phonology, 
morphology, syntax, pragmatics, lexicon).
3.1 Grammaticalisation
Grammaticalisation – the attribution of grammatical character to an erstwhile autonomous 
word (Meillet 1912) – provides the paramount case study of linguistic evolution, and is 
therefore of growing interest to variationists. The diachronic changes that are regularly 
instantiated as a form travels the cline from lexical status to increased grammatical status are 
long attested in the literature, and they are not particularly controversial. These include, 
among others, phonological erosion, reanalysis, metaphor, and extension. Grammaticalisation 
theory – the set of claims that have been made concerning these phenomena – however, has

---

<!-- page 432 -->

Alexandra D’Arcy
414
both strong proponents (e.g. Lehmann 2005) and strong opponents (e.g. Newmeyer 1998). 
One of the primary critiques against grammaticalisation qua theory concerns its status: does 
it have explanatory value in its own right, or is it epiphenomenal, explained by already well-
known principles of linguistic change (Campbell 2001; for a rebuttal, see Haspelmath 2004)? 
Variationist theory, however, as a largely ‘pretheoretical’ method (Laks 1992; Walker 2010: 
24), has little stake in this argument. The method tests predictions made by different linguistic 
frameworks, and while its stance on grammar as a group rather than individual construct is 
not compatible with all theoretical arguments, it is not itself inherently structuralist, formalist, 
generativist, or functionalist. Indeed, it is this characteristic that makes variationist linguistics 
ideally suited to provide key insights on the nature of language in general, including the 
embedding of language change in particular.
One such insight concerns the progression of syntactic change. Kroch (1989) demonstrated 
that when syntactic structure changes, all contexts are affected simultaneously and the 
innovation progresses at the same rate whether it is probabilistically preferred in a context or 
not. This is because the contexts “are merely surface manifestations of a single underlying 
change in grammar” (Kroch 1989: 199). As a consequence, contextual effects are constant 
across time (i.e. the constant rate effect).
This is not the case with grammaticalisation. Forms lose lexico-semantic and phonological 
content, change category membership, and spread gradually to new contexts, in some cases 
specialising for a given function. In this kind of scenario, the constraints governing variation 
must somehow be accommodated, necessarily absorbed or reorganised in the recon gured 
functional complex. Outside the variationist tradition, work on grammaticalisation typically 
concentrates on the set of changes affecting an individual form or collocation. In variationist 
work, the analytical frame includes not only the grammaticalising form, but all forms 
involved in encoding a particular functional meaning. This focus on variable domains enables 
examination of the ways in which coexisting layers in a grammatical subsystem accommodate 
to the incursion of emergent forms. As a consequence, variationist research increasingly 
demonstrates that incremental shifts in the operation of the variable grammar can be used to 
elucidate pathways of change over the history of a language. In particular, structure can be 
discerned from the distribution and conditioning of variant forms, and “this structure, 
instantiated in the quantitative patterning of variants across elements of the context” (Poplack 
2011: 212) provides a diagnostic of the transition between endpoints of change. In other 
words, variationist methods offer a snapshot of the structure of the system at a given period, 
snapshots which can then be compared. Not only does this enable us to trace the rise and fall 
of variant forms, it also enables us to discern their entry points into the system and the 
trajectories of their function.
To investigate the evolution of grammatical systems, variationists tap speech surrogates in 
the historical records (popular plays, journals, informal letters, and the like). This method is 
well-established in historical sociolinguistics (e.g. Nevalainen and Raumolin-Brunberg 
2003), though it is by no means unproblematic. Most historical documents survive by 
accident rather than by design (Labov 1994: 11), and locating relevant diachronic evidence 
has been described as “making the most of bad data” (Labov 1994: 7). Nonetheless, careful 
and judicious use of speech-like written genres has proven insightful on a number of 
theoretical and empirical questions concerning the embedding of linguistic change (e.g. 
Poplack and Tagliamonte 1996; Nevalainen et al. 2011). Ultimately, this work demonstrates 
clearly that grammaticalising forms are sensitive to the variants with which they alternate. As 
they follow a developmental pathway, it is quite common for forms to lose, transfer, and 
acquire constraints, mostly as a reaction to the activity in the remainder of the sector.

---

<!-- page 433 -->

Discourse
415
In their analysis of future temporal reference in Brazilian Portuguese, for example, 
Poplack and Malvar (2007) reveal how, over the course of  ve centuries, change consisted of 
a series of small adjustments to the operation of the variable grammar. The prolonged and 
 nely nuanced nature of this change highlights the sensitive role of structure in  ux, but it is 
also noteworthy given that frequencies tell a much more dramatic story. Distributions alone, 
as in Figure 18.2, suggest cataclysmic shifts in variant use, particularly during the nineteenth 
and twentieth centuries, but analysis of the constraints affecting variant selection reveal 
ongoing, longitudinal, systemic shifts across time.
During the sixteenth, seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, future temporal reference was 
encoded one of two ways: via the synthetic future (cantarei ‘I will sing’) or via periphrasis 
with haver (hei de cantar). Synthesis was the majority and default variant, favoured in frequent 
and unmarked contexts such as contingent constructions and af rmative declaratives. It also 
occurred with the majority of lexical verbs. By the nineteenth century, the effects of contingency 
and sentence type had been transferred and lost: the futurate present (cantaria) subsumed the 
effect of contingents, while af rmative declaratives became the favoured domain of the 
incoming periphrastic form (vou cantar). The synthetic future found itself relegated to residual 
contexts, such as non-speci c adverbials and distal futures, created when the incoming forms 
staked out their niches. For the futurate present, this was motion verbs; for the periphrastic 
form, it was proximal futures. By the twentieth century, only one effect operated on synthesis, 
which maintained a  rm grip on futures with non-speci c adverbial marking, a context for 
which it had no historically signi cant association. Periphrasis, which had emerged as the 
majority form, patterned in complementarity with the futurate present, in a complex grammar 
of variable constraints. These pathways are summarised in (5), in which each line represents a 
diachronic period (sixteenth–eighteenth century; nineteenth century; twentieth century).

The key observation overarching this diachronic scenario concerns the longitudinal 
reorganisation of the way in which future temporal reference operated in Brazilian Portuguese. 
Through the sixteenth to eighteenth centuries,  ve constraints signi cantly affected the 
choice mechanism: sentence type, verb type, contingency, temporal distance, and grammatical 
person of the subject. These effects, the forms associated with each, and even the direction of 
effect itself continuously reorganised over  ve centuries. By the twentieth century, sentence 
type and temporal distance no longer exerted constraint effects, though adverb speci cation 
had come to the fore (with binary effects on the two primary forms (+/–) and a ternary effect 
on the obsolescent synthetic future, which maintained a niche in the system, specialised for 
use with non-speci c adverbs). On its route to obsolescence, the synthetic future was jostled 
about in the system, losing, transferring, and acquiring constraints in reaction to activity 
throughout the future temporal system. Thus, although Figure 18.2 suggests a straightforward 
pattern of replacement, in which the periphrastic future simply generalised across the sector, 
this is not what happened. Individual forms negotiated new functions for the expression of 
futurity, redistributing the workload across time.
3.2 Lexicalisation
Where grammaticalisation has  rmly caught the attention of variationists, fewer have 
explicitly paid heed to lexicalisation.1 However, lexicalisation – institutionalised adoption of 
forms into the lexicon (Brinton and Traugott 2005) – offers a number of intriguing avenues 
for investigations of evolutional pathways, particularly as lexicalisation subsumes a number 
of distinct phenomena (e.g. ‘ordinary’ word formation processes, such as compounding and 
conversion, as well as univerbation, demorphologisation, decliticisation, etc.). Like 
grammaticalisation, the emergence of a new contentful form – with syntactic and semantic 
properties distinct from its source content – involves syntagmatic  xation, coalescence, and 
loss of semantic compositionality (though the outcomes may be quite different; see Brinton 
and Traugott 2005: 105–106). Lexicalisation is not characterised, however, by 
decategorialisation or semantic bleaching. Thus, where grammaticalisation necessarily 
entails reorganisation of the functional complex in which forms evolve, lexicalisation does 
not. In this light, speech act verbs offer an insightful case study for variationist sociolinguistics 
to explore lexicalisation pathways.

---

<!-- page 435 -->

Discourse
417
Over the last two decades, variationists have become increasingly focused on direct 
quotation – the reproduction of speech, thought, attitude, writing, gesture, and sounds. This 
work, spurred by the recent and dramatic innovation of be + like as a quotative verb, is 
traditionally presented with the framework of grammaticalisation (e.g. Romaine and Lange 
1991; Ferrara and Bell 1995; Tagliamonte and Hudson 1999; Tagliamonte and D’Arcy, 
2004). Romaine and Lange (1991: 261), for example, proposed a possible grammaticalisation 
pathway in which the function of like was extended from that of preposition to complementiser, 
a shift that entailed recategorisation (subcategorisation for nominal elements to 
subcategorisation for sentential complements) and extended the domain of like via analogy.2 
From this textual function it then continued to grammaticalise as a discourse marker, 
operating on the interpersonal plane.
Since Romaine and Lange’s proposal, there has been a wealth of research establishing that 
like functioned as a discourse marker long before it functioned as a verb of quotation (e.g. 
D’Arcy 2007, 2008), which raises questions about a pathway from preposition to 
complementiser to discourse marker (i.e. textual to interpersonal). Buchstaller (2014) has 
argued that it is the discourse marker, already fully established before the advent of be like as 
a verb of quotation, that  lls the slot adjacent to be to form the specialised verbal collocate. 
Although it has not (yet) been argued that this syntagm has undergone univerbation, most 
analyses treat be like as a single, compound verb form and not as be plus a discourse marker 
(though see Buchstaller [2014: chapter 1] for a different perspective). It is possible that the 
trajectory is not one of grammaticalisation, but rather of lexicalisation: an optional 
grammatical form has come to function in tandem with a lexical form, with meaning, patterns 
of use, and constraints on use that are distinct from those of its source composites. It is thus 
‘lexical’ in that it must be learned by speakers, along with the variable grammar that 
conditions it use (alongside other forms in the quotative repertoire).
In this light, the nature of the developmental pathway that be like has followed is of 
critical import. Ultimately, grammaticalisation affects an entire system, not simply a form 
that is acquiring a (new) grammatical function. As discussed above, forms lose, transfer, and 
possibly acquire constraints as they are redistributed through a sector, perhaps obsolescing, 
perhaps specialising, perhaps rising to prominence. Recent work targeting the development 
of be like, however, has revealed a remarkably stable variable grammar across its evolutionary 
history (Tagliamonte and D’Arcy 2007a; D’Arcy 2012; Durham et al. 2012), which, though 
short (c. 30 years), has been meteoric. In particular, three constraints have repeatedly been 
shown to have conditioned its use, both in apparent (synchronic) time and in real (diachronic) 
time: grammatical person, mimesis, and content of the quote. Thus, be like is favoured for 
 rst person mimetic thought, and appears to have developed to  ll the emergent niche as 
narrators began to tell stories based on a series of internalised events, attitudes, and thought 
sequences. Despite  uctuations in the strength (i.e. magnitude) of the subject, voice, and 
pragmatic effects on be like, these probabilistic trends have been evident throughout its 
history. In so far as being a signi cant predictor on the use of be like, tense has also been a 
constant in its development. The details of its effect vary regionally, but there is overarching 
systematicity within the operation of tense across time. Thus, although in most varieties the 
trajectory of this constraint entails gradual differentiation of the simple present and the 
historical present (HP; past tense reference with non-present morphology), the effect itself is 
continuously present and does not wholly reorganise (i.e. where this con guration obtains, 
the past tense consistently disfavours be like). This historical pattern is evident in Figure 18.3 
(see also Tagliamonte and D’Arcy 2007a).

---

<!-- page 436 -->

Alexandra D’Arcy
