Figure 18.3 The tense effect on be like across time in New Zealand English (adapted from 
D’Arcy 2012: 364, ﬁ g. 6d)
Further evidence for stability in the development of be like concerns its putative entrypoint 
to the quotative system. It has been suggested that be like was  rst used to introduce thought 
and sound, and only later generalised to introduce direct speech (Ferrara and Bell 1995: 279). 
This would suggest distinct pathways, or at least, distinct junctures in the quotative system. 
Haddican et al. (to appear) argue against this view, providing evidence for a single trajectory 
in which both stative and eventive interpretations of be like emerge simultaneously. In 
particular, the reported thought and direct speech readings have diffused at similar rates, 
which is consistent with the view of a single change in which different contexts are affected 
in tandem.
The diachronic picture that emerges across the history of be like is thus quite distinct from 
that which emerged across the history of the periphrastic future in Brazilian Portuguese 
(indeed, for grammaticalising forms in general). In this latter case, different constraints affect 
use across the transition period, until the endpoint of the change when the recon gured 
system stabilises. In the case of be like, the same constraints have affected its use, in the same 
way, across its full history, regardless of its status as incipient, mid-range, or nearly completed 
(on stages of change, see Labov 1994: 67, 79–83). Thus, where for example the Brazilian 
Portuguese periphrastic future was jostled about in response to changes in its sector (future 
temporal reference), be like was not. Its workload remained constant, irrespective of other 
ongoing, widespread, and diverse changes that were affecting its own sector (direct quotation). 
In fact, the system of direct quotation has undergone whole-scale restructuring over the past 
century and a half (Buchstaller 2011; D’Arcy 2012). In the mid-nineteenth century, the 
quotidian function of quotation was direct speech; quotative say was used 9 out of 10 times 
and the recounting of inner experience was exceptional. The ascendancy of say and speech 
reporting lasted well into the twentieth century, but underlying the overarching stability of 
the repertoire, mimetic encoding was emerging as an active constraint within the system, and 
the pragmatic restriction to speech was loosening as a range of content types began to spread 
and be encoded by an expanding repertoire of forms (D’Arcy 2012). As part of this latter 
development, the reporting of inner states and monologues was developing into a productive 
narrative genre, creating the niche that ultimately emerged as the nucleus of be like. By the 
end of the twentieth century, the system of direct quotation had developed an active and 
richly articulated variable grammar, in which a host of primary forms had a distinct role to 
play. Nonetheless, there was little  uctuation in the roles of individual forms across time.

---

<!-- page 437 -->

Discourse
419
Thus, not only has be like been consistently constrained; the same holds for the traditional 
and archetypal verb of direct quotation, say. This form acquired new constraints over time, 
but these exerted a constant effect from their inception and older constraints remained stable 
as well.
4 Conclusion
Although variationist research is not aligned with any particular linguistic theory or 
framework, it is predicated on a view of language that aligns quite naturally with 
perspectives that incorporate the role of meaning construal in interaction. In particular, 
when Weinreich et al. (1968: 187) explicitly shifted the de nition of language change 
away from individual lects (innovation) to the level of the community (change), they also 
focused the analytical lens squarely on speech in action (see also Labov, 1994: 45, fn.2). 
Discourse is the crucial site of language change, since it is here that speakers must negotiate 
meaning. It is also where they create new meaning. In this sense, ‘language change’ is an 
interactive process, incremented as communities of speakers develop different 
representations for encoding meanings. This differs from the structuralist model, which 
emphasises the role of endogenous properties of language in explaining change, as well as 
from the generative model, which foregrounds the cognitive states of individuals. In 
shifting from analysis of structure and lect, the window also opened on the role of social 
constructs within communities, and it was this view that predominated the  rst two decades 
of variationist research. Central to embedding is the question of social structure. But the 
question of linguistic structure is also vital, and it is here that this chapter has focused, not 
to relegate or minimise the critical role of either social factors or acquisition in shaping and 
constraining change (on social factors, see Michael, this volume; on acquisition, see 
Stanford, this volume), but rather to emphasise the link between established  ndings in 
diachronic linguistics and the variationist enterprise. What emerges from consideration of 
the discursive variable grammar underlying language variation and language change is that 
the established hallmarks of diachronic change (e.g. weakening, levelling, generalisation, 
bleaching, recategorisation, reanalysis, analogy, metaphor, etc.) leave distinct footprints 
across the history of a language, and these footprints have key implications for pathways 
of change. It is the emphasis on form/function asymmetries over the course of change that 
enables variationist linguistics to trace these pathways, underscoring the value of a holistic 
approach to diachrony: “The key to understanding language change is not to look at 
elements atomistically, but to see them in connection with other elements in actual use” 
(Joseph 2004: 62). Grammatical change typically does not proceed in isolation, but affects 
a system as a whole. Thus, to summarise this chapter, through observation not of forms but 
of systems we can discern “not only how they function today, but also how the ever 
changing and con icting needs of their users are permanently at work silently shaping, out 
of the language of today, the language of tomorrow” (Martinet 1952: 125).
Notes
1 
Indeed, lexicalisation has received far less attention in the linguistic literature in general than has 
grammaticalisation.
2 
Once like could occupy the slot for verbs of quotation, a dummy be was inserted in order to license 
it within the frame.

---

<!-- page 441 -->

423
19
Etymology
Robert Mailhammer
1 Introduction and overview
This chapter gives an overview of what etymology is, what it engages in and what issues are 
under debate, focusing on the following key points. First, etymology is both the foundation of 
historical linguistics, the starting point of the historical investigation of a language and at the 
same time one of its sub elds. In order to establish the etymology of an item one must backtrack 
to the chronological stage at which it was transparently formed for the  rst time, and account 
for the changes it subsequently underwent. Hence, etymologies give us a view of a language’s 
history. They make more general statements possible, and they are historical accounts of 
individual items as well. Second, etymology is distinct from describing synchronic processes, 
such as word-formation. It is an inherently diachronic perspective in relating one chronological 
stage of a language to earlier ones. Third, the research questions of etymology can, in principle, 
be applied to bigger and smaller linguistic units with suf cient stability, as etymology is 
concerned with the origin and history of linguistic elements. Hence, a distinction can be made 
between lexical and structural etymology, which has methodical rami cations as well. Fourth, 
etymologies can be graded according to quality, i.e. the likelihood of their correctness. In 
theory there is one correct history of an item, namely the events that actually happened. But 
reconstructing this chain of events and the point of origin is in practice a puzzle with many 
missing parts. Thus, some etymologies are more probable than others, and some of the key 
factors that make a good etymology will be discussed in this chapter. Fifth, etymologies are 
not necessarily con ned to one language. If the item in question came into the language 
through the contact with another language, then a complete etymology must also contain the 
history of this element in that language, and possibly a third or a fourth, if, for instance, a word 
was borrowed successively by several languages. Sixth, connected with this is the 
methodological position of etymologies that contain such a contact element as opposed to 
purely ‘internal’ etymologies. It will be argued that there is no theoretical reason why one 
should be considered a priori more normal, but that what is normal depends on linguistic and 
especially socio-historical circumstances. Consequently, contact etymologies are not per se 
inferior or a last resort, but a viable and sometimes even more likely account. Both avenues 
have in principle to be considered as theoretically equal.
This chapter is structured as follows. Section 2 discusses what etymology is and its 
fundamental signi cance for historical linguistics. Section 3 deals with the elements of an 
etymology and the relevant methodology, and also contains a quality hierarchy for 
etymologies. In section 4 the status of contact etymologies is discussed, before the conclusion 
in section 5 summarises the main points.

---

<!-- page 442 -->

Robert Mailhammer
424
2 What is etymology?
An etymology, as de ned here, is a historical account of the origin and the subsequent 
historical development of a linguistic item (see also e.g. Birkhan 1985: 20; Durkin 2009: 1; 
Krisch 2010: 317). This follows from the inherently diachronic nature of an etymological 
account. A given linguistic item may have only one etymology in this sense, but not 
necessarily only one origin.1
Etymologies are fundamental to the historical investigation of a language. Etymologies 
permit generalisations about historical developments, e.g. the formulation of sound laws, or 
about a particular synchronically attested phenomenon, e.g. a particular type of stem 
formation, and they are used as arguments to answer questions about genetic relationships 
between languages.
The historical investigation of a language generally begins with a comparative analysis of 
single lexemes, which leads to etymologies via the reconstruction of a proto-form. 
Etymologies then serve as the basis for systematic generalisations. For instance, the systematic 
comparison using the Comparative Method (see Hale, this volume) of German Apfel, English 
apple and Dutch appel, based on their earliest attestations in Old High German apful, Old 
English æppel, Middle Dutch appel (and Old Saxon appul), permits the reconstruction of a 
proto-form West-Germanic (WGmc) *applu-. This entails a statement about the changes 
between this proto-form and the attested forms in the daughter languages, for instance that in 
this case a WGmc. *pp changed into an Old High German pf but stayed a geminate stop in 
the other two languages. More etymologies with the same correspondences will then lead to 
the formulation of sound change WGmc. *pp > OHG pf that is an assertion about the history 
of German, according to which geminate stops changed into affricates. Etymological data 
can then be used to verify hypothesised historical connections. Thus, one might wonder why 
the Standard German phrase für nen Appel und’n Ei ‘for next to nothing’ is not *für nen Apfel 
und’n Ei with the usual German word for ‘apple’. From our reconstruction above the answer 
that suggests itself is that Appel is not a genuine High German word but a loanword from a 
Low German dialect, because one would expect an affricate instead of the stop. One can then 
go further and make inferences about potential contact situations, which point to historical 
scenarios. So the fact that the German lexicon contains only a few words that are demonstrably 
of Low German origin points to the dominant role of High German in the genesis of Modern 
Standard German (Schmidt 2004: 109–130).
In cases like these, etymologies do not serve a self-ful lling purpose, i.e. to  nd out about 
the origin of a word. Instead they are arguments in a historical discussion, in which etymology 
is more a fundamental auxiliary tool of historical linguistics. Without etymologies the 
historical investigation of a language is practically impossible.
However, etymological research can be focused more narrowly on aspects of the genesis 
of linguistic elements. From this perspective, more general statements about historical 
developments in a language are not explicitly part of the investigation. This can be illustrated 
with lexical items, the traditional focus of etymology:
Wenn wir ein Wort etymologisch untersuchen, dann richten wir unser Hauptaugenmerk 
auf seine Entstehung: Wir suchen zu zeigen, daß es aus einem nachweisbaren Grundwort 
nach dem Muster eines ebenfalls nachweisbaren Wortbildungstyps geprägt wurde. 
Solange der Zusammenhang zwischen Grundwort und abhängigem Wort noch voll 
durchsichtig ist, gehört diese Fragestellung in die Wortbildungslehre der betreffenden

---

<!-- page 443 -->

Etymology
425
Sprachstufe; sobald aber geschichtliche Belege und geschichtliche Überlegungen eine 
Rolle spielen, be nden wir uns im Bereich der Etymologie.
(Seebold 1980: 431)
[If we examine a word etymologically, we focus on its origin. We attempt to show that 
it was coined based on an evidenced source lexeme following the model of a type of 
word formation that is likewise attested. As long as the connection between the base 
word and the derived word is still fully transparent, this is a problem belonging to the 
domain of word formation of the language examined; as soon as historical attestations 
and historical considerations come to play a role, we are in the domain of etymology.]2
Usually, such studies are undertaken once suf cient historical knowledge has been acquired, 
as investigations of a word’s history are based on generalisations and observed developments. 
However, detailed studies of a word’s history are subsequently used to test hypothesised 
generalisations, which in turn leads to improved etymologies and so forth.
As a result, there are in principle two applications of etymological research, which differ 
in their immediate aims. One is focused on the linguistic history of a language, and the other 
on the linguistic history of a linguistic element. In practice, however, these two perspectives 
cannot always be strictly separated, because every etymology naturally points beyond the 
mere origin of a word. But conceptionally it makes sense to differentiate between the two, 
without claiming that this separation is necessarily rigid in practice. The above quote from 
Seebold also makes clear that etymology usually assumes a diachronic perspective, and is 
thus the core of the historical investigation of a language (Malkiel 1975: 120).3 This is 
because the origin of elements and structures that are fully transparently formed according to 
the rules of synchronic grammar is treated in the grammatical description of a language, and 
is not normally in need of reconstruction. Hence, cases like English hospitalise and capitalise 
are covered by the grammar of English; they are transparent and formed according to 
productive rules of word formation. But the origin of e.g. believe cannot be arrived through 
knowledge of the grammar. A hypothetical analysis that interprets be- as a pre x in analogy 
to belittle, bedazzle, is implausible because neither be- nor -lieve can be connected to any 
meaning or function – as a result the word is synchronically a simplex. An etymological 
investigation, however, shows that historically believe is a complex word, not quite of the 
belittle-type, but with indeed the historically identical pre x be- (Heidermanns 1993: 365; 
Oxford English Dictionary s.v. believe; Kluge 2002: 360).
Traditionally, etymology has been mainly concerned with words, but there is no reason 
why this should necessarily be the case. In principle anything can be studied etymologically, 
simply by asking the question, ‘where did that come from?’. The items investigated may 
range from a phoneme to an entire text, and thus the question might be considered part of 
different disciplines, not even linguistic ones in the case of texts (this has been a core interest 
of philology, for instance). Thus, one might wonder what the origin of the French word 
guerre is, but also where affricates in High German dialects come from, what the origin of 
the third person singular present tense marker of Modern English full verbs is, or what the 
sources of the plot in Shakespeare’s The Merchant of Venice are. As a result, it seems 
unproblematic to extend the notion of linguistic etymology and etymological research beyond 
words and word histories to anything that can be linguistically described.
To sum up, the etymological approach can be applied to any linguistic entity, phonemes, 
lexical items, morphemes and phraseological units and even sentences. It is the focus on the 
origin that de nes the etymological perspective rather than the subject of investigation.

---

<!-- page 444 -->

Robert Mailhammer
426
3 Objectives and methods of etymological research
3.1 The origin of words and structures
This section deals with the kinds of information an etymology aims at supplying, outlines key 
methodology and then turns to the question of how etymologies can be graded according to 
their quality. For reasons of space the focus will be on lexical etymology, but appropriate 
mention of the components of structural etymology will be made where necessary. The 
relevant information typically provided by lexical etymology can be summarised as follows.
The phonological form refers to the segmental and prosodic composition, i.e. the speech 
sounds and information about word stress, tone etc. The in ectional morphology of a word 
speci es whether a word in ects at all, and if it does, what its in ectional pattern is, e.g. 
strong vs. weak verbs in Old English. Changes in in ectional patterns are interesting parts of 
word histories, as they may re ect wider patterns of change, such as the generalisation of 
s-plural forms for nouns in English. They are also important for reconstruction; an in ectional 
pattern can be indicative of a historical process of word formation, such as different noun 
plurals in German (ex-consonantal stem like Zunge ‘tongue’ ~ Zunge-n ‘tongues’ vs. a former 
vocalic-stem Tag ‘day’ ~ Tag-e ‘days’), and differences in in ectional pattern can in uence 
the quality of an etymology. Details about word formation are essential for an etymology in 
the narrow sense, as this is its primary objective. In the above-mentioned case of believe, 
which can diachronically be segmented into a pre x be- and a base -lieve, the etymological 
relations of both the pre x (Proto-Germanic [PGmc.] *bi- ‘close by’) and the base (probably 
PGmc. *-lauba ‘trustworthy’), taken separately, are clear enough, supported by numerous 
formations in all Germanic languages. But actually no other Germanic language has exactly 
the same formation as English with the same meaning, i.e. a re ex of the inherited PGmc. 
pre x *bi- and the base PGmc. *-lauba, meaning ‘believe’. The form that is found shows a 
different pre x, PGmc. *ga-, e.g. German glauben ‘believe’ (< *ga-laub-ija-). (Actually, 
English used to have an exact match, Middle English ileven, which was eventually ousted by 
believe [Oxford English Dictionary s.v. believe].) For an etymology of believe, this is 
relevant, because the fact that it does not have a perfect morphological match in other 
languages casts a shadow of uncertainty on this otherwise perfect etymology. Theoretically 
– though very unlikely in this case – it cannot be ruled out that we are not dealing with a word 
with a completely different etymology, as we cannot anchor the whole word to anything in a 
related language. The existence of other formations based on a single root is also the reason 
why etymologies usually attempt to recover more of or even the entire word family as part of 
the reconstruction process. The case of believe illustrates the necessity of this; if it is not 
examined in terms of its inherited pattern of stem formation, the question whether it is a 
cognate of German glauben cannot be answered with certainty.
The meaning of a word is an essential part of an etymology, and is equally important as 
the formal aspects. This involves reconstructing the earliest meaning and accounting for 
subsequent changes, and is discussed in detail in Urban (this volume).
Syntactic information can be important in determining a word’s origin, and is thus another 
element of an etymology. For instance, Modern English melt re ects two distinct Old English 
verbs, a strong (meltan) and a weak verb ([ge-]myltan, [ge]-miltan). The two differ in 
transitivity; the former is intransitive, while the latter is transitive and in a causative 
relationship in terms of it semantics. This gives a clue to its word formation, because it is the 
expected semantic value of the  rst class of weak verbs, which also agrees with the form of 
the suf x (-an rather than -ian in class II).4

---

<!-- page 445 -->

Etymology
427
This constitutes the core information an etymology typically should provide. In addition, 
speci c cases may require additional information that is important to understand the origin of 
a word. For instance, sociolinguistic information, such as the register or dialectal origin may 
be important components. The history of the English inherited second person singular, OE 
þ
, ModE thou, is impossible to understand without taking into account the pragmatic 
changes that took place in the late seventeenth century, which effectively led to its loss in the 
standard language (see Michael, this volume). Table 19.1 summarises the etymology of 
believe schematically.
Table 19.1 Types of information in lexical etymology

morphology

syllable)
> ME weak verb > ModE

-s, PST -ed)

> ME verbal pre x formation >

1. ‘have con dence faith (in)/

2. ‘believe’ sth.

accusative case)
no essential changes
The information provided by structural etymologies overlap to a great extent, which is why I 
will be less comprehensive here, and focus only on some key aspects. In addition to 
ascertaining the origin of the structure under consideration, e.g. a phoneme, a syntactic 
pattern etc., studies in structural etymology are often used to investigate a part of a lexical 
etymology in more detail. Often this part is either more speculative or relates to a larger 
pattern that needs to be looked at in a bigger context. For instance, the etymology of a word 
may depend on a certain sound change or phoneme whose reality has not been con rmed 
independently. This can be illustrated with the case of English burn and its cognates in all 
Germanic branches (Gothic brinnan, Old Norse brenna, Old High German brennan). Its 
etymological connections are uncertain (Mailhammer 2007: 218). However, if it is assumed 
that the initial b is a re ex of PIE *gu h- as proposed in Seebold (1967), then the word  nds a 
more secure connection (Seebold 1970: 138; Kümmel and Rix 2001: 93, 220).6 It is evident 
that such an etymological investigation often requires existing etymological or at least some 
typological data in order to gauge the plausibility of the proposed development.
This is also true for ‘morphological etymology’, where the etymologisation of, say af xes, 
is dependent on the availability of established formal correspondences. It has also increasingly 
come into focus that entire paradigms need to be investigated if the origin of morphological 
units is to be determined (see e.g. Koch 2013). In addition, the reconstruction of grammatical 
units needs to consider shifts in function, as Koch (2013: 53) observes:

---

<!-- page 446 -->

Robert Mailhammer
428
Reconstruction of grammatical paradigms from a set of related languages requires more 
than a simple comparison of the forms that occur in equivalent paradigmatic cells of 
functionally equivalent lexemes (although it is wise to start from these); successful 
reconstruction further depends on the consideration of the kinds of grammatical changes 
that are likely to affect the grammatical words, which in turn helps in identifying as 
cognates forms which are not found in the equivalent functional slots.
It seems worth mentioning that shifts on the meaning side have to be taken into consideration 
in lexical etymology too in order to identify what Koch and Hercus (2013) call ‘partial’ or 
‘obscure’ cognates. These are cases in which the meaning of cognate words has changed so 
drastically that it is dif cult to connect two formally corresponding words at  rst glance, or 
in which a part of one cognate is different, or where one cognate was replaced and survived 
only in marginal use. The examples of the  rst group adduced by Koch and Hercus (2013: 
35) are English dog vs. German Dogge ‘mastiff’ as well as English deer vs. German Tier 
‘animal’, which are relatively transparent. English silly vs. German seelig ‘blessed’ is a more 
obscure pair and the meaning changes can even turn cognates into practical antonyms, as in 
Latin hostis ‘enemy’ vs. English guest. English believe and German glauben are examples 
for the second group, as the pre x is different. Finally, an example of the third group are 
Amurdak nimirdanmirdan ‘sing sorcery’ and Iwaidja -mirdan ‘sing’, with the usual Amurdak 
word for ‘sing’ being ngurldan (Mawng -miraw(n) is a cognate of Iwaidja  -mirdan).
But the origin of singular morphological properties can also be investigated. The Germanic 
strong verbs have as their morphological hallmark quite regular alternations of their root 
vowel (traditionally called ‘ablaut’) across their paradigms. They are traditionally grouped 
into classes according to the pattern of alternation (so-called ‘ablaut classes’). Although 
some of these patterns are inherited from the parent language, some developed only in 
Germanic. For instance the alternation between a present tense stem with e and a past tense 
singular stem with o found in classes I to V, e.g. in PGmc. *beridi ‘carries’ vs. *bar ‘he/she/
it carried’ (cf. English bear vs. bore) re ects the alternation between one type of present stem 
formation in Proto-Indo-European and the perfect stem, PIE *bhéreti ‘he carries’ vs. *bhebbóre 
‘he has carried/is in a state of having carried’ (cf. Vedic bhárati ‘carries’ vs. Vedic bhabhra, 
cf. Kümmel and Rix [2001: 76–77]).7 In class VI in Proto-Germanic, however, the alternation 
is between a and . Thus, a legitimate question in the spirit of structural etymology is the 
origin of this ablaut alternation, i.e. the ablaut grades in the paradigm of Germanic class VI 
strong verbs. Essentially two solutions have been proposed in the literature. A long-standing 
tradition going back to Neogrammarian research assumes that the class VI ablaut pattern is 
inherited from the parent language, i.e. PIE *a : *È (cf. Latin scab ‘I scratch’ vs. scbi ‘I 
have scratched’), whereas it has also been advanced that it developed in analogy to verbs 
where such an alternation arose through regular sound change, e.g. PGmc. *akanan ‘drive’, 
whose number is substantial (Ringe 2006: 190; Mailhammer 2007: 103). A critical evaluation 
of these proposals would go beyond the scope of this chapter, but one serious problem with 
the  rst proposition is that class VI verbs with genuine a-roots, i.e. roots in which the a is not 
the result of the combination of e and a laryngeal, is very small; Mailhammer (2007: 91) 
identi es only two out of 46 verbs. In addition, the basis for reconstructing an ablaut 
alternation of PIE *a : *È is certainly less  rm than for the familiar *e : *o pattern, which 
many verbs in this class would have had as a result of regular change. Thus, instead of 
proposing the spread of a weakly attested pattern across an entire class of verbs from a basis 
comprising less than 5 per cent of the total number of verbs, it seems preferable to assume the 
spread of a pattern that develops naturally in a substantial number of verbs (Mailhammer

---

<!-- page 447 -->

Etymology
429
2007: 103). However, this does not preclude multiple causation in the sense that both 
solutions are correct: verbs could have an inherited class VI alternation going back to a PIE 
*a : *È pattern, while others could re ect the more standard *e : *o. Both would then have 
fallen together in the Germanic class VI pattern. Thus, the ablaut alternation in the Germanic 
sixth class of strong verbs, seen as a morphological property, would have two different 
sources, which are dependent on the verb in question (see also section 3.2 below).8
A more recent development is the explicit suggestion that units bigger than words can be 
investigated etymologically (except, of course, the investigation of texts from this perspective 
in the philological tradition). Although such structures have frequently been commented on 
in historical descriptions, the question of origin has been made concrete especially after the 
formulation of construction grammar. Barðdal and Eythórsson (2012: 366) refer to 
Kikusawa’s (2003) point that cognate structures can also be syntactic (see also Hale, this 
volume). In spite of this, however, the fact that syntactic structures are the least conventionalised 
units in language does urge some caution. The less conventionalised and locked in form–
meaning pairings are, the harder it is to trace them (cf. Harrison 2003: 216). It is obviously 
much more dif cult to pinpoint when the noun phrase big house was coined as opposed to the 
word house. But conventionalised expressions, formulae etc. are in principle suf ciently 
conventionalised and arbitrary enough to be admissible as subjects of etymological 
investigation (see e.g. Thim [2012] for a recent study of the origin of phrasal verbs in English). 
Especially in ‘syntactic etymology’ the genesis of such conventions can be observed often in 
minute detail. For instance, the development of the passive in English and German involved 
progressively restricting the combinability of elements that previously could be combined 
quite freely, albeit differently in both languages. Such changes in combinatorics can be subtle 
and speci c, so that they may not be visible unless a suf ciently large dataset is examined. In 
the case of the English and German passive, both languages start out with constructions 
comprised of a copula verb (‘be’ and ‘become’) and a past participle. These are fully 
compositional, can in principle be freely combined, and have a passive reading if the verb 
supplying the past participle is transitive. However, by early Middle English the ‘become’ 
copula in the past tense, only combines with participles of telic verbs, and by the end of the 
Middle English period it is lost completely from the lexicon. By contrast, Middle High 
German does not have such a restriction and even extends the combination of ‘become’ with 
atelic verbs. The result is that ‘be’ becomes the passive auxiliary in English, whereas this is 
‘become’ in German (see Mailhammer and Smirnova 2013 for further details).
To sum up this section, the key information supplied by an etymology, irrespective of the 
unit under consideration, is how and when it originated and how it developed. This usually 
has several components and levels within the respective focus on words (lexical etymology) 
or structure (structural etymology). The next section addresses the question “what makes a 
good etymology?”
3.2 The quality of etymologies
In order to answer the question posed at the end of the last section, we must  rst consider the 
methods that etymologists apply in order to arrive at their goals. Unless the history of a 
linguistic item is so well documented that its genesis is actually recorded, this has to be 
inferred. Consequently, virtually all etymologies contain an element of reconstruction. The 
relevant methods are discussed elsewhere in this book, but two important strategies are 
comparative reconstruction and what can be called ‘reconstruction by intermediate steps’. 
The former relies on the comparison of related languages in order to  nd systematic form-

---

<!-- page 448 -->

Robert Mailhammer
430
meaning(-combinatorics)-correspondences, and this is often very useful for the more 
conventionalised parts of language, e.g. phonology and morphology. The latter, by contrast, 
attempts to  nd evidence of the process of change itself, e.g. overlaps of functions in 
morphology, polysemy in semantics or constructional change in syntax. These methods are 
supplemented by others, such as internal and inverse reconstruction, aiming together at 
maximising the different points an etymology can be anchored to. For reasons of space, and 
also because the technical side of linguistic reconstruction is covered in other chapters (see 
Weiss, Hale), focus here on a rarely explicitly addressed property of etymological proposals 
and this is their quality.
The two main points I would like to make here are  rstly, that etymologies may indeed be 
evaluated according to their relative quality, and secondly, that this is best done using a 
transparent and consistent system ensuring that judgements are falsi able and replicable.
The existing methodology usually works with an implicit binary differentiation, ‘correct’ 
vs. ‘incorrect’, but there is no explicit and accepted set of criteria that would allow such a 
differentiation unambiguously and reliably (see e.g. Krisch 2010: 317f). This is not to mean 
that there is no consensus at all – much of it is implicit, and usually  awless etymologies are 
generally accepted. But there are enough cases in which the specialists disagree on the correct 
etymology of a word, and this is also due to a lack of generally accepted and explicit criteria. 
Thus, especially for a non-specialist it is often hard to evaluate etymological proposals and 
this is even more signi cant if larger data sets are examined, for example for lexicostatistical 
or phylogenetic purposes.
But even assuming there is an implicit agreement on what constitutes an excellent 
etymology, it is not at all clear how less-than-perfect etymologies should be evaluated. In 
many cases items simply do not have  awless etymologies. Typically, there are several 
mutually exclusive proposals, which differ in their quality. However, if there is no explicit 
and commonly used set of criteria that would allow a ranking of proposals in such a case, 
then the only choice is to treat these imperfect etymologies as incorrect. But this is heuristically 
not helpful and can in fact be inaccurate, because many good etymologies are not  awless, 
and often even relatively weak etymologies permit the assumption of some kind of 
etymological connection. Especially if the etymological situation of a corpus, i.e. the degree 
to which the origin of its elements is known, is to be assessed, then a qualitative evaluation is 
more  ne-grained and actually more accurate than a simple binary classi cation. This can be 
illustrated with the example of English bring, whose connections outside Germanic are 
‘uncertain’ (Seebold 1970: 137; Kümmel and Rix 2001: 282; Mailhammer 2007: 218). 
Notwithstanding this judgement, there is a proposal that is serious enough to be considered, 
though it is probably not strong enough to consider bring as de nitely etymologised.9 As a 
result, at least one intermediate category between ‘etymologised’ and ‘not etymologised’ 
seems necessary. Mailhammer (2007: 158–159) proposes four grades of quality.
(1) Grades of etymological quality including criteria and examples10
Category A: Exact formal and semantic match in at least two non-adjacent branches of 
the same family.
 
Example: English bear ‘carry’; attested earliest in Old English and safely 
reconstructable to Proto-Germanic as *beranan due to attestations in all 
branches of Germanic can be reconstructed to Proto-Indo-European on 
basis of attestations in e.g. Tocharian, Ancient Greek, and others; Old Indic 
bhárati is an exact formal and semantic match to PGmc. *biridi11

---

<!-- page 449 -->

Etymology
431
Category B:  Transparent formation using a known pattern but no exact match outside 
branch, or only in adjacent languages, or formally and/or semantically 
inexact matches.
 
Example: German beugen ‘bend’; attested earliest in Old High German 
and safely reconstructable to Proto-Germanic *beuganan on account of 
attestations in all Germanic branches; connections outside Germanic 
require assumption of ad hoc but plausible sound change and semantic 
development, but permit setting up a root PIE *bheu gh- that the Germanic 
words can be linked to with suf cient certainty.12
Category C: Connection to roots outside branch uncertain due to serious formal and or 
semantic problems requiring additional assumptions.
 
Example: English see; attested earliest in Old English, and safely 
reconstructable to Proto-Germanic as *sehwanan based on attestations in 
all Germanic branches; no secure cognates; all potential candidates have 
serious formal problems, including the traditional connection, but a 
connection is possible.13
Category D: No known connections outside branch or only uncertain connections in 
adjacent languages.
Example: 
English writhe; attested earliest in Old English; reconstructable to Proto-
Germanic on the basis of Old Norse and West-Germanic as PGmc. 
*wreiþanan; Lith. rie"iù, risti ‘bend, roll up’ is the only possible and 
uncertain connection and insuf cient for reconstructing a Proto-Indo-
European root as connection to the Germanic words, and it is from an 
adjacent branch with known contacts to Germanic.
Such a qualitative ranking can be motivated by considerations in Seebold (1980: 437–444) 
re ecting the idea that the equations a reconstruction is based on may be evaluated with 
respect to the likelihood of their correctness, i.e. their quality. In addition, the quality and 
distribution of the attestations play a role. Words and structures that are better, i.e. more often, 
attested within the same language yield a better basis for a reconstruction than those that are 
attested only once (Seebold 1980: 436), because it is more certain that a particular item existed 
in this form with this meaning in the  rst place. This is clearly an important criterion. However, 
also the languages that support the equations are of signi cance. Fewer attestations in 
geographically more distant languages may carry more weight than more cognates in 
neighbouring languages. The reason for this is obvious: if a word only has cognates in adjacent 
languages this may be due to language contact (borrowing/convergence), and hence the item 
itself may be ultimately from an unrelated language, and may therefore not necessarily re ect 
a genetic relation. Arguably, an etymology for a word or structure that is found only in 
neighbouring languages does not have to be a priori incorrect, but this is without doubt a 
weakening factor. This is re ected in more recent etymological dictionaries and proposals, in 
which such an etymology is generally not accepted as having the highest quality (see Mallory 
and Adams 2006: 108–112; Mailhammer 2007: 153 for further details).
Apart from providing a method to compare etymologies according to a set of transparent 
and consistent criteria (those in (1) are examples), a qualitative ranking of etymologies 
permits the quantitative etymological evaluation of a corpus, e.g. a lexical class, such as 
nouns, a phonological class, such as affricates or the phraseology of a semantic  eld, such as 
hunting. Mailhammer (2007) uses this approach to determine how well connected the 
Germanic strong verbs are within the Indo-European language family, demonstrating that

---

<!-- page 450 -->

Robert Mailhammer
432
almost 50 per cent have etymologies of the lowest quality, which often translates into no 
etymology at all (see Mailhammer 2007: 168–179 for further details). This is then compared 
to the primary, i.e. underived, verbs of Sanskrit and Ancient Greek. Both corpora have 
considerably lower percentages category-D etymologies than Proto-Germanic. The 
implications of such a result can be far-reaching. In this case it shows that considerably less 
is known about the etymological relations than previous work claimed, which then has further 
rami cations, because either this re ects the history of research, or it re ects the history of 
the language. Thus, either the Germanic strong verbs are more under-researched than assumed 
or, if almost half of the primary verbs indeed cannot convincingly be accounted for within the 
language family (assuming that further research has been carried out), this suggests that 
perhaps external in uence by a non-Indo-European language was at work. The second case 
would have implications for how the cultural history of Germanic is to be reconstructed, 
because it has to be assumed that this external linguistic in uence manifested itself in the 
form of speakers of a contact language, irrespective of what such a contact situation might 
have looked like.
What follows from this discussion is that such a methodology – the qualitative grading of 
etymologies – provides us with a measurable indicator of how well items are etymologised 
and how this is distributed quantitatively across a corpus, which can then be used to draw 
further inferences, make comparisons and to uncover gaps in existing knowledge. The 
advantages of such an explicit benchmarking system – transparency and accountability – 
become especially obvious in comparison with a less sophisticated and less transparent 
approaches. For instance, Tischler (1979: 267) concludes that two-thirds of the core 
vocabulary of Hittite are inherited, but it is unclear how this was arrived at, what criteria were 
used and how the quanti cation process worked. Consequently, the  gure of two-thirds is a 
relatively subjective statement, and it is up to the reader to determine its reliability. By 
contrast, a method as outlined above is fully transparent and accountable, as the criteria used 
and the categorisation of every item is explicitly and openly formulated. This enables the 
reader to scrutinise the result on an objective basis. Furthermore, the criteria can be modi ed 
in order to re ect different criteria and standards, thus relativising the results (Mailhammer 
2007: 203–233).
To sum up, this section established that the reconstruction itself is at the heart of an 
etymology and introduced a way of measuring the quality of etymologies that can be used to 
quantify the etymological connectedness of a corpus transparently and objectively. The next 
section will further examine the role of language contact in etymology.
4 Issues in etymological research: contact etymologies
So far we have not explicitly discussed the possibility that the item under etymological 
investigation – or parts of it – originally belonged to a different language. That is, we have so 
far only considered cases of ‘vertical transmission’ in which languages are transmitted 
exclusively from a chronologically earlier stage to a chronologically later stage of the same 
language. However, languages – or, rather, parts of them – can of course also be transmitted 
‘horizontally’ in a situation of language contact, i.e. across languages that are different at a 
given point in time (Figure 19.1). The reality is usually a continuum or mixture of transmission. 
Thus, the etymology of a linguistic item can comprise one or more cases of horizontal 
transmission, resulting in what can be called a ‘contact’ or ‘external’ etymology (the dashed 
line in Figure 19.1). By de nition, this involves (at least) two different languages, A and B.14 
Contact etymologies often stop at the point of the horizontal transmission by stating that e.g.

---

<!-- page 451 -->

Etymology
433
a word is a loanword from language B, but in principle this is only half the job done, as the 
etymology can and should be pursued further until the point of origin in language B (or to 
another point of horizontal transmission and so forth). That is, a contact etymology necessarily 
combines internal and external etymologies, vertical and horizontal transmission.
Language contact has been a somewhat problematic aspect of etymological research for 
mainly two reasons. First of all there is the question of when to investigate the possibility of 
language contact as element of an etymology. Internal etymologies have enjoyed a somewhat 
privileged status as explanations of origin, and explanations based on language contact are 
frequently seen as a last resort to be explored if all attempts at an internal etymologisation 
have failed (see e.g. Thomason and Kaufman 1988: 57–62) or if clear diagnostics, such as the 
Comparative Method, suggest them. I have called this view the ‘Internal Development Bias’, 
and argued that there are at least two serious methodological problems attached to it 
(Mailhammer 2013b: 11 with references). The  rst problem is that vertical transmission is 
not necessarily the more likely or normal path of change. What is ‘normal’ depends on the 
social circumstances and to some degree also on the items in question. In a seminal study 
Milroy and Milroy (1985) identi ed several types of community and social constellations 
that are particularly susceptible to external in uence. They illustrate this with the examples 
of Icelandic and English, whose social situations mirror the role of language contact in their 
linguistic histories. Also, certain parts of the language are more likely to be affected by
“Internal” and “external” etymologies

transmission

transmission

Figure 19.1 Internal and external etymologies

---

<!-- page 452 -->

Robert Mailhammer
434
language contact than others. For instance, there is robust evidence to suggest that nouns are 
more frequently transferred than verbs in situation of language (see Haspelmath and Tadmor 
2009). Second, there are types of language change that are often caused by language contact 
(in appropriate sociolinguistic situations). This concerns, for instance, metatypy (Ross 2003), 
but also the restructuring of in ectional paradigms (Thomason and Kaufman 1988; Matras 
2009: 209f) and so-called ‘Parallel System Borrowing’ (Kossmann 2010), see also Lucas 
(this volume).15
As a result, it seems methodologically unwise to focus only on an internal explanation and 
to only consider external possibilities once all internal possibilities have been exhausted. An 
external etymology has in principle the same theoretical status as an internal etymology. It 
therefore seems sensible to keep an open mind and to  nd an explanation that is most 
consistent with the data irrespective of whether it is internal or external.16
The second reason is that ascertaining that a particular instance of change or a linguistic 
item is a result of language contact has proved dif cult. There are two issues connected to 
this. The  rst is the Internal Development Bias, discussed above. It has often arti cially 
complicated explanations based on language contact to a point where approaches are 
sometimes not even seriously considered because of a competing internal explanation 
irrespective of its quality (Vennemann 2006). As just discussed, however, there is no 
theoretical reason to privilege internal etymologies in such a way. Each case has to be 
examined individually considering the overall situation and internal as well as external 
explanations should be admitted as valid accounts of change.
The third issue has to do with the reconstruction of contact-induced change in general. In 
analogy to the reconstruction of internal change, the task is to demonstrate that the item in 
question is best explained as an outcome of an instance of contact-induced change as opposed 
to chance, internal change or a new formation. The problem is that it is presently impossible 
to infer the type of contact or even the source item just from the hypothesised outcome with 
the same degree of reliability as this is possible for instances of regular internal change 
(Lucas, this volume).17 That is, contact-induced changes often present themselves as 
‘information-destroying’ changes (Sober 1991), i.e. multiple-to-one mappings, in which it is 
impossible to conclude a prior stage just from the outcome of a change.18 Available 
generalisations are at best probabilistic. They are important elements in the argumentation 
but by themselves cannot provide conclusive proof (Ross 2013).
In order to explore the viability of a contact etymology, a hypothesis has to be proposed 
that can plausibly account for the item for which a contact-induced change is hypothesised. 
It would appear that essential elements of such an account are a contact language, a contact 
structure and an account of the actual transfer and the survival of the contact-in uenced item 
in the language that it was transferred into (see Mailhammer 2013b: 16–22 with references). 
There is disagreement on whether a hypothetical contact scenario requires external evidence 
for its existence, i.e. that two languages actually were in contact according to what is known 
about their history, and whether evidence from a hypothesised contact language needs to be 
available (see e.g. Mailhammer 2013b with references). I would like to argue that no such 
evidence is required to form a convincing hypothesis. First of all, the absence of external 
evidence does not prove that a given situation or language did not exist. Proto-languages are 
routinely reconstructed without external evidence. This follows neither from some principle 
that every language must have a proto-language nor from the methodology that is used to 
reconstruct internal change. There are languages without proto-languages and, except in the 
case of exceptionless changes, the reconstruction of internal change is essentially probabilistic 
and thus in principle no different from the reconstruction of contact-induced change. If a

---

<!-- page 453 -->

Etymology
435
particular instance of semantic change that involves a language for whose existence there is 
no external evidence can be reconstructed, this should be possible for contact-induced 
change. It should therefore be methodologically sound to take a linguistic item and to consider 
it as an outcome of contact-induced change if an appropriate hypothesis can be formed even 
if there is no direct evidence of a contact language or a contact situation.
That this is indeed the case can be demonstrated with Ross’ (2013: 34–35) brief 
etymological account of the free pronoun system in the Oceanic language Kaulong. Ross 
notes  rst some un-Oceanic features of the pronoun system: it is signi cantly more complex 
than that of a more typical Oceanic language and the comparative evidence suggests that this 
is the result of an innovation rather than an inheritance. He provides the following explanation 
for this situation:
These complications again witness to bilingually induced change rather than shift-
induced change. There is no longer a Papuan language nearby, but the presence of 
scattered Papuan languages on New Britain attests to the probability that the island was 
occupied by many more Papuan speaking groups before their languages were displaced 
by Oceanic, and the complications of Kaulong re ect the erstwhile presence of a Papuan 
language in which the community was once bilingual.
(Ross 2013: 34–35)
This is an application of what I call the ‘Blueprint Principle’ in Mailhammer (2013b: 15):
If it is assumed or known that an item X (lexical or structural) either in its systemic status 
or in its usage is due to language contact, then a hypothetical scenario is reconstructed 
that explains the occurrence of X by interpreting X as traces of this scenario.
Ross (2013: 34–35) assumes that the pronominal system of Kaulong is the result of language 
contact. He then reconstructs a hypothetical scenario that explains the synchronic state of the 
Kaulong pronoun system as the outcome (traces) of an appropriate type of change. Ross’ 
account demonstrates that the linguistic evidence is perfectly capable to speak for itself, 
which is precisely why linguistics, and etymology in particular, can often go beyond material 
evidence and uncover facts about history that cannot be dug up or genetically detected, 
making it an ideal discipline to investigate the origin of things, not just language (see also 
chapters by Epps and Heggarty, this volume).
Having established basic principles of how contact-induced changes can be reconstructed 
and how horizontal transmission can be accounted for in a contact etymology, I would like to 
turn to an important component of a contact etymology and this is the survival of the changed 
item in the speech community. This aspect has received less attention than the initial transfer 
operation, i.e. the change in the language of the innovator in terms of Milroy and Milroy 
(1985), but it is in fact the crucial point of a contact explanation (Mailhammer 2013b: 18). If 
a change is not taken up by the community and passed on to the next generation of native 
speakers, one cannot speak of a lasting change, and thus there is no basis for a contact 
etymology.19
A solution to this problem has of course a signi cant social component, and there is a 
sizeable body of literature about the social factors involved (Milroy and Milroy 1985 is a 
good starting point). In the interest of space I shall skip over this topic here and instead focus 
on the linguistic aspects of the actuation in language contact (see Mailhammer 2013b with 
references).

---

<!-- page 454 -->

Robert Mailhammer
436
One key point then is to explain how a particular instance of contact-induced change  ts 
in with the language as spoken by speakers that have not yet adopted the change, which will 
be illustrated with a partial account of a case study.
Mailhammer (2006) develops a hypothetical scenario that attributes the origin of the 
system of the Germanic strong verbs20 to the contact with a Punic-in uenced interlanguage 
in a complex scenario of bilingually-induced change, possibly combined with language shift. 
The task is to show that these changes would have been compatible with the language of the 
(monolingual) native speakers of Pre-Proto-Germanic to a point where they eventually even 
were used by them and transmitted to the next generations of speakers.21 I will single out one 
feature to illustrate this point. One particular problem in the explanation of what happened in 
the genesis of the Germanic strong verbs is to account for the change in the verb categories 
and their morphological representation. The Proto-Indo-European parent language possessed 
three morphologically and categorically distinct aspectuo-temporal stems, traditionally 
called present, aorist and perfect, but Germanic has only two temporal categories, present and 
preterit, which morphologically represent the Proto-Indo-European present and perfect stems 
(the aorist disappeared without trace in Germanic, see Mailhammer 2007). This is illustrated 
in Table 19.2.
Table 19.2 Schematic illustration of categorial changes from Proto-Indo-European to Proto-Germanic 
using the root of PIE *gu em- ‘go somewhere, come’ and its re exes in Proto-Germanic (Kümmel and 
Rix 2001: 209)
Stage 1: Pre-Proto-Germanic22
Present
Aorist
Perfect
*gu mî ié-
*gu ém-
*gu e-gu óm-
Stage 2: Proto-Germanic
Present
—
Preterit
*kwem-/*kwum-
—
*kwam-
The pivotal issue is to explain how the perfect took over completely as a past tense, i.e. why 
and how it ousted the aorist as the normal way to express past reference in many contexts. A 
contact-based explanation has to be able to account for why the (presumably Punic-dominant) 
speakers of Punic would choose the perfect as their way to express past tense and not the 
aorist, which would have been what they would have heard from monolingual speakers of the 
Pre-Proto-Germanic, and then how this perfect-dominated version of the Pre-Proto-Germanic 
could be compatible with the more conservative version of Pre-Germanic spoken by 
monolingual (or Pre-Proto-Germanic dominant) speakers.23 Thus, e.g. merely pointing out 
that ‘Baltic-Finnic’ had a categorial system like Germanic, i.e. a temporal contrast between 
present and past tense, and to conclude that ‘Baltic-Finnic’ probably caused the simpli cation 
of the Germanic verbal system in this respect (Scheungraber 2012) is clearly insuf cient. 
Among other things, it is not clear why speakers of ‘Baltic-Finnic’ would have selected the 
perfect in the  rst place as its semantic value was only secondarily associated with the past 
tense. By contrast, the scenario explored in Mailhammer (2006), explains in detail how the 
speakers of Punic could have overgeneralised the perfect on the basis of the formal and 
semantic similarity to their own perfective aspect stem, which already had the value of a past

---

<!-- page 455 -->

Etymology
437
tense in many contexts. However, it also demonstrates how the adoption into Pre-Proto-
Germanic spoken by monolingual speakers could have worked, in particular by pointing to 
the morphological advantages and the semantic compatibility between the contact-in uenced 
system and the conservative system. This is summarised in (2).
(2) Transfer process in the development of the strong verb preterit in Germanic (Mailhammer 
2006)
I) Initial transfer from L1 to ‘interlanguage’ (IL)
a 
Morphological perfect in Punic (L1) and Proto-Indo-European (TL)
 
Punic (L1): perfect expresses past tense/general perfectivity
 
C1aC2VC3-: e.g. Pun. yaton (< *yatan) ‘he gave’
 
Proto-Indo-European (TL): perfect expresses a state resulting from past action
 
C1eC1óC2- (strong stem): e.g. PIE *gu e-gu óme ‘has come’ (cf. Ved. jag ma ‘has 
come’)
 
FORMAL SIMILARITY: stable and predictable formation with salient ablaut24
 
FUNCTIONAL SIMILARITY: perfective aspect with frequent past reference in Punic, 
resultative perfect (with natural af nity to past reference) in Proto-Indo-European
b 
functional transfer of L1 perfect onto TL perfect
 
=> interlanguage (IL) perfect used to express perfectivity; with past reference it is 
frequently used as the main past tense, disregarding verbal aspect
II) Transfer from IL to TL
 
IL perfect is taken over as TL perfect with past tense reference due to linguistic 
advantage (consistent and clearer marking in contrast to aorist with heterogeneous 
marking) and functional similarity (resultative overtones of the perfect).25
5 Summary
This chapter introduced etymology as the fundamental  eld of historical linguistics and as a 
research approach aiming at  nding the origin of linguistic elements. It argued for the 
extension of etymology to structural elements of suf cient stability (lexical vs. structural 
etymology), and spelt out how etymological proposals can be graded according to their 
quality, and how this can be quanti ed. Finally, the chapter discussed the question of contact 
etymology, advocating especially that a methodological bias against contact etymologies is 
theoretically unfounded and should be abandoned.
Notes
1 
If a linguistic item has multiple origins, e.g. the ablaut pattern in Germanic class-VI-verbs (see 
section 3.1), then the question of how many etymologies there are depends on whether these 
etymologies can be integrated into one etymology or whether they are mutually exclusive. In the 
 rst case this results in one etymology with two points of origin, in the second case the relevant 
proposals have to be evaluated with respect to their quality (see section 3.2). Two mutually 
exclusive etymologies of equal quality (i.e. plausibility) very often points to a different solution, 
as both cannot be correct but they can both be incorrect.
2 
All translations are mine unless indicated otherwise.
3 
Mailhammer (2013a: 3) asserts that etymology in a very narrow sense can be ahistorical, i.e. if a 
linguistic etymology makes the statement that e.g. English bear ‘carry’ goes back to Proto-Indo-
European *bhér-e/o-. However, such a narrow application can only be done after etymologies have 
been established and these are as reconstructions, naturally historical.

---

<!-- page 456 -->

Robert Mailhammer
438
 4 Judging from the past tense forms of the weak verbs attested in Middle English, this verb then 
changed classes and assumed the productive weak pattern in -ed, rather than continuing the 
expected formation in -(te), leading to a hypothetical *melt(e). This change is also found in other 
verbs of similar structure, e.g. salt (salted, OE sieltan : sielte), but it casts some doubt on this 
reconstruction of the original structure of the weak verb. And there is no disambiguating Old 
English past tense formation attested.
 5 I assume that this verb was formed in Old English rather than at an earlier stage, but this is irrelevant 
here. The reasons are the following. It is attested later than OE gelfan, which is the common 
Germanic word for ‘believe’, and from the beginning exclusively with the meaning ‘believe’ or 
something closely related, but crucially not with the meaning ‘permit’, which is what would be 
expected based on a potential cognate in Old Frisian and the compositional semantics. If believe 
was Anglo-Frisian or even West-Germanic, one would expect other languages to have re exes 
with the meaning ‘believe’ and English believe to have attestations with the meaning ‘permit’ or 
something similar. That this is due to gaps in the data can, of course, not be ruled out, and it is 
therefore entirely possible that believe has a West- or even Proto-Germanic origin, but I take it as 
the less likely option; the consequences for our purposes here would be negligible anyway.
 6 This is necessarily a simpli ed account. First, the sound change is better supported than alternatives 
but not without exceptions, as Seebold (1967: 115) admits. Second, irrespective of the proposed 
sound change, the Germanic root vowel has to be explained, as the nasals in the Germanic verb 
point to a (thematic) nasal present stem, which usually has zero grade (Kümmel and Rix 2001: 93, 
220). Existing accounts suggest an irregular ablaut grade (‘ablautentgleisung’, see Mailhammer 
2007: 22, fn.29 for further details), but this can be solved if one assumes secondary regularisation 
of the root vowel in line with the prevalent ablaut grade, e-full grade, as proposed in Mailhammer 
(2007: 123–129).
 7 The o in English bore is analogical after the past participle. The root PIE *bher- ‘carry, bring’ 
probably did not form a perfect stem in Proto-Indo-European, the attested perfects are usually seen 
as independent formation in the daughter languages (see Kümmel and Rix 2001: 76–77 for further 
details). For the illustration of the ablaut alternations this is immaterial.
 8 Foreshadowing the discussion in section 3.2, this is not a case of two correct etymologies, because 
the etymologies for the ablaut pattern are in complementary distribution. A given verb can only 
have one or the other, depending on its origin, i.e. lexical etymology. There is actually another 
group of class VI verbs with a different history, so that there may actually be three different 
etymologies for this pattern. For further details see Mailhammer (2007: 89–103).
 9 This proposal argues that the Proto-Germanic ancestor of bring, *brenganan, goes back to a 
contamination of the verb for ‘carry’, PIE *bher- (English bear), and ‘reach’, PIE *h2nekõ -. According 
to this, *brenganan was backformed from an old causative stem br-angija- (Kümmel and Rix 2001: 
284). What supports this assumption is that both verbs are in a suppletive relationship in Ancient 
Greek, but it has to be agreed with Seebold (1970: 137) that it is still uncertain (‘dennoch unsicher’).
10 These grades were developed for lexical items, but they are also applicable to structural units. 
They apply to internal etymologies here. See below for contact etymologies and Mailhammer 
(2007: 151–167) for further details and other sample categorisations.
11 This reconstruction assumes completed raising of the root vowel due to a following high front 
vowel (Ringe 2006: 265). For an alternative reconstruction without a raised vowel see 
Bammesberger (1986: 185). This question does not impact on the certainty of the reconstruction 
and the etymology.
12 See Seebold (1970: 110–111), Kümmel and Rix (2001: 84–85) and Mailhammer (2007: 162) for 
further details.
13 See Seebold (1970: 378–388), Kümmel and Rix (2001: 525) and Mailhammer (2007: 164) for 
further details.
14 Transfer can occur from one language into several others and this can also occur successively. For 
instance, Nahuatl tomatl was borrowed into Spanish and from there into several other European 
languages (Durkin 2009: 146).

---

<!-- page 457 -->

Etymology
439
15 Of course, it is not always possible to know just from looking at the data whether the case 
investigated represents such a type; outcomes of change cannot be equated with processes of 
change. This is not an investigative principle, rather the existence of such cases suggests that a bias 
favouring internal explanations is methodologically questionable.
16 The Internal Development Bias may be less of an issue in more recent literature on language 
contact, but it is still much more alive in more conservative circles of historical linguistics and 
especially in traditional etymological literature.
17 There is, however, a signi cant body of literature in L2-speech production and perception that has 
been successful at predicting L1-induced changes, and it seems in principle possible to transfer 
relevant models and methods to other areas of language (see e.g. Escudero 2005 et passim and So 
and Best 2010).
18 Not all internal changes are necessarily what Sober (1991) terms ‘information-preserving’, i.e. 
one-to-one mappings, and this is especially true for semantic change (see Urban, this volume), but 
it certainly appears that especially formal internal change can be accounted for by the regularity 
hypothesis of language change much more than contact-induced change.
 
In some cases it is to some degree possible to approximate the rigour of the Comparative Method 
if a large enough number of items are transferred that regularities emerge. For instance if a 
substantial number of loanwords display regularity in sound substitutions (as shown e.g. in the 
case study by De Jong and Cho 2012), this can be used to make predictions that can be tested with 
hypothetical, new loanwords.
19 This problem and its fundamental signi cance are of course not particular to contact-induced 
change. It is a part of the ‘actuation problem’ in Weinreich et al. (1968).
20 As mentioned above, these are the ancestors of verbs like sing ~ sang ~ sung in English, i.e. verbs 
that form their tense stems by changing the vowel (ablaut).
21 Punic is a dialect of Phoenician, a West-Semitic language. The hypothetical contact scenario is 
supposed to have occurred between ca. 500 to 200 BC.
 
Matras (2009: 35–36) points out that the success of a contact-innovation involving bilingualism, 
like in this case, depends to a large degree on the ‘acceptability of the innovation’, which in turn 
is in uenced by its comprehensibility.
22 Proto-Indo-European is used as a proxy for the completely unknown Pre-Proto-Germanic.
23 An internal explanation faces the same problem, and as a matter of fact an internal motivation for 
these changes has not been found, though in principle multiple causation is possible (see 
Mailhammer 2006 for further details).
24 In West-Semitic the a in the  rst ablauting slot is characteristic for the perfective base form; in the 
perfect the second slot is  lled by i (mostly intransitive verbs), a (transitive verbs), or u (rare), cf. 
Stempel (1995: 518–519). In the Proto-Indo-European perfect only the strong stem is saliently 
marked by ablaut.
25 Social factors in favour of this transfer scenario included a hypothesised situation of common 
bilingualism lasting for at least a couple of hundred years, which also makes it likely that the 
number of Punic-dominant and bilingual speakers was substantial, and the assumed social prestige 
and dominance of Punic and its speakers as a result of a quasi-colonial situation (see Mailhammer 
2006: 46–47 for further details).
