emigrated to Canada at different ages. Reprinted from Flege (1999, Figure 5.1, based on data 
from Flege, Munro and MacKay 1995).

---

<!-- page 488 -->

James N. Stanford
470
Overall, Eckert (1989, 2000), Kerswill (1996: 196–198), Labov (2001: 463) and other 
researchers  nd that older children and teenagers are in stronger positions of sociolinguistic 
leadership than young children. For example, Kerswill notes that teenagers have wider 
networks than young children, thus giving them a greater in uence on the course of language 
change in a community (p. 198). Young adults can also play an important sociolinguistic 
role, especially when they have prominent social positions in their communities. Zhang 
(2005) reports that young adults in Beijing are constructing a new ‘yuppie’ dialect of 
Mandarin Chinese that indexes an extra-local ‘cosmopolitan’ identity.
To summarise, Kerswill states:
… it appears that neither children nor adults can be seen as the sole originators of change: 
the picture is complex, as it turns out that people of all ages can (and do) modify and 
restructure their language – though exactly what they can change is to some extent age-
related.
(Kerswill 1996:178, emphasis in original)
Therefore, while it is clear that children have a role in language change, it would be good to 
cast a wider net to catch other possible sources as well. To this end, we now turn to language 
variation in the speech of adults, adolescents, and children, as well as in-migrating speakers 
and other ‘leaders of linguistic change’ in the community (Labov 2001: 385–411).
3 ‘All-of-the-above’: language acquisition integrated with 
language change
In variationist approaches to language change, the analyst considers a wide range of possible 
sources of change in a community, integrating possible in uences from children, adolescents, 
and adults. Moreover, the variationist approach provides an “integrated view of variation and 
change,” integrating synchrony and diachrony in a way that Saussure did not (Guy 2003: 
399). Saussure advocated a dichotomy between synchronic and diachronic perspectives (see 
e.g., Trask 1996/2003: 267–268), and he justi ed this research paradigm by noting that 
speakers do not need any knowledge of linguistic history to acquire their native languages.
By contrast, variationist studies suggest a way of “connecting diachrony and synchrony 
that is quite different from the usual conception” (Labov 1989: 87; Weinreich et al. 1968). A 
crucial insight in Labov (1963, 1966) and Weinreich et al. (1968) is that change in progress 
can be investigated by observing synchronic variation (see also Trask 1996/2003: 267–285). 
In other words, synchronic variation is a window through which one can view ongoing 
change in the community. As Labov (1989) and Guy (2003) point out, speakers have some 
knowledge of language history in the sense that they experience different variants side-by-
side in the current form of the language, along with sociolinguistic information tied to each 
variant. While modern speakers may not know the historical origins of particular variants, 
they do have synchronic knowledge of the prestige, formality, social class, or other social 
distinctions which have been passed down from earlier social and/or geographic contrasts 
(Labov 1989: 87). As for changes in progress, because certain older variants tend to be used 
less frequently and tend to be used by older speakers, such variants sound ‘old’ to the younger 
speakers, thus giving speakers a sense of history and knowledge about ongoing changes (Guy 
2003: 398). Speci cally, Sankoff (2013) suggests that children are aware of the ‘age vector’ 
of ongoing language changes in their community:

---

<!-- page 489 -->

Language acquisition and language change 
471
I would argue that children are keen observers of vectors within the speech community, 
and that by the time they are adolescents, they are well aware of the fact that it is old-
fashioned to say, as grandfather does, j’ai parti à huit heures ‘I left at eight o’clock’. 
(Sankoff 2013: 37, cf. Labov 2001: 429)
In fact, Labov (1989: 96) views children as ‘linguistic historians’, and he  nds that “children 
appear to focus sharply on the pattern of social variation, and so reproduce the historically 
preserved variable patterns.”
3.1 Transmission, diffusion, and incrementation
Labov (2007) exempli es the way in which synchronic variation can be used to integrate 
language acquisition and language change. In this approach, transmission is de ned in terms 
of adult-to-child contact, i.e.  rst language acquisition. Transmission is the vertical dimension 
along the tree in Figure 21.2. Systematic features of language are faithfully transmitted from 
generation to generation because of children’s innate ability in language learning. For 
example, the Northern Cities Vowel Chain Shift (NCS) is faithfully transmitted to each new 
generation in the US Inland North region. By contrast, diffusion is the horizontal dimension, 
and it is de ned in terms of adult–adult contact, i.e. speakers who come into contact after 
their childhood language acquisition is complete. The dotted line in Figure 21.2 indicates 
diffusion across languages as adult speakers from different language communities come into 
contact. Finally, incrementation occurs when each new generation of children progressively 
increments the next stage in a shift, i.e. they advance a shift like the NCS one step farther than 
the previous generation (see also Labov 2001: 427–429; Tagliamonte and D’Arcy 2009; 
Labov, Rosenfelder and Fruehwald 2013: 39).
According to Labov (2007), the contrast between transmission and diffusion explains the 
lack of systematicity of NCS vowels among speakers in the St. Louis corridor (Figure 21.3). 
Diffusion results in imperfect learning because adults have an imperfect language-learning 
ability. While each generation of Inland North speakers acquire the systematic chain shift of 
the NCS through faithful transmission from adult to child, St. Louis speakers only acquire an 
incomplete version of the NCS; they only acquire a few sporadic, unsystematic vowel changes. 
Because the St. Louis speakers depend on adult-to-adult diffusion, rather than adult-to-child 
transmission, they produce some individual NCS vowels but they lack the full systematicity 
of the chain shift. Diffusion is similar to second language acquisition in this respect.
Thus, Labov (2007) shows how synchronic data analysis can uncover a relationship 
between language acquisition and language change, thereby illustrating the complementary 
roles that adults and children can play in language change (see Stanford and Kenny [2013] 
for an agent-based modelling approach to the NCS effects in Labov [2007]).
3.2 The acquisition of variation
Another reason to believe that both adults and children are actively involved in language 
change is that they typically follow the same variable rules. Children do not just pick up one 
variant or the other; they learn the variation. Research shows that “… even fairly young 
children produce variants of linguistic forms that are good re ections of the adult variation 
… Child data are variable in many of the same ways that adult data are” (Bybee 2010: 
116–118). The presence of similar rules across different age groups suggests that a wide 
range of speakers in a speech community can potentially be involved in a change together.

---

from Wiley-Blackwell.
Empirical investigations consistently show that children acquire adult-like variable rules at 
very young ages. Roberts (1997), for example, examines 3- and 4-year-old children in a 
daycare centre and  nds that the children had mastered the -t/d deletion constraints of the 
adults in the community, as well as the ability to distinguish mono-morpheme and weak past 
tense forms, just like adults. Similarly, Roberts (1994) and Patterson (1992)  nd that 3- and 
4-year-old children had mastered the constraints on  in/-ing. Fischer reported similar results 
for -in/-ing among schoolchildren in his early variationist study (1958). Even the highly 
complex rules of the Philadelphia short-a were accurately acquired by 3- and 4-year-old 
children (Roberts and Labov 1995). Anderson (1990: 67) determines that between the ages 
of 2 and 4 years old, children acquire a social understanding of the signi cance of linguistic 
variants with respect to age and gender. Other research on young children  nds similar 
results, including Romaine’s (1984) study of Edinburgh schoolchildren.
Since Labov (1963, 1966), many studies have shown that synchronic language variation 
can re ect and construct ongoing change in a community. While some variables may be 
stable over long periods of time, others may be undergoing communal change. For those 
variables that are undergoing change, if both children and adults in a given community are 
experiencing and constructing the same constraints on a given variable, then it seems

---

<!-- page 491 -->

Language acquisition and language change 
473
Figure 21.3 The Northern Cities Shift and the ‘St. Louis Corridor’, where speakers have some 
features of the NCS, but not complete acquisition of the systematic shift (reprinted from 
Labov 2010: 337). Dark symbols indicate speakers with a backed bet vowel (F2 of bet minus 
F2 of hot is less than 375 Hz). Reprinted, with permission, from Wiley-Blackwell.
reasonable to expect that both adults and children may be participating in language change 
together. Such a perspective makes it possible to move beyond a dichotomy of child 
generation versus adult generation when considering language change. After all, as Bybee 
(2010: 118) points out, “[t]he very notion of generation, in fact, is too simplistic a notion 
since children receive input from speakers of all ages.”
3.3 The role of caregivers in language change
Given that children acquire the variable rules and the ‘age vectors’ of the local speech 
community at very young ages (Section 3.2), the next step is to consider what input the 
children use to acquire those rules. In other words, “What are the data that the child pays 
attention to in the process of becoming a native speaker?” (Labov 2012: 265).
3.3.1 The classic model of parent inﬂ uence versus peer inﬂ uence
Research on child acquisition has often assumed a dichotomy between parent in uence and 
peer in uence (e.g., Labov 1964, 1972, 2001; Weinreich et al. 1968: 144–146; Payne 1976, 
1980; Bayard 1995). Results have typically shown that children are more in uenced by peers 
than parents (Peer In uence Model), a  nding which has included children as young as 4 
years old (Labov 2001: 429, citing Roberts and Labov 1995; cf. Labov 2001: 427). Labov 
states that “in the great majority of the cases that we have studied or encountered, children 
follow the pattern of their peers” (1972: 304; Labov 2001: 423–427, 463; Labov 2012: 271).

---

<!-- page 492 -->

James N. Stanford
474
For example, Kerswill and Williams (2000) investigate children of different ages acquiring a 
New Town koine in Milton Keynes, England. For GOAT-class words, young children (4-year-
olds) produced the onset of the diphthong like their caregivers, while older children (8- and 
12-year-olds) produced the local community’s pronunciations. Kerswill and Williams  nd 
that “peer-orientation seems to override all other factors” (2000: 94). They explain the process 
as follows, “[s]tarting from a parent-centered orientation, young children expand their range 
of social contacts to other, often older children, eventually forming distinctive teenage peer 
groups, with their attachment to youth culture and opposition to adult norms” (p. 68).
Likewise, Chambers (2002) suggests that children have an ‘innate accent  lter’ which 
enables them to ‘ lter out’ non-local dialect features, including any non-native pronunciations 
of the parents. Chambers reports on a child named ‘Ethan’ who was raised in English by 
Eastern European immigrant parents in Canada. Ethan’s parents had non-native pronunciations 
of English, including a  apped /r/ in words like cherry [t¶i] and tensed vowels in words like 
cell [se:l]. Ethan unconsciously ‘ ltered out’ these non-native pronunciations. The parents 
carefully observed Ethan’s pronunciation throughout his childhood, and they reported that he 
never used their non-native pronunciations. Moreover, Ethan was not consciously aware that 
his parents had non-native pronunciations until he was some years into school.
Children’s preference for peers over parents is not limited to phonological variation, and 
Henry (1996) provides a syntactic example from Ireland. In Belfast English, an inverted 
imperative construction is observed among older speakers, such as Take you your time for 
You take your time. For this construction, younger speakers (under 20 years old) are diverging 
from the parents’ generation and producing the non-inverted form instead of the inverted 
form (p. 87).
My own childhood experience of  rst language acquisition also illustrates how peer 
in uence outweighs parent in uence. In current generations of North American English 
speakers, there has been a dramatic change in the use of quotatives. Many speakers born after 
the 1950s, including me, tend to use quotative be.like. For such speakers, quotative be.like 
frequently appears in sentences such as She’s like, ‘What do you mean?’ and I’m like, ‘I don’t 
know’, and so they’re like, ‘Whatever…’. But the use of be.like as a quotative is notably less 
common among speakers born before the 1960s. In fact, the change appears to be quite recent 
and quite sudden, appearing in linguistic reports on North America in the early 1980s 
(Tagliamonte and D’Arcy 2009: 75, citing Butters 1982, Joseph and Schourup 1982/1983, 
Schourup 1982; see also Buchstaller 2006: 5; D’Arcy 2005, 2007; Cukor-Avila 2002, 
Durham et al. 2012, Odato 2013 inter alia). Figure 21.4 shows data from Hazen’s study of 
quotative be.like in West Virginia (2011). Analysing speakers in terms of birth year, Hazen 
 nds that speakers born after 1960 are clear leaders of the shift toward be.like. Note how the 
speakers born after 1960 tend to have dramatically higher usage levels of be.like.
For speakers like me, this change has occurred within a single household across a single 
generation of speakers. In other words, transmission has been disrupted. My parents were born 
in the 1930s, and they do not use quotative be.like. My sister and I were born in the 1960s, and 
we both use be.like very frequently. Clearly, at some point in the language acquisition process, 
my sister and I acquired quotative be.like from our peers, not from our parents – despite the 
fact that we lived in the same home with our parents during the  rst 18 years of our lives.
Further evidence for this rapid shift in quotative be.like is found in Tagliamonte and 
D’Arcy’s (2004) real-time studies in Canada (see also Tagliamonte 2009; Tagliamonte and 
D’Arcy 2009: 80–81). Surveys of quotative usage were conducted in 1995 and 2002–2003 
(p. 502). In 1995, the most commonly used quotatives were say, go, and zero (null), although 
be.like was present at that time as a minority variant (13 per cent). By the time of the 2002–

2003 survey, be.like had sharply increased to 58 per cent, making it by far the most frequently 
used quotative in that community. In these studies, the rise of quotative be.like illustrates how 
an entire generation of speakers can rapidly shift away from their parents’ features, most 
likely as a result of changes happening in the preteen/teenage community around them.
A similar case of generational change is found in recent studies of northern New England. 
In New Hampshire, many of the traditional Eastern New England dialect features are rapidly 
receding, including /r/-lessness, fronted FATHER vowels, fronted START vowels, ‘broad-a’ [a] 
in BATH words, and intrusive-/r/ (Stanford, Severance and Baclawski forthcoming; Stanford, 
Leddy-Cecere and Baclawski 2012). Such features had been transmitted from generation to 
generation since the time of the early European settlements in this region in the mid-1700s 
(Kurath 1939; Nagy and Roberts 2004). But among current generations, quantitative analyses 
show that the traditional Eastern New England features are receding among younger speakers, 
who are clearly eschewing the most salient, stereotypical Eastern New England features. For 
example, a young New Hampshire woman was asked whether her speech differs from her 
parents. She answered, ‘Absolutely! I hear my parents say “idear” instead of “idea” all the 
time. Drives me nuts!’ (Stanford et al. 2012: 160).
All of these cases support the notion that child language-learners, including me in my 
childhood experience with quotative be.like, typically prioritise peer in uence over parent 
in uence. Does this principle hold true in diverse non-Western societies?
3.3.2 In search of a more general principle: challenges from less commonly studied 
communities
As outlined above, studies show that children are somehow able to  lter out certain dialect 
features, usually disregarding their caregivers’ language features in order to focus on features 
used by the wider speech community. Labov describes the issue as follows:

---

<!-- page 494 -->

James N. Stanford
476
In some way that is not yet fully understood, children have the capacity to detect at an 
early age the fact that their parents’ language is not fully representative of the speech 
community and so is not the proper target for their language learning.
(Labov 2012: 271)
One reason that this issue is not yet fully understood is that prior work has tended to focus on 
well-known languages in Western, industrialised societies. Research on underrepresented 
indigenous languages can provide new perspectives. For example, Ochs and Schieffelin 
(1982) investigate the Kaluli people of Papua New Guinea, and they  nd that Kaluli mothers 
do not treat their babies as simple, dyadic communicative partners. Unlike the dyadic style of 
many Western middle-class caregivers, Kaluli mothers face their babies outward – towards 
other interlocutors – and they create triadic interactions. Likewise, in Western Samoan 
society, Ochs and Schieffelin  nd complex, non-dyadic interactions as young children are 
expected to address a low-ranking caregiver via a higher-ranking caregiver. Child-directed 
speech can also differ from society to society (Stoll 2009: 98; Heath 1983; Camaioni et al. 
1998; Pye 1986). Such research widens our understanding of children’s possible interactions 
within a community, going beyond simple mother-child dyads and beyond Western 
assumptions about child language development and socialisation.
Societies with clan exogamy provide further insights into language acquisition and 
language change. In the rural villages of the indigenous Sui people of Guizhou, China, 
spouses are required to have different clan origins; a Sui woman and man are not allowed 
to marry if they are from the same clan. Moreover, at the time of marriage, the wife is 
expected to permanently move to the husband’s village. In many such households, the wife 
speaks a clan dialect that is slightly different from the local clan dialect. Many Sui children 
are therefore raised in households where the mother speaks an ‘outsider dialect’ (matrilect), 
while the father and older siblings speak the local clan dialect (patrilect). In this social 
setting, it is possible to observe children as they transition away from the caregiver’s 
dialect and towards the dialect of the local community. Quantitative investigations show 
that Sui children rapidly learn to distinguish these clan dialects; young children may speak 
a mix of matrilect and patrilect, but older children and teenagers are almost fully patrilectal 
(Stanford 2008).
As discussed above, many prior studies have reported that peer in uence outweighs parent 
in uence (Peer In uence Model). But the Sui results suggest that the Peer In uence Model 
may actually be a special case of a more general principle. For Sui, the key linguistic 
distinction falls along clan lines, not along lines of parent-versus-peer. Like children in other 
societies, Sui children select a particular variety over other possible varieties. Yet for Sui 
children, it is not simply a matter of choosing peers over parents. After all, the variety that the 
children acquire (the local clan dialect) includes peers and the father. Likewise, the variety 
that the children reject includes the mother but not the father. The parent-versus-peer 
dichotomy found in other societies may therefore be subsumed by a more general principle: 
a child’s target of language learning is some local community beyond the primary caregiver(s).
3.3.3 Language acquisition and language change in Sui society
A detailed analysis of Sui society illustrates how language acquisition and language change 
can interact in the context of clan exogamy. Sui children have a mix of matrilect and patrilect 
at very young ages, but they eventually become fully patrilectal by late adolescence. These 
effects can be clearly seen in children’s tone pronunciations. Among the six basic tones of

---

<!-- page 495 -->

Language acquisition and language change 
477
Sui, Tone 6 has two variants: in a clan in the South, Tone 6 is pronounced as a high tone, but 
it is a low rising tone in a clan in the North. Figure 21.5 shows the pronunciation of Tone 6 
words by a 3-year-old girl with a North father being raised in the North with a South mother 
as her primary caregiver. Some of her Tone 6 pronunciations are matrilectal (‘monkey’) 
while others are patrilectal (‘ sh’) or in-between (‘chopsticks’); the latter result suggests that 
this child’s pronunciation bears the in uence of both dialects for some words. Very young 
Sui children also show a mix of matrilect and patrilect in other variables, such as lexical 
variables and diphthong variables (ua) and (ia).
A number of intriguing caregiver/child interactions may be observed as well. In Excerpt 
(1) below, the interviewer asks a 3-year-old child to say the word for ‘sock’, and the mother 
intervenes with a matrilectal form. Note how the child ignores the mother’s form and produces 
the patrilect form instead (quoted from Stanford 2008).
Excerpt (1)
Interviewer addressing child: 
nai juli nima ‘What is this called?’
Mother prompts child: 
fan ‘Say it’
Child: 
m:t ‘sock’ [in patrilect]
Interviewer addressing child: 
fan ti pia i ‘Say it again…?’
Child: 
— no response –
Mother whispers in child’s ear: 
j$ ‘sock’ [in matrilect]
Child: 
m:t ‘sock’ [in patrilect]
Figure 21.5 A 3-year-old North girl’s Tone 6 pronunciations divided by word (Reprinted from 
Stanford 2008)
mom 6'fish ' N= 7 
tsu 6'chopsticks ' N= 5 
man 6 'monkey ' N= 3

100 
200 
TIM E 
300 
T^ 
400

0
5

---

<!-- page 496 -->

James N. Stanford
478
In the Sui study, older children were more patrilectal than younger children, and teenagers 
were almost fully patrilectal. In this way, the patrilect is accurately transmitted to each new 
generation, regardless of the presence of in-married mothers speaking other dialects.
Despite this apparently seamless generational transmission as each child grows up 
acquiring the local patrilect, gradual language changes are occurring in Sui villages. Sui 
children are somehow able to accurately learn the patrilect (rather than the matrilect), even as 
they participate in ongoing language changes. In fact, it is possible that some Sui mother–
child relationships involve both transmission and diffusion at the same time. For example, an 
examination of Sui dialect geography suggests the possibility of long-term diffusion in the 
 rst singular pronoun (Stanford 2011), and dialectometry results also suggest the presence of 
long-term diffusion (Stanford 2012). If so, then in-married women may have played a major 
role in this diffusion. After all, in a rural Sui village, married women are one of the strongest 
sources of dialect contact because they have moved to the husband’s village from a variety 
of different clans. Yet these in-married women are also closely involved in child raising. 
Therefore, some Sui children may be simultaneously experiencing transmission and diffusion 
from the same person: the mother. Future research in exogamous societies may make it 
possible to tease apart these relationships in more detail and to determine their role in 
language acquisition and change.
4 Conclusion
Some approaches to language change emphasise young children’s  rst language acquisition as 
a primary driver of change, while other studies explore the role of adolescents and adults. It 
may be useful to consider an ‘all-of-the-above’ community-oriented approach that integrates 
language acquisition and language change: speakers of all ages participate in language change 
as they interact together in a multi-generational community. While young children may not be 
the only drivers of language change, they may periodically reanalyse a component of the 
grammar and instigate signi cant changes. Moreover, in some endangered language 
communities, a generation of young children can affect the linguistic future of the community. 
Within the span of a single generation, parent-to-child transmission can be abruptly broken or 
seriously attenuated when young children fail to receive adequate input in the heritage language. 
This can lead to sudden language loss or sudden grammatical and phonological shifts in the 
youngest generation. In many other cases, however, young children may only have a minimal 
role in language change. Instead, adolescents, young adults, and other socially in uential 
speakers lead the changes (e.g., Kerswill 1996; Eckert 1989, 2000; Labov 2001: 385–411).
While various age cohorts may be involved in language change in a given community, 
different age cohorts have different levels of in uence. Older children and adolescents are 
strongly in uenced by an ‘outward orientation’ (Labov 2012: 267, 272), which causes them 
to conform to a target speech community. This target speech community often – but not 
always – consists of the child’s peers. In light of clan-exogamous societies, the notion that 
peer in uence outweighs parent in uence should be subsumed by a more general principle: 
a child’s target of language learning is some local community beyond the primary caregiver. 
Further, the in uence of caregivers appears to be limited to very young children. In the Sui 
research described here, young children were able to ‘ lter out’ many non-local dialect 
features of their primary caregivers. Such  ltering processes can sometimes result in 
generational changes as children follow their ‘outward bound’ orientation towards a wider 
community. Other examples include the rapid rise of quotative be.like in North America and 
the decline of traditional Eastern New England pronunciations in New Hampshire.

---

<!-- page 497 -->

Language acquisition and language change 
479
The mysteries of language acquisition and language change are likely to continue 
challenging researchers for generations to come. Hopefully, each new generation will work 
towards a fuller understanding of these complex linguistic processes and their role in human 
society.
