<!-- page 521 -->

503
23
Language use, cognitive 
processes and linguistic change
Joan Bybee and Clay Beckner
1 Introduction
Recent work in historical linguistics has bene ted greatly from a better understanding of the 
cognitive processes underlying language production and perception, and from the study of 
the way change takes place during language use. The view of language change that is 
emerging from recent studies is that change is gradual and takes place in small increments 
during usage events, as language users apply cognitive processes to the task of communicating 
with one another. As each usage event has an impact on users’ cognitive representations, 
change can take place in both children and adults. We outline this usage-based perspective in 
section 2 and apply it in subsequent sections as we survey the known psycholinguistic or 
cognitive processes that serve as the mechanisms of language change.
Section 3 is devoted to the major cognitive processes involved in language change that 
have been identi ed in the literature. The cognitive process most fundamental to language 
and language change is categorisation, which we discuss in section 3.1. Categorisation occurs 
on all levels of language and changes as experience changes and as language is used. Related 
to categorisation is the more complex set of relations that constitute analogy. While analogy 
has often been invoked in discussion of language change, we argue in section 3.2 that 
phenomena identi ed as analogical change are best accounted for by mechanisms which 
extend constructions or schemata, rather than by viewing analogy (especially proportional 
analogy) as a separate mechanism. Next (in section 3.3) we discuss the chunking process, by 
which sequences of units come to be stored in memory as single units. We consider chunking 
to be the basis of the formation of constituents and constructions, as well as the mechanism 
that triggers changes in constituent structure. This is followed in section 3.4 by a discussion 
of phonetic reduction both in regular sound change and within sequences established by the 
chunking process. Habituation and semantic bleaching, which occur in the chunking process 
and also in grammaticalisation, are discussed in section 3.5. Related both to chunking and 
semantic bleaching are the processes by which analysability and compositionality are lost in 
chunks, and this is discussed in section 3.6. Grammaticalising constructions and chunks also 
change meaning by inferences made by the listener in conversation. Inferencing has also 
been studied in the laboratory, and we brie y discuss this phenomenon in section 3.7. 
Chunking, phonetic reduction, semantic bleaching, loss of analysability and compositionality 
and change by inference are all related to relatively high token frequency and especially to 
increases in frequency. The  nal cognitive mechanism we discuss (in section 3.8) is a factor

---

<!-- page 522 -->

Joan Bybee and Clay Beckner
504
in that frequency increase. The tendency to repeat a word or construction that has just been 
used, known as ‘priming’ in the psycholinguistic literature, leads to frequency increases and 
is operative in grammaticalisation.
2 Cognition, usage-based theory, and language change
The very notion that cognitive or psycholinguistic processes are involved in language change 
highlights the factors operative during usage events – factors affecting the speaker and 
listener in communicative situations. Our approach does not assign these factors to 
performance, with effects on grammar coming only upon reanalysis during  rst language 
acquisition (cf. Halle 1962; Roberts and Roussou 2003; Lightfoot 2006), but rather we take 
the perspective that all experience with language has an immediate impact on cognitive 
representations, that is, on grammar (Bybee 2006). We argue that the frequency with which 
words and structures are used, the inferences that are drawn during communication and the 
patterns found in discourse and interaction are taken to be among the factors that shape 
grammar. Meaning gained from usage events and their context, including encyclopaedic 
knowledge, are accessed without clear limits on what is linguistic and what is not. Similarly, 
in the phonological domain, phonetic variation is included in the grammar because all 
phonological experience has an impact on representation. Finally, the distinction between 
lexicon and grammar is not rigid; words are included in constructions; the lexicon contains 
many conventionalised phrases (called ‘prefabs’) even if they are predictable in meaning and 
form (Erman and Warren 2000; Wray 2002).
A model that incorporates these points will be equally applicable to psycholinguistic 
phenomena and to language change. In this section we brie y outline some of the features of 
a usage-based model (Barlow and Kemmer 2000; Bybee 2006, 2010).
In order for usage factors to impact language structure, it is necessary to assume a dynamic 
system that changes with experience. Each encounter with an instance of a structure becomes 
part of the cognitive system: if it is considered ‘the same’ as an existing stored set of instances 
(an exemplar), it is added to that exemplar, strengthening it. If it shows differences, it is stored 
close to similar exemplars (Bybee 2000; Pierrehumbert 2001). In this way variation is directly 
represented in clouds of similar exemplars. Also, increases and decreases in frequency and 
changes in distribution of variants are registered in cognitive representations, bringing about 
small grammatical changes that can increase in magnitude over time. Exemplar models are able 
to account for the fact that languages exhibit quasi-stable categories over time, while also 
undergoing gradual change. Other similarities among stored units (words, phrases, constructions) 
are represented in a network that reveals shared phonological and semantic features.
Repeated combinations of words are stored together producing prefabs or ‘chunks’. When 
such chunks have a schematic slot where a set of words or morphemes can occur, as in the 
example X drives Y crazy, mad, insane, up the wall, the word sequences are grouped into 
constructions. We will use this term in a fairly general way to indicate word combinations 
with a particular function and that are at least partially schematic (Goldberg 2006).
Some of the inspiration of usage-based theory comes from a close examination of the 
process of grammaticalisation, the process by which lexical items in constructions take on 
grammatical properties and functions (Bybee, Perkins, and Pagliuca 1994; Hopper and 
Traugott 2003). The clear directionality and similarity of grammaticalisation across languages 
result from the particular cognitive mechanisms that apply repeatedly in language use and 
over time accumulate to produce change. Many of these mechanisms will be discussed 
below. We also see directionality in other types of change, i.e., analogical change and sound

---

<!-- page 523 -->

Language use, cognitive processes and linguistic change 
505
change. In the following sections we discuss the known cognitive processes that operate on 
linguistic meaning and form to shape language in an ongoing, dynamic way. All of the 
cognitive processes discussed have domain-general applications – that is, they are not 
restricted to language but also apply in other cognitive domains (Bybee 2010).
3 Cognitive processes in usage-based change
3.1 Categorisation and changes in categorisation
The most fundamental cognitive process shaping language is categorisation; indeed, 
categorisation is fundamental to all cognition and without it language and everything else 
would be impossible. Categories are  rst established in acquisition, but categorisation is also 
the basis of all language use events. Categorisation occurs when input is sorted for similarity 
to stored representations and assigned to an existing category or when this is not possible, the 
input begins the formation of a new category. Sorting and matching on the basis of similarity 
applies at all levels – from phonetic features to words, phrases, constructions and meaning in 
the broadest sense. Changes in the properties of the members of the category can cause it to 
shift, often very gradually, over time.
Many of the linguistic categories that have been investigated appear to have a prototype 
structure in which members of the category share properties with a central member, but not 
necessarily with all other members of the category. As a result, some members are more 
typical of the category than others, and the categories themselves may stretch over a chain of 
family resemblances (Rosch and Mervis 1975). Evidence for such an organisation has been 
found for phonetic categories (both at the phonemic and allophonic levels; Miller 1994), in 
morphological word classes (Bybee and Moder 1983), in the semantics of words (Geeraerts 
1997), and in the set of items that can occur in a schematic position within a construction 
(Bybee and Eddington 2006).
As mentioned in the last section, we adopt here an exemplar model in which all tokens of 
experience have some impact on cognitive representation. Exemplars are categorised together 
if they are highly similar. The resulting categories are often organised around a high-
frequency exemplar or exemplars; other members may be more or less similar to the central 
member(s). In this type of organisation, prototype effects emerge. But other sorts of 
organisation are also possible and remain to be investigated (Bybee and Eddington 2006).
Exemplar categories change with experience, both in children and adults. For instance, 
when sound change is occurring, new exemplars of phonetic categories are experienced. 
These new exemplars shift the category gradually in a new direction, and low frequency 
exemplars that are very different from the new direction may fall out of use. It is also possible 
for a second centre for the category to emerge in certain contexts and in such a case a category 
can split (Pierrehumbert 2001).
When constructional change is ongoing, a position originally occupied by a single word in 
an expression can be replaced with a semantically similar word, beginning a process of 
schematisation in which a new category is built (Israel 1996; Wilson 2009). For instance, 
Wilson (2009) traces the very productive Spanish quedarse + ADJ ‘to become ADJ’ to an 
early occurrence of quedarse solo ‘to end up alone’. From this one expression others were 
spawned by similarity: quedarse viudo ‘to become a widower’; quedarse huérfano ‘to 
become orphaned’ and so on. Now this adjective position can be found in corpora with 69 
different types distributed over several categories, each with their own central member 
(Bybee and Eddington 2006).

---

<!-- page 524 -->

Joan Bybee and Clay Beckner
506
As we will see below, semantic change in words, phrases and constructions can also create 
new categories of meaning as semantic and pragmatic properties of the context become 
associated with the linguistic form.
3.2 Analogical change
The ability to use analogical reasoning has traditionally been cited as a primary cognitive or 
psycholinguistic factor in language change. (Recent accounts of the traditional view can be 
found in Anttila 2003 and Hock 2003.) Analogical change has been considered one of the 
basic types of change, especially in morphology but also in morphosyntax (see textbook 
treatments in Hock 1986 and Trask 1996). Included under this rubric in recent treatments of 
morphology are analogical levelling and extension, and a few other types of change such as 
folk etymology (see Anderson, this volume). The extension of constructions to new lexical 
items or contexts has also been treated as analogical change (Skousen 1989; Eddington 2000, 
Fischer 2008; Bybee 2010). This section examines a wide range of changes that have been 
called ‘analogical’ and concludes that they all fall under the umbrella of changes in 
categorisation and that a separate mechanism of change dubbed ‘analogical’ is not necessary.
Analogical reasoning is considered a basic type of human reasoning. In domain-general 
terms it is the process of recognising structural similarities in two domains, despite differences 
in the objects in those domains (Gentner 1983). Such a relationship can be stated as a 
proportional analogy as in this example of analogical levelling or regularisation, by which a 
morphological form that required a vowel alternation is replaced by one which does not:
(1) talk : talked :: leap : leaped (rather than leapt)
Indeed, linguistic treatments of analogical change usually cite proportional analogy as the 
most basic type (Trask 1996; Hock 2003). However, most linguists would probably agree 
that the mechanism used by speakers to create a levelled form such as leaped is better 
understood as the generalisation of the productive rule or schema for forming past tense in 
English; that is, given the productive schema [[VERB] + ed ]past, a new verb is added to the 
schematic category and that verb thereby becomes regular.
Similarly, the much rarer change of analogical extension, by which an alternation in a 
morphological context is extended to new items, is also not accomplished by analogical 
reasoning but rather by changes in existing categories. For example, consider the one class of 
Old English Strong Verbs that has added a substantial number of new members – the class 
that includes cling, clung and swing, swung. New members of this class share the property of 
ending in a velar consonant – e.g. sling, slung; hang, hung; and strike, struck; dig, dug. 
However, no four-part proportional analogy can be set up for some of these verbs (in 
particular strike and dig) because the original members of the class all had a nasal and in 
addition, the base vowel was [®]. Thus no proportional model for struck as the past of strike 
is possible. Rather, as argued in Bybee and Moder (1983), a schema for past tense for this 
class of verbs constituted a category with certain phonological properties – the vowel [] and 
a  nal velar (prototypically nasal, but not necessarily so). This phonological sequence 
strongly suggested ‘past’, so speakers came to use it to produce forms such as struck and dug, 
which became established because of their similarity to the existing past tense forms. Note 
that reference need only be made to the past forms of the existing verbs, so that the fact that 
strike appears to have the wrong vowel to be a member of this category is irrelevant in this 
account.

---

<!-- page 525 -->

Language use, cognitive processes and linguistic change 
507
Thus analogical change in morphology (and as mentioned above, syntax) is based on 
categorisation. When the schematic categories that form the open slots in constructions 
take in new items, we can speak of analogical change, but it is important to note that such 
change is almost never triggered by a single instance displaying the pattern, as the 
proportional analogy model would predict. Rather, it requires a schematic category formed 
over at least a handful of members with highly similar form, as in the case of the cling, 
clung verbs (or meaning, in the case of constructional change). In other cases, a schematic 
category may be formed over a large number of members, in which case similarity is less 
important. Among the members of the category, one may be more important in serving as 
the model for a new form, in which case we speak of ‘item-based analogy’ (Skousen 1989; 
Eddington 2000).
Analogical change is subject to usage effects related to the token frequency of the forms 
involved and the type frequency of the schema employed. In general, there is a strong 
tendency for high-frequency in ected forms to resist analogical change while lower-
frequency forms are subject to reformation by productive schemas (Hooper 1976). In an 
exemplar model, high-frequency forms have stronger cognitive representations, which 
makes them easier to access and less likely to be replaced (Bybee 1985). In this case, since 
less-frequent items are more likely to change, it is conceivable that  rst language acquisition 
is involved in the change: less-frequent items might not be learned, making them subject to 
change from widespread patterns (e.g., see Dowman, Kirby, and Grif ths 2006). Beckner 
and Wedel (2010) show via computer simulation that either acquisition or ongoing usage 
could, in principle, account for known patterns of regularisation in morphology. However, 
Bybee and Slobin (1982)  nd that acquisition alone does not account for certain ongoing 
trends in English morphological change. In their experiment, only older children and adults 
are found to produce the past tense of English verbs in a way that is consistent with a 
current pattern of change in English (the regularisation of -t and -d  nal verbs). Nevertheless, 
children and adults make use of similar cognitive processes, and Bybee and Slobin (1982) 
agree that as part of the acquisition process, young children may contribute to morphological 
change.
The other usage factor important to analogical change is the type frequency of the pattern. 
Type frequency refers to the number of distinct lexical types that participate in the schema or 
construction. A number of researchers have noted that productivity (the likelihood that a 
construction is applied to new items) correlates highly with type frequency (MacWhinney 
1978; Bybee 1985; Baayen 1993). A pattern such as the regular -ed past tense in English 
applies to thousands of verbs and is extremely productive, while the minor irregular patterns 
apply to small numbers of verbs and tend not to be productive, unless they have a clear 
phonological de nition and then they are only productive within those phonological 
parameters, as in the example discussed above of verbs in the cling, clung class. 
Morphosyntactic constructions are also more productive if they apply to more items (Bybee 
2006; Barðdal 2008).
In this section we have argued that there is no need for a separate processing mechanism 
based on a four-part proportional analogy. Rather, the innovative application of productive 
and semi-productive schemas and constructions to items that formerly exhibited other 
patterns produces the changes labelled as analogical levelling and extension. One other type 
of change often included under analogy, folk etymology, will be discussed in section 3.7.

---

<!-- page 526 -->

Joan Bybee and Clay Beckner
508
3.3 Chunking
Another domain-general process that is central to understanding linguistic structure is the 
process of chunking. Chunking occurs when repeated sequences in experience come to be 
stored and accessed together (Ellis 1996; Bybee 2010). This process allows us to know what 
to expect next and also what to do next in an established sequence. As Ellis (1996: 107) puts 
it, chunking “is the development of permanent sets of associative connections in long-term 
storage and is the process that underlies the attainment of automaticity and  uency in 
language.” Chunking occurs throughout the life of the individual as experience provides 
more input to the process. Examples of linguistic chunks are syllables, words, prefabs, idioms 
and constructions. Chunks are assigned meaning much as words are: by cross-modal 
association between the form (phonetic or manual) and the meaning gleaned from the context. 
When sequences of words form a chunk, the chunk can be assigned meaning over and above 
what the individual words contribute. When this happens, the words and their contribution to 
the chunk are not lost, but rather begin what can be a long process by which the chunk 
changes meaning with attendant loss of compositionality and analysability. The process of 
meaning change will be discussed in section 3.6.
Chunking is the basis of constituent structure; for example, because demonstratives and 
determiners occur frequently in sequence with nouns, they come to be processed as a chunk, 
which we think of as a constituent or a construction. (But note that many constructions 
contain multiple constituents). As we will see in the examples below, usually the motivation 
for producing two elements in sequence is functional or semantic, so it often turns out that 
constituents are also semantically de nable. However, this is not always true. The cases in 
which chunks form despite the lack of any semantic motivation demonstrate the power of 
repetition in the creation of linguistic structure.
First consider how chunking leads to changes in constituent structure over time. In the 
grammaticalisation literature it is often observed that multiword sequences can give rise to a 
new preposition, even among sequences which cross constituent boundaries such as adposition 
+ noun + GEN + NP (Heine et al. 1991; Svorou 1994). Recent examples in English are on top 
of and in back of, and earlier examples are behind and before. Synchronic accounts of English 
struggle with such phrases because they may appear to still have their complex constituent 
structure, but in fact behave in many ways as if they have become simple prepositions, without 
internal structure (Quirk et al. 1985). Diachronic accounts show a gradual loss of internal 
structure, accompanied by meaning change (Hoffmann 2005; Beckner and Bybee 2009). For 
example, studies of in spite of by Hoffmann and by Beckner and Bybee show a long process 
of change by which the repetition of in spite of renders it a chunk – an access and processing 
unit. Beckner and Bybee propose that at  rst the component words retain their connections to 
the same words in other contexts, as shown by their semantic contribution and their ability to 
be manipulated in discourse. However, as the phrase can also be processed without activating 
the component parts, gradually the connections of the component words to cognate exemplars 
in other constructions is lost. This development allows further meaning change and the loss of 
the ability to modify or manipulate the noun in the construction.
These are the same processes that underlie the ‘decategorialisation’ found in 
grammaticalisation (Hopper 1991). While spite in in spite of  loses its status as a noun, it does 
not become a clitic or af x. However, when chunking occurs in other contexts, for instance 
between two verbs or a verb and auxiliary, the result can be the development of an in ectional 
morpheme. For example in Acholi, a Nilotic language of Africa, the verb bino ‘to come’ has 
become a future pre x, as in this example (Bavin 1983: 151; Heine and Reh 1984: 131):

---

<!-- page 527 -->

Language use, cognitive processes and linguistic change 
509
(2) an a-bi-camo
1S 1S-FUT-eat
‘I will eat’
The related language, Lango, shows an intermediate stage in which bino is an auxiliary with 
less reduction:
(3) an a-bino 
cammo
1S 1S-FUT 
eat
‘I will eat’
The comparative evidence shows that the chunking process, along with the phonetic reduction 
(as discussed in the next section), is gradual and accompanied by semantic change.
In contrast, when chunking occurs across elements that are not semantically related, the 
result can be phonologically fused or contracted forms that retain their compositional meaning. 
For example, it is common in European languages for prepositions and de nite articles to 
contract, as seen in Spanish, a ‘to’ + el ‘MASC. SG. de nite’ > al; de ‘from’ + el > del; Portuguese 
a ‘to’ + o ‘MASC. SG. de nite’ > ao; de ‘from’ + o > do. There is no semantic coherence 
between a directional preposition plus a de nite article; indeed, the article belongs with the 
noun and – in a traditional syntactic analysis – is in a different constituent than the preposition. 
However, because of their sequential contiguity and high frequency of use, the contraction 
occurs, showing how frequency of use plays an important role in chunking (Bybee 2002).
3.4 Phonetic reduction
In the last two examples of the previous section we can observe phonetic reduction within 
high-frequency chunks. Such reduction, in addition to the reduction in regular sound change, 
is due to neuromotor automatisation, a domain-general cognitive process that plays a major 
role in language change (Bybee 2001). As we have just seen, highly-practiced words and 
phrases, including grammaticalising phrases, undergo phonetic reduction, by which the 
gestures that comprise these words and phrases are reduced in magnitude or duration and 
increase in their overlap with one another. Such factors lead to changes such as contraction, 
cliticisation and af xation. This process occurs generally in repeated neuromotor activity and 
is not speci c to language.
Most sound change can also be characterised as the increased reduction and overlap of 
gestures (Bybee 2012), which suggests that even regular sound change is due to neuromotor 
automation. The fact that sound change often affects high-frequency words before it affects 
low-frequency ones supports this point of view (Hooper 1976; Phillips 1984, 2006).
3.5 Semantic bleaching
It has often been observed that grammaticalisation results in the weakening or bleaching of 
lexical meaning into grammatical meaning (Givón 1975 and many subsequent works). For 
instance, when a movement verb construction such as be going to is grammaticalised into a 
future, the semantics of movement in space are lost. Nouns also lose their meaning when they 
grammaticalise in constructions, for instance leading to prepositions. The human or animal 
back is often a source of phrases meaning ‘behind’, as in American English in back of, which 
can refer to the region at the rear of any type of object, not just the human back.

---

<!-- page 528 -->

Joan Bybee and Clay Beckner
510
Semantic bleaching (or generalisation or desemanticisation) occurs when a grammaticalising 
construction gains in frequency, is used in redundant contexts, and spreads to new contexts. 
One cognitive mechanism proposed by Haiman (1994) for bleaching of semantic content is 
habituation, whereby an organism ceases to respond at the same level to repeated stimuli. 
Frequently used words, phrases and constructions lose their semantic impact and expand to 
new contexts. With respect to semantic reduction, note again that the principal mechanism for 
change applies in usage. Because change occurs incrementally when constructions are used, 
higher-frequency constructions undergo more semantic bleaching. See section 3.8 below for 
a discussion of the ways that grammaticalising constructions gain new meanings.
3.6 The loss and regaining of compositionality and analysability
As linguistic units are repeated in the same con gurations, it is common for them to become 
accessed less and less as an assemblage of component parts, and ever more so as a single 
unit. Such dynamics apply to any complex units in language – multimorphemic words, 
multiword sequences, or constructions – and occur in tandem with the other changes 
discussed in this chapter: the development of new units (chunking, section 3.3), the 
diminishment of certain types of meaning (section 3.5), and the development of new 
meanings in context (section 3.7).
Following Langacker (1987), we may identify compositionality and analysability as two 
related, but distinct, ways in which complex linguistic units may gradually lose their internal 
structure. First, compositionality denotes the semantic regularity or predictability of a unit 
with respect to its component parts. Compositionality may decrease when there is diminished 
activation of the components’ meanings, thus allowing uses that would otherwise be 
semantically anomalous. For instance, as discussed in section 3.3, the grammaticalised 
sequence in spite of now no longer activates the semantics of ‘spite’. Since the 1800s, it has 
thus been possible to say that events occur ‘in spite of’ resisting forces, even in the absence 
of any human agent capable of spite (Beckner and Bybee 2009).
Secondly, analysability refers more speci cally to the extent to which speakers activate 
component parts of a complex unit, including their individual morphosyntactic status. 
Langacker (1987: 457) writes that analysability represents “the extent to which speakers are 
cognizant (at some level of processing) of the contribution that individual component 
structures make to the composite whole.” There can be striking examples of diminished 
analysability, showing that some speakers are not consciously aware of word boundaries in 
formulaic sequences. Wray (2002: 298, n3) describes the prevalence of orthographic errors 
in essays by undergraduates, many of which involve the removal of a space from formulaic 
sequences: alot, afterall, aswell as, infact. As Wray notes, the designation of correct spelling 
in such cases is entirely a matter of convention, and it is known that previous changes in 
orthographic conventions have followed behind decreasing analysability: in to > into, with 
out > without, all right > alright.
Most indications of diminished analysability, however, are more subtle, and analysability 
is a matter of degree (as is compositionality). For instance, Hoffmann (2005) examines 
frequent complex prepositions having the form Preposition–Noun–Preposition (in spite of, in 
back of, on behalf of), and  nds that speakers are strongly disinclined to interrupt such 
sequences with additional words, or with  lled pauses. It is possible to interrupt or change 
these sequences, implying that, on re ection, speakers can become aware of internal 
structure.1 However, such alterations are rarer than would be expected by chance, and these 
multiword sequences are largely  xed processing units (see also Beckner and Bybee 2009).

---

<!-- page 529 -->

Language use, cognitive processes and linguistic change 
511
The loss of component structure is veri able in psycholinguistic studies of online 
processes. With respect to word-internal structure, Meunier and Segui (1999)  nd differential 
priming effects based on the surface frequency of morphologically complex words. In their 
experiment (Experiment 1), if the prime is a low-frequency derived word, subsequent 
recognition of the word’s stem is accelerated – indeed, the observed priming effect is no 
different from the stem’s self-priming effect. However, high-frequency derived words do not 
prime their stems to the same degree. This provides evidence that high-frequency derived 
words are not decomposed into stem and af x as readily as their low-frequency counterparts. 
This experiment provides further evidence that frequency of use is an important factor in 
chunking.
Similarly, with respect to processing recurring word sequences, speakers make use of 
multiword units that have diminished internal structure. For instance, eyetracking studies 
demonstrate that when reading text, subjects have shorter and fewer  xations on words that 
are predictable in the context (Underwood, Schmitt, and Galpin 2004), indicating that readers 
use knowledge of larger units which allow diminished attention to component words. Further 
evidence may be found in monitoring studies (Vogel Sosa and MacFarlane 2002; Kapatsinski 
and Radicke 2009), which ask participants to press a response key as soon as they detect a 
target word. Such studies demonstrate that it takes longer to notice the target word within 
high-frequency word sequences. More speci cally, Kapatsinski and Radicke (2009)  nd a 
U-shaped curve for the recognition of the target word up: for low- and mid-range frequencies, 
reaction times gradually improve, but above a certain frequency threshold, reaction times 
slow down. This result indicates that the highest-frequency sequences are accessed as holistic 
units, and “the stronger the whole the weaker the parts” (Kapatsinski and Radicke 2009: 518).
Change within chunks occasionally also re-establishes compositionality that has been lost. 
For instance, the highly subjective phrase far be it from me, used only with  rst person 
reference may have lost some compositionality (e.g. the status of constituency for far from) 
because examples appear in the British National Corpus of far be it for me in the same 
function. This change re-establishes some of the compositionality of the phrase by using the 
subjective phrase for me (Bybee 2010). When this type of change occurs at the word level, it 
has traditionally been called ‘folk etymology’ and designated a type of analogy. An example 
is the compound bridegroom, which in Old English consisted of bryd ‘bride’ + guma ‘man’. 
When the latter word fell out of use, speakers reinterpreted guma as groom, a word referring 
to a man and thus more compositional.
3.7 Inference and other sources of meaning from context
The many studies of semantic change in grammaticalisation over the last few decades have 
highlighted the role of inference-making as a way that new meanings can be imbued in 
grammaticalising constructions (starting with Traugott 1989; Heine et al. 1991; Bybee et al. 
1994). In the grammaticalisation process, meaning is assigned to the highest level chunk – 
the construction – and the meaning of the construction is highly dependent upon the contexts 
in which it is used. Thus the language user’s representation of the meaning includes 
information from different contexts of use. One major source of meaning in discourse context 
are the inferences made by the listener: the listener is charged with  lling in the gaps in the 
speaker’s account and by doing so infers intention, causation, temporal relations and many 
other properties that are necessary for grasping the speaker’s full meaning. Inferences from a 
particular construction that are repeated become associated with that construction 
conventionally and thus become part of its meaning.

---

<!-- page 530 -->

Joan Bybee and Clay Beckner
512
Psychologists have been studying the inferential process for some decades now, especially 
in the way listeners stitch together the meanings of narratives. For example, the experiments 
of Bransford and Franks (1971) show that listeners use world knowledge to draw inferences 
about the situations described in sentences. More relevant to the kinds of semantic change 
found in grammaticalisation, however, are experiments by Owens, Bower, and Black (1979), 
who show that subjects who read a text after receiving information about a character’s 
problems or motives make many more inferences than subjects who read the text without this 
information. In particular, knowing about the character’s problems allows subjects to knit 
together the events in the text and to infer goals, intentions and causes. Owens et al. (1979: 
191) link this ability to social behaviour:
Our ability to identify people’s plans and intentions from their actions and instigating 
circumstances is a social skill we learn and use often in interpersonal affairs.
These studies also show that inferences affect the way a situation is remembered; that is, 
the inferences become a part of the memory for a situation. When such inferences are 
commonly linked to certain constructions, the construction’s meaning changes to include 
the inference.
Not all of the inference types studied in the psychology literature appear operative in 
semantic change. What we  nd in grammaticalisation research are: (i) inferences about an 
agent’s goals and intentions, e.g. when futures from various sources are grammaticalising, a 
common stage is the expression of intention, as I am going to get you some new pyjamas 
(Bybee et al. 1994); (ii) inferences about causation, as when a temporal conjunction since 
takes on causal meaning; (iii) other text-connecting inferences, such as the development of 
concessives from various sources (Traugott and König 1991); and (iv) discourse markers 
including markers of subjectivity (Traugott and Dasher 2002). Some of these inference types 
have been studied experimentally; however, because the experimental literature focuses on 
narratives, some of the more intersubjective meanings that are grammaticalised, especially in 
conversational interaction, are not represented in that research.
3.8 Priming
As language is used, linguistic elements, including speci c phonetic elements as well as the 
form and meaning of words and constructions, are activated in the brain. This activation adds 
a small amount of strength or entrenchment to representations, which in the aggregate makes 
the representation easier to access (thus less likely to change, as we mentioned in section 3.2). 
Each instance of activation fades slowly, so that an item that has just been activated is easier 
to activate again within a short period of time. This fact can make it more likely that the item 
or structure occurs again. This  nding originally came out of experimental studies of lexical 
access (Meyer and Schaneveldt 1971; Forbach, Stanners, and Hochhaus 1974) and was later 
applied to syntactic constructions (Bock 1986). In exemplar models, these  ndings are 
represented by incorporating the time-course of experience with particular tokens; the 
likelihood of using a given item depends on the recency of the exemplar, in addition to its 
cumulative frequency (Pierrehumbert 2001).
Variation studies also report priming effects. The repetition or priming effect was  rst 
identi ed as a factor in variation by Poplack (1980), a study of the gradual deletion of plural 
/s/ in Puerto Rican Spanish. Given the presence of a consonant (either full [s] or reduced [h]) 
on the one hand and deletion on the other, Poplack found that a strong predictor of presence

---

<!-- page 531 -->

Language use, cognitive processes and linguistic change 
513
was the presence on the preceding plural word. Weiner and Labov (1983) studied the factors 
contributing to the use of the agentless passive in English conversation and found that use of 
the construction in one of  ve preceding clauses signi cantly heightened the probability that 
an agentless passive would be used. In addition, many other studies have found priming 
effects to in uence the variant used.
Given that either the innovative or the conservative variant can be subject to priming, what 
role does priming play as a mechanism of change? First, in grammaticalisation when a 
construction is increasing in frequency for the reasons cited in section 3.5, priming effects 
can enhance this frequency increase. A consequence of the increased use of a construction 
can be habituation, as mentioned above, which leads to a decrease in the semantic content of 
the construction, and thus to use in more contexts, increasing its frequency and its availability 
to prime other uses. Thus, priming is one of the basic cognitive mechanisms behind a positive 
feedback loop in language: usage events tend to increase their own future likelihood 
(Pierrehumbert 2001; Beckner et al. 2009). Such a self-organising dynamic can help drive 
language change, but also accounts for language stability in the short term, since conservative 
linguistic variants perpetuate themselves as well.
Two further proposals for priming as contributing to the unidirectionality of change are 
presented in Jäger and Rosenbach (2008). They discuss semantic priming as a factor in 
certain types of semantic change common in grammaticalisation, such as the change from 
spatial meaning to temporal meaning which occurs in tense and aspect markers (such as 
movement verb constructions signalling future or past), and in relational phrases, such as 
the emergence of temporal meaning expressed by before and behind, which originally had 
spatial meaning. Jäger and Rosenbach give evidence of a ‘cognitive asymmetry’ in spatial 
and temporal priming effects, drawing on experiments by Boroditsky (2000). Boroditsky’s 
studies show that spatial meaning primes temporal meaning2 in the interpretation of 
ambiguous, metaphorical statements about time. For instance, ‘Next Wednesday’s meeting 
has been moved forward two days’ may be interpreted to mean that the meeting is 
rescheduled for either Monday or Friday, due to the availability of alternate orientations 
for temporal ‘movement’. Boroditsky’s experiment  nds that the choice of (temporal) 
interpretation is primed by statements in the surrounding context involving spatial 
orientation. However, with respect to the interpretation of spatially ambiguous diagrams, 
no corresponding temporal-to-spatial priming effect occurs. Based on this priming 
asymmetry, Jäger and Rosenbach (2008) argue that use of a spatial term may make the use 
of a metaphorical temporal term more likely, whereas the opposite would not occur. Thus 
priming increases the likelihood of use of a term with temporal meaning, causing it to 
become entrenched and more grammaticalised.
Jäger and Rosenbach also discuss a phonetic priming effect, by which a word repeated in 
the same discourse tends to be shorter and more reduced than the  rst instance of the word 
both in terms of duration (Fowler and Housum 1987) and in terms of factors such as vowel 
reduction and vowel shift (Clopper and Pierrehumbert 2008). This effect is usually discussed 
in terms of predictability: if a word is predictable in the context it tends to be more reduced 
(Jurafsky et al. 2001). However, an interpretation in terms of priming is also possible: 
repetition or semantic priming both make a word more likely in the context, and thus 
contribute to its shortening and reduction. In an exemplar model, each instance of use affects 
the stored representation of the phonetics of the word. Thus a word used often in predictable 
or priming environments will reduce more quickly than other words.

---

<!-- page 532 -->

Joan Bybee and Clay Beckner
514
4 Change in a usage-based model
People outside the  eld of linguistics are often surprised to learn that in the past, some 
branches of theoretical linguistics have maintained an odd tension with psycholinguistics. 
These tensions persisted because psychological factors were considered to be mere 
‘performance’, and thus a priori irrelevant to the proper study of language change (Newmeyer 
1998). Such a view limits language change to the contributions of small children learning 
their  rst language, via imperfect learning.
Although children can indeed be important participants in language change, we have 
identi ed here many cognitive processes promoting change that are operative when adults 
use language. We note also that there are known sociolinguistic constraints that apply in most 
cases of  rst language acquisition: young learners lack social in uence, and thus there is no 
reliable mechanism for propagating innovations introduced during acquisition (for reviews, 
see Stanford, this volume; Croft 2000). Moreover, certain dynamics of language change are 
inconsistent with an imperfect learning mechanism. In this chapter, we have described the 
tendency for high-frequency items to undergo the most phonetic reduction (section 3.4) and 
semantic weakening (section 3.5). In other words, items that afford learners the most 
exposure, and the most practice, are the most prone to change in these respects. Such a 
pattern is the opposite of what an imperfect learning account would predict, but it is consistent 
with a gradient, psycholinguistically-motivated model in which gradual change is ongoing 
throughout the lifespan.
Other changes we have discussed are unlikely to be the work of small children, since they 
require making sophisticated semantic and pragmatic inferences in context (section 3.7). 
Linguistic innovations come to express many grammatical or pragmatic features which are 
salient to adults, but relatively inaccessible to children who are still learning discourse 
conventions. For example, overt complementisers are acquired late by children (Radford 
1990), and thus young children are unlikely to be the speakers to introduce them. Similarly, 
appropriate use of epistemic markers requires the speaker to have a theory of mind, but this 
capacity is still emerging in young children (Bybee 2009).
The usage-based model of change we have described requires acknowledging that 
adult speakers are capable of learning throughout the lifespan, in language just as in other 
areas of cognition. Yet there are indeed differences between adult and child learners. 
Adults are more resistant to change, due to accrued past experience (Pierrehumbert 2001; 
Bybee 2001), and adults may be slower to learn due to decreased neural plasticity. 
However, the evidence indicates that adults’ grammar is less rigid than sometimes 
supposed (Harrington 2006; Sankoff and Blondeau 2007), and adult speakers are capable 
of learning (or innovating) new grammatical conventions systematically (Romaine 1995; 
Singler 2006).
Our own view is that language is a dynamic system, with multiple interacting in uences 
(Beckner et al. 2009; Bybee 2010). Thus when appropriate, in uences from acquisition 
should certainly be incorporated in a model of language change (Bybee and Slobin 1982; 
Bybee 2009; Beckner and Wedel 2010). But in this chapter we have sketched out a broader 
complex systems view, in which characteristics of human cognition and communication 
continually shape the structure of language. In this account, psycholinguistic factors are not 
isolated from grammatical change, but are in fact an integral part of it.

---

<!-- page 533 -->

Language use, cognitive processes and linguistic change 
515
Notes
1 
For example, in writing, in spite of is sometimes interrupted when it is conjoined with another 
sequence that contains the word of, such as In spite – or rather, because – of her fame. In a search 
of the Corpus of Spoken American English (Davies 2008), Beckner and Bybee (2009) found 
several interruptions of this type, all from written sources. However, it is much more common for 
in spite of to be produced as a continuous chunk, even though this usage may incorporate a 
seemingly redundant of in conjoined sequences (In spite of, or perhaps because of, the constant 
threats).
2 
Of course, spatial meanings also prime spatial meanings, and temporal meanings prime temporal 
meanings. These within-domain priming effects were veri ed in Boroditsky’s experiments to 
establish a baseline, but the more interesting questions involve whether spatial concepts prime 
temporal concepts, and vice-versa.

<!-- page 537 -->

519
24
Contact-induced language change
Christopher Lucas
1 Introduction
Sustained academic interest in contact-induced language change goes back at least to the late 
nineteenth century, when Hugo Schuchardt (1884: 5) famously opined that no language is 
entirely free of in uence from other languages (“es gibt keine völlig ungemischte Sprache”). 
Indeed, the ubiquity of language contact has always been apparent to anyone engaged in 
language documentation and description. Until recently, however, the phenomenon of 
contact-induced change has received relatively little attention from more theoretically-
oriented linguists. We can understand why this should be so if we consider the background 
against which modern historical linguistics developed. First of all there was the development 
of the Comparative Method by the Neogrammarians. For them it was natural that contact-
induced changes should be marginalised, since these are necessarily complications to be 
abstracted away from when working out a language’s genetic af liation. Later, with the 
advent of structuralism in the 1920s, there was a signi cant shift away from the study of 
diachronic questions in general; and even when formal linguists began to take a serious 
interest in language change, starting in the 1960s and 1970s (e.g. Kiparsky 1968; Lightfoot 
1979), the focus was squarely on changes that are not the result of language contact (i.e. 
‘internally caused’ changes). Chomsky’s (1965: 3) in uential proposal that “linguistic theory 
is concerned primarily with an ideal speaker–listener, in a completely homogeneous speech-
community” doubtless had much to do with this focus on internally caused change. It was 
probably also connected to a widespread perception that, in the words of Lass (1997: 209), 
“an endogenous explanation of a phenomenon is more parsimonious, because endogenous 
change must occur in any case, whereas borrowing is never necessary.” If purely internally 
caused (= ‘endogenous’) change is in some sense logically prior to contact-induced change, 
then it is methodologically sound to begin with internally caused change, and only once we 
have made progress in understanding the mechanisms that underlie this, to then move on to 
contact-induced change.
Despite this relatively greater interest in internally caused change, there is of course a 
large and ever-expanding descriptive literature on the synchronic outcomes of language 
contact in various situations. Arising out of this descriptive literature, there has also been a 
considerable amount of work that tries to generalise across language-contact situations, the 
main focus of this work being on the putative limits of contact-induced change: which 
linguistic elements can and cannot be ‘borrowed’ (see section 3 for further discussion of this 
term), and in which order, and how sociolinguistic factors can act as constraints on contact-
induced change. These issues are discussed in section 5, but the primary concern of this

---

<!-- page 538 -->

Christopher Lucas
520
chapter is to explore the mechanisms that bring about contact-induced change, rather than the 
various factors that constrain it.
Research of this kind, which attempts to produce explanatory models of how contact-
induced change occurs, remains something of a minority pursuit within historical linguistics. 
Nevertheless, there can be no doubt that discussion of the mechanisms underlying contact-
induced change ought properly to occupy a central role in any model of language change 
which aims at completeness. This is especially clear in light of the basic observation that 
intensive language contact is almost always associated with rapid and profound change, 
whereas, in the rare cases where languages are spoken in virtual isolation, change proceeds 
exceptionally slowly, even in comparison to languages with only a moderate history of 
contact.
To illustrate this point, consider the case of Icelandic – a classic example of an isolated 
language. Icelandic has been extremely conservative relative to other Germanic languages. 
For example, Bergsland and Vogt (1962) show that at least 95 per cent of Icelandic basic 
vocabulary has been retained in the approximately 1,000 years since it split from Old Norse 
– a signi cantly higher percentage than in any of the mainland North Germanic languages as 
presently spoken. Similarly, Trudgill (2011: 20) points out that Icelandic retains a rich system 
of adjectival in ection, with a total of 14 distinct case-number-gender suf xes, as compared 
to the  ve distinctions retained by standard German, and the three retained by Norwegian 
Bokmål. What we see here is that in Icelandic, as in other isolated languages, lack of contact 
is an important factor promoting conservatism. This suggests that contact must play a more 
important role than usually recognised in changes undergone by the many languages that 
have been less isolated than Icelandic. If this is correct, then it is clearly essential that we 
make progress in understanding how contact-induced change occurs, how it does and does 
not differ from internally caused change, and how theoretical work on contact-induced and 
internally caused change can be integrated into a wider framework for understanding 
language change in general.
2 What is contact-induced language change?
To answer this question we  rst need to be clear about what we mean by language change in 
general. Unfortunately, of course, how to de ne language change remains a controversial 
issue. The basic division is between two schools of thought. On the one hand there are those 
who, following Chomsky and earlier writers such as Paul (1880), view the competence of the 
individual speaker (I-language) as the primary object of study in linguistics. On the other hand 
there are those who stress the social dimension of language and focus their research on the 
“orderly heterogeneity” (Weinreich, Labov and Herzog 1968: 100) of usage by different 
social groups within a speech community. The latter school naturally views language change 
as minimally a change common to all members of a particular subgroup of a speech community 
– anything less is merely individual variation, not change. The former school  nds this view 
of change unsatisfying. In particular, they seek an explanation of how individual variation 
comes about: what causes the individual who  rst consistently produces a variant form never 
before observed in the speech community to make this innovation? The typical answer to this 
question, as set out in Hale (1998) for example, is that this individual has acquired a grammar 
that differs in at least this respect from the grammars of other individuals in the speech 
community; and the reason that this individual has acquired a grammar that is innovative in 
this sense is that s/he has been exposed to primary linguistic data (PLD) that is signi cantly 
different to that which previous acquirers were exposed to. For this ‘acquisitionist’ school it

---

<!-- page 539 -->

Contact-induced language change
521
is the acquisition of an innovative grammar by at least one individual that constitutes change. 
The subsequent acquisition of similarly innovative grammars by individuals in the same 
speech community is then usually referred to as ‘diffusion’.
It follows from the above that there will be two correspondingly distinct views on what 
constitutes contact-induced change. From the acquisitionist perspective, change is contact-
induced when the innovating acquirer’s PLD contains elements which are not consistent with 
the natively acquired grammars of older speakers of the same language, and when these 
elements owe their existence to some form of bi- or multilingualism (henceforth bilingualism) 
among the speakers contributing to the acquirer’s PLD.1 From the ‘sociohistorical’ 
perspective, on the other hand, a contact-induced change has occurred whenever the whole 
of some subgroup of a speech community has adopted a speech habit which owes its existence, 
again, to the presence of some form of bilingualism in that community.
Although these two views clearly differ in important ways, what distinguishes contact-
induced from internal change is in fact identical on either view: the presence of bilingualism 
in a speech community has an effect on the usage of some subset of that community. 
Acquisitionist approaches will then want to stress that change will only have occurred once 
this altered usage has led to at least one altered grammar being acquired natively (i.e. by a 
child), while sociohistorical approaches will seek to explain the social strati cation of this 
usage and the dynamics of its diffusion (among adolescents and adults). But a proper 
understanding of how language contact can serve as the input to change must, on either view, 
begin by exploring the different mechanisms by which various types of bilingualism can 
affect usage.
3 The different types of bilingualism and the changes they cause
The best-known and most widely cited approach to contact-induced change is that of 
Thomason and Kaufman (1988). They distinguish two basic types of contact-induced change 
according to whether the language undergoing change has been maintained or shifted to by a 
speech community: ‘borrowing’ and ‘interference through shift’. ‘Borrowing’, for Thomason 
and Kaufman, “is the incorporation of foreign features into a group’s native language by 
speakers of that language: the native language is maintained but changed by the addition of 
the incorporated features” (1988: 37), while ‘interference through shift’ takes place when “a 
group of speakers shifting to a target language fails to learn the target language perfectly” 
(1988: 39). As evidence of the importance of this distinction, Thomason and Kaufman 
emphasise that situations of maintenance and shift are characteristically (though not 
inevitably) associated with different types of contact-induced change (see section 5 for more 
detail on this point). It is far from clear, however, that the question of whether or not a 
community happens to maintain its ancestral language is really crucial to understanding what 
causes contact-induced change, and why it might manifest itself in various ways in different 
contact situations.
In the last decade, Winford (2005, 2007 etc.) and various other authors have advocated 
viewing contact-induced change in terms of an alternative framework – that of Van Coetsem 
(1988, 2000). In contrast to other approaches, Van Coetsem’s framework is not based on 
sociological concepts such as maintenance versus shift, or power and prestige, but on the idea 
of cognitive ‘dominance’: which of two or more languages in an individual or community’s 
repertoire is, in some sense, cognitively ‘primary’. How to give dominance an appropriate, 
precise de nition is discussed below. For now it is suf cient to say that, in most cases at least, 
speakers will be dominant in their ‘native’ or ‘ rst’ language (L1), and non-dominant in any

---

<!-- page 540 -->

Christopher Lucas
522
languages acquired after childhood (L2s). This focus on dominance is motivated by 
appreciation of the point made above: that an essential method for understanding how 
contact-induced change takes place is to investigate how usage of one language is affected by 
an individual’s knowing another. We will see below that, while certain elements of Van 
Coetsem’s framework are open to question, the notion of dominance, properly de ned, forms 
a key part of the explanation of why bilingualism can cause usage that deviates from what 
would be expected, either in terms of an individual’s grammatical competence, or the prior 
norms of a speech community.
In Van Coetsem’s terminology the cover term for in uence from one language on another 
is ‘transfer’. Transfer is from the ‘source language’ (SL) to the ‘recipient language’ (RL). 
Van Coetsem then distinguishes between two main types of transfer, depending on whether 
the ‘agent(s)’ of transfer (i.e. the  rst speakers in a community to produce a particular contact-
induced usage) are dominant in the RL or the SL. If the agents of transfer are dominant in the 
RL, then we have ‘borrowing’. If they are dominant in the SL, we have ‘imposition’.
Neither of these last two terms (or ‘transfer’) are original to Van Coetsem of course, and 
they have been used in numerous different ways in the literature, sometimes as vague semi-
technical terms, sometimes with precise de nitions that differ in important respects from the 
de nitions that Van Coetsem gives them. This is perhaps clearest for the term ‘borrowing’. 
As we have seen, Thomason and Kaufman (1988) use ‘borrowing’ in reference to contact-
induced changes, which are not the result of shift, to a language that is maintained. This is 
quite distinct from Van Coetsem’s use of the term, though in practice most changes which 
meet Thomason and Kaufman’s criterion for ‘borrowing’ will also meet Van Coetsem’s (but 
see Winford 2005 for critical discussion of more controversial cases). More generally, 
‘borrowing’ is very frequently used casually simply to mean contact-induced change of any 
sort. Clearly, then, there is a signi cant risk of confusion inherent in any use of this term. 
Because of this, Van Coetsem also introduces the terms ‘transfer under RL agentivity’ and 
‘transfer under SL agentivity’ as unambiguous synonyms for his uses of ‘borrowing’ 
and ‘imposition’, respectively. As so often, there is a trade-off here between non-ambiguity 
and concision. In the rest of this chapter I will generally prefer the concise terms, but will 
always use them according to Van Coetsem’s de nitions.
One of the most salient subtypes of borrowing (transfer under RL agentivity) is what 
Matras and Sakel (2007) call ‘matter replication’, that is, the transfer of whole lexical items 
(sound–meaning pairings) from the SL to the RL. The traditional term for such transferred 
items is ‘loanwords’. As is well known, once an SL lexical item begins to occur in the context 
of RL usage, it typically does so in an adapted form that conforms to the phonological and 
morphological characteristics of the RL. For example, the Arabic noun faylas
f  ‘philosopher’ 
is a loan of the Greek word  losofos, but it has been adapted to  t the root-and-pattern 
morphology that is typical for Arabic lexical items, giving the ‘broken’ plural falsifa. 
Various phonological adaptations are also apparent. For example, the lack of a mid back 
vowel in the (standard) Arabic three-vowel system means that Greek /o/ is variously realised 
as /a/ and /û/.
The ubiquity of this kind of lexical borrowing, even in speech communities with very low 
levels of bilingualism (i.e. very strong RL-dominance), seems principally to be a function of 
the general human capacity to entertain novel concepts which then need terms that refer to 
them. When, as is often the case, a novel concept is introduced to one culture through contact 
with another, and therefore comes with a pre-existing label, it is unsurprising that this label 
is often retained, albeit with the kinds of adaptations just illustrated. For further reading on 
lexical borrowing, see e.g. Brown (1999) and Haspelmath and Tadmor (2009).

---

<!-- page 541 -->

Contact-induced language change
523
The most obvious and widespread manifestation of imposition (transfer under SL 
agentivity) is phonological: what is known to non-linguists as ‘foreign accent’. A concrete 
example: speakers who are dominant in Greek will frequently substitute an alveolar fricative 
for the English postalveolar fricative (which is lacking in the phonemic inventory of standard 
Greek), thus pronouncing the word shops as [sÿps] instead of [ÿps]. Phonological imposition 
of this sort seems to be a function of the low adaptability in adults of the neurophysiological 
systems responsible for speech production. Van Coetsem (1988: 27–28) cites Walsh and 
Diller (1981: 18) on this point: “Low-order processes such as pronunciation are dependent on 
the early maturing and less adaptive macroneural circuits, which makes foreign accent 
dif cult to overcome after childhood.” Extensive discussion of phonological imposition can 
be found in Van Coetsem (1988, 2000). For a detailed case study on Yiddish, see Louden 
(2000).
Imposition clearly also has manifestations beyond the domain of phonology. Unlike with 
borrowing, imposition of a lexical or syntactic nature tends to fall under Matras and Sakel’s 
(2007) heading of ‘pattern replication’ – cases of transfer where it is not concrete lexical 
material that is transferred but more abstract structural elements. An example from the 
lexicon–syntax interface is given in (1), where a speaker who is dominant in English assumes 
that the thematic role of SOURCE for the French verb emprunter ‘to borrow’ is marked with 
the preposition de rather than the standard French à on the model of English to borrow from 
somebody.
(1) *Je l’ai 
emprunté 
de 
mon 
ami.
I 
it-AUX.1SG 
borrow.PTCP from 
my 
friend
Intended: ‘I borrowed it from my friend.’
(French)
It is intuitively clear that cases such as this, where the syntactic properties of individual 
lexical items are imposed, are the result of SL-dominant speakers extrapolating (incorrectly) 
from a perceived equivalence between an RL item and an SL item. SL-dominant speakers 
will often have suf cient exposure to RL lexical items to perceive them as translation 
equivalents of SL items, but insuf cient exposure to acquire all aspects of their morphosyntax. 
In such cases it is natural for these speakers to assume that the apparently equivalent items 
are identical in all respects, until such time as they are exposed to salient evidence to the 
contrary. For an overview of the extensive literature on syntactic imposition in L2 acquisition, 
see Gass and Selinker (2008: 89–155).
While the dominance relations of the agents of change in the above examples are not in 
doubt, the status of the examples of imposition as genuine changes is of course controversial, 
given the discussion in section 2. In fact, these examples would probably not be considered 
cases of change either by the acquisitionist school (since no native grammar has been 
acquired) or the sociohistorical school (because unless these usages are characteristic of a 
whole well-de ned subgroup of a speech community then they merely re ect idiolectal 
variation, not change). This raises the issue of the appropriate kinds of case studies for 
illustrating the different types of contact-induced change. Ideally, we would select only well-
established historical developments which uncontroversially instantiate change in grammars 
and diffusion throughout a speech community, but for which we also have detailed information 
on the sociolinguistic background of the agents of change. Occasionally cases studies that 
come close to meeting these criteria can be found, for example, among nineteenth- and 
twentieth-century immigrant communities to the United States (e.g. Salmons and Purnell

---

<!-- page 542 -->

Christopher Lucas
524
2010). Usually, however, historical changes that are clearly instantiated in the grammars of 
whole communities of speakers tend to have occurred suf ciently long ago that even basic 
information on the sociolinguistic background of the changes is lacking, and must be inferred, 
meaning accounts of such changes are necessarily rather speculative. Even the question of 
whether or not contact played a role in the changes may be hard to answer with con dence. 
In practice, therefore, except in straightforward cases of lexical borrowing of the matter-
replication type, it makes sense to base our understanding of the mechanisms of contact-
induced change on contemporary experimental research into the effects of bilingualism on 
language usage and processing, which produces examples of the sort illustrated in (1). 
Although the data from these kinds of studies necessarily represent potential, rather than 
actual changes, they have the advantage of providing us with full details on the nature, causes 
and sociolinguistic background of the innovative usages observed. If we assume the 
Uniformitarian Principle (see Labov 1972: 161), we are then justi ed in analysing well-
established historical changes using the same methodology, with the proviso that this analysis 
will have to be based on reasonable assumptions about the dominance relations of the agents 
of change, rather than certain facts.
Before we consider some more case studies with this in mind, we  rst need to interrogate 
further the concept of ‘dominance’ (discussed in detail in Lucas 2009, 2012): is it or is it not 
identical to ‘nativeness’, the property of a language having been acquired from birth and 
throughout childhood? Van Coetsem (2000: 52) explicitly denies that it is, suggesting instead 
that dominance relations are determined by ‘pro ciency’, which usually aligns with 
nativeness, but need not always. However, since Van Coetsem does not provide a de nition 
of ‘pro ciency’, the implications of his position are hard to assess. What seems uncontroversial 
is that, in ordinary cases, L1s have quite different effects on L2s than L2s have on L1s. The 
key question, then, is whether the acquisition of greater ‘pro ciency’ (given an appropriate 
de nition of that term) in an L2 than an L1 can ever lead to the basic character of knowledge 
of an L2 to coming to resemble that of an L1, and vice versa.
The empirical evidence overwhelmingly points to a negative answer to this question, 
except perhaps in extremely rare cases. This can be seen from numerous studies of L2 
acquisition (e.g. Johnson et al. 1996; DeKeyser 2000). To take one example, Abrahamsson 
and Hyltenstam (2009) showed that, of a large group of L2 speakers of Swedish who 
considered their knowledge and usage of Swedish to be ‘native-like’, only a small minority 
of those whose acquisition of Swedish began after the age of 12 were judged to be native-like 
by actual native speakers; and when the Swedish of these potentially native-like L2 speakers 
was subjected to a battery of tests across a range of linguistic domains, none of them 
performed in the range observed for actual native speakers. The study also identi ed 10 early 
L2 acquirers of Swedish (whose exposure began before age 12 but not at birth) who were 
apparently native-like, and even among these only two performed within the actual native-
speaker range on all tests.
The extreme rareness of native-like L2 ability, especially when L2 acquisition begins after 
early childhood, suggests that the nature of acquisition and the type of knowledge it leads to 
are fundamentally distinct for L2s versus L1s. What the best explanation is for this  nding is 
a topic of ongoing debate (see e.g. de Bot 2006, Meisel 2011 for two viewpoints).
Turning to L1s, here we need to ask whether it is possible for an L1 not only to become 
temporarily less accessible than an L2 (which it clearly can), but actually to change its basic 
character. In other words, can a speaker’s knowledge of their L1, as opposed to their ability 
to deploy that knowledge on a particular occasion, become so ‘attrited’ that it more closely 
resembles typical L2 knowledge than typical L1 knowledge? Here again the answer seems to

---

<!-- page 543 -->

Contact-induced language change
525
be negative. This is perhaps most strongly suggested by the various studies (Ås 1962; Fromm 
1970; Footnick 2007), which have investigated the effects of hypnosis on individuals who 
seemed to have undergone such profound levels of attrition that they professed, apparently 
honestly, to having lost all ability to speak or comprehend their L1, which they had not used 
since childhood. Remarkably, these individuals displayed ordinary native-like ability in both 
production and comprehension when under hypnosis, suggesting that the apparent loss of L1 
knowledge was in fact just a temporary, if profound, lack of accessibility. If the basic 
knowledge of an L1 is preserved even in such extreme cases of attrition as these, it is 
reasonable to assume that this is also the case in more typical cases of L1 attrition. (For 
further discussion of L1 attrition at both the individual and community level, see e.g. Schmid 
2002, Köpke 2004, Stolberg and Münch 2010.)
Given these conclusions, it seems appropriate, contra Van Coetsem, to see dominance as 
equivalent to nativeness in all cases. On this basis we can then distinguish four different types 
of transfer that may occur as a result of three different types of bilingualism on the part of the 
agents of change (see Figure 24.1). The  rst two have already been discussed: borrowing is 
when the agents of change are dominant in the RL (i.e. the RL is their L1 and the SL is their 
L2), and imposition is when the agents of change are dominant in the SL (i.e. the SL is their 
L1 and the RL is their L2). The third type of change is also brought about by speakers for 
whom the changing language is an L2, but it does not involve transfer. Following Lucas 
(2009), I give this type of change the label ‘restructuring’: changes that a speaker makes to 
an L2 that are the result not of imposition but of interpreting the L2 input in a way that a child 
acquiring an L1 would not. Finally, we have changes made to an RL by speakers for whom 
both the RL and the SL are L1s, that is, speakers who have undergone the simultaneous 
acquisition of two native languages (2L1 acquisition). Clearly, for such individuals the 
distinction between borrowing and imposition breaks down. Since these kinds of changes 
typically affect both L1s of such individuals, a common consequence is that the languages 
tend to become more similar in various ways. The phenomenon of languages in contact 
becoming more similar over time is typically called ‘convergence’. Here, I extend this label 
to changes brought about speci cally by 2L1 individuals.
Examples and discussion of each of these four types of change are given in the following 
section.
Is there transfer
from an SL to 
an RL?

SL-dominant?

RL-dominant?
