Reconstructed lexical items can provide clues not only to how a people once lived, but also 
to where they lived. Where the daughters of a protolanguage are distributed across a 
geographic region, reconstructed terms for  ora and fauna – provided their meanings can be 
con dently reconstructed as well – can often be matched to a particular location within that 
region, identifying it as the homeland of the protolanguage’s speakers (Sapir 1949 [1916] and 
references above; see also Heggarty, this volume). For example, the presence of reconstructed 
 ora and fauna terms such as ‘pine’, ‘edible agave’, ‘edible prickly pear cactus’, ‘cottontail 
rabbit’, ‘coyote’, and ‘wild turkey’ in Proto-Uto-Aztecan narrows its likely homeland to 
either the southern part of the American southwest (Fowler 1983) or to central Mesoamerica 
(Hill 2011). D’Eeckenbrugge and Brown (in preparation; see also Brown 2009) use ecological 
niche modelling, which takes into account elevation, latitude, and climatic factors, to 
determine the optimal habitats for a set of managed plant species reconstructed to Proto-
Otomanguean (Mesoamerica); they propose a homeland where the majority of the plants’ 
preferred niches intersect. Bowern’s (2007) investigation of fauna terms in languages of the 
Nyulnyulan family of northern Australia considers the distribution of cognates with sea-
related meanings in coastal languages and land-related meanings in inland languages (e.g. 
*juurru ‘mosquito’, ‘shark’, ‘snake’; *bajalbarra ‘dolphin’, ‘eel’; *kandarra ‘tiger snake’, 
‘shark’; *biyalbiyal ‘mangrove log’, ‘saw sh’). The prevalence and internal consistency of 
the sea-related meanings, in contrast to the land-related meanings, leads her to conclude that 
the Proto-Nyulnyulan homeland was on the coast.

---

<!-- page 601 -->

Historical linguistics and socio-cultural reconstruction 
583
Drawing inferences about the past from reconstructed lexica brings up a host of challenges. 
First, and most importantly, the quality of the inferences depends on careful, methodologically 
sound historical work; our inferences are only as good as our reconstructions. Faulty 
reconstructions may derive from undetected loans, particularly when these are Wanderwörter 
that turn up in multiple related languages (e.g. as noted by Danielsen et al. 2011: 175 as a 
potential problem with some of Payne’s Arawak reconstructions; see also Southworth 2005: 
19; Mallory 1989: 112, inter alia). While in principle careful reconstructions based on regular 
sound correspondences should be able to distinguish true inherited forms from loans, doing 
so becomes more dif cult with increased time-depth (in that loans have undergone multiple 
sound changes after entering the language), or with relatively shallow families (where the 
number of diagnostic sound changes may be few). Calques are notoriously dif cult to 
distinguish from inherited forms, as seen in Bloom eld’s (1946) famous example of the 
Algonquian terms for whisky (‘ re-water’).5 The components of the calque in each daughter 
language may be bona  de inherited forms bearing the relevant sound changes (although we 
note that Wörter und Sachen methodology does correctly associate the etymological 
complexity of terms like ‘ re-water’ with a more recent innovation, as discussed below).
A particularly common source of problematic inferences involves the incorrect 
reconstruction of a proto-form’s meaning. The reconstruction of meaning is often far more 
uncertain than that of phonological form; meaning may be obscured by semantic shift across 
the daughter languages, and directionality tends to be dif cult to assess where lexical 
semantic change is involved (as opposed to sound change; see e.g. Hock 1991: 308; Wilkins 
1996). Nevertheless, socio-cultural inferences about the past are crucially dependent on our 
ability to reconstruct meaning as well as form. A well-known example of this challenge is 
that of Proto-Indo-European tree names, which have  gured in debates about the location of 
the family’s homeland. For the reconstructed word *bhÈ o, re exes in Germanic mean 
‘beech’, in Greek ‘oak’, and in Slavic ‘elder’; the original meaning is unclear (see e.g. 
Mallory 1989: 115–116; Fortson 2004: 40). Crucially for Indo-European homeland 
hypotheses, these trees have different ranges; beeches do not occur east of East Prussia, 
whereas elders are common in the east.6 Similarly, debates concerning the dating of Proto-
Indo-European have considered the words relating to wheeled transport, which can be 
matched to an archaeological date of about 4,000 years. For example, while *kwekwlos 
‘wheel’ apparently reconstructs, some scholars have questioned whether it could have been 
independently innovated from *kwel ‘to turn’ (or from derivative forms such as ‘potter’s 
wheel’ or ‘wheel of the sun’) in different daughter languages after the breakup of the 
protolanguage (see e.g. Watkins 1969; Coleman 1988: 450; Beekes 1995: 41; cf. Anthony 
2007: 33–36).
Semantic extensions and markedness reversals can also present challenges to reconstructing 
meaning. When these occur, a term takes on a new, secondary meaning, while still maintaining 
the original meaning; in the case of markedness reversals, the original meaning eventually 
becomes the primary one, and the original term is elaborated with an additional marker to 
indicate its less prototypical status (Witkowski and Brown 1983). Such extensions of meaning 
can easily be independently innovated or diffuse among related languages, and the re ex of 
the original word often persists among both meanings across languages of the family – but it 
is not always obvious which meaning came  rst. An example of such a puzzle is found in 
terms for ‘banana’ (Musa sp.), which overlap in a number of Amazonian languages with 
names for wild relatives within the order Zingiberales (particularly Heliconia sp. or 
Phenakospermum guyannense; commonly termed ‘bastard plantain’ or ‘platanillo’). For 
example, in Hup (Nadahup), we  nd p|h t ‘banana’ and haj’ay p|h t ‘Heliconia sp.’, literally

---

<!-- page 602 -->

Patience Epps
584
‘forest banana’, and in Tembé (Tupi-Guarani) we  nd paku ‘banana’ and paku-ran-’ïw 
‘Heliconia sp.’, literally ‘plantain-other-stem’. The common assumption that the banana was 
brought to the New World by the Europeans would suggest that the Heliconia terms involve 
markedness reversals, but as Balée (2008) argues, a post-Colombian arrival for the banana is 
in fact uncertain, and in some cases (including Tembé) it is the terms for the wild lookalikes 
that actually appear to have been modelled on the name of the domesticate, not the reverse.
Finally, a crucial caveat in using historical linguistics to draw inferences about the past is 
that our inability to reconstruct a word to the protolanguage does not entail its absence in that 
language, or the absence of its referent in the lives of its speakers. While this point is generally 
recognised, it nevertheless surfaces in debates concerning socio-cultural reconstruction. For 
example, Merrill et al.’s (2009) proposal that the Proto-Uto-Aztecan homeland lay in the 
Great Basin, rather than in Mesoamerica or elsewhere (see Hill 2001, inter alia), rests 
crucially on the absence of reconstructed terms for ‘pinyon’ and ‘pine nut’ in the family (see 
Hill 2010; Brown 2010; Merrill et al. 2010). It is also important to note that more negative 
values will tend to appear in families with few primary branches and few languages within 
them, since in such cases it is more likely that the replacement of re exes over time will lead 
to a lack of attested re exes in primary branches, and thus produce gaps in the reconstruction. 
In the Nadahup family, for example, the Nadëb language is the sole representative of one of 
two primary branches; lexical replacement in this language is undoubtedly responsible for 
the lack of reconstructable terms for ‘agouti’ and ‘jaguar’ (see Table 27.1 above), two 
common Amazonian animals that were almost certainly represented in the protolanguage.
2.2 Etymological complexity
Another basic assumption of the Wörter und Sachen methodology is that etymological 
complexity tends to be associated with the relatively recent introduction of a word to a 
language’s lexicon (Sapir 1949 [1916]: 434–435). This observation is most relevant to words 
that are built from native material, particularly via compounding. It is based on the recognition 
that the combination of existing forms to produce new meanings is a key process of lexical 
innovation, and that the identity of the component forms becomes obscured over time by 
sound change and lexicalisation processes (see Mailhammer, this volume). Among the lexical 
meanings listed in Table 27.1, for example, the Hup (Nadahup) term for ‘maize’ is p|h|t yúm, 
literally ‘planting banana’; the relative newness of the word is corroborated by the fact that a 
term for ‘maize’ – which is rarely cultivated by indigenous groups in the Upper Rio Negro 
region even today – cannot be reconstructed for any branch of the family. Similarly, Chacon 
(2013) appeals to etymological complexity in arguing that the speakers of Proto-Tukanoan 
were more forest- than river-oriented; he observes that many Tukanoan names for aquatic 
animals are compounds involving a noun referring to a jungle animal and ‘river’ (e.g. ‘river 
tapir’ = ‘capybara’ or ‘manatee’, ‘river tortoise’ = ‘turtle’, ‘river lizard’ = ‘cayman’).
Of course, the etymological complexity of a word is far from proof of its relative newness, 
and even less that of the concept it represents. Many new terms are not etymologically 
complex at all; their innovation can involve semantic shift (e.g. computer ‘mouse’) or 
clipping of a more complex term (e.g. ‘bus’, from ‘omnibus’). On the other hand, it is 
possible for morphologically complex terms to persist for fairly long periods of time, 
especially when their literal meaning is somehow salient to speakers (although establishing 
their actual presence in the protolanguage may be dif cult since they are also prone to 
calquing and parallel independent innovation). Such persistence appears to be the case, for 
example, with Nadahup words for ‘three’, which are identi able as ‘rubber-tree-seed-

---

<!-- page 603 -->

Historical linguistics and socio-cultural reconstruction 
585
quantity’ across the family (and are still maintained with both phonologically full and 
reduced variants among Hup speakers; see Epps 2006, 2013). Finally, lexical innovation 
can replace existing terms, such as when taboo is involved; terms for ‘bear’ meaning ‘honey 
eater’ in Slavic languages (e.g. Russian medved’) constitute a well-known example of such 
replacement (see Emeneau 1948).
2.3 Loanwords
Loanwords are a key source of clues regarding the dynamics of interaction that has taken 
place among groups in the past, and the spread of particular cultural or technological 
innovations over time and space. When a loan is identi ed and no corresponding term can be 
reconstructed to the protolanguage, we may hypothesise that the loan represented a new 
concept for the speakers of that language at the time the word was adopted. Among the 
Nadahup languages, for example, terms for ‘maize’ have been borrowed into Yuhup (from 
Tukanoan) and Dâw (from Tupi-Guarani), and terms for ‘pineapple’ are loans in Hup (from 
Tukanoan) and in Dâw and Nadëb (from Arawak) – suggesting that these hunter-gatherer 
groups gained familiarity with maize and pineapple via their interaction with their more 
horticulturalist neighbours.
Alternatively, where loans appear to have replaced existing words in the language, this 
may be an indication that their referents were somehow socially or culturally noteworthy. For 
example, Balée (2003) discusses how the borrowing of the Portuguese word cacao in many 
Amazonian languages can be attributed to the rising economic importance of the cacao tree 
(Theobroma cacao), despite the fact that native varieties of cacao exist and have long been 
used by indigenous peoples in the region. Similarly, Comrie’s (2000) discussion of lexical 
borrowing in Haruai (New Guinea) observes that massive borrowing was motivated by 
cultural constraints against uttering the names of one’s relatives by marriage. Since personal 
names were equivalent to many everyday words, Haruai speakers simply borrowed 
replacement words from a nearby language. It is possible that the Nadëb (Nadahup) term for 
‘jaguar’ (a native Amazonian animal that is well known to virtually all indigenous groups of 
the region) was borrowed from Tupi-Guarani for reasons associated with taboo.
Lexical borrowing may also be an important indicator of prestige relations among groups, 
and borrowed terms can offer clues to how this relationship affected speakers’ daily lives. In 
a well-known example of prestige-motivated borrowing, Middle English developed two 
parallel sets of terms for animals on the table (borrowed from French) and in the barnyard 
(maintained in English), re ecting the elite status of Norman French; e.g. ‘mutton’ and 
‘sheep’, ‘poultry’ and ‘hen’, ‘pork’ and ‘pig’, etc. In Mesoamerica, where archaeology has 
identi ed the Olmecs as the source of many regional cultural achievements, the existence of 
Mixe-Zoque loans referring to agricultural items, ritual and calendric concepts, etc. in Mayan 
languages (e.g. ‘squash’, ‘tomato’, ‘to count’, ‘paper’) indicates that the mysterious Olmecs 
were Mixe-Zoque speakers (Campbell and Kaufman 1976).
As with other linguistic sources of inference about the past, there are limitations to what 
we can determine from loanwords. Establishing the direction and source of borrowing is 
crucial to our understanding of the social relations between the groups involved. However, in 
certain instances directionality is unclear, particularly when the word is attested only in the 
donor/recipient languages, and not in their relatives (so cannot be established as inherited in 
either of them). Directionality may likewise be dif cult to establish for Wanderwörter – loans 
that are widely distributed throughout the languages of a region – since these are likely to not 
be inherited on either side of a particular borrowing event (see Hill et al., in preparation).

---

<!-- page 604 -->

Patience Epps
586
Similarly, the language that was the direct source of a loan is not necessarily equivalent to the 
language in which it ultimately originated; sometimes, and particularly with Wanderwörter, 
a loan may be passed along several languages via a borrowing chain, and therefore cannot be 
taken as evidence of direct contact among all the groups concerned. For example, various 
Hup (Nadahup) loans are originally from Portuguese or Nheengatú (Tupi-Guarani) but must 
have entered Hup via Tukano (since Hup speakers have had the most direct contact with 
Tukanoan peoples; e.g. sudada ‘soldier’, pisana ‘cat’).
Another challenge in working with loanwords concerns our inferences about the social 
signi cance of their referents. In many cases a loan is indeed an indicator that its referent was 
in some way signi cant; consider, for example, the Wanderwörter representing the drug 
known as ‘ayahuasca’ (Banisteriopsis caapi) in northwest Amazonian languages (e.g. forms 
resembling kapi in the Upper Rio Negro region, and hibie in northern Peru and southern 
Colombia). However, in other cases it is not clear what, if any, the social signi cance of an 
entity could have been. There is thus a danger of circular reasoning: on one hand, we might 
assume that if it was borrowed, it must have been socially signi cant; on the other, that 
socially signi cant entities are more prone to borrowing.
As a  nal observation – and a kind of converse to the existence of loanwords – there are 
at least a few cases in which a people has shifted its language altogether, but has preserved a 
subset of words from their original language. This vocabulary can be a source of inferences 
about the pre-shift socio-cultural identity of the group. A particularly interesting example 
comes from Bahuchet’s (1993) study of the Aka and Baka Pygmy peoples of the central 
African rain forest, who currently speak Bantu and Oubangian languages, respectively – 
borrowed from distinct farming populations. However, the two Pygmy groups share a set of 
about 650 apparent cognates from an unknown language, which refer mostly to concepts 
associated with hunting and gathering (honey, mushrooms, wild nuts, elephant hunting, etc.). 
This linguistic evidence allows us to infer that these two Pygmy groups once formed a single 
population, and that they have maintained a hunting and gathering orientation over time.
2.4 Wörter und Sachen: a way forward
Clearly, there are many caveats in the application of Wörter und Sachen assumptions to make 
inferences about past societies and cultures, and one need not look far to  nd examples of 
incautious applications leading to questionable – or downright spurious – conclusions. Can 
we delimit a relatively reliable approach to socio-cultural reconstruction using lexical 
information, given these challenges?
The answer is undoubtedly yes: a Wörter und Sachen approach can indeed yield meaningful 
inferences about the past, but only if we do not lose sight of the fact that the reliability of 
those inferences is directly related to the reliability of the reconstructions that support them, 
and becomes more robust as the number of relevant reconstructable lexical items increases. 
For the most part, we can be the most con dent of generalisations drawn from whole lexical 
semantic domains, particularly where these rest on a combination of the Wörter und Sachen 
assumptions described above, and where negative evidence (i.e. a gap in the reconstructed 
lexicon) is least heavily weighted. More precise inferences dealing with particular details of 
ancient lifeways will tend to depend on our historical understanding of speci c lexical items, 
and will therefore almost always be far more speculative.
To illustrate, let us consider the role of horticulture in the history of the Nadahup language 
family of the northwest Amazon. As noted above, Nadahup peoples do practice some 
horticulture today, but have a strong hunting and gathering orientation. As Epps (forthcoming)

---

<!-- page 605 -->

Historical linguistics and socio-cultural reconstruction 
587
investigates, there are several possible explanations for their contemporary subsistence 
pro le: they could be former horticulturalists who have ‘reverted’ to a hunting and gathering 
focus, perhaps in response to the pressures of the European invasion (as Balée 1999 has 
argued for some members of the Tupi-Guarani family); they could be in the midst of a 
relatively abrupt transition to a primarily horticulturalist way of life; or their present way of 
life could represent a fairly stable, diversi ed subsistence pattern that has continued for many 
generations, and is perhaps so ancient that it predates the protolanguage altogether. If we 
examine the vocabulary across several semantic domains – useful wild or managed plants, 
fully domesticated plants, and terms associated with planting and processing agricultural 
produce (particularly bitter manioc) – we can make a set of related generalisations. First, 
many of the words for useful non-domesticates reconstruct to Proto-Nadahup (at least half of 
a sample of about 20 words), and the set contains only a handful of loans and etymologically 
complex words. In contrast, the semantic domains of domesticated plants and other 
agriculture-related vocabulary (about 20 and 15 words, respectively) contain very few terms 
that reconstruct to the protolanguage; among the domesticated plants, these are limited to 
‘tobacco’ and ‘achiote’ (Bixa orellana, a plant that produces a red dye). On the other hand, 
the proportion of etymologically complex terms and loanwords (mostly from Tukanoan and 
Arawak languages) is very high, comprising over half of the words attested across all four 
daughter languages for these domains. These facts suggest that most horticulture-related 
vocabulary is relatively recent in the Nadahup languages, and that horticulture itself (at least 
as a food-production strategy) must also be a fairly recent adoption. However, several terms 
(e.g. ‘hot pepper’, ‘manioc’, ‘pineapple’) do reconstruct in one branch of the Nadahup family 
(in Hup, Yuhup, and Dâw), indicating that some association with horticulture may have 
followed soon after the initial breakup of the family, and has therefore been part of the 
Nadahup way of life for many generations.
A similar approach to Tukanoan prehistory is applied by Chacon (2013), who stresses a 
holistic analysis of different semantic domains. For example, his argument that the early 
Tukanoans were more forest-oriented than they are today is evidenced by a more consistently 
reconstructable set of terms for forest resources and hunting than for large river species and 
 shing. In addition, as noted above, he observes that names for aquatic animals tend to be 
etymologically complex and based on terms for forest creatures, indicating a more recent 
innovation.
2.5 Lexicalisation and semantic change
While processes of semantic change frequently present a serious obstacle to reconstruction 
with respect to determining the meanings of the words attested in a protolanguage, they may 
also have a degree of cultural relevance in their own right. Semantic change involves the 
development of polysemy, motivated by pragmatic inferencing that allows speakers and 
listeners to draw associations between particular ideas (see Urban, this volume). These 
associations themselves draw on what Evans (2003: 23) refers to as “culture-speci c 
encyclopaedic knowledge,” the set of understandings about the world and the relationships 
existing within it that the speaker and addressee share. The cultural basis of these inferences 
is what led Spitzer (1947: 2, cited in Evans 2003: 20) to observe that “of all linguistic branches, 
it is in semantics that the changes due to cultural development can best be seen at work, for 
‘meaning’ is the best barometer of cultural climate.” Thus an investigation of semantic change 
and its role in lexicalisation processes – as well as in grammaticalisation, as addressed below 
– is a potential source of information about the cultural context in which the change occurred.

---

<!-- page 606 -->

Patience Epps
588
A particularly well documented example of a culturally grounded semantic change is the 
development of verbs of cognition (particularly ‘think’ and ‘know’) from verbs meaning 
‘hear’ in Australian languages, as discussed by Evans and Wilkins (2000; see also Evans 
2003). ‘Hear’ as a source for cognition verbs is in direct contrast to the pattern in Indo-
European and other languages, in which cognition verbs consistently derive from ‘see’ 
(Sweetser 1990). Evans and Wilkins argue that the Australian link between hearing and 
cognition is grounded in cultural factors, such as a non-dyadic conversational style (in which 
talk is broadcast and eye contact unimportant) and a prioritisation of hearing in individual 
attention and socialisation.
The greatest challenge in associating semantic change with cultural practices lies in 
establishing that an observed correlation involves a causal link, as discussed by Evans (2003) 
and Simpson (2002). Moreover, since arguments for causality require a  rm grounding in 
ethnography, the possibilities for making well-informed inferences about past socio-cultural 
practices are clearly limited. Nevertheless, where culturally grounded associations are 
strongly implicated in processes of semantic change, as in the Australian hear > think/know 
transition, it follows that the practices involved are likely to have a certain degree of time-
depth.
3 Grammar and discourse
The majority of work on socio-cultural reconstruction has focused on lexicon, which 
represents the most concrete source of insights about the lives of speakers in the past. 
However, there is some room to draw inferences about the past from grammar and discourse, 
particularly relating to interactions among different language groups. In this section, we 
explore connections between changes in grammar and socio-cultural practices, and consider 
possible insights from register, style, and other features of discourse.
3.1 Changes in grammatical systems
Traditionally, culture-speci c practices have been thought to have little relevance to the 
processes associated with grammaticalisation, i.e. reanalysis, extension, and development of 
new meanings and functions. For example, Rumsey (2002: 281) observes that “grammar is a 
relatively autonomous system with functional prerequisites (and perhaps underlying universal 
“parameters”) of its own that tend to favor the development and reproduction of certain 
broadly similar structural features even among historically unrelated languages spoken in 
very different cultural settings”; see also Sapir (1949 [1933]: 26). However, as with semantic 
change within the lexicon, discussed above, the development of new grammatical structures 
may be grounded in culturally speci c practices. Evans (2003: 13) explores the mechanisms 
involved, observing that “cultural selection is shown to shape linguistic structure through 
invisible hand processes that pattern the unintended outcomes (structures in the system of 
shared linguistic norms) of intentional actions (particular utterances by individual agents).” 
These processes relate to the accessibility of particular pragmatic implicatures that draw on 
culturally speci c associations, as well as to the relative frequency of certain associations and 
utterances over others – both prerequisites to grammaticalisation.
As with semantic change, it is often dif cult to directly link particular instances of 
grammaticalisation to speci c cultural practices (see e.g. Simpson 2002; Evans 2003), but in 
some cases a connection appears highly likely. For example, Burridge (2002) describes the 
grammaticalisation of a future marker from the verb ‘count’ in Pennsylvania German, as well

---

<!-- page 607 -->

Historical linguistics and socio-cultural reconstruction 
589
as the more de-grammaticalisation-like development of a lexical verb ‘want’ from the modal 
auxiliary ‘would’. Both processes, she argues, have been driven by the cultural values and 
norms of this Old Order Mennonite community, which discourage blunt expressions of 
prediction and desire in the view that all individual plans and wishes are subordinate to the 
will of God. Another example where a connection between grammaticalisation and socio-
cultural practice seems relatively clear is found in systems of honori cs, as in Korean and 
Thai, which tend to re ect social strati cation. Similarly, the development of ‘kintax’ in 
Australian languages – systems of pronouns and verbal agreement markers that encode 
particular kin relations among the referents, such as ‘you two, husband and wife’ – are 
indicative of the cultural emphasis on kin and moiety relations (Evans 2003).
Again, as discussed for semantic change above, our possibilities for forming inferences 
about past socio-cultural practices from grammatical structures are limited, since we cannot 
normally establish a cultural motivation for their development in the absence of contemporary 
ethnographic relevance. However, structures like those described here take time to 
grammaticalise, and are thus an indication that their socio-cultural foundations do reach back 
into the past. Moreover, where grammatical subsystems such as honori cs or ‘kintax’ can be 
reconstructed to a protolanguage, we have an even clearer indication of the time-depth of 
their pragmatic motivations.
3.2 Grammatical indicators of contact-induced change
Grammar, like borrowed lexicon, may provide clues to past interactions among groups. 
Profound language contact can lead to extensive restructuring of grammatical subsystems 
and categories, with or without the additional borrowing of morphological forms. Such 
restructuring can occur when languages are maintained over time with pervasive bilingualism 
among speakers, and may be so extreme as to produce a high degree of congruence in 
grammatical pro les, even while the languages remain lexically distinct. Grammatical 
convergence of this nature is observed between the Austronesian language Takia and Papuan 
Waskia (see Ross 2001, who terms the outcome ‘metatypy’), and between the East Tukanoan 
languages of the northwest Amazon and Arawak Tariana (Aikhenvald 2002), as well as the 
Nadahup language Hup (Epps 2007, 2008), among many other examples.
Evidence that grammatical convergence has taken place in the past can shed light on the 
extent and dynamics of the interaction that once existed among the groups involved (cf. Ross 
2013). If restructuring has been largely one-sided (as is true for Hup, for example), then it is 
probable that the interaction was also socially or demographically imbalanced. For example, 
in evaluating the nature of the relationship between Aymara and Quechua, Muysken (2012a) 
proposes that the grammatical isomorphism but profound lexical difference between the two 
languages points to an early history in which a small pre-Quechua population was dominated 
by a larger Aymara-speaking group.
Changes in grammar can also provide clues to past contacts where languages have not 
been maintained. In such cases, the speakers of one language will have shifted to another, 
possibly resulting in the death of their original language. Frequently, shift scenarios of this 
kind will give rise to a new version of the target language, distinguished from the prior one 
by certain phonological features and grammatical structures – known as substrate features – 
that have been carried over from the original language of the community. The presence of 
substrate phenomena can be an indication that language shift has indeed taken place in the 
past, and may even help us to determine what the linguistic and ethnic af liation of the 
community was before the shift occurred. For example, Thomason (2009: 111–113) observes

---

<!-- page 608 -->

Patience Epps
590
that a shift from Cushitic to Semitic languages in Ethiopia is well evidenced by certain 
innovative structural features that set Ethiopian Semitic apart from other Semitic languages, 
such as labialised dorsal consonants, a new future tense, and new word-order patterns. 
Similarly, substrate in uence in Ecuadorian Quechua indicates that the pre-shift population 
of the region spoke Shuar and Barbacoan languages (Muysken 2012b: 239).
In other cases, evidence of contact is more limited, and merely suggests to us that we 
should look further to investigate the possibility that it occurred – and perhaps bring 
archaeology or other disciplines to bear on the question as well. Where past contact is in 
question, a linguistic argument that it must have occurred rests heavily on the typological 
rarity of the shared features, as well as their overall number – that is, how likely they are 
to have been independently innovated. Where several very unusual features or ‘quirks’ 
cluster in a region, then contact (or, possibly, very deep genetic relationship) becomes a 
likely explanation. For example, a possible connection between Semitic and/or Berber 
languages and the Insular Celtic languages of the British Isles is suggested by the shared 
presence of a number of typologically rare features, such as initial consonant mutation and 
verb-initial word order (see Gensler 1993). Similarly, the existence of several unusual 
phonological features (ejective consonants, uvular stops, palatal laterals, etc.) in the 
languages of the southern Andes and the Chaco likewise suggests an ancient link (Donohue 
and Michael 2010).
However, the further one goes back in time, the more tenuous one’s inferences become 
regarding the dynamics of contact. For example, where grammatical restructuring between 
two languages can be identi ed, determining whether it is due to convergence (metatypy) or 
shift is often not straightforward. This problem can be seen in Muysken’s (2012a) discussion 
of Quechua and Aymara, where he notes that ancient language shift from Aymara to Quechua 
(followed by the extinction of those varieties of Quechua that were not remodelled) is a 
possible alternative to convergence as an explanation for the similarities that exist between 
these two languages. In other cases, we may question whether any relationship even existed 
at all; for example, in the absence of other evidence, there seems to be no way to be sure 
whether the similarities between Semitic/Berber and Insular Celtic could not have come 
about by chance.
3.3 Insights from discourse
Discourse is also a possible source of insights into speakers’ pasts. We understand discourse 
here as instances of actual language use, patterned into systems of communicative practice 
(see Sherzer and Urban 1986; Beier et al. 2002: 122). It is well known that particular 
discursive conventions may occur widely and across unrelated languages, indicating that 
they are easily transmitted when groups come into contact. For example, discourse forms 
such as ritual wailing – a stylised mode of expression with characteristics associated iconically 
with crying (see Urban 1988) – are widespread across Amazonia (Beier et al. 2002), and 
probably have been present in the region for many centuries, and perhaps much longer.
Like any element of language for which a widespread distribution cannot easily be 
attributed to chance, shared discourse forms suggest relationships among languages – and 
their speakers – that are grounded in past interactions. While contact is often the most likely 
explanation for the spread of such features (see, for example, Epps and Stenzel 2013; 
Hosemann 2013), it may be possible to reconstruct discourse practices for a given language 
family, particularly if the practice exists in a widely distributed set of related languages and 
is absent from many of the languages that intervene between them geographically. Extensive

---

<!-- page 609 -->

Historical linguistics and socio-cultural reconstruction 
591
work on this topic has been carried out with Indo-European languages; see in particular 
Watkins (1995), who discusses the evidence for a common Indo-European tradition involving 
particular formulaic expressions (e.g. ‘imperishable fame’), themes (e.g. killing a dragon or 
serpent), metrics, and stylistics (e.g. alliteration and patterns of parallelism).
Efforts to reconstruct discourse styles, motifs, and genres can be compared to the 
reconstruction of cultural features more generally, which may be combined with information 
from lexical reconstruction. Where similar cultural practices are associated with a group of 
related languages – and, crucially, are largely absent from intervening, unrelated language 
groups, given the likelihood of diffusion – it may be possible to conclude that these practices 
existed among the speakers of the protolanguage. Such efforts are well known in Indo-
European studies, although they have often drifted into highly speculative territory; see, for 
example, Dumézil (1958, 1968) and Benveniste (1973), who addressed such areas as Proto-
Indo-European exchange practices, social status, law, and religion; see also discussions in 
Renfrew (1987: Ch. 10) and Mallory (1989: Ch. 4–5). Further examples of the application of 
the comparative method to non-linguistic practices include reconstructions of ethnobiological 
knowledge (Balée 2000) and practices such as tattooing (Walker et al. 2012) among the far-
 ung Tupi-Guarani groups of Amazonia.
Variations in register and style within particular languages may also provide clues to 
their speakers’ histories. Conventionalised differences between male and female speech 
may be indications of past contact and intermarriage; for example, seventeenth-century 
Black Carib (Garifuna), an Arawak language of the Caribbean, had distinct male and 
female varieties (still retained today in a few lexical doublets). The men’s variety involved 
a largely Carib lexicon, and appears to have emerged through mainland Caribs’ conquest 
of the islands and intermarriage with local Arawak women (Taylor and Hoff 1980). 
Similarly, ritual and shamanic language varieties sometimes exhibit forms that have been 
borrowed from other languages, but are not found in the everyday variants of the language. 
Such borrowings may be loanwords or even whole sections of text, and provide evidence 
for the sharing of ritual practices and belief systems among groups in the past. In the 
Amazon, songs in other languages are frequently found in the ritual repertoires of 
indigenous groups; for example, the text of the kapiwaya song cycle performed by 
Tukanoan and Nadahup peoples in the Upper Rio Negro region appears to be derived 
largely from Arawak (Piedade 1997; Jonathan Hill p.c.), and provides evidence for a strong 
Arawak cultural in uence in the past. Another potential contribution of ritual language to 
socio-cultural reconstruction lies in its frequent preservation of archaisms, which may 
represent the last trace of re exes inherited from the protolanguage. For example, in Piro 
(Arawak, Amazonia) the retention of a cognate term for ‘hammock’ in mythic discourse 
(and its absence from the everyday lexicon) provides evidence that the Piro abandoned 
hammocks when they came into contact with groups on the western fringe of the Andes 
that do not use hammocks (Gow 2002; Facundes 2002).
4 Conclusion
Historical linguistics offers a rich source of information about prehistory. Where inherited 
words and elements of grammar and discourse can be con dently reconstructed, they provide 
insights into the lives of speakers at the various stages de ned by nodes of the family tree. 
The investigation of lexical innovations, loanwords, and other traces of language contact and 
change give us clues to subsequent developments and to speakers’ interactions with other 
peoples. Language offers unprecedented possibilities to explore the less tangible aspects of

---

<!-- page 610 -->

Patience Epps
592
past lifeways, such as ritual and intellectual culture and ephemeral objects that leave no 
material footprint. When linguistic information is combined with insights from other 
disciplines, we obtain a powerful, multi-pronged approach for investigating the past.
As this discussion has indicated, however, there are substantial challenges in making solid 
inferences about the past from historical linguistic data. Most importantly, our insights into 
past society and culture must be built on careful, methodologically sound linguistic work. 
Undetected loans, spurious reconstructions, and faulty assessments of semantic shift will 
result in anachronistic projections, misplaced homelands, and erroneous historical accounts. 
We must not lose sight of the fact that the inferences we draw from historical linguistics are 
necessarily hypotheses, with varying degrees of probability. As argued here, the most reliable 
inferences about prehistory draw on broad semantic domains and are informed by a holistic 
picture in which linguistic reconstruction, borrowing, and innovation are all considered 
together as pieces of the same puzzle. Less controversial inferences will thus tend to be 
relatively general, as opposed to the much more speci c – but shaky – conclusions that may 
be drawn from individual data points (or their absence!).
Moreover, when bringing insights from historical linguistics together with information 
from archaeology, genetics, and other approaches to investigating human histories, it is 
crucial that each discipline’s contribution stand on its own feet (see the chapters by Heggarty 
and Pakendorf, this volume). If historical linguists take archaeological  ndings into account 
in interpreting linguistic data, for example, then these interpretations cannot be argued later 
to corroborate the archaeological evidence. Only where each discipline’s contribution 
emerges independently of the others can we be sure that we are building a reliable picture of 
the past.
As we add to the growing wealth of documentation and description of the world’s 
languages, and apply these data to deepen our understanding of their histories, we can look 
forward to fuller and richer portraits of past societies and cultures. For many regions of the 
world, such as Australia and South America, the explosion of quality descriptive and 
historical work in recent decades is now generating a rich set of new insights about prehistory. 
It is an exciting time to be engaged in historical linguistics and socio-cultural reconstruction.

<!-- page 616 -->

598
28
Prehistory through language 
and archaeology
Paul Heggarty
1 Language as a window on the past
Unlike most of this book, this chapter is not about the histories of languages themselves. 
Rather, it explores what those histories can tell us of the populations and societies that spoke 
those languages through the past. There has been a tendency in other disciplines to overlook 
how linguistics can open up its own ‘window on the past’, in some respects just as surely as 
can our genes, or the material culture remains left by our ancestors and analysed by 
archaeologists, and even early historical records. Although there is great potential here, it 
remains a challenge to work out exactly what the (historical) linguistic record really tells us. 
Moreover, that record in turn cannot safely be read without the complementary perspectives 
of the other disciplines, which historical linguists themselves have likewise tended to 
overlook. This chapter approaches the cross-disciplinary task from both directions, then: how 
historical linguistics might not only enrich, but also learn from, its sister disciplines in the 
study of the past.
Archaeology, genetics and historical linguistics all differ radically in both their data 
sources and analytical methods. In principle that should only make the various disciplines all 
the more complementary, but in practice the sub plots seem to contradict one another as often 
as they concur. Still, there was only one past, so the goal is a common one: to converge our 
different, partial perspectives into a more holistic understanding, coherent across all of our 
disciplines, of what really went on in human prehistory.
On the broadest level, the lessons lie in how language lineages form complex, unequal 
patterns from one part of the world to the next. Some regions attest to powerful convergence 
(e.g. the linguistic areas of the Balkans, Northern Asia or Mainland South-East Asia), while 
others show acute lineage diversity (e.g. New Guinea). Elsewhere, vast swathes of territory 
are deserts of linguistic diversity,  lled only by a single, deep-time language family such as 
Indo-European, Afro-Asiatic, Sino-Tibetan or Austronesian. All of these patterns cry out for 
explanations in the (pre)histories of human populations.
For an illustration on a  ner scale, while the Romance language family is known to derive 
directly from the Latin of the Roman Empire, in fact the distributions of the two do not match 
closely at all. Most of the Empire’s western half continues to this day the legacy of speaking 
‘in Roman’ (Latin rmnic	, whence ‘Romance’), but around the Eastern Mediterranean no 
such lasting imprint remains. The contrasts speak volumes about the different nature, scale 
and strength of Roman impact in different regions of the Empire, linguistic data all too often

---

<!-- page 617 -->

Prehistory through language and archaeology 
599
overlooked by archaeologists and (pre)historians. Similarly, Germanic-speaking tribes 
overran and ruled much of Western Europe for many centuries, but their linguistic fates paint 
a very variable picture of their impacts. The Franks and Burgundians in France, Lombards in 
Italy, and Visigoths in Spain, for example, all vanished into linguistic oblivion (while the 
local Romance prevailed). In most of Britain, however, the Germanic speech of the Angles, 
Saxons, Frisians and others soon rose to dominance, ultimately to become English. In the 
latter case, archaeologists and geneticists have long debated the respective strengths of native 
(Celtic) and incoming (Germanic) populations, but rarely invoke the striking linguistic 
contrasts that contribute their own valuable perspective.
2 Archaeology, genes and language: weaving the records together
2.1 Language, genes, culture, ‘peoples’, migrations?
How, then, might one best correlate the linguistic record of the past against the archaeological 
one? Most helpful to start with is in fact a cautionary tale of how not to go about it. Early 
thinking relied  rstly on monolithic, discrete concepts of languages, cultures, peoples, races, 
and migrations; and secondly, on simplistic equations between them all. The result was all 
too often that archaeologists, linguists and geneticists simply “built on each other’s myths,” 
as Renfrew (1987: 287) puts it. By the mid-twentieth century this had rightly engendered a 
generalised distrust of grand cross-disciplinary synopses.
Genetics and physical anthropology have long since given the lie to simplistic and 
mutually-exclusive categorisations of ‘peoples’, let alone ‘races’ (AAPA 1996). And 
although languages are typically passed on from parents to children, together with genes, 
they can also just as well be learnt by speakers irrespective of their own human genetic 
ancestry. Even a single bilingual generation can suf ce to mediate a language switch, and 
break for good the link between a given population’s genetic and linguistic lineages. Such 
issues are covered mostly by Pakendorf (this volume), however, while this chapter will focus 
on how historical linguistics relates to archaeology. Here too, out-dated visions of ‘cultures’ 
and identities as monolithic entities, and the re ex to invoke long-range migrations of 
‘peoples’, have given way to a more balanced vision in which more general processes of 
demographic and cultural change and dispersal often make for less romantic but more 
plausible explanations. Historical linguistics seems not to have fully caught up with this new 
understanding. It has tended to rely, for example, on traditional approaches of ‘reconstructing 
culture’ through language, or migrations inferred from splits in language family trees, in 
order to propose scenarios for prehistory.
Many of those scenarios are now being increasingly questioned by updated archaeological 
thinking, and by applications to language data of a range of new quantitative techniques 
based on modelling in terms of cultural evolution. This has drawn renewed attention to 
language data as a source on human prehistory, and sparked a vigorous debate between old 
methods and new. In this climate, this chapter makes no apologies for being provocative to 
both perspectives. Rather, it relishes a fascinating moment at which to be exploring language 
prehistory, as new insights challenge many of the old certainties, reinvigorating the  eld and 
throwing it wide open once more. However the methodological debate plays out, we can look 
forward to great potential for rapid progress in uncovering what language can tell us of our 
past.

---

<!-- page 618 -->

Paul Heggarty
600
2.2 Real-world cause … linguistic effect
If the equation ‘language equals genes equals culture’ is too naïve, how should we go about 
our task? To  nd the real equation that links our disciplines to each other, we need to return 
to  rst principles, to ask how it is that language(s) can inform us of the past at all.
The basic answer is a simple one, and lies in the nature of language itself as a ‘social 
animal’. It does not just happen that language lineages spread, interact, diverge or converge, 
as if in a social, cultural and demographic vacuum. What determines the (pre)histories of 
language lineages, and shapes how they relate to each other, are just the real-world contexts 
that impacted upon the populations that spoke them. This relationship is unambiguously one 
of cause and effect; and in a direction that is equally ineluctable. Whether, how, and which 
particular languages diverge or converge is not just some natural law of ‘what languages do’ 
or ‘how languages evolve’. Processes in the real-world context – demographic growth or 
collapse, migrations, conquest, or more subtle socio-political and cultural changes – are the 
cause (see section 6 below); they alone determine entirely the linguistic effects of divergence, 
diversity and convergence.
2.3 Language change is not language divergence
Lest this cause-and-effect principle be misunderstood, let us clarify immediately that 
language change must be distinguished from language divergence. Much of this book has 
been concerned with the mechanics of how language changes, as a question internal to 
historical linguistics; and external forces certainly do not normally determine the form and 
nature of whatever particular language changes arise. The detail of why Latin pluvit ‘it rains’ 
changed to plou¡ in Romanian, for example – or to (il) pleut in French, piove in Italian, llueve 
in Spanish and chove in Portuguese – has precious little to do with archaeology or history.
What external forces do determine, meanwhile, is whether those changes (whatever 
linguistic form they take) either develop independently and differently, or come to be shared, 
from one region to the next. That is, real-world contexts dictate not so much which particular 
changes occur, but which patterns of divergence may emerge from any changes. External 
processes determine how coherent are the populations that speak any given language lineage 
through time and geographical space. They further govern which population groups or 
cultural characteristics, language among them, spread at which times, and in which ways and 
directions.
Within Indo-European, for instance, the Romance, Germanic and Slavic branches each 
diverged into its own sub-family, whereas Albanian and Armenian remained as singleton 
branches. All underwent continuous change through time, but in some branches the changes 
patterned into major internal divergence, while in others they did not. All lineages changed, 
so language change per se cannot account for these very different outcomes. The explanation 
is not a linguistic one at all, but echoes the contrast between, in the cases of Romance, 
Germanic and Slavic, the lasting expansive impacts of Rome and of the forces that hastened 
its fall; and for Albanian and Armenian, the absence of expansive drivers of such scale. The 
patterns observed across languages – of diversity, relatedness, divergence and convergence 
– are outcomes at the receiving end of a cause- and -effect relationship, moulded directly by 
‘forces of history’ that are independent of language itself.

---

<!-- page 619 -->

Prehistory through language and archaeology 
601
2.4 A non-uniform prehistory
Within historical linguistics (e.g. Ringe et al. 2002: 60–63), it is common to invoke the 
general principle of uniformitarianism: that the mechanisms behind (language) change 
cannot be assumed to have been different in the past from those we observe in the present, 
nor from one part of the world to the next. Certainly, at the micro-levels of the mechanics of 
language change, and of communication and social interaction between individuals, 
uniformitarianism is a reasonable default assumption.
As just pointed out, however, this chapter is not primarily about such mechanisms of language 
change or interaction between individuals. Rather, here we need to abstract away from those 
micro-level mechanisms to the forces that determined the “history of everybody over the last 
13,000 years,” as Diamond (1997) puts it. For only by understanding the very unequal “fates of 
human societies” (Diamond’s alternative subtitle) can we account for the parallel fates of their 
respective languages. At this macro-level, nothing could be further from the truth than to 
imagine that the forces impacting upon human societies – above all, how some of those societies 
impacted upon others – have been constant worldwide and over time through the Holocene.
From a pre-Neolithic stage at which all humans lived in small-scale hunter-gatherer bands, 
only in some regions did a ‘Neolithic Revolution’ (principally the development of farming) 
set in train the eventual rise of ‘complex societies’ – complex in the archaeological senses of 
social strati cation, and territorial and demographic scale. Moreover, the Neolithic itself 
played out in very different ways from one time-period and one part of the world to the next. 
Indeed in many regions, farming and complex societies never arose indigenously at all; and 
even where they did, deep gulfs persisted between the cultures, technologies and farming 
‘packages’ of the indigenous civilisations of the Americas and those of the Old World, for 
instance.
Far from appealing to ‘uniformitarianism’, then, when it comes to the basic forces that 
shaped human prehistory it is only the very lack of any uniformity in how they applied across 
time and space that can explain, for example, the glaringly unequal impacts that the societies 
of Europe and the Americas had upon each other, or those that Bantu-speakers wrought on 
the populations (and thus languages) of much of Africa. World prehistory is patently not a 
story of equals and uniformitarianism – so nor is the linguistic panorama that it has shaped. 
Today’s catastrophic collapse in human linguistic diversity stands in stark contrast to the 
many previous millennia, over which conditions had been such that that diversity had arisen, 
not vanished (section 6.1).
2.5 Linking the disciplines: through processes
Patterns of language diversity or relatedness, and of divergence or convergence, are thus 
outcomes moulded directly by ‘forces of history’ independent of language itself. These are 
precisely the contexts that archaeologists too seek to uncover. For the same processes leave 
their imprints likewise in the material culture left behind by those same human societies, as 
well as in the bioarchaeological and genetic records (Pakendorf, this volume). It is here, then, 
through these processes, that the basic link between our disciplines lies.
Of the various aspects of the linguistic panorama, research on prehistory has hitherto 
focused very heavily on the world’s major language families, and how to account for them. 
As hinted at the close of this chapter in section 7, this focus is in fact rather unhelpful for the 
broadest perspective on (pre)history. But because historical linguistics itself has always 
focused on language families, inevitably this chapter must too. And for the (pre)historian, the

---

<!-- page 620 -->

Paul Heggarty
602
process that any language family effectively denotes is one of signi cant territorial expansion, 
for without it that lineage would simply continue as a single, small, coherent language. The 
key equation that links linguistics to archaeology (and genetics), then, is not that languages 
equal cultures, but that any language family equals some expansive process(es) – whether 
demographic, socio-political, cultural, or other.
2.6 Linking the disciplines: three levels
For any one family, debates tend to focus above all on its  rst major dispersal, on various 
levels. First, in geographical space, the language family’s homeland is the small original 
region where its ancestral proto -language was spoken, and out of which it  rst began to 
expand. Second, on the time dimension, the date of that  rst expansion gives us the family’s 
time-depth, by  rst setting in train its geographical divergence.
This already identi es two key levels around which the search for correspondences 
between linguistics and archaeology revolves: the ‘where’ and ‘when’ of language prehistory, 
i.e. geography and chronology. For a convincing match, the expansive processes that 
archaeologists detect in their record, and the effects (language families) that linguists see in 
theirs, obviously must together all point to the right place at the right time. Speci c methods 
have been proposed to analyse linguistic data in ways that can pinpoint, or at least narrow 
down, when and where a given (proto -)language was spoken.
There is another level too, just as essential, but less tangible and thus far harder to devise 
speci c quantitative techniques for, so it is approached more in terms of general theoretical 
models. As already stressed, a language family does not just come to exist for no reason. So 
there must also be a good  t between our disciplines on a third level of causation, the ‘why’ 
of language prehistory. This requires above all that any candidate for a real-world cause must 
be commensurate in scale with whatever linguistic effect it is invoked to explain.
An ideal family to illustrate these three questions of where, when and why is Indo-
European, for the two main hypotheses on its origins – actually  rst advanced by 
archaeologists, not historical linguistics – paint radically different scenarios on all three 
levels. They place the Indo-European homeland either on the Eurasian Steppe just north of 
the Black and Caspian Seas (e.g. Mallory 1989), or in the northern arc of the Fertile Crescent, 
through central-eastern Anatolia (Renfrew 1987). They set the time-depth when it  rst began 
to disperse and diverge at around either six millennia ago, or nine. And they see the process(es) 
by which it spread to such dominance as either the domestication of the horse, nomadic 
pastoralism and long-range migrations, or the spread of farming by ‘demic diffusion’.
Most of the rest of this chapter is structured around these three levels of when, where and 
why. Sections 4, 5 and 6 take each in turn to survey the main methods and models employed, 
both traditional and new. Naturally, discussion of individual illustrative cases has to remain 
concise here, but further coverage on each can be found in the seven corresponding continent-
by-continent chapters on language prehistory within Renfrew and Bahn (2014). Many cases 
continue unresolved, so the aim here is not just to set out the methods and models, but 
critically to assess the value and reliability of each, in particular in the light of the relevant 
aspects of archaeological understanding, often rather overlooked in historical linguistics 
itself. Before starting our survey, however, there is one fundamental and general issue to 
address  rst, for it underlies the thinking behind so many of the individual methods and 
models, both traditional and new: the ‘family tree’ concept.

---

<!-- page 621 -->

Prehistory through language and archaeology 
603
3 Models: trees, evolution … and real population history
However appealing the neatness of binary branches and evolutionary models may seem, the 
cause -and -effect relationship immediately suggests concerns when applied to language. It 
would seem naïve to assume that the trajectories by which human societies developed through 
time and geographical space, and how they impacted upon each other, should yield outcomes 
that would always, or even primarily, take the form of discrete changes of state and sharp 
branching separations, ‘preferably binary’. And the phenomena that have shaped those 
societies in so many complex, non-discrete ways are the same that determined the relationships 
between their languages.
This is not to deny that there is much to be gained from modelling. Provided that it is 
intelligently done, in full awareness of the inherent idealisations, modelling can certainly 
open up powerful analytical approaches not feasible without the more easily manageable 
structures that the tree idealisation permits. Bayesian approaches, in particular, are devised 
precisely to handle uncertainties such as those entailed by cross-cutting signals incompatible 
with any single branching pattern (Dunn, this volume). The danger lies in still sticking too 
close to the comfortable tree idealisation, however, when hypothesising on the real-world 
population contexts that might underlie the patterns in our linguistic data. Traditional 
historical linguistics also remains too closely wedded to its branching classi cations, as 
almost an ideal in itself – note for instance Robinson (1992: 249, 263), or how Ringe et al. 
(2002: 78, 110) talk of the ‘failure’ to  nd a ‘perfect phylogeny’ because of the ‘problem of 
Germanic’ and its ‘recalcitrant data’. As we shall shortly see, even in the best-studied of all 
language families, abstract debates among historical linguists have often included majority 
proposals that seem to  y in the face of what is otherwise known about population prehistory. 
Not surprisingly, such debates all too often end up bogged down in sterile disputes on which 
is the correct ‘tree’ for the family; disputes destined to remain never-ending in the many 
families where there probably never was a neat branching tree in the  rst place, as known 
population histories often imply.
English itself provides a clear example. Most linguistic reference works still explicitly 
identify its closest relative as Frisian, once the dominant language of the North Sea coast and 
offshore islands of the northern Netherlands and north-eastern Germany. Frisian does hold a 
handful of characteristics in common with English, and not with other continental Germanic 
varieties. Yet our historical and archaeological understanding of Germanic-speaking 
settlement of England allows for no dominant role either for the ancient Frisii, or for any 
other single group that may be more directly ancestral to today’s Frisians. There was 
conspicuously not just one group involved, and others like the Jutes, Angles and Saxons all 
loom larger. The historical linguist’s construct of Anglo-Frisian sits very uncomfortably with 
all else we know of the origins of the population whose language ultimately came to be 
English, arisen out of a great mixing of close Germanic dialects, within which any emergent 
‘Frisian’ characteristics were just one contribution. On all non-linguistic evidence, it seems 
implausible that a uniquely Anglo-Frisian branch could ever really have existed, which 
seriously misrepresents how English actually arose. Romance too, and Indo-European as a 
whole, likewise illustrate that even for the best known of all language families, centuries of 
research has still not led linguists to an agreed clear branching sequence. The most plausible 
explanation is simply that there never was one in the  rst place, in what actually happened in 
(pre)history. The real ‘problem’ is the model.
From the perspective of a realistic vision of population prehistory, it is above all in shaping 
historical linguists’ expectations that the tree model has led us most astray. It is as if the

---

<!-- page 622 -->

Paul Heggarty
604
default assumption in the discipline still remains that language families should diverge into 
tree patterns, and that the dialect continuum can safely be seen as an exceptional case. Worse 
still, continua are often effectively dismissed, as if fundamentally de ned by splits, but where 
contacts between the branches have since inconveniently confused that true underlying 
picture. In some modelling applications, dialect continua may have to be analysed in terms of 
“splits then contact,” but that is still to con ate different population histories (see Heggarty 
et al. 2010: 3829–3831).
Language data themselves, across great swathes of the world, deny the tree idealisation. 
Dialect continua, not neatly branching trees, characterise much of the Arabic-speaking world, 
Bantu over most of the southern half of Africa, the Sinitic family across China, the Indic 
languages of most of India, and large parts of major New World families such as Algonquian 
– to mention just a few examples. Even in Europe, dialect continua have only receded in 
recent centuries, with the rise of new social and national structures. Those should not mislead 
us into assuming that language families most typically diverge into neat branching structures: 
for most of history they appear not to have done so.
Excessive adherence to the tree idealisation has arguably been the single biggest spanner 
in the works that has frustrated and confused attempts to  t our languages more plausibly and 
coherently into population (pre)history as seen by other disciplines. So far-reaching are its 
repercussions for many of the methods now to be explored that we shall necessarily meet 
some of them below; see also Heggarty et al. (2010) and François (this volume).
4 Language chronologies
4.1 Dating by amount of change and divergence
A range of techniques aspire to date when a particular language family began to spread 
geographically, and thus diverge. At the heart of the main approaches is that every language 
lineage changes through time. This is of course why, once a (proto -)language is somehow 
spread into different regions no longer in close contact, changes accumulating differently from 
one region to the next will lead it to diverge into a family of related languages. So there is in 
principle some relationship between amount of time elapsed and amount of change and 
divergence observable within a family. Various techniques have sought to harness this 
relationship in reverse, to get from language change data to estimated dates. Here there is space 
only to discuss the two best known techniques, but together they illustrate the issues involved.
To linguists observing the early days of radiocarbon dating in archaeology, there seemed 
an enticing parallel in the ‘decay’ of cognacy counts in basic vocabulary. This was the 
inspiration behind glottochronology, devised in the 1950s by Morris Swadesh (with a number 
of variants since). Much more recently, a very different method has been introduced by 
Russell Gray and colleagues. This draws on Bayesian techniques  rst developed for 
phylogenetic analyses in the biological sciences. These techniques are explained more fully 
by Greenhill (this volume) and Dunn (this volume), and only summarised here for the 
purposes of our focus on the archaeological perspective.
The two techniques have in common  rstly the type of change that both use as data: the 
differences that accumulate between the vocabularies of related languages as they diverge, as 
some of the words that they inherited in common (‘cognates’) shift away from the original 
meaning or drop out of use. Secondly, both techniques are ‘calibrated’ – albeit by very 
different means – against known cases of language divergence over recent, historically 
attested timespans.

---

<!-- page 623 -->

Prehistory through language and archaeology 
605
There, however, the similarities end. Firstly, glottochronology dates from a simple, overall 
amalgamated count of how many cognate losses separate each individual pair of languages 
in a family. The Bayesian phylogenetic approach, meanwhile, makes use of the full detail in 
the patterns of exactly which cognates are shared across which combinations of languages 
across the entire family. Vast numbers of ‘family tree’ con gurations are hypothetically 
possible, which are therefore sampled for those most highly compatible with the patterns 
across the cognate sets in the data.
Secondly, glottochronology assumes one speci ed, general and constant rate of loss of 
cognates in all languages. A simple formula returns how long it would have taken, at that 
 xed rate, for any given number of cognate losses to arise. In the Bayesian approach, wherever 
within a particular family any reference dates are historically known, they are applied to 
calibrate the corresponding tips or nodes in the trees. All modern languages are thus calibrated 
to the present day, and past languages to the date-ranges of the written records that attest to 
them, as far as those are known archaeologically or from references in the texts themselves: 
Hittite tablets, Oscan inscriptions, Tocharian and Gothic texts, and so on (a total of 34 
calibrations in Bouckaert et al. 2012: Fig. S2). From such calibrations a time-depth for the 
entire family tree is in effect extrapolated, though crucially the ‘clock’ used to do this is a 
‘relaxed’ one, which can accommodate signi cant variation in rates of change over time: “on 
average within 47% of the mean rate” for Bouckaert et al.’s (2012: SI 7, 24) Indo-European 
database, for instance. The overall result is not one tree with one date, but a distribution of 
date estimates from thousands of trees selected as those most plausible given the language 
relationships represented by the input data (Gray and Atkinson 2003:  g. 1b–e).
These two main approaches can produce markedly different results, as in the Indo-
European case. Glottochronology gave a time-depth of 5,500 to 6,500 years, in line with the 
Steppe hypothesis (see section 1.1 above) and traditional thinking on other levels too (sections 
4.2, 5.1 and 6.3). Bouckaert et al.’s (2012) Bayesian phylogeny, however, yields results that 
instead match the nine millennia or so entailed by the Anatolian hypothesis (although 
Bouckaert et al.’s [2013] correction gives a rather younger estimate: see Heggarty [2014]). 
Many historical linguists remain far from convinced by the Bayesian phylogenetic approach, 
but have struggled to explain why it should not be at least as good as, if not an improvement 
upon, glottochronology, long viewed as “decisively discredited” by many in their own 
discipline (Dixon 1997: 36). As best appreciated from Dunn (this volume), the latest 
phylogenetic techniques are in a different league to simplistic glottochronology, in how fully 
they make use of the detail in the language data, and in the degree of sophistication developed 
to handle a range of issues already encountered in the biological sciences, and which offer 
partial workarounds for many of the concerns that arise when applied to language data too. 
(For more details, see Heggarty [2014].) This is our best shot yet at a method explicitly to 
measure amounts and pace of change in core vocabulary (and variation in both).
The perspectives of history and archaeology, though, do raise objections to the logic of 
dating by amount of change and divergence. Certainly, the cross-disciplinary parallel with 
carbon dating is deceptive. Above all, language is not subject to laws of the physical 
environment of the same ineluctable nature as those that govern radiocarbon isotope decay. 
Rather, recalling the cause- and -effect relationship (section 2.2), language divergence is 
determined instead by the many processes that shape the contexts through which speaker 
populations live, and which are anything but constant over time.
Rates of change (and divergence) can and do  uctuate in  ts and starts, under external 
impacts, such as those of Norse and then Norman French on English (see Heggarty 2010). And 
even without any such impacts, the basic determiner of whether and to what extent a single

---

<!-- page 624 -->

Paul Heggarty
606
ancestral language diverges into multiple descendant varieties is the degree of coherence of its 
speech community. That coherence is governed not by language itself, but again by the unstable 
‘forces of history’: demographic, geographical, social, political, cultural, and so on. Latin-
speaking settlement was largely contemporaneous over what thereafter became the Romance 
dialect continuum across south-western Europe. As attested by Roman authors themselves, 
regional divergence began all but immediately (and necessarily so for different substrate effects 
from one region to the next). The time-depth since divergence began is thus more or less the 
same for any two varieties of Romance: the two millennia or so since  rst Roman settlement.
The net degrees of divergence observed across the family today, however, vary greatly 
between different points within the old Romance continuum. Provençal is less divergent 
from Catalan than from Galician, for instance. But this is not because of different ‘split dates’ 
between the speech in these different regions; there is no sense in which Catalan ‘split’ earlier 
or later from one or the other. Degrees of divergence across Romance re ect not different 
start dates, but how many of the innovation waves spread far enough to be shared by any two 
given regions within the continuum.
Any measures of language divergence can thus also re ect differences not in time-depth 
but in the degree of coherence of speaker communities, especially across a continuum. Much 
of what determines that coherence is geographical proximity, which thus often makes for a 
rough proxy for degree of divergence. Catalonia is simply not so far from Provence as Galicia 
is, and their respective divergence measures are effectively largely a function of speaker 
community coherence over distance, not time. Glottochronology fails to recognise this other 
determiner of language divergence, and thus churns out dates that are historical nonsense. 
This has been clear ever since Rea (1958) showed how the original formula calculates that 
Italian ‘split’ from Catalan in 1516, and from Portuguese in 1440. Even if one tries to correct 
these dates through an adjusted rate of change, that will still produce historically meaningless 
variation in dates, when the start of divergence across all of Romance effectively began at the 
same time, Roman expansion.
How far might the latest phylogenetic techniques overcome the objections? Certainly, the 
model incorporates not a  xed universal rate of change but multiple calibrations speci c to 
each family, and above all a relaxed clock. Furthermore, Bayesian aspects of the approach 
used are purposely devised to handle and work around uncertainties, including in chronological 
calibrations. On these grounds, this technique should be more promising than glottochronology.
Nonetheless, concerns remain. In Bouckaert et al. (2012: SI Fig. S1), Romance is still 
necessarily presented as a binary branching tree (by maximum clade credibility, see Dunn, 
this volume: section 4.3.2), with dates still patently unrealistic in historical terms. The 
Spanish–Portuguese ‘split’, for instance, centres around AD 1530, when those lineages were 
already clearly distinct. The time-depth of the root of Romance is accurate in Bouckaert et al. 
(2012), but presumably thanks to Classical Latin being available as a calibration point. All 
further ‘split dates’ within Romance, however, long postdate the actual expansion of the 
Roman Empire. Bouckaert et al.’s sub-tree does not match what really went on as the family 
diverged, and their map sequence reconstructs Romance spread into Iberia, for example, over 
a millennium too late (Bouckaert et al. 2012: SI Movie). The problem remains as with 
glottochronology: the degree to which languages diverge is not only a function of a sequence 
of ‘splits’ extended through time, but that is effectively the only way in which this model can 
represent it, for it allows no role for speaker community coherence. For an exploration of the 
wider implications for Indo-European as a whole, see Heggarty (2014).
The repercussions are widest and most disconcerting for the critical issue of calibration 
itself. Our very impressions of how rapidly languages typically change and diverge over time

---

<!-- page 625 -->

Prehistory through language and archaeology 
607
have been shaped in rather circular fashion. For Indo-European itself has served for many 
linguists as an impressionistic benchmark for how quickly languages may change and diverge 
– but over a timeframe largely ‘calibrated’ on the assumption of the traditional Steppe 
hypothesis. Given all the uncertainties surrounding linguistic dating, it is ill-advised to 
presume such hypothetical matches between a language family and a particular archaeological 
culture or expansion, in order to invoke dates for the latter as if they were calibrations for the 
former. This truly does constitute “building on each other’s myths.” Note the repeated 
objections to this effect among the many critical commentaries published with Holman et 
al.’s (2011) recent proposal of another dating technique, their ASJP chronology, also 
ultimately based on the logic of dating by measures of change and divergence.
On calibration, too, other disciplines in population (pre)history bring a sobering 
perspective. Their experience gives no good reason to assume any great uniformity or 
regularity through time either in the coherence of speaker populations, or in their impacts 
upon each other. Rather, awareness of just how varied were the trajectories of human societies 
across the globe gives strong grounds to expect the opposite. Researchers with a quantitative 
penchant often (conveniently) presume that stepping back to a ‘big picture’ scale should even 
out any small-scale irregularities over the long run, so that some ‘average’ rate of change 
might after all be workable enough for linguistic dating. But in archaeological and 
anthropological thinking, the default vision is quite the contrary. If long-term trends are 
sought, they are not so much constant over time as exponential. Change is very uneven, but 
continuous and ever faster and more far-reaching since the ‘Neolithic Revolution’: change in 
the demographic and geographical scales of human societal units (from bands to tribes, 
chiefdoms, states and ‘civilisations’); in the disparities between them in complexity, 
organisation and technology; in population densities; in the ability to travel great distances, 
and thus the propensity to interact with and impact upon each other; and so on. All these 
potential drivers have in the long run been intensifying over time, so presumably their 
linguistic impacts should have accelerated too. Yet all we have to go on for our impressions 
or ‘calibrations’ of rates of language change over time are just those very latest periods for 
which we have reliable histories. That is, our calibrations are themselves biased towards the 
atypically rapid change of the most tumultuous recent times, and unreliable for extrapolating 
back ever earlier into prehistory.
