645
30
Indo-European
Methods and problems
Benjamin W. Fortson IV
1 The Indo-European family and its subgrouping
As of this writing, we stand close to the bicentennial of the 1816 publication of Franz Bopp’s 
Über das Conjugationssystem der Sanskritsprache in Vergleichung mit jenem der 
griechischen, lateinischen, persischen und germanischen Sprache (On the Conjugational 
System of the Sanskrit Language in Comparison with that of the Greek, Latin, Persian, and 
Germanic Language), widely regarded as the  rst of the foundational scienti c works of 
Indo-European linguistics. The passage of three or four succeeding generations saw the 
solidi cation of the  eld’s scienti c methods, in the wake of which much progress was made 
in reconstructing the phonology, morphology, and lexicon of the parent speech. There is 
much in current models of Proto-Indo-European (PIE) that those late nineteenth-century 
scholars would instantly recognise, but also much that would be entirely new: two whole 
branches of the family, Anatolian and Tocharian, were as yet undiscovered, and the 
rami cations of those discoveries for our picture of the proto-language are still being worked 
out. While some issues may appear to have long been settled, in other areas even the most 
basic of questions have remained considerably less tractable.
This chapter focuses on introducing the non-specialist reader to a very small selection of 
such questions, ones that have recently received attention and that are of general or 
methodological interest. It includes some reactions to new scholarship, interleaved with basic 
description of the family and a limited outline of reconstructed PIE.1 Section 1 discusses 
some recent work on subgrouping, principally a set of proposals by Andrew Garrett, and in 
Section 2 some issues in the phonological and morphological reconstruction of PIE will be 
addressed.
Ten branches of the family have been identi ed, which are, from the earliest to the latest 
documented appearance, Anatolian, Indo-Iranian, Greek, Italic, Celtic, Germanic, Armenian, 
Tocharian, Balto-Slavic, and Albanian. A few poorly known ancient languages, such as 
Thracian and Messapic, belong to the family as well but cannot be af liated with any of the 
known branches. Analysis of the reconstructed lexicon of PIE together with archaeological 
 nds suggests to most Indo-Europeanists that the proto-language was spoken in the steppe 
region north of the Black and Caspian Seas at least as late as the invention of the wheel 
around the mid-fourth millennium BC. See Anthony (2008). This is, interestingly, nearly

---

<!-- page 664 -->

Benjamin W. Fortson IV
646
exactly where the homeland was situated by the sixteenth- and seventeenth-century 
proponents of the ‘Scythian hypothesis’, the earliest recognition of the Indo-European family 
(cf. Campbell and Poser 2008: 19ff.). By the time of late antiquity in the West, IE languages 
extended over an area stretching from extreme western Europe all the way to China. All but 
the Anatolian and Tocharian branches have living members today.
Indo-European linguistics is not just concerned with reconstructing Proto-Indo-European, 
but also with accounting for the evolution of all of its daughters. Because of the considerable 
time-depth separating these from their common ancestor (in excess of three millennia in the 
case of our own Germanic branch), this task is not an easy one. The time-depth is also at least 
partly responsible for the dif culty the  eld has had in establishing subgroups within the 
family. Typical depictions of the Indo-European family tree are in the form of a starburst, 
with the branches radiating out from a single ancestral node, rather than a sequence of 
successive bifurcations like those established for many other language families. A growing 
consensus, though, brings the IE tree at least somewhat more in line with the norm in 
regarding Anatolian as the  rst group to branch off, followed by Tocharian. For the residue 
that would yield all the other branches, however, it has been enormously dif cult to agree on 
an internal phylogeny, and many contradictory groupings have been proposed.
The only criterion that can establish subgrouping is that of the shared non-trivial innovation 
(see chapters by Weiss, Hale and François, this volume). As all historical linguists know, it is 
not always clear whether a given feature represents an innovation or an archaism, and features 
can be shared due to later diffusion rather than common inheritance. Even what de nes a 
particular branch can require revision in light of new evidence. As tellingly illustrated by 
Garrett (2006), the picture of Proto-Greek changed radically once Mycenaean was deciphered 
in the 1950s: many innovations originally attributed to Proto-Greek and considered de nitional 
of this branch were not found in Mycenaean and thus must have arisen well after Proto-Greek 
unity, diffusing from one already differentiated dialect to the next.
Once the post-Proto-Greek innovations are segregated out, Garrett provocatively claimed 
that little if anything is left that distinguishes Greek from any of its neighbours or from a late 
(western) variety of PIE itself (‘Proto-Nuclear-IE’, as it is often called). According to him, 
the very task of subgrouping is not valid for PIE. In his view, at least some, and possibly all, 
branches of IE arose through local convergence events within what was originally a dialect 
continuum: over time, pockets of speakers/dialects disappeared, leaving gaps in the 
continuum, and within the remaining populations, linguistic features converged to produce 
the semblance of a coherent Indo-European dialect or branch. He wrote,
[A]t some early date – say, at the beginning of the second millennium BCE – the dialects 
that were to become Celtic, or Italic, or Greek, shared no properties that distinguished 
them uniquely from the other dialects… [T]he differentiation among dialects that were 
to become Celtic, for example, was no more or less than between any pair of dialects. … 
[T]he formation of a Celtic subgroup of Indo-European, the formation of an Italic 
subgroup, and even the formation of ‘Greek’ itself may have been secondary Sprachbund 
phenomena… These would represent linguistic areas, not merely the  nal landing sites 
of three discrete Indo-European subgroups after some millennial peregrination from the 
steppes.
(Garrett 1999: 152–153)
Garrett’s conclusions are approvingly cited in Clackson (2007: 14–16).

---

<!-- page 665 -->

Indo-European
647
The rest of this section will present a brief discussion of these views; of course the full 
complexity of these matters cannot be adequately dealt with here. At least two sets of issues 
are raised by these studies, one empirical-cum-interpretative (whether Greek or any of the 
other branches is suf ciently differentiated from its relatives to be considered a valid branch) 
and one methodological (whether traditional subgrouping is possible or warranted for IE). 
The material on which Garrett bases his conclusions about Greek purports to include the 
major phonological and morphological changes, but actually some important sound changes 
are left out (cf. Oettinger 2012: 224–225 and Rau 2012), and consideration of hundreds of 
potentially relevant items from in ectional and especially derivational morphology and the 
lexicon is not included.2 It therefore seems premature to claim that Greek does not have 
suf cient unique innovations to qualify as a ‘real’ branch before all of this material is 
thoroughly analysed (and technically, one non-trivial innovation is suf cient anyway).
Aside from underreporting the differences between Greek and the other branches, 
Garrett downplays some of the innovations that he does report. For example, he regards the 
Greek loss of the PIE ablative, whose functions were taken over by the genitive, as hardly 
signi cant because its loss “had begun in IE inasmuch as its forms were parasitic on the 
genitive in the singular and on the dative in the plural” (2006: 140; see below for an outline 
of the PIE case system). But there is no comparative basis for saying that loss of the ablative 
“had begun in IE.” First, outside of one nominal declension, the athematic (see below), the 
ablative was in fact fully distinct from the genitive in the singular, and some branches 
(Italic, Iranian) even extended that distinction to the athematics. Second, it was not a 
foregone conclusion that the ablative would eventually be lost: it is fully alive in half of the 
branches (Anatolian, Tocharian, Indo-Iranian, Italic, Celtic [Celtiberian only], and 
Armenian). And third, where it was absorbed by other cases, the developments are varied: 
it took over the functions of the genitive in thematic nouns in most of Balto-Slavic, was 
subsumed by the dative in Germanic and Old Prussian but by the instrumental in Gaulish, 
and only in Albanian (a branch geographically contiguous with Greek) was it also subsumed 
by the genitive. Garrett is mostly correct that in the plural the ablative was formally 
identical to the dative (though it is not true for the two oldest branches, Anatolian and 
Tocharian, whose ablative morphology is different from the rest of the family), but even 
this situation was modi ed in Balto-Slavic and Greek, where the genitive took over the 
ablatival functions of the dative-ablative ending in the plural. Thus the Greek development 
(considering singular and plural together) is a unique pairing of mergers; as such it deserves 
serious consideration as a phylogenetically meaningful innovation. Similarly, it can be 
questioned whether Garrett is correct that the innovated Greek dative-locative plural in  -si 
(modi ed from earlier *-su) is really “trivial” (ibid.) when no other branch underwent the 
same development except (again) the geographically contiguous Albanian, where however 
the ending functions as ablative, not dative or locative.
The other part of Garrett’s claim is that the differences within Greek (at a dialectal level) are 
on a par with the differences between Greek and other branches, and suggest the remnants of 
overlapping isoglosses from an old dialect continuum. It is entirely possible that, at some early 
date, the ancestors of the subsequent branches did form a dialect continuum, and it would be 
very interesting to try to recover information about it and the relative locations of the populations 
speaking those dialects. But the material adduced by Garrett does not lead ineluctably to the 
establishment of dialect continua. Thus he claims that the different West and East Greek  rst 
plural verb endings,  -mes (West) and -men (East), are shared isoglosses (in their terminations) 
with, respectively, Italic *-mos to the west and Anatolian * -wen to the east (1999: 149; 2006: 
142). But since * -mes/* -mos is also found in several other branches (including notably Indo-

---

<!-- page 666 -->

Benjamin W. Fortson IV
648
Iranian and Tocharian far to the east) and is agreed to be an inherited ending, it is a retention in 
Greek and not diagnostic of post-PIE dialectal diffusion. That means East Greek -men must be 
a post-Proto-Greek novelty; it could well have been acquired by contact with Anatolian as 
Garrett suggests (and as has been suggested for some other East Greek grammatical features), 
but again only after Greek had differentiated from its immediate ancestor.
Even if the evidence for an old dialect continuum were solid, the larger point is that it still 
would not vitiate attempts to establish subgroups, which is part of the task of reconstruction. 
This issue extends beyond Indo-European to reconstructive linguistic work on any language 
family. A reconstructed proto-language is a hypothetical grammar (or set of linguistic 
features) from which two or more other grammars (the sets of features being compared; 
informally, the daughter languages) can each be derived via transformations (linguistic 
innovations); the derivational pathway leading from the proto-language to each descendant is 
visually represented by a straight line in a family tree for heuristic purposes. Since the proto-
language and any intervening subgroup nodes (also proto-languages) are scholarly constructs 
that, to put it very informally, contain all and only the features that their descendants have or 
once had in common, they are by de nition not internally differentiated, unlike the real world 
of dialect continua from which they must be kept distinct.3 This means that if one retrojects 
innovated features shared variously by different daughters onto a dialect continuum, one 
shirks the task of reconstruction; and once one posits a dialect continuum to account for the 
comparative data, one cannot escape positing one for every earlier stage in nitely far back in 
time without being inconsistent (the ‘turtles all the way down’ problem).
It should also be added that dialect continua do not prevent the construction of traditional 
trees and subgroups, when carefully analysed and when the recoverable data are suf ciently 
rich. See, for example, Toulmin (2009), who uses both purely linguistic evidence and 
sociohistorical material to arrive at a diagram of the Indo-Aryan Kamta–Rajbashani–Northern 
Deshi Bangla subgroup that combines a traditional binary-branching tree (based on the 
linguistic evidence alone) with a visual representation of the spread (‘propagation’, in his 
words) of particular isoglosses (based on sociohistorical research to determine which groups 
had prestige at different times). More systematic and detailed internal reconstruction of the 
morphological developments of each branch will probably aid in the subgrouping of IE.
On balance, it seems too early to conclude that “the true  liation of the IE family is 
unknown, and it may be unknowable” (Garrett 2006: 143).
2 Proto-Indo-European grammar and some of its challenges
2.1 Phonology
According to the general consensus, the consonantal phonemic inventory of Proto-Indo-
European contained  fteen stops divided among three manners (voiceless, voiced, and what 
is traditionally termed voiced aspirated) and  ve places of articulation (bilabial, dental/
alveolar, palatal, velar, and labiovelar), yielding voiceless p t £ k kw, voiced b d ¤ g gw, and 
voiced aspirated bh dh ¤h gh gwh. The remaining consonants were the liquids l and r, the nasals 
n and m, the sibilant s (allophonically z before a voiced stop), the glides y and w (also written 
i , u ) and three so-called ‘laryngeals’ of disputed phonetic realisation standardly symbolised 
as h1, h2, and h3 (cover symbol, H). These were allophonically vocalised or supported 
epenthetic vowels when between consonants. Consonantal re exes of h2 and h3 survived in 
Anatolian; the vocalised laryngeals turned into short vowels in most branches. Otherwise the 
laryngeals only left indirect traces, principally the following: the second and third laryngeals

---

<!-- page 667 -->

Indo-European
649
‘coloured’ a neighbouring short e to a and o, respectively, and all three of them assimilated 
to preceding short vowels in the sequence VHC, resulting in compensatory lengthening of the 
vowel. PIE vowels included both long and short i e a o and u (technically, short i and u were 
just allophones of y and w); additionally, the liquids, nasals, and laryngeals had syllabic or 
vocalised allophones when adjacent to segments of lower sonority, indicated traditionally 
with a subscript circle (l¥ , r¥ , m¥ , n¥ ).
In the realm of suprasegmentals, PIE had a mobile pitch-accent system characterised by high 
intonation on or at the beginning of accented syllables, with the placement of the accent 
determined lexically rather than by the phonetic properties of particular syllables. Sometimes 
the position of the accent was determined by morphological properties, such as membership in 
a particular derivational or in ectional category (see below on the nominal accent-ablaut 
classes). The position of the accent could be lexically contrastive (as still the case in Vedic 
Sanskrit and Greek). All words except for a handful of enclitics bore an accent; the latter 
included postpositive conjunctions, clitic personal pronouns, and likely some discourse particles.
The stop inventory has been the source of numerous controversies. Chiefest has been the 
typological naturalness of the three reconstructed manners of articulation, a problem that 
stems from a declaration of Roman Jakobson in 1957 that he knew of no parallel for a 
language having voiced aspirated stops without the corresponding voiceless aspirates. 
(Earlier reconstructions of Proto-Indo-European did in fact include voiceless aspirates, but 
they have in the main been rejected for lack of decisive comparative evidence. Voiceless 
aspirates in Sanskrit, when not due to borrowing, arose from a combination of plain voiceless 
stop and *h2.) Certain distributional facts have also raised questions that appear to be related 
to this issue, such as the unexpectedly low number of reconstructable roots containing *b, 
and the fact that certain classes of stops could not co-occur within the same root (e.g. roots 
beginning and ending with a plain voiced stop are rare to non-existent).
Proposed solutions all involve reinterpreting one or more of the stop series as having had 
a different manner of articulation from what is traditionally posited. Most prominent has been 
the glottalic theory (originally Gamkrelidze and Ivanov 1973; Hopper 1973), which posits 
ejectives in place of the traditional voiceless series, alongside (in one version of the theory) 
voiceless and voiced stops that could be allophonically realised as breathy. The implosive 
theory (most recently Weiss 2009) posits an original voiceless – voiced – voiced implosive 
(or non-explosive) contrast. These models can provide more satisfying bases for understanding 
the distributional oddities alluded to above, and help to appease typological qualms. But 
since there is no direct comparative evidence among the daughter languages for any of the 
alternatively proposed series, meaning that ascribing them to immediately pre-breakup PIE 
complicates the prehistories of the attested branches, many scholars are more sympathetic to 
these alternative scenarios if they are ascribed to ‘pre-PIE’ rather than PIE. Some would then 
add that the typological rarity of the traditional PIE inventory rendered it unstable, such that 
it soon evolved into more stable systems in the nascent daughter branches. But this of course 
requires the contradictory claim of a ‘stable’ pre-PIE system that nonetheless underwent 
change, ad hoc, to an ‘unstable’ one, only to revert to a ‘stable’ system once again in the 
daughters. Typological rarity anyway does not necessarily entail diachronic instability, and 
all languages are ‘unstable’ in so far as they all undergo change in every generation (cf. 
Wichmann, this volume).
The traditionally reconstructed stop inventory remains the best model achievable by the 
Comparative Method, which is not to say that it must be correct; but it would require revision 
only if it could be shown to be impossible for natural human language. Our knowledge is not 
yet suf cient to prove or disprove its naturalness, though Weiss (2009) observes that in at

---

<!-- page 668 -->

Benjamin W. Fortson IV
650
least one documented sound change (affecting a variety of Central Tai in Cao Bang province, 
northern Vietnam), a series of breathy stops were created independently of the creation of 
aspirates.
2.2 Morphophonemics; types of inﬂ ection
The basis of Indo-European word formation was the root, an abstract entity that expressed a 
particular semantic concept but did not occur on its own. Roots normally consisted of a pair 
of consonants or consonant clusters, each  anking an interior vowel. The vowel was subject 
to various changes discussed below; for citational purposes, e (in a minority of roots, a) is 
considered the basic vowel, e.g. *sed- ‘sit’, *dheh1- ‘put’, *ters- ‘dry’, *spreg- ‘scatter’, 
*terh2- ‘cross over’, *tei- ‘dwell’, *sal- ‘salt’. Words were derived from roots by a variety 
of additive and transformative processes. The typical PIE word consisted of root plus suf x 
(which could be zero) plus in ectional ending (which could also be zero); word formation 
could also make use of pre xation, reduplication, in xation, and compounding. The 
transformative processes comprise the system of ablaut (or apophony), a set of alternations 
in the root-internal vowel producing  ve so-called ablaut grades. Thus from the root *sed- 
‘sit’ could be formed two so-called full-grades (e-grade *sed- and o-grade *sod -), zero-grade 
(*sd- ), lengthened e-grade (*sd-), and lengthened o-grade (*s½d-). (The vowel alternations 
in many English irregular or ‘strong’ verbs like sit/sat or sing/sang/sung directly continue the 
Proto-Indo-European system of ablaut.) Alternations among the ablaut grades, often 
combined with shifting of the accent and the addition of af xes, were employed in numerous 
derivational and in ectional processes. It is important to note that the grades taken alone have 
no inherent meaning; it is only within the context of the derivational and in ectional properties 
of a given word or complex of words formed from a given root that a particular grade signals 
membership in a particular category, just like the e of held signals past tense but the e of swell 
signals the present, and vice versa for the o of hold and (archaic and dialectal) swoll.
Due in large part, though not exclusively, to sound change, the productivity of the original 
system of ablaut mostly declined in the daughter branches, its functions increasingly 
supplanted by af xation in derivation and af xation or analytic constructions in in ection. 
Only Slavic experienced a major prehistoric proliferation of new ablaut grades, analogically 
created. Modern English is a typical example of what ultimately happened everywhere: many 
lexicalised relics of old ablaut are still found, with some new vowel alternations having 
entered the scene secondarily (from umlaut, e.g. man/men, foul/ l-th, or from a combination 
of ablaut and umlaut, e.g. drink/drench); but productive word formation and in ection 
exclusively utilise af xation and periphrasis.
Proto-Indo-European nominals, pronominals, and verbs were highly in ected. Up to 
nine cases have been reconstructed for nouns, adjectives, and pronouns: nominative, 
vocative, accusative, genitive, ablative, dative, instrumental, ablative, locative, and 
probably allative or directive. Singular, dual, and plural numbers were distinguished, 
though with some case syncretism in each, especially in the dual. A separate collective 
plural in *-(e)h2 was productive in Anatolian and traces of it are found in several other 
branches. This formation is identical with the neuter plural, as well as to a suf x used to 
form abstract nouns. The abstract noun-forming suf x has feminine gender in the non-
Anatolian branches, where it is additionally used to form oppositional feminines. Whether 
(and if so, how) the collective, abstract, and feminine are related is the subject of much 
ongoing debate, as is the question of whether PIE already had a distinct feminine 
grammatical gender. See the collection of papers in Schuhmann and Neri (forthcoming) for

---

<!-- page 669 -->

Indo-European
651
recent work on this issue. Anatolian only distinguished animate and neuter; usually the rise 
of an oppositional feminine is viewed as an innovation (of a type found commonly at the 
higher end of the animacy scale in other languages) that occurred after Anatolian split off 
from the family.
There were two general types of in ection for nouns and adjectives, and also verbs: 
thematic and athematic. In thematic in ection there was an ablauting ‘theme vowel’ or stem 
vowel *-o/e- before the grammatical desinence. In thematic nouns and adjectives, the vowel 
was usually -o-, and this class of nouns is therefore called o-stems; in thematic verbs, the 
stem underwent more pervasive ablaut (see further below). The actual case-endings are 
mostly the same for thematic and athematic stems, though there are some notable differences 
that are as yet not entirely explained (e.g. athematic genitive singular *-(o/e)s versus thematic 
*-osyo).
2.3 Nominal inﬂ ection and derivation
Anatolian in general presents a somewhat different picture of nominal in ection from the 
other daughters, especially in most of the plural oblique cases (dative-locative, ablative, and 
instrumental): the ablative and instrumental endings were indifferent to number, and the 
labial formant (*-bh- or, in Germanic and Balto-Slavic, * -m-) used to form the dative, 
ablative, and instrumental plurals in the other branches is absent from the case system. Also 
missing is the locative plural ending *-su. It has been repeatedly suggested that these endings 
resulted from a grammaticalised suf xation of originally independent postpositions that 
arose after the early split between pre-Anatolian and the rest of IE, before which these cases 
were underdifferentiated in the plural. Many believe that the next branch to split off was 
Tocharian, which also does not evince any of this plural case morphology. Normally 
Tocharian is supposed to have had, and then lost, most of the original stock of IE endings; it 
actually preserves more than half of them in the singular (nominative, vocative [in Tocharian 
B], accusative, either the genitive or the dative [it is unclear which], and ablative [in Tocharian 
A]) but, tellingly, only the nominative and accusative survive of the original plural set (the 
rest of the plural endings were refashioned agglutinatively by adding the singular endings to 
the plural oblique [< accusative] stem). It is thus thinkable that Tocharian also re ects a stage 
where most of the plural oblique cases were underdifferentiated.
In PIE, athematic in ection was usually accompanied by shifts in the location of the 
accent and concomitant changes in the ablaut grades of the morphemes in a word, whereas in 
thematic in ection paradigmatic accent and ablaut were  xed. Several general patterns have 
been observed in the paradigms of athematic nouns. In describing these, one speaks in terms 
of a cline of apophonic ‘strength’ with lengthened grade being the ‘strongest’ and zero-grade 
being the ‘weakest’ grade, with full-grade (i.e. e-grade or o-grade) being in the middle. 
Typically, only one morpheme per word is in a ‘strong’ grade and bears the accent, the rest 
usually being in the zero-grade. In most athematic nominal paradigm-types (called accent-
ablaut classes), the location of the strong grade shifts one or two morphemes to the right as 
one moves down the paradigm from the so-called ‘strong’ cases (nominative, vocative, and 
singular accusative) to the ‘weak’ cases (the rest, though one type of locative, called the 
endingless locative, tends to pattern with the strong cases). For example, strong stem *mén-
ti- ‘mind’ moved the full grade and accent to the derivational suf x -t(e)i- in the weak stem 
*mnî -téi-; strong stem *h2éus-os- ‘dawn’ moved them all the way to the in ectional ending in 
the weak stem *h2us-s-´, and strong stem *ph2-tér- ‘father’ did likewise, weak stem *ph2-tr-´.

---

<!-- page 670 -->

Benjamin W. Fortson IV
652
A few showed no mobility of accent but simply change of ablaut in the root syllable, such as 
*pód- ‘foot’, weak stem *péd-.
Of typological interest is the mutual interconnectedness of the accent-ablaut classes within 
the system of internal derivation, whereby a noun or adjective could be derived from another 
noun simply through transfer to a different class. Typical derivatives formed in this way 
include nouns or adjectives of appurtenance, possessive adjectives, collectives, and second 
members of exocentric (possessive) compounds. In the normal procedure for internal 
derivation, the weak stem of the base became the strong stem of the derivative, which 
essentially means that in the derivative the accent (and with it the strongest grade) is shifted 
one syllable to the right vis-à-vis the base. Thus from the noun *rót-u ‘strength’, weak stem 
*krét-u- (with change of ablaut in the root but no shift in accent) was formed the adjective 
*rét-u- ‘having strength, strong’, weak stem *rî t-éw- (belonging to a different accent-ablaut 
class where the accent and strong grade shift from the root to the suf x in the oblique cases). 
See Nussbaum (1986) and Kim (2013).
In a number of other instances, a derivative was built in this manner not from a weak stem, 
but from the locative case. For example, the word for ‘earth’ had nominative singular *dhéh-
½m (directly continued in Hitt. t	kan) and locative *dhh-m-én; to this locative a new noun 
with nominative *dhh-ém-½n ‘one on the ground/earth, earthling, human’ was built, the source 
of e.g. Lat. hom ‘human being’ and Old English guma ‘man’. Several other types of so-called 
decasuative formations (sic; from Latin csus ‘case’) have been recognised as well and are 
currently a hot topic of research, including those formed by suf xation rather than internal 
derivation (e.g. locative *dóm-i ‘at home’  adjective *dóm-i-yo- ‘located at home, belonging 
to the home’ > Vedic Sanskrit dámiya-; instrumental *h1rudh-eh1 ‘with redness’ forming the 
basis for the stative verb *h1rudh-eh1-ye/o- ‘be red’ > Latin rub	re, Old High German rot	n).
Advances in understanding derivational morphology extend outside the athematic nouns 
to the thematic nouns as well, with many discrete processes now recognised: e.g., possessive 
derivatives could be formed by suf xing an accented thematic vowel *-ó-; adjectives could 
be substantivised by retracting the accent; thematic adjectives could be turned into i-stems to 
form abstract nouns; and many others. All of these  ndings have elucidated an impressive 
number of derivational pathways that provide powerful modelling tools for the etymologist 
and that allow a great variety of formations to be coherently related. For example, Greek 
op n ‘attendant’, Latin socius ‘comrade’, and Vedic Sanskrit sákh ‘friend’, all derivatives 
of the root *sekw- ‘follow’, can be connected by starting with an o-grade action noun 
*sokw-eh2 ‘a/the following’, which formed the base for a thematic adjective *sokw-h2-ó- 
‘characterised by the act of following, i.e. following, attending’, from which an i-stem abstract 
*sókw-h2-i- ‘attendance’ was derived; this in turn spawned two derivatives, a new thematic 
adjective *sokw-h2-i-ó- ‘characterised by attendance’, the source of Latin socius (itself a 
substantivisation of the adjective), and, by internal derivation, an animate i-stem noun with 
nominative singular *sókw-h2-½i meaning ‘one characterised by attending’, the direct source 
of Vedic sákh. The original abstract noun *sokw-eh2 underwent suf xation with the Greek 
agent-noun suf x -n- to produce op n above. (The sequence *eh2 became  prehistorically; 
after the labiovelar stop *kw, *h2 disappeared, but aspirated the stop in Sanskrit.)
Such derivational chains are met with frequently in contemporary literature. A 
methodological danger they pose is that they are dif cult, if not impossible, to falsify: on 
paper one can derive almost anything from anything else with the right combination of 
derivations, analogies, and imagination. An important task for the future is establishing 
constraints on their application, as by trying to determine how long any given process 
remained productive in the prehistories of each daughter branch.

---

<!-- page 671 -->

Indo-European
653
The PIE pronominal system and numerals will not be discussed here. For an overview see 
Fortson (2010a: 140–147), and for more detailed treatments G. Schmidt (1978), Gvozdanovi 
(1992), and Katz (1998).
2.4 Preverbs, verbal morphology and syntax
There was a class of locational/spatial (secondarily temporal) adverbials that variously 
function as preverbs, adverbs, and adpositions in the daughter languages. In Anatolian, Indo-
Iranian, and sometimes Homeric Greek their syntactic position was not  xed, which can lead 
in some cases to more than one possible structural analysis. For this reason it is often claimed 
that the proto-language did not distinguish these usages and did not yet know a category of 
true adpositions. But since unambiguous adpositions are robustly attested in all the daughter 
languages, there is no good reason to deny their existence in PIE. It is also often averred that, 
as adpositions, they were all originally postpositional and only later became prepositional. 
Postpositions are the rule in Anatolian and form the majority in Tocharian and Indo-Iranian. 
But the comparative material is complicated and requires more careful study, and not all 
postpositions are necessarily more archaic (see e.g. Fortson 2010b on Italic). In their function 
as preverbs these adverbials tend diachronically to lose their status as independent lexemes 
and to univerbate with verbs (nominal derivatives of such preverb–verb combinations were, 
however, univerbated from the start). This process is not attested for the oldest Indo-Iranian 
but had already affected two non-productive preverbs in Anatolian, pe- ‘hither’ and u- ‘away, 
hence’, whose functions were also atypical of preverbs in being essentially deictic.
By the close of the nineteenth century, the following ‘classical’ picture of PIE verbal 
morphology had been constructed. Finite verbs could distinguish tense, mood, voice, and 
aspect in three persons and three numbers (singular, dual, plural). There were three basic verb 
aspect stems: present (imperfective), aorist (perfective), and perfect (stative-resultative). Not 
all verbs could form all three stems; some, for example, appear only to have formed present 
stems, some others only aorists. From the present stem were formed a non-past and past 
tense, the present and imperfect; from the aorist was formed a past tense. The perfect is 
somewhat set apart from the present–aorist opposition, not only in possessing a different set 
of personal endings but in being a stative or resultative rather than a tense per se. This is true 
of many of the perfects in older texts in Indo-Iranian and Greek, as well as of isolated perfects 
in other branches (e.g. Lat. memin ‘I remember’), but the resultative use is also robustly 
attested from early on and was likely already a feature of the perfect in the proto-language. 
An indicative, subjunctive, and optative mood have been reconstructed, available to all three 
tense-stems. The subjunctive was used modally and as a future tense, and is the source of 
futures in several daughters. There were two voices or diatheses, active and middle, the latter 
functioning secondarily as a passive and forming the source of most passive verb endings in 
the daughters. The choice of diathesis was in many cases lexical rather than oppositional: 
some verbs conjugated only in the active, some only in the middle, and some in one voice in 
one tense and in another in another tense. Verbs that conjugated only in the middle are called 
middle verbs (the source of the ‘deponent’ verbs in the Classical languages) and frequently 
formed active perfects; the perfect endings are in fact extremely similar to the middle endings, 
and the nature of the relationship is one of the most hotly contested areas of the  eld. For one 
detailed theory see Jasanoff (2003). A present imperative (in both second and third persons), 
as well as active and middle participial suf xes, complete the picture. Whether PIE had true 
in nitives (aside from a gamut of verbal nouns that could function in quasi-in nitive function)

---

<!-- page 672 -->

Benjamin W. Fortson IV
654
is not fully clear, though at least one formation (built from a stem *-dhyo-) is continued only 
in in nitives and is a candidate for a genuine PIE in nitive suf x. See Fortson (2013).
The discovery of Tocharian and Anatolian in the twentieth century forced many to rethink 
large parts of this picture. Though the oldest attested branch, Anatolian lacks many of the 
formations posited in the ‘classical’ picture of PIE verb: it has only a present and a preterite 
indicative and imperative in the active and middle. This has raised the question as to whether 
Anatolian simply lost the other categories, or whether PIE itself had not yet developed them, 
or some combination of the above. It is normally agreed that the ancestor of Anatolian had an 
imperfect and an aorist that fell together as the preterite. On many other issues, however, 
there is no consensus, and the meagreness of the available evidence only intensi es debate. 
Whether the subjunctive existed in the ancestor of Anatolian, for example, currently hinges 
on the interpretation of a single set of imperatives in Hittite (see Jasanoff 2012).
The personal endings are classi able according to at least three overlapping functional 
parameters – active versus middle, primary (non-past) versus secondary (past), and perfect 
versus non-perfect. Morphologically the endings fall into two broad groups: the perfect and 
(secondary) middle endings are essentially identical save for the ablaut of the desinential 
vowel, and are entirely different from the active endings. The primary endings were mostly 
derived from the secondary endings by the addition of an  -i (hic et nunc particle) in the active 
and -r in the middle. The distinction between thematic and athematic in ection is of little 
moment for the verb endings, except for the notable exception of the  rst singular active 
present, which was *-mi in athematic verbs but *-o-h2 in thematic verbs. The  rst singular 
middle also contains *h2 (*-h2o(r)), one of several facts that led to an in uential theory that 
the thematic conjugation originally arose from the middle (Watkins 1969).
Complicating the internal history of the different in ectional types in the verb is, again, 
Anatolian. This branch has two conjugational classes, one whose singular endings (in the 
present) go back to *-mi *-si *-ti (the normal PIE primary endings) and another re ecting the 
set *-h2ei *-th2ei *-ei, which are the PIE perfect singular endings plus the hic et nunc particle. 
This second conjugation is called the  ¦i- conjugation. Countless theories have been proposed 
over the near century since Hittite was deciphered in order to make the Anatolian facts jibe 
with the facts from the other IE branches. Formally, vanishingly few if any of the ¦i-
conjugation verbs can actually continue PIE perfects, which led Jasanoff (most recently and 
exhaustively articulated in 2003) to the view that PIE itself had a ‘h2e-conjugation’ continued 
directly in the Anatolian ¦i-conjugation. Under this approach, outside Anatolian, h2e-
conjugation presents were generally remade as thematic presents; possibly the 1sg. thematic 
ending * -o-h2 contains the old *-h2e of the h2e-conjugation. This view is part of a much 
broader theory about the entire prehistory of the Indo-European verb which is still being 
evaluated by specialists. Even more so than a century ago, virtually every fundamental 
question about the verbal system of PIE is subject to intense debate.
Diachronic syntax still being in many ways in its infancy, and with our lacking the ability 
to reconstruct PIE sentences, little aside from some fairly basic syntactic rules and phenomena 
can be hazarded for the proto-language, such as WH-movement and topicalisation. For 
discussion of general issues see Fortson (2010a: 152–168).
3 Conclusions
Scholarly  elds, like languages, are dead when they cease to change. This fate will not soon 
befall Indo-European linguistics. In addition to the spate of unanswered questions that we 
have sampled in this brief survey, every archaeological season sees the discovery of new

---

<!-- page 673 -->

Indo-European
655
epigraphic and documentary material in virtually every branch. Many of these discoveries 
have expanded our knowledge in dramatic ways – and have simultaneously added to the 
questions for specialists to debate.
Notes
1 
Much more detailed descriptions of Proto-Indo-European and its descendants are widely available; 
modern overviews include Ramat and Ramat (1998), Clackson (2007), and Fortson (2010a).
2 
Garrett is aware of this material in a broad sense (“Greek in the second millennium already had a 
distinctive derivational, lexical, and onomastic pro le,” 142) but does not detail any of it. He 
ascribes the derivational/lexical innovations to an early dialectal phase predating the changes to 
phonology and (in ectional) morphology (143), and forwards a speculative explanation for this 
putative two-stage development (147). Whether or not one follows him, the large quantity of 
innovations comprising his  rst stage must be stressed more than it is in his discussion. It is not 
clear how this stage is to be distinguished from a set of traditional subgrouping events. (I would 
like to thank Andrew for discussing some of his ideas with me; he would probably not agree with 
much of my assessment.)
3 
The con ict between idealised family-tree diagrams and the messier real world that is at the heart 
of Garrett’s broader methodological concerns is of course an old one, dating back at least as far as 
Johannes Schmidt’s (1872) proposal of the ‘wave model’ alternative to the family-tree model, 
with which Garrett’s approach has much in common.  See also chapters by Hale and François in 
this volume.

<!-- page 675 -->

657
31
The Austronesian language family
Ritsuko Kikusawa
1 An overview
The Austronesian language family consists of some 1,200 languages, which are widely 
dispersed throughout the islands of Southeast Asia and the Paci c, with a few members 
spoken on continental Asia (see Figure 31.1). Malagasy, spoken in Madagascar located off 
the east coast of Africa, is an enclave of Austronesian in the west, while Hawaiian, Rapanui 
(spoken on Easter Island), and MÈori (New Zealand) are the languages spoken farthest to the 
north, east and south, respectively. This geographical span was accomplished as a result of 
highly developed prehistoric sailing technology, and was the largest of any language family 
before the spread of Indo-European languages in the colonial period. The membership of the 
languages in this family is considered to be generally well established with regular sound 
correspondences and shared lexical items (see Figure 31.2), and this makes Austronesian 
probably the biggest clearly recognisable language family in the world. The member 
languages vary from those with millions of speakers (such as Malay/Indonesian, Javanese, 
and Tagalog) to a large number of languages with only a handful of speakers.
The existence of ‘similar’ languages covering a vast area reaching from the western edge of 
the Indian Ocean to the eastern Paci c was recognised by European travellers from the beginning 
of the seventeenth century, during the period when sailors began exploring the Paci c and Indian 
Oceans. The name Maleisch-Polynesisch (‘Malayo-Polynesian’) was proposed for this group of 
languages by F. Bopp in 1841, then the term Austronesian (‘southern islands’) by Wilhelm 
Schmidt in 1906. The  rst major systematic reconstruction of Proto-Austronesian phonology 
and vocabulary is seen in Dempwolff’s work (Dempwolff 1934, 1937, 1938), following 
pioneering works by H. N. van der Tuuk (1971), H. Kern (1913–1928) and E. Stresemann 
(1927). Contributions by various scholars have added since to our knowledge regarding the 
genetic relationships among member languages, features of proto-languages within the group 
and changes that have taken place in their daughter languages, to form what is currently known 
as the Austronesian language family, a synopsis of which will be presented below. For the latest 
summaries of the current status of Austronesian historical linguistics and references to the major 
contributions in the  eld, see Lynch et al. (2002) for Oceanic languages, Adelaar (2005) for 
western Austronesian languages, and Blust (2013) for an overview of the whole family.
The historical comparison and reconstruction of Austronesian languages has been based 
almost solely on data drawn from the description of contemporary languages. There is no 
equivalent in Austronesian languages of Latin or Sanskrit corpora, upon which the reconstruction 
of Indo-European languages is heavily based. The oldest attested documentary materials are 
stone inscriptions of Old Cham (fourth century AD), Old Malay (seventh century AD)

Rotuman
Tongan
Polynesian
Amis
Figure 31.2 An Austronesian family tree.
*Western Malayo-Polynesian is not considered an established subgroup. It is a label referring to a group 
of languages that do not share the deﬁ ning innovations of the Central-Eastern Malayo-Polynesian group.
and those in Old Javanese and Balinese (ninth century AD), which however because of the 
phonological uncertainty of the scripts used (ultimately derived from Devanagari) and their 
limited textual content, do not provide useful information for historical comparison and 
reconstruction (Adelaar 2005: 3–5). This situation, contrasting with that of the Indo-European 
language family where philological approaches form part of the foundation for its historical

---

<!-- page 678 -->

Ritsuko Kikusawa
660
study, can be seen as a test case of the application of the Comparative Method to contemporary 
languages. Examining what has been successful and what has been problematic in Austronesian 
historical linguistics therefore has the potential to take us beyond the Comparative Method as 
it is currently applied. In this chapter, some aspects are introduced from such a view point.
I will  rst take a look at earlier reconstructions of some Proto-Austronesian phonemes, 
starting from the very  rst application of the Comparative Method to a set of Austronesian 
languages that had been recognised as ‘similar’, to current reconstructions of proto-phonemes. 
This will give us some idea about the transition in Austronesian historical linguistics in the 
past 80 years (see section 2).
I next discuss issues related to subgrouping hypotheses and how they are displayed. The 
Austronesian language family consists of some subgroups that are considered to be clearly 
de nable versus those that are not so. For the former, some of their de ning shared innovations 
are described, while for the latter, an explanation will be proposed to account for the problems 
that such groups present when attempting to determine their genetic relationships. The 
problem of con icting sound correspondences and overlapping patterns of innovation raises 
the problem of how best to diagram the various relationships that exist between related 
languages (see section 3).
One of the traditions in Austronesian historical linguistics, where comparison establishing 
sound correspondences has strictly been applied, is to distinguish direct and indirect 
inheritance based on the sound correspondences; indirect inheritance is associated with those 
correspondences that are the result of language contact between languages that are genetically 
related. Speci c examples are presented from Rotuman, a language spoken in the north of 
Fiji, to illustrate the distinction between the two types of inheritance (Biggs 1965; Schmidt 
2003). The lexical reconstructions that follow from the identi cation of distinct correspondence 
sets allow inferences about culture history to be made.
It is not only lexicon that spreads from one language to another. Diffusion of syntactic 
structures in certain bilingual communities, without the spread of phonology and lexicon, has 
been described by Ross (1996b) and labelled ‘metatypy’ (see also Ross 2013 for a re ned 
description of the processes involved in the spread of syntactic structures as a result of 
bilingualism among adolescents and pre-adolescents, and language shift by adults). This not 
only raises the question of the genetic relationships of the languages involved, but is relevant to 
an understanding of the socio-political factors that bring such a situation about (see section 4).
Austronesian languages are typologically very diverse. In section 5 some of the rich 
syntactic and morphosyntactic diversity will be described from a diachronic perspective. 
Finally, we will mention some of the language families that have been proposed as sister 
language families to Austronesian (see section 6).
One of the major results of the historical study of this language family is that the homeland 
(Taiwan) can be unambiguously determined, and the general direction of migration of the 
spread of the family through the Philippines and areas south and ultimately into the Paci c 
can be associated with linguistic innovations. Much has also been learned about prehistoric 
contact between the groups, contributing linguistic evidence in support of anthropological 
and archaeological evidence for extensive trade networks in the Paci c and elsewhere (cf. 
chapters by Heggarty and Epps, this volume).
2 Early reconstruction of the Proto-Austronesian sound system
Since Dempwolff (1934–1938), the Comparative Method has been carefully applied to 
Austronesian languages to test and re-de ne the genetic relationships between them that had

---

<!-- page 679 -->

The Austronesian language family
661
long been assumed to exist based on the strong lexical similarities (a methodology which 
matches the recognition and development of the subgrouping relationships of Indo-European, 
Nichols 1996). The results have changed considerably over time, as data from new languages 
have become available and the perception of scholars toward the proto-system changed. 
As an example, the transition in chronological order of the reconstructions of coronal 
consonants that have been proposed by different scholars, starting with Dempwolff is shown 
in Table 31.1.
Dempwolff ((1) in Table 31.1) established his proto-system (his Ur-Austronesisch, and 
what today is called Proto-Malayo-Polynesian or Proto-Extra-Formosan) without access to 
data from any indigenous languages of Taiwan. As data from these languages became available 
and were recognised as constituting part of the Austronesian language family (as, for example, 
by Dyen (2), and Tsuchida 1976 (3)), they were included in the comparisons and the number 
of reconstructed sounds increased considerably. Reconstructed symbols were considered to be 
representations of distinct sound correspondences that had been found in the daughter 
languages, regardless of whether the correspondences were the result of regular sound change 
in the language or the result of other factors, such as what today would be recognised as 
complementary distribution, phonemic split or borrowing from related languages. They took a 
strict position in representing each set of distinct sound correspondences with one symbol, 
forming what Dempwolff referred to as a system of reference, combining reconstructed sounds 
that were considered to be probably real sounds in the proto-language with  ctive ones, those 
that labelled a sound correspondence set but which could not be considered to be real sounds. 
It should be remembered that Dempwolff considered himself to be a Neogrammarian and did 
not endorse the newly understood concept of the structuralist phoneme with its concepts of 
contrast and complementary distribution (see Dahl 1973: 8–9 for discussion). As a result, at 
their peak, reconstructed consonants alone probably amounted to more than 50 (with Dyen 
distinguishing six different sibilant correspondence sets with the labels S1–S6, as well as *x1–
*x2, and *X, and multiple correspondences labelled *D, *R, *H, etc.). A table of approximate 
equivalences among reconstructed sound systems is found in Ross (1995: 54).
The reduction of the number of symbols in subsequent reconstructions re ects a change in 
the perception of scholars as to what a proto-language represents. It also represented a 
recognition of the structuralist view of the phoneme as a contrastive unit that could be

Ritsuko Kikusawa
662
expressed by allophonic units in complementary distribution or free variation. A proto-
language began to be seen as representing a real language spoken at a stage in people’s lives, 
rather than as simply a system of reference to patterns of existing sound correspondences 
found among its daughter languages. As such, the number of sounds in the proto-language 
and their systematic relationships needed to show a realistic system. This provided some 
constraints on the analyses, and forced scholars to examine possibilities such as contact 
relationships and possible later innovations. Consonants in this system were gradually 
reduced accordingly, as can be seen in Table 31.1.
The Proto-Austronesian sound system as now reconstructed is typically considered to re ect 
a system that could have existed in a real language. A reconstruction of the consonants of Proto-
Austronesian (PAn), the ancestor language of all the Austronesian languages, is presented in 
Table 31.2. Vowels and vowel-semivowel sequences have been reconstructed as: *a, *e, *i, *u, 
* -iw, * -uy, *- ay, * -aw. These symbols and those appearing in Table 31.2, are typically used in 
PAn lexical reconstructions and represent keyboard adaptations of phonetic symbols, established 
before the days of Unicode fonts. Phonetic values vary depending upon the researcher, but some 
have claimed the following: *e schwa [	]; *S a voiceless palatal fricative [ç]; *C a voiceless 
palatal affricate [tç]; *z a voiced palatal affricate []; *j a voiced velar fricative []; *R a voiced 
uvular trill [Ã]; *L a voiced lateral fricative []; and so on (see Ross 1992 for discussion).

3 Subgrouping hypotheses and problems
3.1 Reconstruction
A commonly accepted subgrouping of the Austronesian languages is shown in Figure 31.2. 
Subgrouping hypotheses of Austronesian languages are de ned by shared innovations and 
are two-sided. On one side, the membership of the languages and the genetic relationship 
among distant languages can be said to be fairly well established. On the other side, we have 
some language groups that cannot be clearly de ned.
The Malayo-Polynesian (MP, or, Extra-Formosan) group, consisting of all the Austronesian 
languages outside of mainland Taiwan, is one of the best de ned subgroups of Austronesian, 
determined by the innovations shown in (1). While some Formosan languages (i.e. the

---

<!-- page 681 -->

The Austronesian language family
663
aboriginal languages of Taiwan) share the phonological mergers shown in (a) and (b), the 
innovations shown in (c) to (f) are uniquely shared by MP languages.
(1) PMP innovations
a 
PAn *C and *t > PMP *t (merger)
b 
PAn *L and *n > PMP *n (merger)
c 
PAn *S > PMP *h (a glottal spirant)
d 
Change of the earlier second person plural genitive pronoun (*=mu) to a second 
person singular pronoun
e 
Development of a  rst person dual pronoun (*kita) not found in Formosan languages, 
alongside expanded forms for the  rst person inclusive pronoun (Reid 2011)
f 
Shared innovated lexical items, e.g., *siwa ‘nine’, which shows an irregular re ex 
of *S of PAn *Siwa ‘nine’; and *buhek ‘head hair’, which shows a metathesised 
re ex of PAn *bukeS ‘head hair’.
Possible PAn and PMP verb systems and other morphosyntactic features have been 
reconstructed, summarised in Adelaar 2005 (see also Liao 2011 for other morphological 
innovations that may be reconstructable to PMP). Likewise, Central-Eastern-Malayo-
Polynesian languages (CEMP) and Central-Malayo-Polynesian languages (CMP) are de ned 
by shared phonological, lexical, and other identi able innovations (CEMP as an innovation 
de ned subgroup, and CMP as an innovation linked group, Blust [1993]), although others 
have questioned the validity of some of the evidence that has been proposed (Donohue and 
Grimes [2008], and see Blust [2009] for a response).
The Oceanic language group, a branch of Eastern-Malayo-Polynesian, is considered to be 
another well-established subfamily and its internal subgrouping is also reasonably well 
understood (Ross 1995; Lynch et al. 2002) The innovations in Proto-Oceanic (POc) are 
commonly discussed in reference to PMP, which is a more clearly reconstructed group than 
the direct ancestor of Oceanic, namely, Eastern-Malayo-Polynesian. Shared innovations in 
Oceanic languages include a set of mergers and splits. The mergers applied to the pairs PMP 
*p/*b, *d/*r, *s/(*Z)/*j and *k/*g. Subsequently, some of the merged sounds split into a pre-
nasalised stop and a regular stop (so-called ‘oral’ and ‘nasal’ grade consonants). For example, 
the sounds *p and*b in PMP may be re ected as either *p (oral grade) or *b (nasal grade) in 
POc. Examples are presented below.
Oral grade:  
PMP *pitu ‘seven’; *bulan ‘moon’
 
 
> POc *pitu ‘seven’; POc *pulan ‘moon’
Nasal grade: PMP *punay ‘pigeon’; *beRek ‘pig’
 
 
> POc *bunay ‘pigeon’; POc *boRok ‘pig’
Possible conditions for this split have been discussed in the literature. Other characteristics of 
the Oceanic group are also summarised in Lynch et al. (2002).
Based on the general outline of the split of languages mentioned above, migration routes via 
which early Austronesian people settled the Paci c have been proposed (Figure 31.1). The 
success of determining early Paci c migration routes is particularly due to the fact that 
geographical locations and groups of languages are relatively easy to relate to each other, 
characteristics not always available with languages that are spoken on a continent. For a recent 
summary of migration hypotheses of Austronesian peoples, see, for example, Bellwood (2010).

---

<!-- page 682 -->

Ritsuko Kikusawa
664
3.2 Issues arising in establishing subgrouping hypotheses
Although the general outline of Austronesian subgrouping is agreed on, there are some 
problems in identifying subgroups. Questions regarding the status of major subgroupings are 
mostly related to those involving languages spoken in the west.
Ross (1995: 67) notes that innovations pattern across languages in two different ways, 
namely, separation and dialect differentiation. The former, Ross claims, results in discrete 
bundles of innovations shared by groups of languages, re ecting independent developments 
in separate groups, where two or more communities speaking the same proto-language were 
geographically separated, forming clearly de ned subgroups, such as Malayo-Polynesian 
discussed in the previous section. The latter results in overlapping bundles of innovations 
when groups that originally formed a dialect chain develop as separate languages, forming a 
linkage, a situation that must have characterised early stages of the dispersal of Austronesian 
languages in Taiwan, and as we will see below, the Philippines.
The  rst order subgroups are already problematic (Box A in Figure 31.2). The details of 
the relationship among Formosan languages (constituting nine of the ten  rst-order subgroups 
of Austronesian, according to Blust [1999]) are not agreed upon, and as a consequence the 
higher order subgrouping of this family is not clear (a summary appears in Tryon 1995 and 
Ross [2012]). Hypotheses have been proposed based on shared sound changes (Blust 1999); 
however, different proposals have been made based on other, apparently con icting evidence. 
Sagart (2004), for example, places weight on apparently shared lexical innovations among 
the numerals, so that he groups together languages which share *pitu ‘7’ as the Pituish group, 
while other Austronesian languages (including Malayo-Polynesian) which share re exes of 
the higher numerals *walu ‘eight’ and *Siwa ‘nine’ form his Walu-Siwaish, a subgroup of 
Pituish. The Formosan languages that do not have re exes of these numerals, such as Luilang, 
Saisiyat and Pazeh, constitute, according to Sagart,  rst order branches of Proto-Austronesian. 
Ross (2009, 2012) on the other hand proposes that Proto-Austronesian had four primary 
branches, Puyuma, Tsou, Rukai and Nuclear Austronesian (from which developed all other 
languages in the family), on the basis of proposed innovative morphology that de nes his 
four groups, but which is claimed to be absent in the  rst three languages.
It is widely agreed today that western Malayo-Polynesian languages, while clearly constituted 
of a considerable number of fairly well de ned subgroups, do not themselves form a subgroup 
de ned by shared innovations which distinguish them from those that de ne Central-Eastern-
Malayo-Polynesian. Languages and subgroups in this category are listed in Figure 31.2 
underneath the label ‘western Malayo-Polynesian’. At a local level within western Malayo-
Polynesian, deciding whether a group of languages can be identi ed as a genetically related 
subgroup or not is often a problem. For example, Blust (2005), on the basis of a number of 
uniquely shared lexical items, considers that the languages of the Philippines form a single 
group that developed from an ancestral language (Proto-Philippines) after a period of mass-
extinction of earlier Philippine languages. This is claimed to be the result of an expansion of an 
early agricultural group in the search for new lands that occurred a thousand years after the  rst 
dispersal of Malayo-Polynesian languages. Others however reject the suggestion because there 
is no evidence of phonological, morphological, or syntactic innovations that distinguish these 
languages as a group from other Malayo-Polynesian languages (Ross 2005; Pawley 2006; Reid 
2010). Furthermore there is no archaeological or other non-linguistic evidence to support it. 
The alternate claim is that language groups in the Philippines have developed from a Malayo-
Polynesian dialect chain (or linkage) that must have spread through the Philippines into 
Indonesia and Oceania in the  rst millennium after the movement out of Taiwan. Lexical

---

<!-- page 683 -->

The Austronesian language family
665
diffusion across dialect and language boundaries as a result of active trading networks provides 
a reasonable explanation for Blust’s shared lexical items. The situation is even more murky 
with Central-Malayo-Polynesian languages, which are spoken in the Maluku islands and 
surrounding areas adjacent to the area where Papuan languages are spoken, and are likely to 
have non-Austronesian substrates. Many of the proposed de ning features are not found 
throughout the group and Blust considers this group to be a linkage.
3.3 Micro- and macro-comparison, reconstruction, and the tree model
While the traditional family tree model is commonly used to represent genetic relationships 
of Austronesian languages as in Figure 31.2, the limitations of the tree model have often been 
highlighted in Austronesian historical linguistics and supplementary or alternative models 
have been proposed. For example, Geraghty (1983) proposes the Tokalau-Fijian-Polynesian 
hypothesis, where he argues that layers of dialect continua with different distributions can be 
identi ed based on shared innovations found in Central-Paci c languages, a hypothesis 
which does not  t the traditionally used family tree model (Box B in Figure 31.2). Ross 
(1988) proposes horizontal double lines to indicate a reconstructable dialect continuum, or 
‘linkage’, to be integrated into tree models replacing a node when applicable. Recently, 
François and Kalyan (2011) proposed a model that they have named ‘historical glottometry’ 
(see also François this volume). Both of these cases result from the application of the 
Comparative Method to detailed sets of language data spoken in geographically contiguous 
areas, and can be referred to as micro-level comparison and reconstruction. This is opposed 
to traditional macro-level comparison, in which reconstruction is based on comparison of 
languages that are relatively distant both geographically and temporally since their dispersal.
In a micro-level comparison and reconstruction, because of the proximity of the languages, 
the results of the application of the Comparative Method – sound correspondences and shared 
lexical and morphosyntactic innovations – include not only the results of (vertical) 
transmission, but also diffusion (or, horizontal transmission) and possibly drift. This is 
probably true with the developmental processes of any set of related languages, however, it 
becomes problematic when languages examined are geographically and temporally close.
It should be noted that so far it has primarily been the results of family-wide comparison 
(or macro-comparison) that have been used to establish migration hypotheses. It is possible 
that those based on micro-comparison will suggest hypotheses of a different sort. Questions 
arising from micro-level comparison and reconstruction ultimately relate to the question of 
the degree to which a proto-language is considered a real language having the properties of 
one as spoken today, including all of its social, geographical and other dialectal variety. 
However, before the reconstruction of any of these features becomes possible, the 
identi cation of linguistic features that are the end result of the different paths of language 
change, namely, transmission, diffusion and drift need to be examined. These are discussed 
in section 4 below.
4 Identifying transmission, diffusion, and drift
4.1 Direct and indirect inheritances
One of the outcomes of the tradition of detailed and careful examination of sound 
correspondences to determine genetic relationships among member languages in Austronesian 
historical linguistics is the method to identify contact relationships among closely related

---

<!-- page 684 -->

Ritsuko Kikusawa
666
languages. While borrowed items are commonly identi ed through irregular sound 
correspondences, among closely related languages borrowed items often also result in sets of 
regular correspondences. Rotuman (an Eastern-Oceanic language) is well-known for 
displaying these multiple layers of correspondences, but it was Biggs (1965) who proposed 
that it was the sound correspondence sets that occurred in basic vocabulary that were the 
result of direct inheritance from the proto-language, while other sound correspondence sets 
identi ed borrowed items. Some of the sound correspondences found in Rotuman are listed in 
(2). Example words contrasting the two correspondences are shown in (3). In addition, it has 
long been known that there are at least two different strata in the borrowed items, which were 
identi ed by Biggs based on different re exes of some phoneme correspondences.
(2) Two layers of sound correspondences found in Rotuman (from Schmidt 2003)

Meanings: (1) ‘to fetch water’; (2) ‘to serve kava’; (3) ‘head lice’; (4) ‘to push  ngernails into skull’; 
(5) ‘to spread out’; (6) ‘to tell’.
Biggs preferred the term ‘indirect inheritance’ to ‘borrowings’, for
all of the words with etymologies were once part of a language ancestral to Rotuman in 
the comparativist’s sense. Some of them however re-entered Rotuman from a collateral 
related language after undergoing changes other than those which affected forms which 
had remained continuously in the Rotuman line.
(Biggs 1965: 390)
Schmidt (2003), following on Biggs (1965), identi es multiple lexical strata in Rotuman and 
suggests temporal and geographical sources for the borrowings (see Figure 31.3). Other 
papers reporting on stratal inheritance in other Austronesian languages include Dyen (1956) 
for Ngaju-Dayak (a Barito language of Indonesia), Blust (1991) for Tiruray (a southern 
Philippine language), and Ross (1996a) for Yapese (an Oceanic language spoken in 
Micronesia).
4.2 Metatypic diffusion of syntactic patterns
A related problem, not dealing with phonology, but with morphosyntactic diffusion or 
calquing in contact situations is discussed by Ross (1996b) under the name of ‘metatypy’. 
This occurs as the result of bilingualism, during which a language that is emblematic of a

---

<!-- page 685 -->

The Austronesian language family
667
Figure 31.3 Borrowed items in Rotuman and their sources (Based on Schmidt 2003: 235)
community reorganises its semantics and subsequently its morphosyntax on the model of 
another completely unrelated language that functions as an intercommunity language (see 
Lucas, this volume). The language Ross uses to discuss the phenomenon is Takia (a Western 
Oceanic language), which is spoken on Karkar Island, alongside Waskia, a non-Austronesian 
language of the Madang branch of Trans-New Guinea languages. The result is a language 
whose morphology is clearly Oceanic, but whose syntax is Papuanised.

<!-- page 686 -->

Ritsuko Kikusawa

4.3 Lexical reconstruction and its contribution to the understanding of 
culture history
As has been seen with the case of Rotuman, reconstruction of lexical items and the clari cation 
of culture history based on the reconstructions are intimately connected. Knowledge of 
historical events helps to identify the source of borrowed terms, while linguistic reconstruction, 
in particular of lexical items, plays an important role in clarifying the understanding of the 
prehistory of the speakers’ community (Epps, this volume). In fact, Blust (1995: 454) claims 
that the study of culture history of Austronesians is the area where “linguistic comparison 
offered a window on the past … long before archaeological research was suf ciently 
advanced to provide an alternative perspective.”
The principle is that when a term is reconstructable for a proto-language, then its referent 
can be assumed to have existed and been recognised by the speakers in the society where this 
language was spoken. When a term undergoes horizontal transmission (either between 
genetically related or non-related languages), it can be assumed either that the speakers of the 
languages were in contact with each other, either directly or indirectly, and that the referent 
material was conveyed along with the term referring to it. For Proto-Austronesian, as well as 
many daughter proto-languages, we have an accumulation of reconstructed vocabulary, which 
are found in (Blust and Trussel n.d.), and Wolff (2010). A currently four-volume lexicon of 
reconstructed cultural items, social organisation, cosmology and  ora and fauna for Proto-
Oceanic is now available (Ross et al. 1998, 2003, 2008, 2011). Discussion relating linguistic 
reconstruction to culture history appears in Blust (1995), Pawley (2002, 2007) and Zorc (1994).
5 Morphosyntactic comparison and reconstruction
With typologically diverse morphosyntactic features found throughout the family, 
morphosyntactic comparison and reconstruction in Austronesian languages are both 
interesting and challenging tasks. In earlier works, ‘grammatical reconstruction’ often referred 
to the lexical reconstruction of function words, which include various af xes, case-marking 
prepositions, determiners and clitic pronouns (e.g., Pawley, 1970, 1973). Such reconstruction 
was typically conducted within groups of typologically homogeneous languages, where the 
form–function connection is reasonably consistent. Recently, some work has been done on 
the reconstruction of verb morphology, associating it with known syntactic patterns, for early 
stages of Austronesian languages (Wolff 1973; Ross 2009). However, there is much still to be 
done. The general characteristics of the languages in the family meet the conditions listed by 
Harris (2008) which she thinks favour reconstructability of syntactic patterns: (i) [sentence] 
patterns that are especially complex; (ii) syntax that has a morphological correlate; and (iii) 
the existence of phonological correspondence. On the other hand, analyses are relatively free 
from the negative conditions that Harris (2008) considers made the syntactic reconstruction of 
Indo-European languages dif cult. For example, many Austronesian languages typically do 
not have strict clausal word order, and the developmental path of languages with a  xed word 
order can be explained through the examination of the fossilised positions of core arguments, 
the analyses of grammatical roles of such arguments combined with the valency system 
(Aldridge 2010; Kikusawa 2003; Starosta et al. 2009a). Austronesian historical syntax then 
has good potential to contribute to knowledge about syntactic change.
There are two major topics of interest in recent works that involve syntactic reconstruction. 
These are the development and change of argument structures, which in Austronesian linguistics 
are commonly described as ‘voice systems’, and the development and change of case-marking 
systems, where changes between ergative and accusative systems come into question.

---

<!-- page 687 -->

The Austronesian language family
669
Austronesian languages, in particular Formosan and many western Austronesian ones, the 
so-called ‘Philippine-type’ languages, are known for their elaborate verb morphology, by 
which not only ‘voice’ contrasts are signalled, but also tense, aspect and modal functions are 
assigned. A reconstruction of PAn verbal morphology is cited from Adelaar (2005) in Table 
31.3.
Descriptive problems centre on terminological issues, such as how to label the single 
nominal argument of an intransitive clause and its formally corresponding argument in a 
transitive construction, which has been referred to by such terms as subject, topic, focus, 
pivot, nominative, or absolutive among others. Other problems involve theoretical issues, 
such as whether the systems should be described as accusative, ergative, active, symmetrical 
or something else. Yet other arguments consider whether the different structures are in fact 
‘voice’ contrasts in the European sense of the term (Himmelmann 2005), whether the so-
called ‘passive’ structures of early descriptions are in fact passives (Reid 2009; Winkler 
2011, etc.), whether it is transitivity that is the key notion in distinguishing different structures, 
and whether some of the syntactic alternations are actually applicative constructions. Thus 
the historical comparison and reconstruction of data from these languages  rst faces the 
problem of how to handle sets of data where inconsistent analyses have been applied, as well 
as the lack of an established methodology.
Another topic of ongoing interest is the ‘ergative–accusative controversy’ of Proto-
Polynesian. While both ergative and accusative systems are found in Polynesian languages, 
debate centres on the case alignment of their historical source, with various authors claiming 
that the ergative alignment of PMP was retained through to Proto-Polynesian and that the 
predominantly accusative structures of Oceanic languages are evidence of an ergative to 
accusative drift (Clark 1976; Kikusawa 2002, and others), while others consider that Proto-
Polynesian had an accusative alignment and the ergative structures of its daughter languages 
are innovative (Ball 2007; Chung 1978 and others). One of the interesting points about this 
issue is that the researchers all agree upon the sentence structures that must have existed in 
Proto-Polynesian. However, it is the analysis of the forms that mark the case of NPs in each 
sentence structure and the transitivity (of the verb) that cannot be agreed upon. It appears that 
there is a general tendency that those who apply a generative theoretical framework come to 
the conclusion that the proto-language was accusative, while those who try to incorporate the 
Comparative Method and lexically reconstructed forms into their analysis come to the 
conclusion that it was ergative.
While many of the issues involve typological considerations, the accumulated work on 
sound correspondences based lexical reconstruction enables the identi cation of cognate 
clause structures and probable directions of change, thus combining pattern comparison with
